source.title: Platform Operator Lifecycle Automation Cookbook
source.author: Marcus Lin
source.content:
Summary:
This cookbook describes a standardized, automation-first approach to the platform operator lifecycle at Redwood Inference. It covers operator onboarding, preflight validations, automated certification and proctoring, staged change rollouts, offboarding, and continuous skills/knowledge validation. The goal is to reduce manual handoffs, compress time-to-competency, and ensure consistent operator responses during incidents and maintenance windows.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Platform and SRE operator lifecycle activities that touch the platform operator surface (prefetch/cache operators, deployment operators, capacity operators, regional failover).
- Audience: Platform Engineering (primary), Eng-SRE, Team Leads, Hiring/People Ops (secondary).

Goals:
1. Automate repetitive operator onboarding tasks so new operators reach certified readiness within 10 business days.
2. Guarantee reproducible preflight checks for all major runbooks with >= 95% automation coverage.
3. Ensure every major rollout includes an automated preflight, synthetic smoke, and an operator-verified rollback gate.
4. Provide continuous microlearning and certification refreshes using CI-driven quizzes and synthetic scenarios.

Definitions and abbreviations:
- Operator: Platform team member responsible for day-to-day platform operations and incident response.
- Preflight: Automated checks executed before a change is rolled to production.
- Proctoring job: An automated or human-verified certification step.
- Certification: A lightweight check (practical + MCQ) that validates operator competency for specific responsibilities.

Ownership:
- Document owner: Platform Engineering (eng-platform)
- Lead maintainer: Marcus Lin (Platform Reliability)
- Escalation owner for automation failures: Eng-SRE oncall

Lifecycle phases and automation patterns:
1) Candidate on-boarding (days 0–3)
   - Automated tasks: account provisioning (IAM groups), repo ACLs, tool access (PagerDuty, VictorOps), credential vault access (KMS role binding), Slack channels and runbook links.
   - Human tasks: meet-the-mentor pairing, shadow two game-day exercises.
   - Automation pattern: onboarding-orchestrator pipeline triggered from People Ops PR that runs terraform apply for IAM, creates a GitHub team, and provisions a training environment namespace. Sample CLI: 
     - hrctl create-hire --name "Jane Doe" --team platform --role operator
     - ./ops-scripts/onboard.sh --github-team platform-operators --iam-role op-basic

2) Preflight & certification (days 2–7)
   - Automated preflight harnesses executed via CI (Argo Workflows/GitHub Actions). Preflight outputs a JSON artifact with pass/fail, diagnostics, and remediation suggestions.
   - Certification: Two-stage automatic proctoring flow: (a) automated practical scenario (synthetic incident run) that the candidate must remediate in a sandbox; (b) short MCQ quiz that opens once practical passes.
   - SLO: Certification job must succeed in < 90 minutes end-to-end under normal conditions.

3) Ongoing operator hygiene (continuous)
   - Weekly microlearning: short quizzes pushed to Slack via the operator-bot with automated scoring.
   - Quarterly synthetic scenarios: runbook drills injected into a sandbox and graded. Failure triggers a remediation plan and mandatory retraining.

4) Offboarding and access revocation (within 1 business day)
   - Offboarding pipeline triggered from People Ops ticket. Automation removes group memberships, revokes vault access, and rotates any shared credentials. A final reconciliation job verifies removal.

Automation architecture and components:
- Orchestrator: Argo Workflows for long-running preflight and certification pipelines.
- Registry: A single-source-of-truth YAML registry (repos/platform-operator-lifecycle/registry/) that defines persona -> responsibilities -> certification matrix.
- Runner pool: Dedicated CI runners with network-isolated sandboxes used for synthetic incident injections.
- Notifier: operator-bot (Slack) + email for asynchronous prompts and confirmations.
- Audit: All pipeline runs emit structured audit-events to the platform-audit-log Kafka topic.

Standardized artifacts and schemas:
- Preflight artifact (JSON fields):
  - run_id (string)
  - persona (string)
  - checks (list of {name, status, diagnostics_url})
  - duration_ms (int)
  - outcome (pass|fail|warn)
- Certification record (CSV/JSON): operator_id, cert_scope, cert_version, issued_at, expires_at, proctor_id

Example preflight workflow (high-level steps):
1. Pull change metadata from PR (files changed, models touched, config flags).
2. Run static checks (schema validation, feature flag gating).
3. Simulate traffic profile with a synthetic client for 30s.
4. Run smoke assertions (SLO regression, error-rate, KV cache hit rate).
5. Publish artifact and notify operator for manual gates.

Sample automation YAML (Argo-style, illustrative):
---
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Workflow
metadata:
  generateName: preflight-deploy-
spec:
  entrypoint: preflight-pipeline
  templates:
    - name: preflight-pipeline
      steps:
        - - name: static-checks
            template: static-checks
        - - name: synthetic-load
            template: synthetic-load
        - - name: smoke-asserts
            template: smoke-asserts
    - name: static-checks
      script:
        image: python:3.11
        command: ["python", "-u", "/scripts/static_checks.py"]
    - name: synthetic-load
      script:
        image: ghcr.io/redwood/synthetic-client:latest
        command: ["/bin/synthetic-run"]
        args: ["--profile", "canary-30s"]
    - name: smoke-asserts
      script:
        image: ghcr.io/redwood/platform-tools:latest
        command: ["/bin/asserts"]
        args: ["--thresholds","/configs/smoke_thresholds.yaml"]

Operator runbook templates (use for human + automation integration):
- Routine capacity change (5–10% pool resize):
  1) Open PR against infra-configs with resize target and workload forecasts.
  2) CI kicks preflight; if PASS -> schedule maintenance window in calendar service.
  3) Run automated canary (1–3 hosts) and run smoke asserts.
  4) Operator verifies metrics and clicks a single-button approve in the console which triggers the roll.
  5) Post-roll automated verification job runs (token latency, cache hit, GPU utilization).
  6) If any verification fails, automated rollback runs and notifies PagerDuty.

- Emergency model swap (token surge/failure):
  1) Trigger emergency-model-swap workflow (button or CLI).
  2) Workflow activates hot-model variant in target region, re-routes < 5% traffic gradually, and runs real-time quality checks.
  3) If model-quality alarms (per-eval regression) exceed threshold, workflow automatically lowers traffic and pages the model-owner.

Dashboards and alerting guidance for lifecycle automation: 
- Dashboard groups per lifecycle phase: Onboarding health, Certification pass-rate, Preflight success-rate, Operator response times, Offboard reconciliation.
- Recommended SLI thresholds (example):
  - Preflight success-rate (rolling 30d): target >= 95%
  - Average certification completion time: target <= 2 business days after sandbox access granted
  - Offboard reconciliation completion: target <= 24h
- Alerts to create:
  1) preflight-failure-spike: fires when failure rate > 10% in 1h with > 10 runs
  2) certification-backlog: > 20 pending cert jobs older than 48h
  3) offboard-party-failure: missing reconciliation artifact after offboard job failure

Incident playbooks for automation failures (runbook excerpt):
1) Detect: preflight-failure alert triggers.
2) Triage: operator inspects latest preflight artifact, checks diagnostics_url, and looks for common failures (schema mismatch, synthetic step timeout, network partition).
3) Remediate: if transient (time-series spike), re-run preflight with increased timeout; if systemic (schema change), open infra-config PR and revert offending change if necessary.
4) Post-incident: update registry with root cause, add synthetic test covering the regression, and schedule a 2-day training.

Continuous improvement and metrics to track:
- Time-to-certify (median + 90th percentile)
- Preflight automation coverage (ratio of automated checks to total checks in all runbooks)
- Mean time to reconcile offboard failures
- Number of synthetic scenarios added after incidents (per quarter)

Roadmap items (next 12 months):
- Q2 2027: Add adaptive preflight: preflight dynamically configures test intensity based on PR change surface area.
- Q3 2027: Integrate ML-driven proctoring to grade incident remediations for nuance and operator decision quality.
- Q4 2027: Cross-team certification transfer workflow so platform operators can delegate temporary responsibilities to product SREs.

Runbook maintenance and authoring rules:
- Keep runbooks executable: every runbook must be runnable from the CI runner in a sandbox environment.
- One-runbook-one-responsibility: split complex runbooks into atomic substeps with clear input/outputs.
- Every runbook must include: preflight-id, owner, expected artifact location, rollback command, and audit-emit statement.

Appendix A — Example shell helpers (operator use):
- Re-run a preflight with diagnostics: 
  ./ops-scripts/rerun_preflight.sh --run-id 2027-02-09-abc123 --debug
- Force revoke user and verify: 
  ./ops-scripts/offboard.sh --user j.doe@example.com --verify

Appendix B — Example monitor rule (Prometheus-like pseudo):
- preflight_failure_rate = sum(rate(preflight_runs_failed_total[5m])) / sum(rate(preflight_runs_total[5m]))
- Alert: preflight-failure-spike if preflight_failure_rate > 0.10 for 1h and preflight_runs_total > 10

Appendix C — Frequently asked questions:
Q: How long before automation is required for a runbook?
A: Any runbook called >= 3x per quarter must have >= 50% automation coverage.

Q: Who signs off changes to the certification matrix?
A: The Platform Lead (owner) + Eng-SRE manager must sign off any scope expansion.

Document change log:
- 2026-11-15: Drafted initial cookbook (Marcus Lin)
- 2026-12-08: Added certification flow and example Argo YAML (Priya Kapoor)
- 2027-02-10: Published with roadmap and dashboard SLI thresholds (Diane Chen)

Contact: platform-automation@redwood-inference.internal for questions or to propose registry changes.

Contact: platform-automation@redwood-inference.internal for questions or to propose registry changes.

source.title: Cost-aware SLI Degradation Incident Response Playbook
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body:
Overview:

This playbook describes detection, triage, and mitigation steps for incidents where service-level indicators (SLIs) degrade due to cost-driven behavior (e.g., aggressive autoscaling, batch sizing, routing to high-cost capacity) rather than pure software faults. It documents practical on-call actions, decision criteria for manual intervention, and post-incident follow-up requirements.

Purpose:
- Reduce time-to-mitigation for SLI degradation caused or amplified by cost controls.
- Standardize operator decisions when balancing latency, throughput, and spend.
- Provide reproducible mitigation steps that preserve customer-facing availability and reduce unexpected billing exposure.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood API and Dedicated serving surfaces (hosted endpoints, model routing, GPU pools).
- Targets degradations where cost signals (billing spikes, quota enforcement, eviction events, rapid instance churn) correlate with SLI deterioration.
- Not intended for pure application logic bugs, DB outages, or network-caused region failures (see separate runbooks).

Key SLIs and definitions:
1) Frontend latency SLI: p95 response time for generation requests at token window 0-256 = `frontend_latency_p95_0_256` (Prometheus metric: redwood_request_duration_seconds_bucket).
2) Token throughput SLI: tokens/sec per-customer across model route = `tokens_per_sec_by_tenant`.
3) Error SLI: rate of 5xx responses and model fallback events = `request_error_rate_5xx + model_fallback_rate`.
4) Cost SLI (derived): normalized cost-per-token over 10m window = `cost_per_token_10m` (billing export + telemetry join).

Alert tiers and thresholds (recommended):
- Warning (page): frontend_latency_p95_0_256 > 600ms for 10m AND cost_per_token_10m up 2x vs baseline for 15m.
- Critical (page + phone): frontend_latency_p95_0_256 > 1.2s for 5m OR sustained region-side evictions > 1% of GPU pool in 5m.

PromQL detection examples (copy into Prometheus alert editor):
- latency-warning:
  
  avg_over_time(histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))[10m:5m]) > 0.6

- cost-spike-indicator:
  
  increase(redwood_billing_tokens_total[15m]) / max(sum(increase(redwood_request_count_total[15m])) by (route), 1) > 2 * oncall_baseline_cost_per_token

Dashboard panels to inspect (Grafana):
- Global SLI heatmap: p50/p95/p99 side-by-side with cost_per_token_10m and evictions_by_region.
- Tenant scatter: tokens/sec vs p95 latency per tenant (last 15m).
- Pool health: GPU allocation, preemption_count, spare_capacity_pct.
- Model-routing trace: request path and fallbacks for sample request IDs.

Initial triage checklist (steps 1-6; do them in order):
1) Confirm alert: open linked dashboard and cross-check SLI graphs for the last 30m. Record timestamps and alert IDs.
2) Identify correlation with cost signals: check billing spike annotation, autoscaler activity log, and eviction metrics.
3) Determine blast radius: run the tenant scatter query to find whether a single tenant or many tenants are impacted.
   - Query example (Prometheus):
     topk(10, sum(rate(redwood_tokens_generated_total[5m])) by (tenant))
4) Check recent deploys and config changes: review canary/orchestration logs and rollout events in the last 60m.
5) Check capacity pressure: examine GPU pool memory utilization and queued requests (metrics: gpu_mem_pct, queue_length_by_pool).
6) If cost/eviction signals are present, move to cost-mitigation decision matrix. If not, escalate to SRE for network/infra diagnostics.

Decision matrix: when to act manually vs let automated policies run
- If degrade is localized to one tenant and tokens/sec spikes: throttle tenant (manual soft-quota) and notify customer success.
- If degrade spans >10% tenants and eviction rate >0.5%: open capacity (temporarily increase burst pool or enable reserved capacity override).
- If cost_per_token rises >3x baseline but latency remains within warning band: defer aggressive scaling; enable KV prefix cache warming and adjust batching heuristics.

Mitigation playbook (ordered mitigations, from least to most disruptive):
1) Soft throttles (per-tenant): reduce max_tokens or enforce a temporary per-minute token cap via the request-gateway. Command: `redwood-ctl tenant-throttle set --tenant <TENANT> --tokens-per-minute 10000 --reason "mitigation:cost-surge"`
2) Route to compatible cheaper model variant: update routing policy for affected route to `model:x-small-quant` with 1% canary then 10% step. CLI: `redwood routing apply --route <ROUTE> --policy cheaper-variant --step 10`
3) Adjust batching window heuristics (operator-only): lower max_batch_size for latency-critical routes to reduce tail latency from queuing. Config edit (example YAML):

  max_batch_size: 8
  max_batch_wait_ms: 8

4) Temporarily increase burst capacity / capacity override: only if autoscaler has throttled up/down frequently and we see drop in 5xx. Runbook CLI: `infra-ops capacity-override --pool gpu-pool-eu-west --add 2 --ttl 30m --owner oncall`
5) If root cause is evictions due to spot preemption, shift critical customer traffic to dedicated (reserved) nodes and mark preemptible pools as low-priority.

Rollback and safety checks:
- Always run small-scale validation after a mitigation (10 requests/run) to verify p95 improvement and error rate.
- Validate cost telemetry for the 15m window after action to avoid chasing false positives.
- Annotate incident timeline in the alert ticket with mitigation commands and IDs.

Escalation and ownership:
- First responder: platform oncall (engineering) — own triage & execute mitigations.
- If mitigations fail within 30 minutes: escalate to SRE lead and runtime-owner (eng-serving-runtime).
- For customer-facing throttles: inform Customer Success (CS) and Solutions Engineering within 30 minutes.

Post-incident requirements (RCA expectations):
1) Publish a short incident summary within 48 hours to `/incidents` with timeline, root cause, mitigations, and next steps.
2) Complete a permanent fix backlog ticket (label: sev:postmortem) owned by runtime or platform with target 30-day remediation.
3) Add or tune alerts to reduce false positives and better separate cost-driven vs infra-driven degradations.

Playbook test and rehearsal:
- Quarterly table-top: run a simulated cost-surge scenario using replayed billing events and synthetic traffic. Verify operator run time < 20 minutes for typical mitigations.
- Monthly dashboard smoke test: run a script that validates panel queries and alert fire conditions against a synthetic metrics importer.

Instrumentation and telemetry requirements (implementation checklist):
- Ensure cost_per_token_10m is surfaced and annotated with billing export IDs.
- Eviction metrics must be tagged by pool, region, and instance-type.
- Tenant attribution should be available for top N customers (10) with per-route breakdown.

Config table (operator knobs):
- soft_throttle_tpm_default: 15000
- max_batch_size_latency_routes: 8
- burst_capacity_ttl_default: 30m
- cost_spike_multiplier_threshold: 2.5

Example Prometheus alert rule (YAML):

groups:
- name: cost-aware-sli.rules
  rules:
  - alert: CostAwareFrontendLatencyWarning
    expr: >
      avg_over_time(histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, route))[10m:5m]) > 0.6
      and increase(redwood_billing_tokens_total[15m]) / max(sum(increase(redwood_request_count_total[15m])) by (route), 1) > 2 * oncall_baseline_cost_per_token
    for: 10m
    labels:
      severity: page
    annotations:
      summary: "Frontend p95 rising with concurrent cost spike (route={{ $labels.route }})"

Operators: quick commands and queries (cheat sheet)
- List hot tenants by tokens/sec: `promql "topk(20, sum(rate(redwood_tokens_generated_total[5m])) by (tenant))"`
- Check GPU pool evictions: `promql "increase(gpu_preemptions_total[15m]) by (pool)"`
- Apply a soft throttle: `redwood-ctl tenant-throttle set --tenant <TENANT> --tokens-per-minute <N> --reason "mitigation"`
- Rotate to cheaper model: `redwood routing apply --route <ROUTE> --policy cheaper-variant --step 5`

Retention and audit:
- All manual mitigations MUST be annotated in the incident ticket with a timestamp and operator username.
- Audit logs for routing changes and capacity overrides are retained for 90 days by default.

Roadmap and improvements (short-term):
- Automate a safe first-mitigation step: automatic per-tenant soft-throttle for top-5 noisy tenants when cost_per_token > threshold.
- Add an alert variant that auto-attaches billing-extractor traces to the ticket.
- Build a runbook automation that can execute the cheaper-model route change in a canary-first manner with automatic rollback.

Appendix: useful links and references
- Runbook: /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026
- Dashboard: grafana/redwood/platform/cost-aware-sli-heatmap
- Playbook template: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/operator-playbook-template

Contact owners:
- Platform oncall alias: #platform-oncall
- Runtime owners: eng-serving-runtime@redwood.com
- Billing telemetry: infra-observability@redwood.com

source.title: operator-surface-modernization-roadmap
source.author: Ava Chen
source.summary: A two-year roadmap to modernize the platform operator surface: centralizing operator tools, improving telemetry and SLO feedback loops, and reducing cognitive load for on-call and maintenance workflows.
source.body:
Overview

This document describes the Operator Surface Modernization roadmap for the eng-platform team. The goal is to deliver a coherent operator experience across Dedicated, Hosted, and Private deployments by improving tooling, dashboards, telemetry, runbooks, and automation that operators use during normal operations and incidents. This is a prioritized, cross-functional plan with measurable success criteria and a staged rollout approach.

Goals (Why)

- Reduce mean-time-to-detect (MTTD) for platform degradations by 40%
- Reduce mean-time-to-recover (MTTR) for common GPU-pool and routing incidents by 30%
- Decrease operator cognitive load by standardizing runbooks and consolidating dashboards into a single Operator Cockpit
- Improve SLO observability and root-cause feedback loops so product teams can make faster trade-offs between cost and latency

Success metrics (What we will measure)

- MTTD and MTTR for top-10 incident classes (baseline: Q2 2026)
- Number of manual runbook steps per remediation (target: <= 5 for prioritized incidents)
- Time spent on on-call escalations per week (target: -25% YOY)
- Operator satisfaction (quarterly survey target: >= 4.2/5)
- Percentage of production traffic covered by Operator Cockpit pages (target: 90%)

Scope (In-scope / Out-of-scope)

In-scope:
- Operator-facing UI and dashboards used by platform/operators (Operator Cockpit)
- Primary runbooks for GPU pools, KV-prefix cache, model swap, routing fallbacks, and capacity evictions
- Telemetry schema standardization and SLO feedback pipelines
- Automation playbooks (safe remediation actions operators can trigger)

Out-of-scope (for this roadmap):
- Public developer dashboards consumed by customers
- Major rewrite of serving runtime kernels (runtime perf work continues separately)

Milestones and timeline (high level)

- Q4 2026: Discovery & pilot
  1. Operator interviews and event walk-throughs (4 teams: Platform, Serving Runtime, SRE, Solutions)
  2. Inventory operator tools and existing dashboards; map critical pain points
  3. Deliver Pilot Operator Cockpit for GPU pools + KV prefix cache (read-only)

- Q1 2027: Core build
  1. Implement unified telemetry schema (tokenized SLI tags, event IDs)
  2. Build writable Operator Cockpit surfaces for safe remediations (drain/evict/scale)
  3. Publish canonical runbooks and integrate with cockpit execution hooks

- Q2 2027: Automations and SLO feedback
  1. Deploy automated remediation playbooks behind feature flags (canary to single region)
  2. Add SLO causal traces into the cockpit (trace links + precomputed impact scores)
  3. SRE/Operator training + gamedays

- Q3 2027: Wider rollout & metrics
  1. Rollout to multi-region production pools; iterate on UX from operator feedback
  2. Dashboard consolidation and alert rationalization (reduce alert count by 30%)
  3. Quarterly retrospective and roadmap refresh

Implementation plan (how)

1. Discovery artifacts (Q4 2026):
   - Operator interview notes (template) and event replay correlating dashboards, traces, and logs
   - Critical incident exemplar list (top 10 classes) and the current remediation flow for each

2. Telemetry foundation (Q1 2027):
   - Adopt the centralized telemetry schema: 
     - EventID (string)
     - SLI_TAGS (list of tokens): [route_id, model_variant, tenant_priority]
     - IMPACT_SCORE (numeric string)
   - Backfill SLI tags in trace ingestion pipeline (Kafka topic: telemetry-ingest-v2)

3. Operator Cockpit (Q1–Q2 2027):
   - UX principles:
     - Single-pane focusing on active degradation vectors
     - Clear separation between monitoring (read-only) and actions (writable)
     - Action gating with preflight checks and undo windows
   - Key screens:
     - Overview: active degradations, SLO burn chart, top impacted tenants
     - Resource view: GPU pool health, eviction queues, preflight checks
     - Incident view: correlated traces, recent remediation history, suggested next steps

4. Runbooks and safe-actions (Q1–Q3 2027):
   - Canonical runbooks authored in this repo and surfaced in cockpit; runbooks will include:
     - Preconditions (observability thresholds)
     - Step-by-step with CLI and UI action links
     - Validation checks and rollback steps
   - Example safe action: "EvictLowPriorityBatchJobs"
     - Preconditions: pool_utilization > 85% for 5m AND preemptible_jobs_percent > 30%
     - Steps (automated): scale_eviction_queue --pool {pool_id} --target 25% --dry-run=false
     - Undo: scale_eviction_queue --pool {pool_id} --target 0 --dry-run=false

Monitoring & SLOs

- SLOs to expose in cockpit (examples):
  - Serving latency P95 per model route: 200ms (target)
  - Token throughput per GPU: 1.2M tokens/hour (target)
  - Prefix-KV cache hit ratio: >= 88% (target)
  - GPU pool availability: 99.95% monthly uptime objective

- Alerts rationalization pattern:
  1. Replace low-signal CPU/Memory alerts with composite SLI alerts (e.g., cache_hit_drop causes latency increase)
  2. Tokenize alerts with SLI_TAGS so routing and tenant ownership are visible in pager payloads
  3. Route low-impact alerts to async channels (Slack #platform-deferred) and high-impact SLO burn to on-call pager

Runbook snippets (example table)

| Incident class | Quick trigger | First 3 operator steps | Safe-action available |
|----------------|---------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| GPU pool evictions | eviction_rate > 3/min | 1. Confirm pool_id and eviction queue
 2. Check recent kernel OOM traces
 3. Check prioritized tenants | EvictLowPriorityBatchJobs |
| Prefix cache thrash | cache_hit_ratio < 70% | 1. Identify hot prefixes
 2. Flush targeted prefixes
 3. Throttle large tenants | FlushPrefixSet |
| Model-swap failure | model_start_errors > 5/min | 1. Verify image compatibility
 2. Swap to fallback variant
 3. Monitor latency | SwapToFallbackModel |

Training & rollout (people)

- Training plan:
  - Operators: hands-on cockpit sandbox + playbook walkthroughs (2 weeks per region)
  - SRE rotation: pair with Platform on-call for first 6 weeks post-rollout
  - Product & Solutions: tabletop exercises for customer-impact scenarios

- Communication:
  - Pre-rollout notes to stakeholders 2 weeks prior
  - Release notes and runbook changes in the regular release cadence
  - Post-rollout survey after 4 weeks

Dependencies and constraints

- Telemetry ingestion pipeline changes (owner: eng-infra)
- Minor API additions to runtime to expose safe-action hooks (owner: eng-serving-runtime)
- RBAC changes and audit logging to track operator actions (owner: security-and-compliance)
- Capacity for pilot automation (QA & staging clusters)

Risks & mitigations

- Risk: Unsafe automations cause cascading degradations
  - Mitigation: Start with read-only actions, then gated automated playbooks with canary region, kill-switch, and undo window
- Risk: Operators default to automation and bypass critical thinking
  - Mitigation: Preflight validation steps and mandatory confirmation messages for actions that affect >5% of traffic
- Risk: Telemetry schema changes break third-party consumers
  - Mitigation: Contracted schema versioning and migration window (six weeks)

Owner, stakeholders, and escalation

- Roadmap owner: Ava Chen (eng-platform)
- Primary stakeholders: Eng-Serving-Runtime (Diego Martinez), Eng-Infra (Priya Nair), Eng-SRE (Marta Singh), Security (Noah Patel), Customer Success (Liam O’Reilly)
- Escalation path (operator-visible): Operator -> Platform on-call -> Runtime on-call -> CTO on-call (for Sev1)

Appendix A: Example config fragments

- Telemetry tag example (JSON line to produce into telemetry-ingest-v2):
  {"event_id":"E-2027-operator-evict-001", "sli_tags": ["route:chat:default", "tenant:acme", "priority:low"], "impact_score": "0.38"}

- CLI safe-action example:
  # Dry-run eviction of low priority batch jobs
  redwoodctl operator evict-jobs --pool gpu-pool-7 --priority low --dry-run

Appendix B: Retro cadence (how we'll iterate)

- Quarterly retrospective focusing on operator workflow metrics and alert noise
- Bi-weekly UX feedback sessions during pilot and first two quarters of rollout
- Monthly dashboard and SLI hygiene sweep owned by eng-platform metrics guild

Next steps (immediate)

1. Approve discovery budget and schedule operator interviews (owner: Ava)
2. Create telemetry schema PR and start consumer migration plan (owner: Priya)
3. Provision pilot cockpit environment and seed with GPU pool data (owner: Diego)

Contacts

- Document owner: ava.chen@redwood-inference.internal
- Slack: #eng-platform, paging: platform-oncall

source.title: Observability cost-sandboxing pilot retrospective
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry sprawl, measure actual cost impact of new instrumentation, and shorten feedback loops for SLI owners.

Background:

- Problem statement:
  - Over the past 18 months we onboarded ~120 new telemetry consumers (dashboards, alerts, continuous guards). Many were created without explicit ownership or cost estimates.
  - Result: increasing ingestion bills, alert noise, and SLI signal duplication across teams.

- Pilot hypothesis:
  1) A sandboxed pathway (dev -> sandbox -> prod) with cost accounting and a lightweight review gate will reduce unnecessary production telemetry by >= 30% for a sampled set of changes.
  2) Cost visibility to authors will change instrumentation design choices (sampling, aggregation, retention).

Scope and participants:

- Scope: ingestion-level metrics, histogram series, and derived SLI series generated by platform and model-serving subsystems. Logs and traces were out-of-scope for the initial pilot.
- Participating teams: eng-platform (obs infra), eng-serving-runtime, eng-sre, two product engineering squads (Search + Chat).
- Pilot coordinator: Ava Chen (eng-platform).

Timeline:

1) 2026-09-01: Kickoff, requirements and success metrics defined.
2) 2026-09-15: Sandbox environment provisioned with cost tagging and chargeback wiring.
3) 2026-09-20 — 2026-10-31: Instrumentation and consumer onboarding; weekly sandbox review meetings.
4) 2026-11-01 — 2026-11-14: Promotion decisions and wrap-up measurements.

Design of the sandbox workflow:

- Steps for a telemetry change:
  1. Author creates instrumentation proposal (PR + Telemetry Change Template). Template fields: purpose, expected cardinality, retention needs, estimated cost (monthly), candidate SLI mapping.
  2. Deploy to sandbox prefix (metrics.*.sandbox.<team>) for 2 weeks. Sandbox enforces: reduced retention (default 7d), stricter cardinality caps, and cost tags.
  3. Weekly sandbox review: authors present usage, sample charts, and cost delta. Reviewers: obs-owners + impacted SLI owners.
  4. Promotion checklist: ownership assigned, sampling/aggregation validated, alert thresholds exercised, cost estimate agreed.
  5. Promote to prod with agreed retention and tags.

Telemetry cost model used (summary table):

| Item | Unit price (example) | Notes |
|------|---------------------:|-------|
| Ingested metric series (per million samples) | $0.90 | Synthetic example using our cost model (ingest + storage) |
| Histogram bucket writes (per million) | $1.20 | Higher due to cardinality and bucket overhead |
| Retention per GB/month | $10.00 | Tiered after 30 days |
| Alert evaluation (per 1k evaluations) | $0.05 | Includes rule engine compute |

Key metrics / success criteria and results:

- Reduction in production-series growth for piloted teams: 38% (target >=30%).
- Total monthly cost delta for piloted additions (if promoted without change): $4,900 projected. After sandbox tuning and sampling decisions, promoted cost delta: $1,400 (71% reduction).
- Average time from proposal -> promotion: 16 days (target <= 21 days).
- Number of duplicated SLI series removed prior to promotion: 12.
- Developer sentiment (survey): 86% found the feedback useful; 32% reported extra overhead as a minor pain point.

What worked well:

- Cost visibility changed choices: several teams moved from 1s resolution histograms to 60s aggregated series, saving estimated 45% of the per-series cost.
- The enforced retention cap and cardinality guard prevented runaway series during early testing.
- Cross-team reviews caught three near-duplicate SLI series that would have caused noisy dashboards and alert churn.
- The sandbox review meetings were lean (30–45 minutes) and focused on measurable signals rather than design debates.

What did not work / risks observed:

- Onboarding friction: the initial template was too long and several early proposals missed required fields. We shortened the template after two weeks.
- Engineering velocity concern: some teams felt the 2-week sandbox window delayed urgent instrumentation; we need an expedited path for low-risk changes.
- Tooling gaps: our rollout lacked automated diff-of-costs per proposal — authors manually estimated costs (error-prone).
- Edge cases where sandbox retention/aggregation hid real production problems (e.g., tail-latency percentiles that only appear at 90-day retention).

Decisions and follow-up actions (owner + due date):

1) Telemetry Change Template v2 (shortened, compulsory cost-checkboxes) — Owner: Miguel Torres — Due: 2026-12-10.
2) Fast-track promotion for emergency/bugfix telemetry with 48-hour review SLA — Owner: Priya Desai — Due: 2026-12-05.
3) Implement automated cost delta calculator in the sandbox UI (PoC) — Owner: eng-platform/obs infra — Due: 2027-01-31.
4) Expand pilot to include traces (sampling decisions only) — Owner: Oliver Wu — Due: 2027-02-28.
5) Update SLI runbook: add guidance when long-retention signals are required and define escalation for retention mismatch — Owner: eng-sre — Due: 2026-12-20.
6) Create a tagging standard for sandbox-promoted series: metrics.<team>.<name>.prod / metrics.<team>.<name>.sandbox — Owner: eng-platform — Due: 2026-12-01 (completed).

Playbook: Promoteable telemetry checklist (for reviewers):

- Ownership assigned (team + owner)
- Cost estimate present and approved
- Cardinality tests performed (synthetic load)
- Retention level justified and documented
- Alert rules stress-tested in sandbox (false positive simulation)
- Duplicate/overlapping series check completed

Lessons learned (operational principles):

- Visibility before enforcement: displaying estimated cost early is more effective than arbitrary caps.
- Lightweight governance works better than heavy approval workflows — keep review meetings < 1 hour and focused on metrics.
- Provide an escape hatch for urgent changes to avoid blocking incident work.
- Measurement windows must align with signal characteristics: include a policy for long-retention signals that require staged retention increases with review.

Appendix A — Telemetry Change Template (condensed):

- Title:
- Owner (name + slack):
- Purpose / user story:
- SLI mapping (if applicable):
- Expected cardinality (unique series, labels):
- Resolution (1s / 10s / 60s):
- Retention required (days):
- Estimated monthly cost delta (USD):
- Promotion risk level (low / medium / high):
- Test plan (load profile, synthetic checks):

Appendix B — Sandbox operational knobs:

- Default retention: 7 days (override with reviewer approval)
- Default resolution enforced: 60s for histograms unless explicitly justified
- Cardinality cap: 100k unique series per service prefix in sandbox

Appendix C — Survey summary (n=22 engineers):

- Net positive usefulness: 86%
- Suggested improvements: automated cost previews (70%), shorter template (55%), clearer fast-track criteria (40%).

Conclusion:

The sandboxing pilot met its core hypotheses: it reduced unnecessary production telemetry growth, materially cut projected ingest costs for promoted series, and improved cross-team SLI hygiene. The main trade-off is modest added process overhead; we recommend rolling out a lightweight, automated version of the sandbox workflow across all platform-owned telemetry with the fast-track path enabled for incident work.

Action items are recorded above; eng-platform will coordinate implementation and report progress in the next roadmap checkpoint (2027-01). For questions or to join the pilot expansion, contact Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai) or join #obs-sandbox on Slack.

The main trade-off is modest added process overhead; we recommend rolling out a lightweight, automated version of the sandbox workflow across all platform-owned telemetry with the fast-track path enabled for incident work.

Action items are recorded above; eng-platform will coordinate implementation and report progress in the next roadmap checkpoint (2027-01). For questions or to join the pilot expansion, contact Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai) or join #obs-sandbox on Slack.

source.title: billing-integrity-synthetic-tenant-suite-2026
source.author: Marcus Li
source.body:
Overview
========
This document defines the Billing Integrity Synthetic Tenant Suite (BISTS): a canonical set of synthetic tenant workloads, dashboards, alerts, and operator runbooks used to validate end-to-end billing and chargeback fidelity for Redwood platform releases and capacity/topology changes. The suite is intended for platform engineers, SREs, and finance engineering to detect regressions in token accounting, multi-tenant attribution, and billing pipeline transformations before customer impact.

Why this exists
----------------
- Past incidents have exposed mismatches between runtime token attribution (inference runtime) and downstream billing pipelines (ingest, aggregation, invoice generation).
- We need a reproducible, automated way to: (a) generate representative charge patterns, (b) exercise telemetry and ingestion paths, and (c) reconcile expected vs actual charged units with tolerances.

Goals and success criteria
--------------------------
1. Detect >95% of classes of token-attribution regressions within a single CI/infra job.
2. Produce an actionable reconciliation report within 30 minutes of a synthetic run.
3. Provide dashboards and alerts that map directly to operator decisions (refund, roll forward, investigate).
4. Keep false positive rate <3% for alerts on stable platforms.

When to run the suite
---------------------
- Nightly on the platform staging cluster (default).
- Pre-release: mandatory before major runtime/kernel/aggregation changes, capacity reshuffles, quantization/codec updates, or billing pipeline upgrades.
- Post-incident: to validate fixes and detect regressions introduced by remediation.

High-level components
---------------------
- Synthetic Tenant Generator (STG): generates tenant-shaped traffic (requests, session lengths, priming tokens).
- Attribution Probe Agents (APA): lightweight instrumented clients that tag, sign, and assert token attribution at the source.
- Ingest-to-Billing Validator (IBV): consumer that pulls raw telemetry from ingestion S3/kinesis and compares to runtime traces.
- Reconciliation Dashboard & Report (RDR): prebuilt Grafana dashboards and a reconciler that outputs CSV/JSON summary with deltas and anomaly scoring.

Test scenarios (canonical list)
--------------------------------
1) Baseline steady-state: 10 tenants, varied model families, low variance requests.
2) Burst credit exhaustion: tenants exhaust burst credits and trigger soft-throttles.
3) Model variant fallback: induce fallback to cheaper/compatible model and validate price mapping.
4) Cross-region routing: same tenant requests routed through region A then failover to region B mid-session.
5) Quantized kernel path: force quantized execution and ensure per-token accounting remains consistent.
6) KV-cache warmup and eviction: mix of cached prefixes and cold starts to validate prefix caching accounting.
7) Multi-tenant queuing: queue demultiplexing at high concurrency to validate attribution under pressure.
8) Billing pipeline schema change: replay against a shadow pipeline after synthetic schema migration.

For each scenario the suite records: expected_token_sum, observed_runtime_tokens, ingestion_tokens, aggregated_billed_units, per-tenant attribution map, and timestamp drift.

Metrics to capture
------------------
- expected_token_sum (from STG)
- runtime_token_emitted (service telemetry)
- ingestion_token_count (ingest path)
- billed_unit_count (post-aggregation)
- attribution_mismatch_count = billed_unit_count - expected_token_sum
- percent_mismatch = 100 * attribution_mismatch_count / max(1, expected_token_sum)
- reconciliation_latency_ms (time between runtime emit and billing aggregation)
- tenant_fanout_variance and cross_region_routing_fraction

Tolerance thresholds (initial)
------------------------------
| Signal | OK | Warning | Critical |
|-------|----:|--------:|--------:|
| percent_mismatch | <=0.5% | 0.5% - 2% | >2% |
| reconciliation_latency_ms | <= 5,000 | 5,000 - 20,000 | >20,000 |
| attribution_mismatch_count (absolute) | <=10 | 11 - 200 | >200 |

Note: thresholds are configurable per-commit and should be tightened over time as the suite matures.

Dashboards and alerting checklist
--------------------------------
- Dashboard: "BISTS: Reconciliation Overview" (Grafana)
  - Panels: per-tenant mismatch heatmap, reconciliation latency histogram, ingestion vs runtime tokens time-series, top offenders table.
- Alert rules (Grafana/Alertmanager):
  1. BISTS_Reconciliation_Critical: trigger if percent_mismatch > 2% for >5 minutes across >3 tenants. (PagerDuty P1)
  2. BISTS_Latency_Warning: reconciliation_latency_ms > 20s for >10 minutes. (Slack-infra-alerts)
  3. BISTS_TopOffenders_Trend: rolling 1h growth in mismatch count > 50% (Ops channel).
- Alert context must include: synthetic_run_id, scenario_id, tenant_ids, expected_sum, billed_sum, reconciliation_delta, and link to raw trace.

Operator runbook: execute a synthetic run and validate
--------------------------------------------------
1. Kickoff
   - From CI or operator shell run: `make bists-run SCENARIO=baseline RUN_ID=$(date +%s)`
   - STG will print `expected_token_sum` per-tenant to S3://redwood-bists/<run_id>/manifest.json
2. Monitor
   - Open Grafana dashboard: BISTS Reconciliation Overview -> watch panels for spikes.
3. Initial reconciliation (automated)
   - Trigger the IBV reconciler: `./ibv --run-id <run_id> --out reports/<run_id>.json`
   - Expected output: report with fields `expected_tokens`, `runtime_tokens`, `ingest_tokens`, `billed_tokens`, `mismatch_pct` per tenant.
4. Triage rules
   - If mismatch_pct > 2% for any tenant: escalate to platform-sre (pagerduty) and tag incident with `bists-critical`.
   - If reconciliation_latency_ms > 20s: check ingestion pipeline (kinesis/backfill workers), and run `kinesis-inspect --stream billing-ingest --from <run_start>`
   - If ingestion_tokens != runtime_tokens and ingestion latency is small: investigate ingestion prom counters (producer drop, encoder failures).
5. Validation steps before declaring resolution
   - Re-run the same scenario with `--trace=true` to capture request-level spans.
   - Run `./replayer --source S3://redwood-bists/<run_id>/requests/ --target shadow-billing` to validate transformed pipeline off-main.
   - Confirm reconciler report shows mismatch_pct <= 0.5% and commit notes into the incident.

Automation and CI integration
----------------------------
- Nightly job: `bists-nightly-staging` runs all canonical scenarios in parallel across a staging cluster slice and stores reports in S3 under `/bists/nightly/<YYYY-MM-DD>/`.
- Pre-release gate: a short-form BISTS smoke (baseline + burst) must complete with all critical alerts cleared before a canary rollout. Failure blocks the pipeline.
- PR checks: small synthetic run (baseline) is executed on major runtime/aggregation PRs. Developers receive reconciler output as part of the PR status.

Data retention and privacy considerations
---------------------------------------
- Synthetic tenant data contains no customer PII. Use deterministic tenant IDs prefixed with `bists-` and rotate keys monthly.
- Retain raw synthetic traces for 90 days for debugging; aggregated reports and reconciler outputs retained for 2 years for finance audits.

Ownership and SLAs
------------------
- Owner: platform-observability team (primary), billing-platform team (secondary).
- Oncall: platform-sre handles immediate paging (P1), billing-eng owns reconciler fixes and pipeline patches.
- SLA: Nightly BISTS job should complete within 2 hours with reconciler report generated.

Appendix A — sample synthetic tenant manifest (manifest.json)
------------------------------------------------------------
{
  "run_id": "bists-20260915-1234",
  "scenario": "baseline",
  "tenants": [
    {"tenant_id": "bists-t-001", "expected_tokens": 124532, "model": "redwood-texl-70b"},
    {"tenant_id": "bists-t-002", "expected_tokens": 50321, "model": "redwood-texl-7b"}
  ],
  "started_at": "2026-09-15T03:02:00Z"
}

Appendix B — CLI quick commands
--------------------------------
- Run single scenario locally (staging):
  `make bists-run SCENARIO=kv-warmup RUN_ID=local-$(date +%s)`
- Generate a reconciliation report: 
  `./ibv --run-id local-1620000000 --out /tmp/bists-report.json`
- Replayer to shadow pipeline: 
  `./replayer --source s3://redwood-bists/local-1620000000/requests/ --target shadow-billing --dry-run`

Appendix C — common investigation checklist
-------------------------------------------
1. Confirm run_id and scenario in dashboard context.
2. Open reconciler report and verify the top 5 tenants by absolute mismatch.
3. Inspect runtime spans for misplaced tenant_id tags. (Look for missing `tenant_id` or header truncation.)
4. Check ingestion service logs for encoder errors and backpressure signs.
5. Verify downstream aggregation schema mapping (price table joins).
6. If pipeline schema changed recently, run an isolated replay.

Contacts and escalation
-----------------------
- platform-observability oncall: @oncall-platform-sre (PagerDuty P1)
- billing-eng lead: Ravi Kumar (ravi@redwood.ai)
- finance reconciliation: Jenna Morales (jenna@redwood.ai)

Change log
----------
- 2026-02-10 (Marcus Li): created, initial scenarios and thresholds.
- 2026-06-01 (Ava Chen): added burst-credit and cross-region scenarios; added nightly CI integration notes.
- 2026-09-15 (Marcus Li): tightened thresholds, added reconciler CLI examples and retention policy.

source.title: Emergent Behavior Lab — Operator Simulation Recipes and Dashboard Library
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This page documents a repeatable lab for reproducing and triaging emergent platform behaviors (e.g., cross-tenant KV cache storms, prefix-cache thrash, inter-model tail-latency cascades) and the dashboard artifacts operators should use during exercises and incidents. The lab is designed for platform operators, SREs, and reliability engineers who run postmortems, runbooks, and training.

Scope: covers simulation recipes, dashboard templates, runbook fragments, operator checklists, curriculum for training sessions, and a short roadmap for expanding the lab. This is not a step-by-step system upgrade guide; see the platform runbooks for those procedures.

Goals
- Provide deterministic, reproducible scenarios that manifest realistic emergent failure modes in the runtime.
- Produce operator dashboard views that surface root-cause signals quickly.
- Codify remediation sequences and guarded rollback paths for each scenario.
- Use the lab for quarterly operator training and retro-grade validation.

Safety and constraints
- Always run simulations in the lab tenancy or a dedicated non-production region. Never run high-impact scenarios in regions with live production traffic.
- Each recipe includes kill-switch steps and timeboxed windows. Default simulation window: 15 minutes.
- Customer-sensitive datasets are not allowed; use synthetic prompt sets and seeded synthetic tenants.

Owner and contacts
- Lab owner: Priya Natarajan (Platform Reliability)
- Primary operators: Platform Operator Guild (pager: #platform-ops)
- Escalation: Eng-SRE on-call -> Platform Lead (paging policy documented in /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-handbook)

Simulation Recipes (high level)
1) KV prefix cache storm (short-tail eviction)
- Objective: cause heavy prefix-cache eviction across a shard cohort to observe cold-start latency and downstream queueing.
- Preconditions: lab kv-cache configured with 4 shards; synthetic tenant cohort of 20 tenants.
- Steps:
  1. Seed workloads: run prefix-generator tool (see CLI below) to create 10k unique prefixes across tenants over 60s.
  2. Start a rapid churn workload: 30 RPS per tenant for 120s with random prompt prefixes.
  3. At t=45s, inject a throttled background prefetch disable command to force read-throughs (operator flag: runtime.prefetch.enabled=false).
  4. Monitor dashboards for eviction-rate, cold-start P50/P95/P99, and prefix-miss ratio.
- Expected signals:
  - Eviction-rate spikes > 10k/s.
  - Cold-start P99 > 800ms for the model-serving layer.
  - Request queue length increases on shard nodes.
- Guard rails:
  - Kill-switch: runtime.prefetch.enabled=true; warmup sweep script run; abort workload.

2) Cross-tenant token-burst cascade
- Objective: replicate cross-tenant token surges causing multi-tenant scheduler inversion and unfair throughput.
- Preconditions: multi-tenant allocator with soft-weights; quota-buckets set.
- Steps:
  1. Create 3 high-priority tenants (A/B/C) and 20 low-priority tenants.
  2. Ramp Tenant A from 0 -> 300 RPS within 30s while simultaneously starting Tenant B/C at 200 RPS each.
  3. Observe tenant fairness metrics, allocator preemption rate, and tenant-drop events.
  4. Trigger a burst-mitigation script that applies temporary weights to rebalance (operator action).
- Expected signals:
  - Soft-weight overrides engaged; tenant-throttle events logged.
  - SLO burn for affected low-priority tenants.
- Guard rails:
  - Auto-scaler override (operator): scale-dedicated-pool --tenant=low-priority --add 2 GPUs
  - If scale fails, move low-priority tenants to cold lane via routing rule.

3) Kernel-swap jitter and JIT kernel fallback
- Objective: cause a runtime kernel selection misprediction leading to increased per-token latency and GPU utilization spikes.
- Preconditions: runtime configured with JIT kernel switch enabled.
- Steps:
  1. Deploy a model variant with a new kernel profile to the canary pool.
  2. Feed mixed sequence-length traffic (mean 1024, variance 200) for 10 minutes.
  3. Force a partial kernel mismatch via a mocked sequence-length spike generator.
  4. Observe kernel-swap counts, per-kernel latency histograms, and GPU SM utilization.
- Expected signals: kernel-swap rate > baseline by 5x, P99 token latency uplift.
- Rollback: revert kernel profile in canary and trigger warmup pass.

Dashboard Library (templates)
- Purpose: prebuilt dashboard panels for rapid triage. Each panel should be grouped into an incident view: Health, Admission, Scheduling, Execution, Cost.

Suggested panels (table):
| Group | Panel name | Important KPIs | Thresholds | Suggested pinning |
| Health | Cluster node-loss | node_down_count, avg_node_load | node_down_count > 0 | Top-left |
| Admission | Token-admit-rate | admitted_tokens/s, admission_latency_p50/p99 | admission_latency_p99 > 200ms | Top row |
| Scheduling | Allocator-preemption-rate | preemptions/s, tenant_eviction_count | preemptions/s > 5 | Middle |
| Execution | GPU-sm-utilization | gpu_sm_pct, per_gpu_queue_length | sm_pct > 90% | Middle-right |
| Cost | Token-cost-delta | token_cost/minute, token_rate_change_1m | token_cost/min > +5% baseline | Bottom row |

Dashboard variables and derived metrics
- Derived metric: cold_start_ratio = cold_start_count / total_requests (use pipeline tag: request_type=cold_start).
- Variable examples: tenant (dropdown), model_version (dropdown), region, heap_shard_id.

Runbook fragments (actionable sequences)
- Triage template (first 5 minutes):
  1. Identify scope: use tenant variable to check cross-tenant impact.
  2. Isolate admission vs execution: compare admission_latency_p99 vs token-p90-exec-latency.
  3. If admission_latency_p99 high -> investigate admission queue and upstream rate limits.
  4. If execution latency high and GPU sm > 85% -> consider soft-eviction and scale-out.
  5. Document all operator actions in the incident log with timestamps.

- Eviction mitigation sequence (when eviction-rate abnormal):
  1. Apply tenant soft-weights to the top offending tenants: allocator.apply_weights --tenant <id> --weight 0.5
  2. If prefetch disabled, re-enable prefetch per shard: runtime.prefetch.enabled=true
  3. Warm up evicted prefixes via warmup-sweeper: warmup-sweeper --shard <id> --duration 180
  4. Monitor cold_start_ratio until it returns to baseline.

Operator checklists (to be printed in on-call pack)
- Pre-run checks before simulation: verify lab tenancy, check quota tokens, ensure canary flag off, confirm SSO tokens valid.
- During run checks: confirm kill-switch availability, maintain Slack channel for play-by-play, record timestamps for critical metric inflection points.
- Post-run checks: run telemetry sanity sweep, archive dashboard snapshots, create a retro note and attach incident artifacts.

Training curriculum (quarterly)
- 0:30 — Intro and safety brief.
- 1:00 — Walkthrough of dashboard templates and variables.
- 2:00 — Run one KV prefix cache storm scenario (operators practice triage).
- 3:00 — Run one token-burst cascade (coverage of allocator actions).
- 4:00 — Debrief and fill out training assessment form.

Retro and expected outcomes
- Each simulation run requires a brief retro: what signals were missing, which dashboards were misleading, operator cognitive load items (time to decision), and automation candidates.
- Capture agreed action items with owners and due dates in the lab backlog (Board: platform-lab/EMERGENT-LAB).

Telemetry and instrumentation checklist (minimum)
- Required traces and metrics:
  - request.latency.{p50,p95,p99} (tagged by tenant, model_version)
  - runtime.kernel_swap.count (tagged by kernel_id, model_version)
  - kvcache.eviction_rate, kvcache.miss_rate, kvcache.prefetch_enabled
  - allocator.preemptions, allocator.weight_changes
  - gpu.sm_utilization, gpu.queue_length_per_device
- Logs: correlate service-request-id across admission -> scheduler -> executor. Include tenant id and model_version in log headers.

Sample CLI snippets
- Prefix generator (Python entrypoint):
  ./lab-tools/prefix-generator --tenants 20 --prefixes 10000 --duration 60 --rps 30

- Warmup sweep example (YAML job spec):
  job_name: warmup-sweep
  target_shards: [0,1,2,3]
  duration_seconds: 180
  concurrency: 4

Sample alert rules (text)
- Alert: KVCache-Eviction-Spike
  - Expr: increase(kvcache_eviction_rate[1m]) > 10000
  - For: 1m
  - Labels: severity=page, team=platform-ops
  - Runbook: link to eviction mitigation sequence above

Roadmap (next 12 months)
- Q3 2026: Add synthetic tenant fidelity matrix (mapping synthetic traffic to customer workload archetypes). Owner: Platform Lab.
- Q4 2026: Integrate lab scenarios with CI (PR gated synthetic smoke tests for runtime changes). Owner: Eng-Platform CI.
- Q1 2027: Expand dashboard templates to include a guided-runbook mode: in-dashboard operator actions and one-click kill-switch.

Maintenance and governance
- Lab assets (dashboards, scripts, workload definitions) live in repo: git.redwood/platform-lab (branch protected).
- Review cadence: quarterly review of scenarios and dashboards. Responsible: Platform Lab Steering committee.

Appendix: example incident timeline (abridged)
- 00:00 Start token-burst ramp (Tenant A)
- 00:45 Admission latency P99 begins to rise
- 01:10 Preemptions detected and low-priority SLOs burn
- 01:13 Operator applies soft-weight override
- 01:18 Low-priority SLOs stabilize, P99 admission latency improves
- 01:25 Warmup-sweeper started to remediate cold prefixes

References and links
- Platform lab repo: git.redwood/platform-lab
- Runbooks: /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks
- Dashboard gallery: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts

Revision history
- 2026-01-12 — doc created by Priya Natarajan
- 2026-04-02 — added kernel-swap recipe and dashboard table (reviewed by Liam Chen)
- 2026-07-15 — updated training curriculum and roadmap (reviewed by Marisol Vega)

## Overview
This page specifies the dashboards and alerts required for the tiered “workload-aware batching defaults” rollout (fast / standard / cost-efficient). The goal is to make tier behavior visible, explainable, and safe to operate under production traffic.

This spec is written for:
- Runtime / Scheduling: validate scheduler behavior and diagnose tail latency regressions.
- SRE: set actionable alerts tied to SLO tiers and overload behavior.
- Platform / Console: power “effective tier” and “why this profile” views.

## Goals
1) Provide per-tier latency visibility (histograms + percentiles) for both streaming and non-streaming traffic.
2) Surface scheduler health: queue depth, wait time, admission control/load shedding, and starvation indicators.
3) Validate economics: throughput, batching efficiency, and cache hit rate.
4) Detect regressions quickly: canary vs baseline comparisons and automated rollbacks/abort criteria.
5) Control telemetry cost: strict cardinality rules and sampling guidance.

## Non-goals
- Not a full end-to-end app tracing spec (we only include critical spans/attributes needed for tier decisions).
- Not a customer-facing dashboard spec (Console will reuse a subset with different UX).

## Operational definitions (used throughout)
- **Tier**: one of {fast, standard, cost_efficient}. Tier is either selected by route policy, customer override (Dedicated), or default selection logic.
- **Effective tier**: the tier actually used for a request after evaluating overrides + constraints + overload mode.
- **Streaming vs non-streaming**:
  - Streaming: measure TTFT (time-to-first-token) and time-per-output-token separately.
  - Non-streaming: measure end-to-end request latency and prefill/decode breakdown where available.
- **Canonical SLO targets**: defined in “Latency SLO tiers definition: fast / standard / cost-efficient”. Alerts should reference those numbers rather than hard-coding per panel.

## Dashboard structure
Create the following Grafana dashboards in the Observability Pack:

### A) “Tiered Batching: Executive Overview” (primary on-call entry)
Audience: SRE + runtime on-call
Refresh: 30s
Time ranges: 15m / 1h / 6h / 24h
Default filters (templated variables):
- deployment_mode: hosted | dedicated | private
- region
- tier (effective)
- model_family (coarse)
- streaming: true | false

Panels (ordered):
1) **Traffic volume**
   - RPS by tier and streaming flag
   - Query: sum(rate(redwood_inference_requests_total{job="runtime", tier=~"$tier", streaming=~"$streaming"}[5m])) by (tier)

2) **Latency percentiles (tier-aligned)**
   - Streaming TTFT: p50/p95/p99 by tier
   - Non-streaming total latency: p50/p95/p99 by tier
   - Query (example; histogram-based):
     - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{tier=~"$tier", streaming="true"}[5m])) by (le, tier))

3) **Tail latency vs SLO (burn-style view)**
   - For each tier: show p99 (fast), p95 (standard), and p95 (cost_efficient) as configured in the SLO doc.
   - Panel includes an annotation stream from canary enablement events.

4) **Scheduler queue health**
   - Queue depth (requests) and queued tokens (if available)
   - Queue wait time percentiles
   - Queries:
     - max(redwood_scheduler_queue_depth{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)
     - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_queue_wait_seconds_bucket{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (le, tier))

5) **Batching efficiency**
   - Batch size distribution (requests per batch) and batch tokens distribution
   - Batching “fill ratio” (actual / max)
   - Queries:
     - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_batch_size_requests_bucket{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (le, tier))
     - avg(redwood_batch_fill_ratio{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)

6) **Overload/admission control**
   - Rejections / shed counts by reason
   - “Overload mode active” gauge by tier
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier, reason)
     - max(redwood_scheduler_overload_mode_active{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)

7) **Cache health**
   - Prefix/KV cache hit rate
   - Cache evictions
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_cache_hits_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) / (sum(rate(redwood_cache_hits_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) + sum(rate(redwood_cache_misses_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])))
     - sum(rate(redwood_cache_evictions_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (cache_type, tier)

8) **Cost / throughput proxy**
   - Output tokens/sec by tier
   - GPU utilization (if available) and “tokens per GPU-second” (derived)
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_output_tokens_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier)

9) **Profile selection explainability**
   - Reason-code distribution (default, override, constraint, overload)
   - Query:
     - sum(rate(redwood_batching_profile_selected_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier, reason_code)

### B) “Tiered Batching: Scheduler Deep Dive”
Audience: runtime/scheduling engineers
Refresh: 10s
Variables: add hardware_class, model_arch, scheduler_variant (if applicable)
Panels:
- Prefill vs decode service time histograms
- Continuous batching cycle time
- Head-of-line blocking indicators (mixed length buckets)
- Starvation guard triggers count
- Max-wait budget utilization (fast tier focus)

### C) “Tiered Batching: Rollout / Canary Health”
Audience: release engineering + SRE
Refresh: 30s
Panels:
- Canary cohort traffic share over time (feature flag exposure)
- Canary vs control: delta p99/p95 by tier (fast p99 emphasized)
- Error rate delta (5xx/timeout) by tier
- Telemetry cardinality/cost guard panel (see below)

### D) “Tiered Batching: Dedicated Overrides & Precedence”
Audience: Dedicated support + platform
Refresh: 1m
Variables: tenant_id (restricted), cluster
Panels:
- Effective tier vs configured tier counts
- Override precedence reason codes (customer_override vs route_policy vs tier_default)
- “Override not taking effect” diagnostics (constraint/fallback reason)

Confidentiality note: Dashboard D includes tenant_id and must be in a restricted folder with access limited to SRE + Dedicated support + platform on-call.

## Required metrics (contract)
The following metrics are required for the dashboards above. Names are the target names; implementations may expose equivalent metrics but must include the listed labels.

### Latency metrics
1) redwood_inference_request_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: end-to-end request duration (non-streaming and streaming-complete)
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family, streaming

2) redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: time-to-first-token for streaming
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family

3) redwood_inference_decode_token_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: per output token latency during decode (streaming and non-streaming)
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family

### Scheduler health
4) redwood_scheduler_queue_depth
- Type: gauge
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

5) redwood_scheduler_queue_wait_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

6) redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total
- Type: counter
- Labels: tier, region, model_family, reason
- Reason enum (stable set): overload, queue_full, max_wait_exceeded, policy_denied

7) redwood_scheduler_overload_mode_active
- Type: gauge (0/1)
- Labels: tier, region

### Batching efficiency
8) redwood_batch_size_requests_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

9) redwood_batch_tokens_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

10) redwood_batch_fill_ratio
- Type: gauge
- Meaning: actual_batch_tokens / configured_max_batch_tokens (or equivalent)
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

### Cache
11) redwood_cache_hits_total / redwood_cache_misses_total
- Type: counters
- Labels: cache_type (prefix|kv), tier, region, model_family

12) redwood_cache_evictions_total
- Type: counter
- Labels: cache_type, tier, region

### Explainability / reason codes
13) redwood_batching_profile_selected_total
- Type: counter
- Meaning: emitted once per request for the profile selection decision
- Labels: tier, region, model_family, reason_code
- reason_code enum (stable set): tier_default, route_policy, customer_override, model_constraint, overload_mode, fallback_variant

## Cardinality controls (must-follow)
We will not ship tiered batching observability if it requires high-cardinality labels that materially increase telemetry cost.

Hard rules:
- DO include: tier, region, deployment_mode, streaming, model_family.
- DO NOT include by default: route_id, prompt_hash, tenant_id, request_id.
- Dedicated tenant_id:
  - Allowed only in restricted metrics or logs, and only for specific troubleshooting dashboards (Dashboard D).
  - Must be sampled and/or gated behind a feature flag.

Model label guidance:
- Use model_family (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen) and optionally model_size_bucket (e.g., 7b/13b/70b). Avoid full model_version strings in metrics.

Sampling guidance:
- Histograms: keep bucket counts minimal and aligned to SLO ranges (review with Logan Wright before adding buckets).
- Reason-code counters: unsampled (low cost) but limited to stable enums.

Telemetry cost guard:
- Add a panel to Dashboard C tracking:
  - active time series for runtime job
  - ingestion rate (samples/sec)
  - top label cardinality offenders (if supported by backend)

## Alerts (tiered)
Alerts are split into paging (urgent, customer-impacting), ticket (needs investigation), and info (trend monitoring). All alerts must include tier, region, deployment_mode, and model_family when available.

### Paging alerts (SRE on-call)
1) Fast tier tail latency breach (streaming TTFT)
- Condition: fast tier p99 TTFT exceeds the fast-tier SLO target for >10 minutes
- Query (template):
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{tier="fast"}[5m])) by (le, region, model_family)) > $FAST_TTFT_P99_SLO_SECONDS
- Routing: page SRE; notify #eng-runtime

2) Fast tier tail latency breach (non-streaming request)
- Condition: fast tier p99 request latency exceeds target for >10 minutes

3) Admission rejects spike (fast + standard)
- Condition: admission rejections > 1% of requests for 10 minutes
- Query:
  - (sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total{tier=~"fast|standard"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_inference_requests_total{tier=~"fast|standard"}[5m]))) > 0.01
- Notes: This typically indicates overload-mode entry, queue backpressure misconfiguration, or a capacity incident.

### Ticket alerts (runtime/SRE follow-up)
4) Standard tier p95 regression
- Condition: standard tier p95 request latency above SLO target for 30 minutes

5) Queue wait time regression (any tier)
- Condition: p95 queue wait > 2x 7-day baseline for 30 minutes
- Baseline method: use a rolling baseline panel; if automated baseline is unavailable, use a static threshold during canary and revisit post-launch.

6) Batching efficiency regression (cost_efficient focus)
- Condition: batch_fill_ratio drops below expected band for 1 hour (indicates under-batching, often due to mis-detected workload signals or max-wait too tight)

7) Cache hit rate drop
- Condition: prefix+kv hit rate drops by >20% relative to 7-day baseline for 1 hour (control for traffic mix)

### Info alerts (trends / noise-controlled)
8) Reason code shift
- Condition: overload_mode or fallback_variant reason codes exceed 5% of selections for 1 hour
- Action: investigate capacity/constraints; ensure we are not silently operating in degraded mode.

## Regression detection and rollout gates (canary)
During staged rollout, Dashboard C is the source of truth for go/no-go.

Minimum “green” criteria to expand exposure:
- Fast tier:
  - p99 TTFT and p99 request latency do not exceed SLO targets.
  - No sustained admission reject rate > 1%.
- Standard tier:
  - p95 does not exceed SLO targets.
- Cost-efficient:
  - Throughput proxy improves vs control OR batching efficiency improves without breaching standard-tier latency thresholds for shared fleets.
- Telemetry:
  - No metric cardinality spikes; runtime active time series within agreed budget.

Abort criteria (immediate rollback):
- Any paging alert sustained for >10 minutes across ≥2 regions.
- Telemetry ingestion increases by >25% sustained for 30 minutes (indicates cardinality leak).

## Annotations and incident workflow integration
- Add automatic Grafana annotations for:
  - feature flag exposure changes (tiered batching enablement percentage)
  - runtime releases (1.22.x)
  - config rollouts affecting batching knobs
- On alert fire, link to:
  - runtime runbook: “Overload behavior when tiered batching is enabled”
  - rollback procedure: kill-switch instructions
  - perf-canary baseline refresh procedure

## Ownership and maintenance
- Primary owners: Observability (Allison Grant, Caleb Johnson) + SRE (Aisha Bello)
- Runtime point-of-contact: Julian Moore
- Review cadence:
  - Weekly during rollout (until GA)
  - Monthly thereafter or when adding new model families/hardware classes

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Whether decode token latency should be the customer-facing “streaming smoothness” metric, or if we should publish an additional chunk cadence metric.
- Whether baseline comparisons should be computed centrally (preferred) vs embedded per-alert with recording rules.

## Change log
- 2026-02-02: Initial spec created (Allison Grant)
- 2026-03-18: Added reason-code panels and cardinality hard rules after early canary telemetry concerns (Allison Grant, Logan Wright)
- 2026-04-10: Finalized paging vs ticket thresholds, added Dedicated override dashboard restrictions (Aisha Bello, Sean Gallagher)
