## Change log
- 2026-02-02: Initial spec created (Allison Grant)
- 2026-03-18: Added reason-code panels and cardinality hard rules after early canary telemetry concerns (Allison Grant, Logan Wright)
- 2026-04-10: Finalized paging vs ticket thresholds, added Dedicated override dashboard restrictions (Aisha Bello, Sean Gallagher)
text:
## Overview
This page specifies the dashboards and alerts required for the tiered “workload-aware batching defaults” rollout (fast / standard / cost-efficient). The goal is to make tier behavior visible, explainable, and safe to operate under production traffic.

This spec is written for:
- Runtime / Scheduling: validate scheduler behavior and diagnose tail latency regressions.
- SRE: set actionable alerts tied to SLO tiers and overload behavior.
- Platform / Console: power “effective tier” and “why this profile” views.

## Goals
1) Provide per-tier latency visibility (histograms + percentiles) for both streaming and non-streaming traffic.
2) Surface scheduler health: queue depth, wait time, admission control/load shedding, and starvation indicators.
3) Validate economics: throughput, batching efficiency, and cache hit rate.
4) Detect regressions quickly: canary vs baseline comparisons and automated rollbacks/abort criteria.
5) Control telemetry cost: strict cardinality rules and sampling guidance.

## Non-goals
- Not a full end-to-end app tracing spec (we only include critical spans/attributes needed for tier decisions).
- Not a customer-facing dashboard spec (Console will reuse a subset with different UX).

## Operational definitions (used throughout)
- **Tier**: one of {fast, standard, cost_efficient}. Tier is either selected by route policy, customer override (Dedicated), or default selection logic.
- **Effective tier**: the tier actually used for a request after evaluating overrides + constraints + overload mode.
- **Streaming vs non-streaming**:
  - Streaming: measure TTFT (time-to-first-token) and time-per-output-token separately.
  - Non-streaming: measure end-to-end request latency and prefill/decode breakdown where available.
- **Canonical SLO targets**: defined in “Latency SLO tiers definition: fast / standard / cost-efficient”. Alerts should reference those numbers rather than hard-coding per panel.

## Dashboard structure
Create the following Grafana dashboards in the Observability Pack:

### A) “Tiered Batching: Executive Overview” (primary on-call entry)
Audience: SRE + runtime on-call
Refresh: 30s
Time ranges: 15m / 1h / 6h / 24h
Default filters (templated variables):
- deployment_mode: hosted | dedicated | private
- region
- tier (effective)
- model_family (coarse)
- streaming: true | false

Panels (ordered):
1) **Traffic volume**
   - RPS by tier and streaming flag
   - Query: sum(rate(redwood_inference_requests_total{job="runtime", tier=~"$tier", streaming=~"$streaming"}[5m])) by (tier)

2) **Latency percentiles (tier-aligned)**
   - Streaming TTFT: p50/p95/p99 by tier
   - Non-streaming total latency: p50/p95/p99 by tier
   - Query (example; histogram-based):
     - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{tier=~"$tier", streaming="true"}[5m])) by (le, tier))

3) **Tail latency vs SLO (burn-style view)**
   - For each tier: show p99 (fast), p95 (standard), and p95 (cost_efficient) as configured in the SLO doc.
   - Panel includes an annotation stream from canary enablement events.

4) **Scheduler queue health**
   - Queue depth (requests) and queued tokens (if available)
   - Queue wait time percentiles
   - Queries:
     - max(redwood_scheduler_queue_depth{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)
     - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_queue_wait_seconds_bucket{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (le, tier))

5) **Batching efficiency**
   - Batch size distribution (requests per batch) and batch tokens distribution
   - Batching “fill ratio” (actual / max)
   - Queries:
     - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(redwood_batch_size_requests_bucket{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (le, tier))
     - avg(redwood_batch_fill_ratio{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)

6) **Overload/admission control**
   - Rejections / shed counts by reason
   - “Overload mode active” gauge by tier
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier, reason)
     - max(redwood_scheduler_overload_mode_active{tier=~"$tier"}) by (tier)

7) **Cache health**
   - Prefix/KV cache hit rate
   - Cache evictions
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_cache_hits_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) / (sum(rate(redwood_cache_hits_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) + sum(rate(redwood_cache_misses_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])))
     - sum(rate(redwood_cache_evictions_total{cache_type=~"prefix|kv", tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (cache_type, tier)

8) **Cost / throughput proxy**
   - Output tokens/sec by tier
   - GPU utilization (if available) and “tokens per GPU-second” (derived)
   - Queries:
     - sum(rate(redwood_output_tokens_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier)

9) **Profile selection explainability**
   - Reason-code distribution (default, override, constraint, overload)
   - Query:
     - sum(rate(redwood_batching_profile_selected_total{tier=~"$tier"}[5m])) by (tier, reason_code)

### B) “Tiered Batching: Scheduler Deep Dive”
Audience: runtime/scheduling engineers
Refresh: 10s
Variables: add hardware_class, model_arch, scheduler_variant (if applicable)
Panels:
- Prefill vs decode service time histograms
- Continuous batching cycle time
- Head-of-line blocking indicators (mixed length buckets)
- Starvation guard triggers count
- Max-wait budget utilization (fast tier focus)

### C) “Tiered Batching: Rollout / Canary Health”
Audience: release engineering + SRE
Refresh: 30s
Panels:
- Canary cohort traffic share over time (feature flag exposure)
- Canary vs control: delta p99/p95 by tier (fast p99 emphasized)
- Error rate delta (5xx/timeout) by tier
- Telemetry cardinality/cost guard panel (see below)

### D) “Tiered Batching: Dedicated Overrides & Precedence”
Audience: Dedicated support + platform
Refresh: 1m
Variables: tenant_id (restricted), cluster
Panels:
- Effective tier vs configured tier counts
- Override precedence reason codes (customer_override vs route_policy vs tier_default)
- “Override not taking effect” diagnostics (constraint/fallback reason)

Confidentiality note: Dashboard D includes tenant_id and must be in a restricted folder with access limited to SRE + Dedicated support + platform on-call.

## Required metrics (contract)
The following metrics are required for the dashboards above. Names are the target names; implementations may expose equivalent metrics but must include the listed labels.

### Latency metrics
1) redwood_inference_request_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: end-to-end request duration (non-streaming and streaming-complete)
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family, streaming

2) redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: time-to-first-token for streaming
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family

3) redwood_inference_decode_token_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: per output token latency during decode (streaming and non-streaming)
- Labels: tier, deployment_mode, region, model_family

### Scheduler health
4) redwood_scheduler_queue_depth
- Type: gauge
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

5) redwood_scheduler_queue_wait_seconds_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

6) redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total
- Type: counter
- Labels: tier, region, model_family, reason
- Reason enum (stable set): overload, queue_full, max_wait_exceeded, policy_denied

7) redwood_scheduler_overload_mode_active
- Type: gauge (0/1)
- Labels: tier, region

### Batching efficiency
8) redwood_batch_size_requests_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

9) redwood_batch_tokens_bucket
- Type: histogram
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

10) redwood_batch_fill_ratio
- Type: gauge
- Meaning: actual_batch_tokens / configured_max_batch_tokens (or equivalent)
- Labels: tier, region, model_family

### Cache
11) redwood_cache_hits_total / redwood_cache_misses_total
- Type: counters
- Labels: cache_type (prefix|kv), tier, region, model_family

12) redwood_cache_evictions_total
- Type: counter
- Labels: cache_type, tier, region

### Explainability / reason codes
13) redwood_batching_profile_selected_total
- Type: counter
- Meaning: emitted once per request for the profile selection decision
- Labels: tier, region, model_family, reason_code
- reason_code enum (stable set): tier_default, route_policy, customer_override, model_constraint, overload_mode, fallback_variant

## Cardinality controls (must-follow)
We will not ship tiered batching observability if it requires high-cardinality labels that materially increase telemetry cost.

Hard rules:
- DO include: tier, region, deployment_mode, streaming, model_family.
- DO NOT include by default: route_id, prompt_hash, tenant_id, request_id.
- Dedicated tenant_id:
  - Allowed only in restricted metrics or logs, and only for specific troubleshooting dashboards (Dashboard D).
  - Must be sampled and/or gated behind a feature flag.

Model label guidance:
- Use model_family (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen) and optionally model_size_bucket (e.g., 7b/13b/70b). Avoid full model_version strings in metrics.

Sampling guidance:
- Histograms: keep bucket counts minimal and aligned to SLO ranges (review with Logan Wright before adding buckets).
- Reason-code counters: unsampled (low cost) but limited to stable enums.

Telemetry cost guard:
- Add a panel to Dashboard C tracking:
  - active time series for runtime job
  - ingestion rate (samples/sec)
  - top label cardinality offenders (if supported by backend)

## Alerts (tiered)
Alerts are split into paging (urgent, customer-impacting), ticket (needs investigation), and info (trend monitoring). All alerts must include tier, region, deployment_mode, and model_family when available.

### Paging alerts (SRE on-call)
1) Fast tier tail latency breach (streaming TTFT)
- Condition: fast tier p99 TTFT exceeds the fast-tier SLO target for >10 minutes
- Query (template):
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{tier="fast"}[5m])) by (le, region, model_family)) > $FAST_TTFT_P99_SLO_SECONDS
- Routing: page SRE; notify #eng-runtime

2) Fast tier tail latency breach (non-streaming request)
- Condition: fast tier p99 request latency exceeds target for >10 minutes

3) Admission rejects spike (fast + standard)
- Condition: admission rejections > 1% of requests for 10 minutes
- Query:
  - (sum(rate(redwood_scheduler_admission_reject_total{tier=~"fast|standard"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_inference_requests_total{tier=~"fast|standard"}[5m]))) > 0.01
- Notes: This typically indicates overload-mode entry, queue backpressure misconfiguration, or a capacity incident.

### Ticket alerts (runtime/SRE follow-up)
4) Standard tier p95 regression
- Condition: standard tier p95 request latency above SLO target for 30 minutes

5) Queue wait time regression (any tier)
- Condition: p95 queue wait > 2x 7-day baseline for 30 minutes
- Baseline method: use a rolling baseline panel; if automated baseline is unavailable, use a static threshold during canary and revisit post-launch.

6) Batching efficiency regression (cost_efficient focus)
- Condition: batch_fill_ratio drops below expected band for 1 hour (indicates under-batching, often due to mis-detected workload signals or max-wait too tight)

7) Cache hit rate drop
- Condition: prefix+kv hit rate drops by >20% relative to 7-day baseline for 1 hour (control for traffic mix)

### Info alerts (trends / noise-controlled)
8) Reason code shift
- Condition: overload_mode or fallback_variant reason codes exceed 5% of selections for 1 hour
- Action: investigate capacity/constraints; ensure we are not silently operating in degraded mode.

## Regression detection and rollout gates (canary)
During staged rollout, Dashboard C is the source of truth for go/no-go.

Minimum “green” criteria to expand exposure:
- Fast tier:
  - p99 TTFT and p99 request latency do not exceed SLO targets.
  - No sustained admission reject rate > 1%.
- Standard tier:
  - p95 does not exceed SLO targets.
- Cost-efficient:
  - Throughput proxy improves vs control OR batching efficiency improves without breaching standard-tier latency thresholds for shared fleets.
- Telemetry:
  - No metric cardinality spikes; runtime active time series within agreed budget.

Abort criteria (immediate rollback):
- Any paging alert sustained for >10 minutes across ≥2 regions.
- Telemetry ingestion increases by >25% sustained for 30 minutes (indicates cardinality leak).

## Annotations and incident workflow integration
- Add automatic Grafana annotations for:
  - feature flag exposure changes (tiered batching enablement percentage)
  - runtime releases (1.22.x)
  - config rollouts affecting batching knobs
- On alert fire, link to:
  - runtime runbook: “Overload behavior when tiered batching is enabled”
  - rollback procedure: kill-switch instructions
  - perf-canary baseline refresh procedure

## Ownership and maintenance
- Primary owners: Observability (Allison Grant, Caleb Johnson) + SRE (Aisha Bello)
- Runtime point-of-contact: Julian Moore
- Review cadence:
  - Weekly during rollout (until GA)
  - Monthly thereafter or when adding new model families/hardware classes

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Whether decode token latency should be the customer-facing “streaming smoothness” metric, or if we should publish an additional chunk cadence metric.
- Whether baseline comparisons should be computed centrally (preferred) vs embedded per-alert with recording rules.

## Change log
- 2026-02-02: Initial spec created (Allison Grant)
- 2026-03-18: Added reason-code panels and cardinality hard rules after early canary telemetry concerns (Allison Grant, Logan Wright)
- 2026-04-10: Finalized paging vs ticket thresholds, added Dedicated override dashboard restrictions (Aisha Bello, Sean Gallagher)

source.title: Cross-region signal fusion and fallback runbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------
1) Latency telemetry (highest priority for interactive SLOs)
   - Metrics: request_p95_ms, request_p99_ms from runtime-gateway and regional-edge-proxy.
   - Dashboards: dash/cross-region-latency-fusion -> panel "p95 by region & route".
2) Token cost / execution-time signals (priority for cost-driven routing)
   - Metrics: avg_tokens_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens_estimated from billing-proxy.
3) Queue depth and backpressure (priority for safety/failure detection)
   - Metrics: queue_depth, slow_consumer_count from batch-router.
4) KV-cache-hit-rate and prefetch cohort fidelity
   - Metrics: kv_cache_hit_ratio, prefetch_miss_streak.

Fusion heuristics (how alerts are decided)
-----------------------------------------
- The fusion engine (service: signal-fuser) computes a composite score per region R:

  composite_score(R) = w1 * normalized_p95(R) + w2 * normalized_cost_delta(R) + w3 * backpressure_penalty(R) - w4 * cache_health_bonus(R)

  where (recommended weights): w1=0.6, w2=0.2, w3=0.15, w4=0.05.

- Normalization: each input is mapped to 0..1 using rolling baseline windows (last 24h).
- Composite score thresholds:
  - Informational: score > 0.35
  - Action recommended: score > 0.55
  - Auto-fallback candidate: score > 0.75 (AND persistent for >= 5 minutes)

Alerting and dashboards
-----------------------
- Alert definitions (Grafana/Alerter):
  1) alert: CrossRegionLatencyWave_ActionRecommended
     expr: composite_score > 0.55 for 5m
     severity: P2 (Platform Operational)
     runbook: this doc -> Operator actions (page anchor)
  2) alert: CrossRegionFallbackCandidate
     expr: composite_score > 0.75 for 5m AND avg(queue_depth) > 50% for 3m
     severity: P1 (Immediate)
     auto-remediation: initiate canary re-route to fallback model/region (see automated fallbacks).
- Dashboard link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
- Panels to check: p95 by region, composite heatmap, queue depth heatmap, token-cost delta by tenant.

Operator actions (ordered)
-------------------------
1) Triage
   - Open the cross-region dashboard and confirm composite score growth is not caused by a single noisy tenant.
   - Query: topk(10, sum by (tenant) (rate(request_tokens[5m]))) and compare to baseline.
2) Contain
   - If backpressure present on batch-router, increase worker concurrency for the affected allocation:

     kubectl -n runtime scale deployment batch-router-uswest2 --replicas=6

   - If kv-prefix-cache shows sustained prefetch_miss_streak > 0.2, flush targeted prefixes:

     curl -X POST "https://ops-api.internal/cache/flush" -d '{"prefixes": ["tenant-1234-user-ctx"]}'

3) Mitigate (manual fallback)
   - If composite_score > 0.75 and operators confirm regional instability, perform a controlled reroute using traffic-shepherd:

     # preview
     traffic-shepherd preview-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10

     # commit (staggered):
     traffic-shepherd apply-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10 --stagger 2m --owner @oncall-platform

   - Record the reroute ID and annotate the incident ticket.
4) Escalate
   - If reroute does not restore p95 within 10 minutes or causes cost > +40% vs baseline, escalate to Platform SRE on-call and open incident channel #inc-platform-regional-failover.

Automated fallback configuration
-------------------------------
- Auto-fallbacks are enabled when alert CrossRegionFallbackCandidate fires and composite_score is sustained for 7m. Auto-fallback policy:
  - Step 1: Canary 5% traffic to fallback region/model for 3m.
  - Step 2: If fallback improves composite score by >= 20% within 3m, ramp to 30% for 5m.
  - Step 3: If still improving, ramp to 100% over 10 minutes.
- Overrides: tenants with "no-fallback" contract label are exempt. The auto-fallback actor uses the service regional-allocator and writes an audit entry to fallback-log.

Rollback and safety checks
-------------------------
- Always validate output quality for canary traffic using the canary-eval job:

  kubectl -n eval-job logs canary-eval-<id>

  The job compares a predefined prompt-set S against baseline and reports 3 metrics: semantic_similarity (>=0.92 pass), generation_stability (<0.15 drift), latency_improvement_ms.
- If semantic_similarity < 0.90 for canary, abort fallback and revert routing:

  traffic-shepherd revert-reroute --id <reroute-id> --owner @oncall-platform --reason "canary-quality-failure"
- Safety circuit: any automated fallback that increases estimated token-cost > +60% triggers immediate rollback and hands control to operators.

Post-incident remediation checklist
----------------------------------
1) Annotate the incident ticket with composite_score graphs, reroute IDs, eviction/restart events, and canary-eval summaries.
2) Run a root-cause hypothesis session (30–60m) with owners from runtime, allocator, and infra. Capture findings in: /confluence/eng-platform/retrospectives/<incident>-postmortem (link).
3) If the incident involved cache fidelity, schedule a prefetch-cohort audit and add to backlog with priority P2.

Runbook run-through (rapid script)
----------------------------------
1) Open: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
2) Confirm composite_score alert and top offending tenants.
3) Run: kubectl get deployment batch-router-uswest2 -o wide
4) If queue_depth high: scale batch-router replicas (see contain step).
5) If composite_score persists: preview and apply traffic-shepherd reroute (see mitigate).
6) Monitor canary-eval and artifact logs for 15 minutes before ramping.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- Primary owner: platform-ops (oncall rotation via PagerDuty: Platform SRE).
- Secondary owner: runtime-team (Slack: #runtime).
- Fallback automation owner: allocator-team (PagerDuty: Allocator SRE).

Appendix: CLI snippets and JSON examples
---------------------------------------
- Example composite_score query (PromQL):

  sum by(region) (
    0.6 * (request_p95_ms / (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[24h]))))) +
    0.2 * cost_delta_normalized +
    0.15 * clamp_max(queue_depth / 1000, 1) -
    0.05 * kv_cache_hit_ratio
  )

- Sample fallback audit entry (JSON):

  {
    "reroute_id": "rr-20260302-84f2",
    "from": "us-west-2",
    "to": "us-east-1",
    "routes": ["interactive-text", "chat-generic"],
    "percent": 10,
    "trigger": "CrossRegionFallbackCandidate",
    "canary_eval": {"semantic_similarity": 0.948, "stability": 0.08},
    "operator": "@oncall-platform"
  }

References and related reading
----------------------------
- Regional failure mode mapping: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/regional-failure-mode-mapping-and-observability-patterns-2026
- Multiregion tenant routing guide: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/multiregion-latency-aware-tenant-routing-guide-2025
- Prefetch cohort best-practices: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Change history
--------------
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2026-02-10: Added automated fallback policy and canary-eval tie-in (reviewers: Liam Chen).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized weights and example queries; published.

- Always validate output quality for canary traffic using the canary-eval job:

  kubectl -n eval-job logs canary-eval-<id>

  The job compares a predefined prompt-set S against baseline and reports 3 metrics: semantic_similarity (>=0.92 pass), generation_stability (<0.15 drift), latency_improvement_ms.
- If semantic_similarity < 0.90 for canary, abort fallback and revert routing:

  traffic-shepherd revert-reroute --id <reroute-id> --owner @oncall-platform --reason "canary-quality-failure"
- Safety circuit: any automated fallback that increases estimated token-cost > +60% triggers immediate rollback and hands control to operators.

Post-incident remediation checklist
----------------------------------
1) Annotate the incident ticket with composite_score graphs, reroute IDs, eviction/restart events, and canary-eval summaries.
2) Run a root-cause hypothesis session (30–60m) with owners from runtime, allocator, and infra. Capture findings in: /confluence/eng-platform/retrospectives/<incident>-postmortem (link).
3) If the incident involved cache fidelity, schedule a prefetch-cohort audit and add to backlog with priority P2.

Runbook run-through (rapid script)
----------------------------------
1) Open: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
2) Confirm composite_score alert and top offending tenants.
3) Run: kubectl get deployment batch-router-uswest2 -o wide
4) If queue_depth high: scale batch-router replicas (see contain step).
5) If composite_score persists: preview and apply traffic-shepherd reroute (see mitigate).
6) Monitor canary-eval and artifact logs for 15 minutes before ramping.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- Primary owner: platform-ops (oncall rotation via PagerDuty: Platform SRE).
- Secondary owner: runtime-team (Slack: #runtime).
- Fallback automation owner: allocator-team (PagerDuty: Allocator SRE).

Appendix: CLI snippets and JSON examples
---------------------------------------
- Example composite_score query (PromQL):

  sum by(region) (
    0.6 * (request_p95_ms / (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[24h]))))) +
    0.2 * cost_delta_normalized +
    0.15 * clamp_max(queue_depth / 1000, 1) -
    0.05 * kv_cache_hit_ratio
  )

- Sample fallback audit entry (JSON):

  {
    "reroute_id": "rr-20260302-84f2",
    "from": "us-west-2",
    "to": "us-east-1",
    "routes": ["interactive-text", "chat-generic"],
    "percent": 10,
    "trigger": "CrossRegionFallbackCandidate",
    "canary_eval": {"semantic_similarity": 0.948, "stability": 0.08},
    "operator": "@oncall-platform"
  }

References and related reading
----------------------------
- Regional failure mode mapping: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/regional-failure-mode-mapping-and-observability-patterns-2026
- Multiregion tenant routing guide: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/multiregion-latency-aware-tenant-routing-guide-2025
- Prefetch cohort best-practices: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Change history
--------------
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2026-02-10: Added automated fallback policy and canary-eval tie-in (reviewers: Liam Chen).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized weights and example queries; published.
text:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------
1) Latency telemetry (highest priority for interactive SLOs)
   - Metrics: request_p95_ms, request_p99_ms from runtime-gateway and regional-edge-proxy.
   - Dashboards: dash/cross-region-latency-fusion -> panel "p95 by region & route".
2) Token cost / execution-time signals (priority for cost-driven routing)
   - Metrics: avg_tokens_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens_estimated from billing-proxy.
3) Queue depth and backpressure (priority for safety/failure detection)
   - Metrics: queue_depth, slow_consumer_count from batch-router.
4) KV-cache-hit-rate and prefetch cohort fidelity
   - Metrics: kv_cache_hit_ratio, prefetch_miss_streak.

Fusion heuristics (how alerts are decided)
-----------------------------------------
- The fusion engine (service: signal-fuser) computes a composite score per region R:

  composite_score(R) = w1 * normalized_p95(R) + w2 * normalized_cost_delta(R) + w3 * backpressure_penalty(R) - w4 * cache_health_bonus(R)

  where (recommended weights): w1=0.6, w2=0.2, w3=0.15, w4=0.05.

- Normalization: each input is mapped to 0..1 using rolling baseline windows (last 24h).
- Composite score thresholds:
  - Informational: score > 0.35
  - Action recommended: score > 0.55
  - Auto-fallback candidate: score > 0.75 (AND persistent for >= 5 minutes)

Alerting and dashboards
-----------------------
- Alert definitions (Grafana/Alerter):
  1) alert: CrossRegionLatencyWave_ActionRecommended
     expr: composite_score > 0.55 for 5m
     severity: P2 (Platform Operational)
     runbook: this doc -> Operator actions (page anchor)
  2) alert: CrossRegionFallbackCandidate
     expr: composite_score > 0.75 for 5m AND avg(queue_depth) > 50% for 3m
     severity: P1 (Immediate)
     auto-remediation: initiate canary re-route to fallback model/region (see automated fallbacks).
- Dashboard link: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
- Panels to check: p95 by region, composite heatmap, queue depth heatmap, token-cost delta by tenant.

Operator actions (ordered)
-------------------------
1) Triage
   - Open the cross-region dashboard and confirm composite score growth is not caused by a single noisy tenant.
   - Query: topk(10, sum by (tenant) (rate(request_tokens[5m]))) and compare to baseline.
2) Contain
   - If backpressure present on batch-router, increase worker concurrency for the affected allocation:

     kubectl -n runtime scale deployment batch-router-uswest2 --replicas=6

   - If kv-prefix-cache shows sustained prefetch_miss_streak > 0.2, flush targeted prefixes:

     curl -X POST "https://ops-api.internal/cache/flush" -d '{"prefixes": ["tenant-1234-user-ctx"]}'

3) Mitigate (manual fallback)
   - If composite_score > 0.75 and operators confirm regional instability, perform a controlled reroute using traffic-shepherd:

     # preview
     traffic-shepherd preview-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10

     # commit (staggered):
     traffic-shepherd apply-reroute --from us-west-2 --to us-east-1 --routes "interactive-*" --percent 10 --stagger 2m --owner @oncall-platform

   - Record the reroute ID and annotate the incident ticket.
4) Escalate
   - If reroute does not restore p95 within 10 minutes or causes cost > +40% vs baseline, escalate to Platform SRE on-call and open incident channel #inc-platform-regional-failover.

Automated fallback configuration
-------------------------------
- Auto-fallbacks are enabled when alert CrossRegionFallbackCandidate fires and composite_score is sustained for 7m. Auto-fallback policy:
  - Step 1: Canary 5% traffic to fallback region/model for 3m.
  - Step 2: If fallback improves composite score by >= 20% within 3m, ramp to 30% for 5m.
  - Step 3: If still improving, ramp to 100% over 10 minutes.
- Overrides: tenants with "no-fallback" contract label are exempt. The auto-fallback actor uses the service regional-allocator and writes an audit entry to fallback-log.

Rollback and safety checks
-------------------------
- Always validate output quality for canary traffic using the canary-eval job:

  kubectl -n eval-job logs canary-eval-<id>

  The job compares a predefined prompt-set S against baseline and reports 3 metrics: semantic_similarity (>=0.92 pass), generation_stability (<0.15 drift), latency_improvement_ms.
- If semantic_similarity < 0.90 for canary, abort fallback and revert routing:

  traffic-shepherd revert-reroute --id <reroute-id> --owner @oncall-platform --reason "canary-quality-failure"
- Safety circuit: any automated fallback that increases estimated token-cost > +60% triggers immediate rollback and hands control to operators.

Post-incident remediation checklist
----------------------------------
1) Annotate the incident ticket with composite_score graphs, reroute IDs, eviction/restart events, and canary-eval summaries.
2) Run a root-cause hypothesis session (30–60m) with owners from runtime, allocator, and infra. Capture findings in: /confluence/eng-platform/retrospectives/<incident>-postmortem (link).
3) If the incident involved cache fidelity, schedule a prefetch-cohort audit and add to backlog with priority P2.

Runbook run-through (rapid script)
----------------------------------
1) Open: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dash/cross-region-latency-fusion
2) Confirm composite_score alert and top offending tenants.
3) Run: kubectl get deployment batch-router-uswest2 -o wide
4) If queue_depth high: scale batch-router replicas (see contain step).
5) If composite_score persists: preview and apply traffic-shepherd reroute (see mitigate).
6) Monitor canary-eval and artifact logs for 15 minutes before ramping.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- Primary owner: platform-ops (oncall rotation via PagerDuty: Platform SRE).
- Secondary owner: runtime-team (Slack: #runtime).
- Fallback automation owner: allocator-team (PagerDuty: Allocator SRE).

Appendix: CLI snippets and JSON examples
---------------------------------------
- Example composite_score query (PromQL):

  sum by(region) (
    0.6 * (request_p95_ms / (histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[24h]))))) +
    0.2 * cost_delta_normalized +
    0.15 * clamp_max(queue_depth / 1000, 1) -
    0.05 * kv_cache_hit_ratio
  )

- Sample fallback audit entry (JSON):

  {
    "reroute_id": "rr-20260302-84f2",
    "from": "us-west-2",
    "to": "us-east-1",
    "routes": ["interactive-text", "chat-generic"],
    "percent": 10,
    "trigger": "CrossRegionFallbackCandidate",
    "canary_eval": {"semantic_similarity": 0.948, "stability": 0.08},
    "operator": "@oncall-platform"
  }

References and related reading
----------------------------
- Regional failure mode mapping: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/regional-failure-mode-mapping-and-observability-patterns-2026
- Multiregion tenant routing guide: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/multiregion-latency-aware-tenant-routing-guide-2025
- Prefetch cohort best-practices: /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

Change history
--------------
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Natarajan (draft).
- 2026-02-10: Added automated fallback policy and canary-eval tie-in (reviewers: Liam Chen).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized weights and example queries; published.

source.title: Converged Platform Capability Catalog and Ownership Matrix
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary:
This document defines a converged catalog of platform capabilities (reusable services, sidecars, and shared abstractions), the ownership model for each capability, required SLOs and monitoring signals, integration contracts, and a lightweight onboarding + deprecation process. Its goal is to reduce duplication, clarify operational responsibility, and provide a repeatable checklist for teams that want to adopt or extend platform capabilities.

Audience:
- Platform engineers and service owners
- Product/feature teams integrating platform capabilities
- Platform SRE & oncall operators

Scope:
- Leaf capabilities provided by the platform runtime and platform-managed sidecars (e.g., prefix-cache, batching service, tenant-slice controller, cost-telemetry), and the integration contracts that consumers must implement.
- Does NOT include customer-managed application business logic or ephemeral developer tooling.

Key definitions:
- Capability: A platform-provided, versioned service or contract (API, sidecar, runtime hook) intended for cross-team reuse.
- Owner: The team responsible for runbook, SLOs, changes, and rollouts for a capability.
- Consumer: Any product/service that depends on a capability.

1) Capability catalog (summary table)
| Capability | Description | Owner team | Minimum SLOs | Primary telemetry | Integration type | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Prefix/KV Cache | Shared prefix caching for repeated prompt prefixes and KV state | eng-serving-runtime | 99.9% availability, 95th pct tail RTT < 20ms | cache_hit_rate, prefix_miss_ratio, p95_latency | Sidecar + runtime hook | Supports shard hints for multi-tenant isolation |
| Batcher | Continuous batching service for small requests | eng-platform | 99.95% availability, tail latency SLO see per-tenant contracts | batch_size_dist, queue_wait_ms, drop_rate | Sidecar / local agent | Batching policy configurable per-route |
| Tenant-Slicer | Priority slicing and credit accounting for tenants | eng-platform | 99.9% availability | tenant_throughput, credit_balance, evictions | Controller + policy DSL | Critical for dedicated pools |
| Cost Telemetry Aggregator | Token-level cost attribution service and export | eng-platform/finance-infra | 99.9% delivery to warehouse (24h) | tokens_attributed, attribution_delay_seconds | HTTP push + kafka export | Produces daily cost-report artifacts |
| Model-Variant Router | Runtime routing policy engine for model fallbacks | eng-serving-runtime | 99.95% policy-eval success | routing_decisions, fallback_count, eval_latency | Library + config | Supports canary and A/B tag semantics |
| Observability Envelope | Standardized SLI/trace headers & context propagation | eng-platform | 99.99% header preservation | trace_propagation_rate, header_trim_rate | Library + sidecar | Used by all other capabilities for consistent dashboards |

2) Ownership matrix (detailed responsibilities)
- eng-serving-runtime: owning teams operate the runtime components (prefix-cache, model-variant router). Responsibilities: kernel compatibility testing, release notes, perf bench artifacts, hotfixes, capacity planning.
- eng-platform: owns tenant-slicing, batching orchestration, cost telemetry ingestion, and platform SDKs. Responsibilities: SLA definition, rollout orchestration, quota semantics, runbooks, and billing signals.
- finance-infra (cross-functional): owns the downstream cost schema and warehouse exports. Responsibilities: reconciliation, cost model changes, cross-team billing questions.

3) SLOs, SLIs, and alerting guidance (per capability)
- Always define a primary availability SLI (successful API or RPC path) and at least one latency SLI (p95 or p99 depending on capability).
- Example (Prefix/KV Cache):
  - Availability SLI: successful cache GET/SET / total attempts; target 99.9% monthly.
  - Latency SLI: p95 end-to-end cache lookup latency < 20ms.
  - Alert thresholds: pages at SLO burn > 10% sustained for 30m, warn at 3%.

- Monitoring signals to emit for every capability:
  - availability (success rate),
  - p50/p95/p99 latency,
  - error class distribution (5xx, 4xx),
  - consumer-side backpressure metrics (queue_wait_ms, drop_rate),
  - capacity headroom (utilization ratio).

4) Integration contract template (example)
- Consumers must:
  1. Adopt the platform observability envelope (trace headers + tagging).
  2. Implement the capability adapter interface (example shim below).
  3. Provide a capability-consumer registration PR that declares expected QPS, typical request size, and latency requirements.

Sample adapter (YAML) for Batcher integration:
-----BEGIN SAMPLE YAML-----
adapter_version: v1
consumer_service: payments-api
expected_qps: 120
avg_payload_tokens: 48
batching_policy:
  max_delay_ms: 10
  max_batch_tokens: 4096
  fairness: weighted
-----END SAMPLE YAML-----

5) Onboarding checklist for adding a new consumer
1. Fill the consumer registration template and submit to the capability owner (link to registration form in team wiki).
2. Run integration tests using the local-sidecar shim (steps below).
3. Deploy to canary with traffic < 2% for 24h, monitor SLI dashboards.
4. Confirm cost/telemetry artifacts are visible in the Cost Telemetry Aggregator.
5. Gradually ramp to target traffic following the rollout playbook; document the rollout in the consumer’s PR.

Local sidecar test commands (example):
- Start shim container: docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/shim-config.yaml: /etc/shim/config.yaml platform/local-batcher-shim:latest
- Run smoke tests: ./tools/platform-integration-test --capability=batcher --consumer=payments-api

6) Rollout & release policy for capabilities
- Semantic versioning for capability contracts (major.minor.patch).
- Backwards-incompatible (major) changes require:
  - 90-day deprecation announcement, automated analyzers to detect consumers, and a migration guide.
- Minor changes: feature flags + opt-in during canary.
- Patch changes: immediate; owners must publish release notes.

7) Monitoring dashboards & alert playbook (recommended panels)
Per-capability dashboard should include:
- SLI trend (30d), burn rate panel, top contributing error codes, consumer-level throughput table, recent deploys timeline, and cost per-tenant heatmap.
Alert playbook pattern:
1. Paging alert -> page oncall for owning team if SLO burn > 10% sustained 30m.
2. Secondary notify channel to consumer owners if consumer-specific errors exceed threshold.
3. Automated mitigation: temporary throttle or routed fallback to a compatible variant.

8) Cost and chargeback signals
- Emit a token attribution row per request to the Cost Telemetry Aggregator with fields: timestamp, tenant_id, tokens_consumed, capability_id, cost_model_version.
- Owners must produce a daily cost rollup export that finance-infra can consume.
- Dashboards: monthly cost by tenant, cost per-1000 tokens, top-10 cost accounts.

9) Risk assessment & fallback strategies
- For every capability provide:
  - A compatible fallback variant (e.g., cache miss -> direct model call with rate-limit),
  - A grace degradation path (serve stale KV for read-only lanes),
  - A safety circuit breaker that transitions to degraded mode and notifies owners.

10) Capability lifecycle (add, change, deprecate)
- Add: capability owner publishes spec -> consumer onboarding template -> integration & canary -> production.
- Change: follow semantic versioning; for non-breaking changes owners must provide migration notes.
- Deprecate: 90-day notice + automated consumer discovery + mandatory migration plan.

11) Runbook excerpt (operational play steps)
Situation: sudden rise in prefix-cache miss rate and p99 latency
- Immediate steps:
  1. Check prefix-cache dashboard for sharding imbalance and evictions.
  2. Run: ./tools/cache-inspect --shard-heatmap | jq .top_evictions
  3. If a single shard > 80% eviction: trigger shard rebalancer (manual safe-mode): /ops/rebalancer reassign --shard X --drain-rate 0.2
  4. If overall p99 > 500ms and traffic > 80% of capacity: enable temporary read-through rate limit: curl -X POST https://platform/api/controls/throttle -d '{"capability":"prefix-cache","target_pct":80}'
  5. Page eng-serving-runtime oncall and attach the cache diagnostics bundle.

12) Roadmap alignment (next 12 months)
- Q2 2026: Standardize observability-envelope v2 and adopt by 80% of capabilities.
- Q3 2026: Launch capability-contract-linter to detect breaking contract changes in PRs.
- Q4 2026: Automated consumer discovery pipeline for deprecation notices.

13) Governance & cross-team decision flow
- Capability owners propose major changes via a lightweight ADR; reviews required from consumer representatives and platform SRE.
- Quarterly capability review: owners present SLO health, onboarding velocity, and cost trends.

14) Contacts and escalation
- Primary owner (eng-platform): platform-capabilities@redwood.ai (pager: #platform-oncall)
- eng-serving-runtime oncall: #runtime-oncall
- Finance-infra: finance-infra@redwood.ai

Appendices
A) Example capability-consumer registration checklist (bullet form)- Service name, owner, expected qps, avg tokens, acceptable tail latency, required availability, retry semantics, canary schedule.

B) Useful queries (example)
- Top consumers by token spend (BigQuery):
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(tokens) AS tokens FROM `redwood.cost_events` WHERE event_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY tokens DESC LIMIT 50;

C) Change log
- 2026-02-10: Created initial catalog and onboarding checklist (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified SLO thresholds and added shard rebalancer steps (Daniel Park).

Related reading:
- /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

  3. If a single shard > 80% eviction: trigger shard rebalancer (manual safe-mode): /ops/rebalancer reassign --shard X --drain-rate 0.2
  4. If overall p99 > 500ms and traffic > 80% of capacity: enable temporary read-through rate limit: curl -X POST https://platform/api/controls/throttle -d '{"capability":"prefix-cache","target_pct":80}'
  5. Page eng-serving-runtime oncall and attach the cache diagnostics bundle.

12) Roadmap alignment (next 12 months)
- Q2 2026: Standardize observability-envelope v2 and adopt by 80% of capabilities.
- Q3 2026: Launch capability-contract-linter to detect breaking contract changes in PRs.
- Q4 2026: Automated consumer discovery pipeline for deprecation notices.

13) Governance & cross-team decision flow
- Capability owners propose major changes via a lightweight ADR; reviews required from consumer representatives and platform SRE.
- Quarterly capability review: owners present SLO health, onboarding velocity, and cost trends.

14) Contacts and escalation
- Primary owner (eng-platform): platform-capabilities@redwood.ai (pager: #platform-oncall)
- eng-serving-runtime oncall: #runtime-oncall
- Finance-infra: finance-infra@redwood.ai

Appendices
A) Example capability-consumer registration checklist (bullet form)- Service name, owner, expected qps, avg tokens, acceptable tail latency, required availability, retry semantics, canary schedule.

B) Useful queries (example)
- Top consumers by token spend (BigQuery):
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(tokens) AS tokens FROM `redwood.cost_events` WHERE event_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY tokens DESC LIMIT 50;

C) Change log
- 2026-02-10: Created initial catalog and onboarding checklist (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified SLO thresholds and added shard rebalancer steps (Daniel Park).

Related reading:
- /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026
text:
Summary:
This document defines a converged catalog of platform capabilities (reusable services, sidecars, and shared abstractions), the ownership model for each capability, required SLOs and monitoring signals, integration contracts, and a lightweight onboarding + deprecation process. Its goal is to reduce duplication, clarify operational responsibility, and provide a repeatable checklist for teams that want to adopt or extend platform capabilities.

Audience:
- Platform engineers and service owners
- Product/feature teams integrating platform capabilities
- Platform SRE & oncall operators

Scope:
- Leaf capabilities provided by the platform runtime and platform-managed sidecars (e.g., prefix-cache, batching service, tenant-slice controller, cost-telemetry), and the integration contracts that consumers must implement.
- Does NOT include customer-managed application business logic or ephemeral developer tooling.

Key definitions:
- Capability: A platform-provided, versioned service or contract (API, sidecar, runtime hook) intended for cross-team reuse.
- Owner: The team responsible for runbook, SLOs, changes, and rollouts for a capability.
- Consumer: Any product/service that depends on a capability.

1) Capability catalog (summary table)
| Capability | Description | Owner team | Minimum SLOs | Primary telemetry | Integration type | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Prefix/KV Cache | Shared prefix caching for repeated prompt prefixes and KV state | eng-serving-runtime | 99.9% availability, 95th pct tail RTT < 20ms | cache_hit_rate, prefix_miss_ratio, p95_latency | Sidecar + runtime hook | Supports shard hints for multi-tenant isolation |
| Batcher | Continuous batching service for small requests | eng-platform | 99.95% availability, tail latency SLO see per-tenant contracts | batch_size_dist, queue_wait_ms, drop_rate | Sidecar / local agent | Batching policy configurable per-route |
| Tenant-Slicer | Priority slicing and credit accounting for tenants | eng-platform | 99.9% availability | tenant_throughput, credit_balance, evictions | Controller + policy DSL | Critical for dedicated pools |
| Cost Telemetry Aggregator | Token-level cost attribution service and export | eng-platform/finance-infra | 99.9% delivery to warehouse (24h) | tokens_attributed, attribution_delay_seconds | HTTP push + kafka export | Produces daily cost-report artifacts |
| Model-Variant Router | Runtime routing policy engine for model fallbacks | eng-serving-runtime | 99.95% policy-eval success | routing_decisions, fallback_count, eval_latency | Library + config | Supports canary and A/B tag semantics |
| Observability Envelope | Standardized SLI/trace headers & context propagation | eng-platform | 99.99% header preservation | trace_propagation_rate, header_trim_rate | Library + sidecar | Used by all other capabilities for consistent dashboards |

2) Ownership matrix (detailed responsibilities)
- eng-serving-runtime: owning teams operate the runtime components (prefix-cache, model-variant router). Responsibilities: kernel compatibility testing, release notes, perf bench artifacts, hotfixes, capacity planning.
- eng-platform: owns tenant-slicing, batching orchestration, cost telemetry ingestion, and platform SDKs. Responsibilities: SLA definition, rollout orchestration, quota semantics, runbooks, and billing signals.
- finance-infra (cross-functional): owns the downstream cost schema and warehouse exports. Responsibilities: reconciliation, cost model changes, cross-team billing questions.

3) SLOs, SLIs, and alerting guidance (per capability)
- Always define a primary availability SLI (successful API or RPC path) and at least one latency SLI (p95 or p99 depending on capability).
- Example (Prefix/KV Cache):
  - Availability SLI: successful cache GET/SET / total attempts; target 99.9% monthly.
  - Latency SLI: p95 end-to-end cache lookup latency < 20ms.
  - Alert thresholds: pages at SLO burn > 10% sustained for 30m, warn at 3%.

- Monitoring signals to emit for every capability:
  - availability (success rate),
  - p50/p95/p99 latency,
  - error class distribution (5xx, 4xx),
  - consumer-side backpressure metrics (queue_wait_ms, drop_rate),
  - capacity headroom (utilization ratio).

4) Integration contract template (example)
- Consumers must:
  1. Adopt the platform observability envelope (trace headers + tagging).
  2. Implement the capability adapter interface (example shim below).
  3. Provide a capability-consumer registration PR that declares expected QPS, typical request size, and latency requirements.

Sample adapter (YAML) for Batcher integration:
-----BEGIN SAMPLE YAML-----
adapter_version: v1
consumer_service: payments-api
expected_qps: 120
avg_payload_tokens: 48
batching_policy:
  max_delay_ms: 10
  max_batch_tokens: 4096
  fairness: weighted
-----END SAMPLE YAML-----

5) Onboarding checklist for adding a new consumer
1. Fill the consumer registration template and submit to the capability owner (link to registration form in team wiki).
2. Run integration tests using the local-sidecar shim (steps below).
3. Deploy to canary with traffic < 2% for 24h, monitor SLI dashboards.
4. Confirm cost/telemetry artifacts are visible in the Cost Telemetry Aggregator.
5. Gradually ramp to target traffic following the rollout playbook; document the rollout in the consumer’s PR.

Local sidecar test commands (example):
- Start shim container: docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/shim-config.yaml: /etc/shim/config.yaml platform/local-batcher-shim:latest
- Run smoke tests: ./tools/platform-integration-test --capability=batcher --consumer=payments-api

6) Rollout & release policy for capabilities
- Semantic versioning for capability contracts (major.minor.patch).
- Backwards-incompatible (major) changes require:
  - 90-day deprecation announcement, automated analyzers to detect consumers, and a migration guide.
- Minor changes: feature flags + opt-in during canary.
- Patch changes: immediate; owners must publish release notes.

7) Monitoring dashboards & alert playbook (recommended panels)
Per-capability dashboard should include:
- SLI trend (30d), burn rate panel, top contributing error codes, consumer-level throughput table, recent deploys timeline, and cost per-tenant heatmap.
Alert playbook pattern:
1. Paging alert -> page oncall for owning team if SLO burn > 10% sustained 30m.
2. Secondary notify channel to consumer owners if consumer-specific errors exceed threshold.
3. Automated mitigation: temporary throttle or routed fallback to a compatible variant.

8) Cost and chargeback signals
- Emit a token attribution row per request to the Cost Telemetry Aggregator with fields: timestamp, tenant_id, tokens_consumed, capability_id, cost_model_version.
- Owners must produce a daily cost rollup export that finance-infra can consume.
- Dashboards: monthly cost by tenant, cost per-1000 tokens, top-10 cost accounts.

9) Risk assessment & fallback strategies
- For every capability provide:
  - A compatible fallback variant (e.g., cache miss -> direct model call with rate-limit),
  - A grace degradation path (serve stale KV for read-only lanes),
  - A safety circuit breaker that transitions to degraded mode and notifies owners.

10) Capability lifecycle (add, change, deprecate)
- Add: capability owner publishes spec -> consumer onboarding template -> integration & canary -> production.
- Change: follow semantic versioning; for non-breaking changes owners must provide migration notes.
- Deprecate: 90-day notice + automated consumer discovery + mandatory migration plan.

11) Runbook excerpt (operational play steps)
Situation: sudden rise in prefix-cache miss rate and p99 latency
- Immediate steps:
  1. Check prefix-cache dashboard for sharding imbalance and evictions.
  2. Run: ./tools/cache-inspect --shard-heatmap | jq .top_evictions
  3. If a single shard > 80% eviction: trigger shard rebalancer (manual safe-mode): /ops/rebalancer reassign --shard X --drain-rate 0.2
  4. If overall p99 > 500ms and traffic > 80% of capacity: enable temporary read-through rate limit: curl -X POST https://platform/api/controls/throttle -d '{"capability":"prefix-cache","target_pct":80}'
  5. Page eng-serving-runtime oncall and attach the cache diagnostics bundle.

12) Roadmap alignment (next 12 months)
- Q2 2026: Standardize observability-envelope v2 and adopt by 80% of capabilities.
- Q3 2026: Launch capability-contract-linter to detect breaking contract changes in PRs.
- Q4 2026: Automated consumer discovery pipeline for deprecation notices.

13) Governance & cross-team decision flow
- Capability owners propose major changes via a lightweight ADR; reviews required from consumer representatives and platform SRE.
- Quarterly capability review: owners present SLO health, onboarding velocity, and cost trends.

14) Contacts and escalation
- Primary owner (eng-platform): platform-capabilities@redwood.ai (pager: #platform-oncall)
- eng-serving-runtime oncall: #runtime-oncall
- Finance-infra: finance-infra@redwood.ai

Appendices
A) Example capability-consumer registration checklist (bullet form)- Service name, owner, expected qps, avg tokens, acceptable tail latency, required availability, retry semantics, canary schedule.

B) Useful queries (example)
- Top consumers by token spend (BigQuery):
  SELECT tenant_id, SUM(tokens) AS tokens FROM `redwood.cost_events` WHERE event_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY tenant_id ORDER BY tokens DESC LIMIT 50;

C) Change log
- 2026-02-10: Created initial catalog and onboarding checklist (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-03-01: Clarified SLO thresholds and added shard rebalancer steps (Daniel Park).

Related reading:
- /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/platform-operator-snapshot-and-rollback-patterns-2026
- /confluence/eng-platform/how-tos/cache-prefetch-prioritization-for-llm-workloads-2026

source.title: Service poison-pill safety and isolation guide
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service degradation (examples: pathological prompts that exhaust KV caches, malformed inputs that trigger infinite loops in model runtime, or tenant-driven token-surges).

Scope & applicability
---------------------
Applies to: inference-gateway, batching-worker, kv-prefix-cache, model-eval-sandbox, and the routing layer (router and model-variant-fallbacker). Does NOT replace tenant-level security policies; complements quota and billing controls.

Goals
-----
- Detect poison-pill behavior quickly with deterministic signals.
- Isolate affected tenants/requests before systemic impact.
- Provide safe automated mitigation with operator-safe kill paths.
- Offer clear post-incident steps and test harnesses to prevent regressions.

Definitions
-----------
- Poison-pill: a request or set of requests that cause runaway CPU/memory, unbounded token generation, excessive I/O, or errorstorm cascades.
- Sweeper: an automated operator process that identifies and quarantines suspect traffic.
- Quarantine: short-term routing and resource restrictions that allow investigation without impacting other tenants.

Detecting poison-pills (signals)
--------------------------------
Primary signals (alert if condition sustained for 30s unless otherwise noted):
1) Error amplification: service-level 5xx rate > 2% AND contributing request path error growth > 5x baseline over 1 minute.
2) Resource surge: container memory growth > 30% in 60s or resident set > 85% of container limit.
3) Token explosion: single request token generation > 50k tokens OR token-emit-rate > 10k tokens/s.
4) KV miss storm: kv-prefix-cache read latency > 500ms and miss ratio > 60% for a tenant prefix.
5) Tail latency spike: p99 latency increases > 10x baseline and correlated across > 3 nodes in a pool.
6) Circuit-breaker saturation: > 75% of router circuits tripped for a tenant.

Automated safeguards (default actions)
-------------------------------------
- Fast-fail fences: inference-gateway applies per-request synthetic limits (timeout=120s, token_limit=20000) unless tenant override is configured.
- Token-rate limiter: burstable token bucket per-tenant (ref config table below). Exceeded -> 429 + route to canary fallback.
- Quarantine-router: when threshold breached, request routing shifts tenant to isolated queue slice with reduced concurrency (default concurrency=2) and cached fallback model.
- Safe preemption: batching-worker will evict the largest in-flight requests from offending tenant first (LIFO within tenant).
- Memory guard: kernel oom_score_adj review hook and cgroup soft kill paths that prefer restarting the worker process over whole-node reboot.

Operator isolation and remediation runbook (step-by-step)
---------------------------------------------------------
Trigger: 'Quarantine-router' alert OR manual detection via observability console.
1) Triage (0-2 minutes)
   a) Open incident channel: #platform-ops / incident-{YYYYMMDD}-{short}.
   b) Run quick query: 
      - kubectl top pods -n inference | grep <pool-name>
      - curl -sS "https://observability/api/query?expr=token_emit_rate{tenant=\"TENANT_ID\"}" | jq .
   c) Identify tenant_id, request_id(s), and service(s) involved.
2) Immediate isolation (2-5 minutes)
   a) Apply enforced tenant throttling: 
      - opa policy patch: opa eval --data policies.json 'patch-policy("tenant/limit/ID")'
   b) Move traffic to quarantine slice: 
      - rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant TENANT_ID --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
   c) If KV miss-storm detected: mark prefix read-only and enable warmup cache for other tenants.
3) Stabilize (5-20 minutes)
   a) If memory leak suspected: capture heap profile and stacktrace: 
      - kubectl exec -it pod/<pod> -- /bin/sh -c "curl localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap > /tmp/heap.pprof"
   b) If inflated token counts: temporarily enable model-variant-fallbacker to a conservative model: 
      - rtrctl swap-fallback --service inference-gateway --from model-xlarge --to model-xsmall --reason "poison-pill mitigation"
   c) Restart only the offending worker process where possible: 
      - kubectl rollout restart deployment/batching-worker -n inference --field-selector metadata.name=<pod> --grace-period=30
4) Remediation and business follow-up (20-90 minutes)
   a) Collect request payloads (sanitizing PII): s3://redwood-incident-bucket/{inc}/payloads/
   b) Run deterministic replay in the model-eval-sandbox with instrumentation (cpu/mem/token traces).
   c) If caused by customer prompt abuse, open support ticket and apply billing/contract actions per customer policy.
5) Restore and monitor (90-240 minutes)
   a) Gradually lift quarantine by shifting 10% traffic increments every 10 minutes while observing SLOs.
   b) Post-restore synthetic test: run 100 synthetic inferences with worst-case prompt families.

Safe-kill checklist (quick reference)
------------------------------------
- Has traffic been routed away from shared pools? (yes/no)
- Are core dumps and pprof collected? (yes/no)
- Is restart targeted to container/process rather than node? (yes/no)
- Have relevant owners been paged? (yes/no)
- Is rollback plan prepared (stable image / config)? (yes/no)

Config table: default thresholds and owner contacts
-------------------------------------------------
| component            | primary_threshold                   | auto-action                          | owner_team | oncall |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|--------|
| inference-gateway    | p99 > 2s OR token_rate > 10k/s     | quarantine-traffic, swap-fallback    | serving    | pg-serv |
| batching-worker      | memory growth > 30% / 60s          | evict-inflight restart worker        | infra      | pg-infra |
| kv-prefix-cache      | miss-rate > 60% & latency > 500ms  | prefix-readonly, prefetch warm cache | platform   | pg-cache |
| model-eval-sandbox   | token-gen > 50k/request            | throttle, sandboxed-replay           | research   | pg-ml |
| router               | circuits tripped > 75%             | route to quarantine slice            | platform   | pg-router |

(Owners map: serving=eng-serving-runtime; infra=eng-infra; platform=eng-platform; research=eng-applied-ml)

CLI examples and snippets
-------------------------
- Isolate tenant: rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant T-42 --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
- Capture trace for request: curl "https://inference-gateway/internal/trace?req_id=REQ123" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ANALYST_TOKEN" > /tmp/REQ123.trace
- Query token emit rate (Grafana API): 
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAF_TOKEN" "https://grafana/api/datasources/proxy/1/api/v1/query?query=token_emit_rate%7Btenant%3D%27T-42%27%7D" | jq .

Rollout and feature gating for a new "sweeper"
------------------------------------------------
Purpose: introduce a controlled automatic quarantining component that can be toggled by region/cluster.
Phased rollout:
1) Feature flag off in prod, enabled in staging with synthetic poison-pills.
2) Canary: enable for 1% of traffic in us-west with verbose logging.
3) Metrics gating: require stable latency, no cross-tenant error correlation, and <0.1% false positive rate for 48h.
4) Gradual ramp: 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 7 days with daily review.
Fallback: immediate feature-flag off + revert config if SLO regression observed.

Testing and exercise plan
-------------------------
- Chaos experiments: weekly gameday simulating token-surges and kv-prefix storms against a non-prod replica.
- Unit/integration: simulate OOM via fault-injector and assert quarantine transitions.
- Synthetic library: maintain a corpus of pathological prompts (stored in repo infra/poison-corpus) used in CI for release gating.

Post-incident RCA template (to be attached to incident)
------------------------------------------------------
- Incident ID:
- Start / End:
- Services impacted:
- Root cause summary (2-3 sentences):
- Detection timeline (timestamps):
- Mitigation actions taken and times:
- Why the mitigation worked / did not work:
- Persistent changes planned (config, code, policy):
- Owner for follow-up tasks and ETA:

Roles & ownership
------------------
- First responder: platform oncall (pg-platform). Responsible for initial isolation actions.
- Runtime engineer: eng-serving-runtime. Responsible for heap traces and kernel-level restarts.
- Cache owner: eng-platform (kv-team). Responsible for prefix freeze and warmups.
- Customer success: engage for customer-facing communication if tenant is externally managed.

References & links
------------------
- Observability SLI guide: ../dashboards-and-alerts/token-burst-forensics-and-debugging-workflow-2026.json
- Emergency model swap runbook: ../runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026.json
- KV cache rebalancer: ../systems-and-services/operator-workflows-for-llm-kv-cache-sharding-and-rebalancing-2026.json

Appendix: Example OPA policy (sanitized)
----------------------------------------
{\n  \"id\": \"tenant-rate-limit\",\n  \"effect\": \"deny\",\n  \"conditions\": { \"tenant\": \"T-42\", \"token_rate\": { \"gt\": 10000 } },\n  \"action\": \"isolate\"\n}

Document maintenance
--------------------
- Owner: eng-platform (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any major poison-pill incident.
- Additions to the synthetic corpus must be reviewed by eng-applied-ml and infra.

This guide should be used in conjunction with per-service runbooks and security policy. If you find gaps or have observed a new poison pattern, file a PR against this page and tag the reviewers listed above.
