- Services impacted:
- Root cause summary (2-3 sentences):
- Detection timeline (timestamps):
- Mitigation actions taken and times:
- Why the mitigation worked / did not work:
- Persistent changes planned (config, code, policy):
- Owner for follow-up tasks and ETA:

Roles & ownership
------------------
- First responder: platform oncall (pg-platform). Responsible for initial isolation actions.
- Runtime engineer: eng-serving-runtime. Responsible for heap traces and kernel-level restarts.
- Cache owner: eng-platform (kv-team). Responsible for prefix freeze and warmups.
- Customer success: engage for customer-facing communication if tenant is externally managed.

References & links
------------------
- Observability SLI guide: ../dashboards-and-alerts/token-burst-forensics-and-debugging-workflow-2026.json
- Emergency model swap runbook: ../runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026.json
- KV cache rebalancer: ../systems-and-services/operator-workflows-for-llm-kv-cache-sharding-and-rebalancing-2026.json

Appendix: Example OPA policy (sanitized)
----------------------------------------
{
  \"id\": \"tenant-rate-limit\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"conditions\": { \"tenant\": \"T-42\", \"token_rate\": { \"gt\": 10000 } },
  \"action\": \"isolate\"
}

Document maintenance
--------------------
- Owner: eng-platform (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any major poison-pill incident.
- Additions to the synthetic corpus must be reviewed by eng-applied-ml and infra.

This guide should be used in conjunction with per-service runbooks and security policy. If you find gaps or have observed a new poison pattern, file a PR against this page and tag the reviewers listed above.
text:
Summary
-------
This guide defines detection, automated mitigation, and operator isolation procedures for "poison-pill" requests and workloads in the Redwood inference platform. A poison-pill is any request or workload pattern that rapidly escalates resource usage or error propagation and risks cross-tenant/service degradation (examples: pathological prompts that exhaust KV caches, malformed inputs that trigger infinite loops in model runtime, or tenant-driven token-surges).

Scope & applicability
---------------------
Applies to: inference-gateway, batching-worker, kv-prefix-cache, model-eval-sandbox, and the routing layer (router and model-variant-fallbacker). Does NOT replace tenant-level security policies; complements quota and billing controls.

Goals
-----
- Detect poison-pill behavior quickly with deterministic signals.
- Isolate affected tenants/requests before systemic impact.
- Provide safe automated mitigation with operator-safe kill paths.
- Offer clear post-incident steps and test harnesses to prevent regressions.

Definitions
-----------
- Poison-pill: a request or set of requests that cause runaway CPU/memory, unbounded token generation, excessive I/O, or errorstorm cascades.
- Sweeper: an automated operator process that identifies and quarantines suspect traffic.
- Quarantine: short-term routing and resource restrictions that allow investigation without impacting other tenants.

Detecting poison-pills (signals)
--------------------------------
Primary signals (alert if condition sustained for 30s unless otherwise noted):
1) Error amplification: service-level 5xx rate > 2% AND contributing request path error growth > 5x baseline over 1 minute.
2) Resource surge: container memory growth > 30% in 60s or resident set > 85% of container limit.
3) Token explosion: single request token generation > 50k tokens OR token-emit-rate > 10k tokens/s.
4) KV miss storm: kv-prefix-cache read latency > 500ms and miss ratio > 60% for a tenant prefix.
5) Tail latency spike: p99 latency increases > 10x baseline and correlated across > 3 nodes in a pool.
6) Circuit-breaker saturation: > 75% of router circuits tripped for a tenant.

Automated safeguards (default actions)
-------------------------------------
- Fast-fail fences: inference-gateway applies per-request synthetic limits (timeout=120s, token_limit=20000) unless tenant override is configured.
- Token-rate limiter: burstable token bucket per-tenant (ref config table below). Exceeded -> 429 + route to canary fallback.
- Quarantine-router: when threshold breached, request routing shifts tenant to isolated queue slice with reduced concurrency (default concurrency=2) and cached fallback model.
- Safe preemption: batching-worker will evict the largest in-flight requests from offending tenant first (LIFO within tenant).
- Memory guard: kernel oom_score_adj review hook and cgroup soft kill paths that prefer restarting the worker process over whole-node reboot.

Operator isolation and remediation runbook (step-by-step)
---------------------------------------------------------
Trigger: 'Quarantine-router' alert OR manual detection via observability console.
1) Triage (0-2 minutes)
   a) Open incident channel: #platform-ops / incident-{YYYYMMDD}-{short}.
   b) Run quick query: 
      - kubectl top pods -n inference | grep <pool-name>
      - curl -sS "https://observability/api/query?expr=token_emit_rate{tenant=\"TENANT_ID\"}" | jq .
   c) Identify tenant_id, request_id(s), and service(s) involved.
2) Immediate isolation (2-5 minutes)
   a) Apply enforced tenant throttling: 
      - opa policy patch: opa eval --data policies.json 'patch-policy("tenant/limit/ID")'
   b) Move traffic to quarantine slice: 
      - rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant TENANT_ID --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
   c) If KV miss-storm detected: mark prefix read-only and enable warmup cache for other tenants.
3) Stabilize (5-20 minutes)
   a) If memory leak suspected: capture heap profile and stacktrace: 
      - kubectl exec -it pod/<pod> -- /bin/sh -c "curl localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap > /tmp/heap.pprof"
   b) If inflated token counts: temporarily enable model-variant-fallbacker to a conservative model: 
      - rtrctl swap-fallback --service inference-gateway --from model-xlarge --to model-xsmall --reason "poison-pill mitigation"
   c) Restart only the offending worker process where possible: 
      - kubectl rollout restart deployment/batching-worker -n inference --field-selector metadata.name=<pod> --grace-period=30
4) Remediation and business follow-up (20-90 minutes)
   a) Collect request payloads (sanitizing PII): s3://redwood-incident-bucket/{inc}/payloads/
   b) Run deterministic replay in the model-eval-sandbox with instrumentation (cpu/mem/token traces).
   c) If caused by customer prompt abuse, open support ticket and apply billing/contract actions per customer policy.
5) Restore and monitor (90-240 minutes)
   a) Gradually lift quarantine by shifting 10% traffic increments every 10 minutes while observing SLOs.
   b) Post-restore synthetic test: run 100 synthetic inferences with worst-case prompt families.

Safe-kill checklist (quick reference)
------------------------------------
- Has traffic been routed away from shared pools? (yes/no)
- Are core dumps and pprof collected? (yes/no)
- Is restart targeted to container/process rather than node? (yes/no)
- Have relevant owners been paged? (yes/no)
- Is rollback plan prepared (stable image / config)? (yes/no)

Config table: default thresholds and owner contacts
-------------------------------------------------
| component            | primary_threshold                   | auto-action                          | owner_team | oncall |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|--------|
| inference-gateway    | p99 > 2s OR token_rate > 10k/s     | quarantine-traffic, swap-fallback    | serving    | pg-serv |
| batching-worker      | memory growth > 30% / 60s          | evict-inflight restart worker        | infra      | pg-infra |
| kv-prefix-cache      | miss-rate > 60% & latency > 500ms  | prefix-readonly, prefetch warm cache | platform   | pg-cache |
| model-eval-sandbox   | token-gen > 50k/request            | throttle, sandboxed-replay           | research   | pg-ml |
| router               | circuits tripped > 75%             | route to quarantine slice            | platform   | pg-router |

(Owners map: serving=eng-serving-runtime; infra=eng-infra; platform=eng-platform; research=eng-applied-ml)

CLI examples and snippets
-------------------------
- Isolate tenant: rtrctl isolate-tenant --tenant T-42 --slice quarantine-2026-03 --concurrency 2
- Capture trace for request: curl "https://inference-gateway/internal/trace?req_id=REQ123" -H "Authorization: Bearer $ANALYST_TOKEN" > /tmp/REQ123.trace
- Query token emit rate (Grafana API): 
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAF_TOKEN" "https://grafana/api/datasources/proxy/1/api/v1/query?query=token_emit_rate%7Btenant%3D%27T-42%27%7D" | jq .

Rollout and feature gating for a new "sweeper"
------------------------------------------------
Purpose: introduce a controlled automatic quarantining component that can be toggled by region/cluster.
Phased rollout:
1) Feature flag off in prod, enabled in staging with synthetic poison-pills.
2) Canary: enable for 1% of traffic in us-west with verbose logging.
3) Metrics gating: require stable latency, no cross-tenant error correlation, and <0.1% false positive rate for 48h.
4) Gradual ramp: 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 7 days with daily review.
Fallback: immediate feature-flag off + revert config if SLO regression observed.

Testing and exercise plan
-------------------------
- Chaos experiments: weekly gameday simulating token-surges and kv-prefix storms against a non-prod replica.
- Unit/integration: simulate OOM via fault-injector and assert quarantine transitions.
- Synthetic library: maintain a corpus of pathological prompts (stored in repo infra/poison-corpus) used in CI for release gating.

Post-incident RCA template (to be attached to incident)
------------------------------------------------------
- Incident ID:
- Start / End:
- Services impacted:
- Root cause summary (2-3 sentences):
- Detection timeline (timestamps):
- Mitigation actions taken and times:
- Why the mitigation worked / did not work:
- Persistent changes planned (config, code, policy):
- Owner for follow-up tasks and ETA:

Roles & ownership
------------------
- First responder: platform oncall (pg-platform). Responsible for initial isolation actions.
- Runtime engineer: eng-serving-runtime. Responsible for heap traces and kernel-level restarts.
- Cache owner: eng-platform (kv-team). Responsible for prefix freeze and warmups.
- Customer success: engage for customer-facing communication if tenant is externally managed.

References & links
------------------
- Observability SLI guide: ../dashboards-and-alerts/token-burst-forensics-and-debugging-workflow-2026.json
- Emergency model swap runbook: ../runbooks/emergency-model-swap-and-token-surge-mitigation-2026.json
- KV cache rebalancer: ../systems-and-services/operator-workflows-for-llm-kv-cache-sharding-and-rebalancing-2026.json

Appendix: Example OPA policy (sanitized)
----------------------------------------
{
  \"id\": \"tenant-rate-limit\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"conditions\": { \"tenant\": \"T-42\", \"token_rate\": { \"gt\": 10000 } },
  \"action\": \"isolate\"
}

Document maintenance
--------------------
- Owner: eng-platform (Priya Natarajan). Review cadence: quarterly or after any major poison-pill incident.
- Additions to the synthetic corpus must be reviewed by eng-applied-ml and infra.

This guide should be used in conjunction with per-service runbooks and security policy. If you find gaps or have observed a new poison pattern, file a PR against this page and tag the reviewers listed above.

source.title: Optimization Config diff engine + semantic validation (2026-02)
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.content:
Overview
Optimization Configs are immutable, versioned JSON payloads used by the control plane to drive runtime behavior (batching, caching, and quantization). To make safe apply/rollback operationally safe, we maintain:
1) a deterministic, human-friendly diff between two versions, and
2) semantic validators that detect dangerous or invalid changes before rollout.

This page documents the current (2026-02) diff format, validation semantics, and rule catalog used by:
- Optimization Config Service (/validate and /apply)
- Console diff view and gating UX
- Rollout controller policy decisions

What changed since the original ENG-4822 implementation
- Batching bounds are now capability-aware: the control plane applies conservative global limits AND enforces per-hardware maxima from runtime capability snapshots.
- Cache shard limits were increased (256 -> 512) to support higher-parallelism Dedicated fleets, with added guardrails for oversharding.
- Quantization validation now supports a "staged" profile state for limited preview rollouts (flagged), while keeping "promoted" as the default requirement for customer-facing applies.
- Diff redaction rules are now explicit allow/deny lists instead of a broad regex-only approach to reduce accidental over-redaction.

Goals
- Prevent high-risk or invalid config changes from being applied (especially at large blast radius).
- Provide deterministic diff output for Console, audit logs, and rollout policy.

Non-goals
- Approval workflows and change management policy (handled by rollout controller + org processes).
- Auto-fixing configs (we validate and explain only).

Definitions
- "Prev" = currently active config version for a target scope.
- "New" = proposed config version being validated/applied.
- "Scope" = where the config is applied (tenant, region, deployment mode, shard set, etc.).

Deterministic diff engine
Input
- config_version_a (prev JSON as stored)
- config_version_b (new JSON as stored)

Output
- Stable ordering with recursive key sorting.
- Path-based changes using JSON Pointer internally (RFC6901), rendered as dotted paths for UI.
- Change type: add | remove | modify.
- Old/new values stringified with size caps.
- Optional per-change risk annotation (low | medium | high) using a field registry.

Ignored / non-functional fields
- The diff engine ignores known non-functional metadata fields by default:
  - description
  - labels
  - ui_notes
(If these fields become operationally meaningful in the future, they must be removed from the ignore list and added to the risk registry explicitly.)

Unknown / forward-compat fields
- Unknown fields are diffed and surfaced; they do not cause validation failure.
- Unknown fields default to medium risk until registered (rollout policy may still gate on medium-risk deltas depending on scope).

Arrays
- If an array is order-insensitive (explicitly marked in the registry), the diff is computed as a set keyed by a stable key (e.g., regions keyed by region_id).
- Otherwise, arrays are diffed by index.

Redaction
- Redaction is applied at diff rendering time only (the stored config remains unchanged).
- Redaction uses:
  - an explicit list of redacted paths (preferred), plus
  - a fallback heuristic for keys containing secret/token/api_key/private_key.
- Redacted values are rendered as "REDACTED" while keeping the path and change type.

Diff summary
The service produces a diff_summary for consumers that do not want to parse the full diff:
- counts: added/removed/modified
- risky_paths: list of high-risk (and optionally medium-risk) paths

Semantic validation
Validators run during:
- /validate (preflight)
- /apply (re-validation to avoid TOCTOU issues when referenced entities change)

Validator contract
Each validator is a pure function:
(new_config, prev_config, context) -> errors[], warnings[], annotations[]

Context includes:
- deployment_mode (Hosted | Dedicated | Private)
- model_id
- tenant_id
- region
- runtime_capabilities snapshot (from runtime catalog)
- feature_flags
- apply_scope (e.g., canary shard vs full region)

Error and warning requirements
- Every message must include a stable rule_id (e.g., VAL-BATCH-001).
- Message text must be stable and searchable; avoid including large payloads.

Batching validators
Hard bounds (errors)
- VAL-BATCH-001: max_concurrent_sequences must be within [1, 4096].
- VAL-BATCH-002: target_queue_delay_ms must be within [0, 250].
- VAL-BATCH-003: max_batch_tokens must be within [1024, 262144] AND <= runtime_capabilities.max_batch_tokens for the selected hardware class.
  - Note: runtime capability maxima vary by GPU and kernel; control plane rejects values above the capability snapshot rather than relying on runtime clamping.

Relationship constraints (errors)
- VAL-BATCH-004: max_batch_tokens must be >= max_sequence_length + safety_margin_tokens.
  - safety_margin_tokens default: 256 (subject to runtime overrides via capabilities).
- VAL-BATCH-005: If continuous_batching.enabled is true, scheduler must be one of: rw-continuous, rw-continuous-v2.

Warning-level checks (warnings)
- VAL-BATCH-101: Increasing max_batch_tokens by >2x vs prev is a warning (throughput/stability risk).
- VAL-BATCH-102: Setting target_queue_delay_ms < 5ms is a warning (throughput collapse risk).

Cache validators
Compatibility (errors)
- VAL-CACHE-001: prefix_cache.enabled cannot be true unless runtime_capabilities.prefix_cache_supported is true for the model family.
- VAL-CACHE-002: cache.max_kv_cache_gb must be <= node allocation cap for the deployment mode.
  - Hosted/Dedicated: cap derived from fleet profile.
  - Private: cap derived from declared node profile (or rejected if unknown).
- VAL-CACHE-003: cache.shard_count must be a power of two and <= 512.
- VAL-CACHE-004: cache.shard_count must not exceed runtime_capabilities.recommended_cache_shards (warning if exceeded; error if > 2x recommended).

Cross-field constraints (errors)
- VAL-CACHE-005: If prefix_cache.enabled is true, cache.key must include model_id and tokenizer_hash to avoid cross-version poisoning.

Warning-level checks (warnings)
- VAL-CACHE-101: Disabling cache when prev enabled emits a warning with expected p95 impact callout.
- VAL-CACHE-102: Enabling cache for long-context models without eviction_policy set is a warning.

Quantization profile validators
Reference integrity (errors)
- VAL-QUANT-001: quant.profile_id must exist.
- VAL-QUANT-002: For customer-facing applies, the profile state must be "promoted".
  - Exception: "staged" is allowed only when feature flag quant_allow_staged_profiles is enabled AND apply_scope is canary-only.
- VAL-QUANT-003: quant.profile_id must be compatible with model_id:
  - architecture
  - tensor parallelism degree
  - weights format

Quality / rollout guardrails (warnings or errors depending on scope)
- VAL-QUANT-101: If profile_id changes vs prev, mark as HIGH RISK.
  - Canary-only scope: warning requiring evaluation gate configuration.
  - Larger scopes (region-wide or tenant-wide): error unless evaluation gate is configured for the apply.

Private deployment nuance (errors)
- VAL-QUANT-004: If deployment_mode == Private and quant.profile_source == remote, reject by default.
  - Override: allow only with feature flag private_allow_remote_quant_artifacts AND an explicit allow_remote_artifacts setting in the apply request.

API surface expectations (service-level)
/validate response must include:
- ok: boolean
- errors[]: blocking
- warnings[]: non-blocking
- diff_summary: counts + risky_paths

Notes:
- Exact JSON payload shape is owned by the Optimization Config Service API page; this document defines semantic expectations and required fields.

Risk registry
We maintain a registry mapping:
- field path -> risk level
- field path -> diff mode (set vs list)
- field path -> redaction rule

Default guidance
- quant.profile_id: high risk
- cache.key, cache.max_kv_cache_gb: high risk
- batching.max_batch_tokens: medium/high depending on delta magnitude and scope

Operational considerations
- /apply must re-resolve referenced entities (quant profile metadata and runtime capabilities) to avoid stale validation.
- Diff value rendering must cap lengths to prevent log blowups.
- All rule_ids must be unique and stable; changing semantics requires either a new rule_id or a clear deprecation note.

Testing requirements
- Unit tests for diff stability:
  - reordered maps
  - null vs missing
  - arrays (set vs list modes)
  - redaction behavior
- Unit tests for each validator rule including warning vs error boundaries.

Ownership
- Primary owner: Eng Platform (Control Plane)
- Runtime capability source of truth: Serving Runtime Catalog
- Console consumers: Product Engineering (Console)

Release / rollout
- Initial implementation landed in control-plane-2026.01.
- Updates described on this page shipped in control-plane-2026.02.

Appendix: Rule ID index (current)
Batching: VAL-BATCH-001..005, VAL-BATCH-101..102
Cache: VAL-CACHE-001..005, VAL-CACHE-101..102
Quant: VAL-QUANT-001..004, VAL-QUANT-101

text:
Overview
Optimization Configs are immutable, versioned JSON payloads used by the control plane to drive runtime behavior (batching, caching, and quantization). To make safe apply/rollback operationally safe, we maintain:
1) a deterministic, human-friendly diff between two versions, and
2) semantic validators that detect dangerous or invalid changes before rollout.

This page documents the current (2026-02) diff format, validation semantics, and rule catalog used by:
- Optimization Config Service (/validate and /apply)
- Console diff view and gating UX
- Rollout controller policy decisions

What changed since the original ENG-4822 implementation
- Batching bounds are now capability-aware: the control plane applies conservative global limits AND enforces per-hardware maxima from runtime capability snapshots.
- Cache shard limits were increased (256 -> 512) to support higher-parallelism Dedicated fleets, with added guardrails for oversharding.
- Quantization validation now supports a "staged" profile state for limited preview rollouts (flagged), while keeping "promoted" as the default requirement for customer-facing applies.
- Diff redaction rules are now explicit allow/deny lists instead of a broad regex-only approach to reduce accidental over-redaction.

Goals
- Prevent high-risk or invalid config changes from being applied (especially at large blast radius).
- Provide deterministic diff output for Console, audit logs, and rollout policy.

Non-goals
- Approval workflows and change management policy (handled by rollout controller + org processes).
- Auto-fixing configs (we validate and explain only).

Definitions
- "Prev" = currently active config version for a target scope.
- "New" = proposed config version being validated/applied.
- "Scope" = where the config is applied (tenant, region, deployment mode, shard set, etc.).

Deterministic diff engine
Input
- config_version_a (prev JSON as stored)
- config_version_b (new JSON as stored)

Output
- Stable ordering with recursive key sorting.
- Path-based changes using JSON Pointer internally (RFC6901), rendered as dotted paths for UI.
- Change type: add | remove | modify.
- Old/new values stringified with size caps.
- Optional per-change risk annotation (low | medium | high) using a field registry.

Ignored / non-functional fields
- The diff engine ignores known non-functional metadata fields by default:
  - description
  - labels
  - ui_notes
(If these fields become operationally meaningful in the future, they must be removed from the ignore list and added to the risk registry explicitly.)

Unknown / forward-compat fields
- Unknown fields are diffed and surfaced; they do not cause validation failure.
- Unknown fields default to medium risk until registered (rollout policy may still gate on medium-risk deltas depending on scope).

Arrays
- If an array is order-insensitive (explicitly marked in the registry), the diff is computed as a set keyed by a stable key (e.g., regions keyed by region_id).
- Otherwise, arrays are diffed by index.

Redaction
- Redaction is applied at diff rendering time only (the stored config remains unchanged).
- Redaction uses:
  - an explicit list of redacted paths (preferred), plus
  - a fallback heuristic for keys containing secret/token/api_key/private_key.
- Redacted values are rendered as "REDACTED" while keeping the path and change type.

Diff summary
The service produces a diff_summary for consumers that do not want to parse the full diff:
- counts: added/removed/modified
- risky_paths: list of high-risk (and optionally medium-risk) paths

Semantic validation
Validators run during:
- /validate (preflight)
- /apply (re-validation to avoid TOCTOU issues when referenced entities change)

Validator contract
Each validator is a pure function:
(new_config, prev_config, context) -> errors[], warnings[], annotations[]

Context includes:
- deployment_mode (Hosted | Dedicated | Private)
- model_id
- tenant_id
- region
- runtime_capabilities snapshot (from runtime catalog)
- feature_flags
- apply_scope (e.g., canary shard vs full region)

Error and warning requirements
- Every message must include a stable rule_id (e.g., VAL-BATCH-001).
- Message text must be stable and searchable; avoid including large payloads.

Batching validators
Hard bounds (errors)
- VAL-BATCH-001: max_concurrent_sequences must be within [1, 4096].
- VAL-BATCH-002: target_queue_delay_ms must be within [0, 250].
- VAL-BATCH-003: max_batch_tokens must be within [1024, 262144] AND <= runtime_capabilities.max_batch_tokens for the selected hardware class.
  - Note: runtime capability maxima vary by GPU and kernel; control plane rejects values above the capability snapshot rather than relying on runtime clamping.

Relationship constraints (errors)
- VAL-BATCH-004: max_batch_tokens must be >= max_sequence_length + safety_margin_tokens.
  - safety_margin_tokens default: 256 (subject to runtime overrides via capabilities).
- VAL-BATCH-005: If continuous_batching.enabled is true, scheduler must be one of: rw-continuous, rw-continuous-v2.

Warning-level checks (warnings)
- VAL-BATCH-101: Increasing max_batch_tokens by >2x vs prev is a warning (throughput/stability risk).
- VAL-BATCH-102: Setting target_queue_delay_ms < 5ms is a warning (throughput collapse risk).

Cache validators
Compatibility (errors)
- VAL-CACHE-001: prefix_cache.enabled cannot be true unless runtime_capabilities.prefix_cache_supported is true for the model family.
- VAL-CACHE-002: cache.max_kv_cache_gb must be <= node allocation cap for the deployment mode.
  - Hosted/Dedicated: cap derived from fleet profile.
  - Private: cap derived from declared node profile (or rejected if unknown).
- VAL-CACHE-003: cache.shard_count must be a power of two and <= 512.
- VAL-CACHE-004: cache.shard_count must not exceed runtime_capabilities.recommended_cache_shards (warning if exceeded; error if > 2x recommended).

Cross-field constraints (errors)
- VAL-CACHE-005: If prefix_cache.enabled is true, cache.key must include model_id and tokenizer_hash to avoid cross-version poisoning.

Warning-level checks (warnings)
- VAL-CACHE-101: Disabling cache when prev enabled emits a warning with expected p95 impact callout.
- VAL-CACHE-102: Enabling cache for long-context models without eviction_policy set is a warning.

Quantization profile validators
Reference integrity (errors)
- VAL-QUANT-001: quant.profile_id must exist.
- VAL-QUANT-002: For customer-facing applies, the profile state must be "promoted".
  - Exception: "staged" is allowed only when feature flag quant_allow_staged_profiles is enabled AND apply_scope is canary-only.
- VAL-QUANT-003: quant.profile_id must be compatible with model_id:
  - architecture
  - tensor parallelism degree
  - weights format

Quality / rollout guardrails (warnings or errors depending on scope)
- VAL-QUANT-101: If profile_id changes vs prev, mark as HIGH RISK.
  - Canary-only scope: warning requiring evaluation gate configuration.
  - Larger scopes (region-wide or tenant-wide): error unless evaluation gate is configured for the apply.

Private deployment nuance (errors)
- VAL-QUANT-004: If deployment_mode == Private and quant.profile_source == remote, reject by default.
  - Override: allow only with feature flag private_allow_remote_quant_artifacts AND an explicit allow_remote_artifacts setting in the apply request.

API surface expectations (service-level)
/validate response must include:
- ok: boolean
- errors[]: blocking
- warnings[]: non-blocking
- diff_summary: counts + risky_paths

Notes:
- Exact JSON payload shape is owned by the Optimization Config Service API page; this document defines semantic expectations and required fields.

Risk registry
We maintain a registry mapping:
- field path -> risk level
- field path -> diff mode (set vs list)
- field path -> redaction rule

Default guidance
- quant.profile_id: high risk
- cache.key, cache.max_kv_cache_gb: high risk
- batching.max_batch_tokens: medium/high depending on delta magnitude and scope

Operational considerations
- /apply must re-resolve referenced entities (quant profile metadata and runtime capabilities) to avoid stale validation.
- Diff value rendering must cap lengths to prevent log blowups.
- All rule_ids must be unique and stable; changing semantics requires either a new rule_id or a clear deprecation note.

Testing requirements
- Unit tests for diff stability:
  - reordered maps
  - null vs missing
  - arrays (set vs list modes)
  - redaction behavior
- Unit tests for each validator rule including warning vs error boundaries.

Ownership
- Primary owner: Eng Platform (Control Plane)
- Runtime capability source of truth: Serving Runtime Catalog
- Console consumers: Product Engineering (Console)

Release / rollout
- Initial implementation landed in control-plane-2026.01.
- Updates described on this page shipped in control-plane-2026.02.

Appendix: Rule ID index (current)
Batching: VAL-BATCH-001..005, VAL-BATCH-101..102
Cache: VAL-CACHE-001..005, VAL-CACHE-101..102
Quant: VAL-QUANT-001..004, VAL-QUANT-101

source.title: FP8/INT8 benchmark + rollout dashboards and alerting spec
source.author: Allison Grant
source.body:
## Overview
This page defines the Grafana dashboards and alerting rules used for the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint and for staged rollout monitoring (perf canary → expanded cohorts). It standardizes:
- What we measure (TTFT, TPOT, throughput, errors, GPU health, scheduler signals)
- How we slice results (model/GPU/region/kernel-mode/workload)
- How we alert (thresholds, noise suppression, sample-size rules, routing)

**Primary consumers:** Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling), SRE, and Infra during benchmarking and rollout.

**Out of scope:** Customer-facing charts/claims. See Product guidance and benchmarking standards for what can be externally shared.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1) Make benchmark runs comparable and debuggable (kernel wins vs scheduler/queuing artifacts).
2) Provide fast rollback signals during canary (TTFT/TPOT regressions, error spikes, GPU memory regressions).
3) Support the ADR/kernel auto-selection rollout model: enable per model × region × GPU SKU × kernel-mode.

### Non-goals
- Replace deep profiling (Nsight/CUPTI). Dashboards should help *choose the next trace to capture*, not replace traces.
- Provide a single global threshold for all models/SKUs. We use threshold profiles by SKU + workload class.

---

## Data sources and metric conventions
### Data sources
- **Runtime metrics (Prometheus):** request/queue/cost/perf histograms and counters emitted by the serving runtime.
- **GPU exporter (Prometheus):** utilization, memory used, power/clock, ECC, throttling where available.
- **Perf canary service:** cohort membership + comparison-to-baseline outputs.
- **Benchmark runner metadata:** git SHA, container image tag, CUDA/driver, GPU clocks, model hash, config ID.

### Naming and tags (must-have dimensions)
All panels and alerts should be filterable by the following labels (exact label keys below). If a metric is missing one of these, it is not eligible for canary gating.

**Required labels**
- `model` (hosted catalog name; includes version pin where applicable)
- `model_family` (optional; e.g., llama, mistral, qwen)
- `gpu_sku` (e.g., `h100-sxm`, `a100-80gb`, `l4`)
- `region` (e.g., `us-east`, `eu-west`)
- `kernel_mode` (one of: `baseline`, `fp8`, `int8`)
- `workload_mode` (one of: `prefill`, `decode`, `mixed`)
- `route` (API route or internal route name; benchmark harness uses `bench/<suite>/<case>`)
- `cohort` (for rollout: `control`, `canary`, `treatment`; benchmark runs use `benchmark`)

**Strongly recommended labels**
- `scheduler_profile` (e.g., `default`, `mixed_prefill_decode_v2`)
- `batching_policy` (e.g., `cb_enabled`, `cb_disabled`)
- `quant_profile` (e.g., `fp8_e4m3fn`, `int8_w8a8_per_channel`)

### Percentiles and aggregation rules
- TTFT and TPOT are tracked as histograms; dashboard defaults:
  - **TTFT:** p50 and p95
  - **TPOT:** p50 and p95
- For canary alerting, we alert on **p95** and sometimes **p99** for customer-impacting cohorts.
- Compare like-for-like: same `(model, gpu_sku, region, route, workload_mode)` and same time window.

### Minimum sample size / noise suppression (rollout)
Alerts that compare canary vs control must satisfy:
- At least **N = 200 requests** per slice per window (default 10m), or the alert is suppressed.
- A **warm-up exclusion** of the first 5 minutes after enabling a kernel-mode in a cohort.
- If fewer than 3 windows meet N over the last 30m, the slice is marked **low confidence** and does not trigger auto-rollback.

---

## Dashboard inventory
### 1) FP8/INT8 — Benchmark overview (suite health)
**Purpose:** Verify benchmark runs are stable and comparable; catch environment drift quickly.

**Key panels (default grouping: model × gpu_sku):**
- Run metadata table: git SHA, runtime image, driver/CUDA, clock-lock status, node pool, MIG/partitioning
- TTFT p50/p95 by kernel_mode (baseline/fp8/int8)
- TPOT p50/p95 by kernel_mode
- Throughput (tokens/s) by kernel_mode
- Error rate and retry rate
- Variance view: p95 bands across repeated runs (shows measurement stability)

**Notes:** This dashboard is used during the benchmark sprint daily triage. Any anomalous spikes should be correlated with driver/clock differences before filing a kernel regression.

### 2) FP8/INT8 — Kernel comparison (prefill vs decode)
**Purpose:** Isolate where wins come from: prefill kernels (matmul/attention) vs decode kernels + KV cache behavior.

**Panels:**
- Split latency: `prefill_ms` vs `decode_ms` (stacked time series)
- TTFT vs prompt length buckets
- TPOT vs generation length buckets
- Kernel_mode win/loss heatmap by seq-len regime (short/medium/long)
- Launch overhead proxy: requests/sec vs GPU util (spot underutilization)

**Required filters:** `workload_mode` and prompt/gen bucket.

### 3) FP8/INT8 — Scheduler + queueing breakdown
**Purpose:** Validate that end-to-end improvements are not being masked by queueing/backpressure; catch regressions caused by scheduler knobs.

**Panels:**
- Queue depth p50/p95
- Time-in-queue p50/p95
- Batch size distribution (prefill and decode)
- Continuous batching window / effective delay
- Backpressure counters (rate)
- Concurrency (inflight requests) by cohort

**Interpretation tip:** A kernel that is faster in microbench can look worse end-to-end if it increases fragmentation or reduces effective batch sizes.

### 4) GPU health + capacity guardrails
**Purpose:** Ensure benchmark and canary results are not polluted by throttling, ECC, or memory pressure; detect fleet issues.

**Panels:**
- GPU utilization (%) by node and aggregated
- GPU memory used (GiB) and memory headroom
- Power draw / clocks (where available)
- Throttling indicators (thermal/power)
- ECC corrected/uncorrected errors
- Node-level noisy neighbor signal (CPU steal, network saturation) if available

**Guardrail:** Any benchmark case where throttling is detected should be labeled **invalid** and re-run with clock pinning and isolated capacity.

### 5) Perf canary — rollout monitoring (control vs canary)
**Purpose:** Operational dashboard for staged enablement of new kernel modes.

**Panels (default: model × region × gpu_sku):**
- Canary enablement status (flag state and cohort allocation)
- TTFT p95: canary vs control (absolute + % delta)
- TPOT p95: canary vs control (absolute + % delta)
- Tokens/s: canary vs control
- Error rate (5xx, timeouts) and fallback rate
- Fallback reasons (shape-guard, unsupported path, safety rollback)

**Callouts:**
- A small TTFT regression can be acceptable if TPOT and tokens/s improve and no customer SLOs are violated; default policy is still to rollback on TTFT p95 breaches unless explicitly waived.

---

## Standard panels and definitions (what every dashboard should include)
To avoid dashboard drift, each FP8/INT8 dashboard should include:
1) **Scope selector row:** `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`, `cohort`, `workload_mode`
2) **SLO summary tiles:** TTFT p95, TPOT p95, error rate, fallback rate
3) **Confidence tiles:** request count per slice, warm-up status, variance indicator
4) **Artifact links:** last benchmark artifact (runner output), related Nsight traces (if captured)

---

## Alerting specification (perf canary + fleet safety)
### Alert routing
- **Page (PagerDuty / SRE on-call):** customer-impacting regressions in canary/treatment cohorts, or fleet health issues.
- **Slack (#eng-runtime, #eng-sre):** informational and investigation alerts.
- **Ticket (Linear/Jira):** repeated low-severity issues or noisy alerts requiring tuning.

### Canary alert thresholds (default profiles)
Thresholds are applied on **canary vs control** deltas for the same slice.

**Profile A: H100 (hosted primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +20ms** for 2/3 windows (10m windows)
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +6%** and **> +0.8ms/token** for 2/3 windows
- Error rate (5xx + timeouts): **> +0.15% absolute** for 2 windows
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.0% absolute** (indicates shape-guard or hidden correctness/perf issues)

**Profile B: A100 (legacy primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +25ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +1.0ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.20% absolute**
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.5% absolute**

**Profile C: Cost SKU (L4 / similar)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +12%** and **> +30ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +1.2ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.25% absolute**
- GPU memory headroom regression: **< 1.5GiB** sustained (risk of OOM)

**Notes on thresholds**
- The percent + absolute dual condition avoids false positives on very low-latency slices.
- Profiles can be tightened after initial stabilization.
- Any change to profiles must be reviewed by SRE + Runtime perf owner (Noah Patel) and documented in the perf-canary threshold PR.

### Auto-rollback triggers
Auto-rollback is enabled only when all conditions hold:
- Slice meets minimum sample size (see above)
- Regression persists for >= 20 minutes
- No ongoing incident declared for unrelated infrastructure events

**Rollback triggers (any):**
- TTFT p95 breach (per profile)
- TPOT p95 breach (per profile)
- Error rate breach (per profile)
- GPU OOM events > 0 in canary cohort for that slice

**Rollback action:** disable `kernel_mode=fp8` or `kernel_mode=int8` for the affected `(model, region, gpu_sku)` slice; keep baseline enabled.

### Fleet safety alerts (benchmark + canary)
These do not require control/canary comparison.
- GPU thermal throttling detected on benchmark nodes: warn in #eng-runtime (re-run required)
- ECC uncorrectable error > 0: page SRE, cordon node pool
- GPU memory used > 97% sustained for 10m for a slice: warn (risk of OOM during longer-context traffic)
- Queue depth p95 above configured limits for 15m: warn + suggest scheduler/batching investigation

---

## Dashboard/alert implementation notes
### Tagging requirements
If the following tags are missing, the metric will not be used for gating:
- `kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`, `model`, `region`, `cohort`

Owners:
- Runtime metric tagging: Serving Runtime (Scheduling) — Julian Moore
- Dashboard implementation: Eng Platform Observability — Allison Grant

### Recommended PromQL patterns (examples)
**TTFT p95 by cohort (example pattern):**
- Use `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (rate(runtime_ttft_ms_bucket[5m])))`

**Delta calculation (canary vs control):**
- Compute p95 for each cohort and then calculate `(canary - control) / control` for % deltas.

**Minimum sample size (requests):**
- `sum by (model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (increase(runtime_requests_total[10m]))`

### Annotation and event overlays
All rollout dashboards must show annotations for:
- Flag enable/disable events (kernel-mode changes)
- Runtime release version changes
- Driver/container changes on node pools

---

## Operational checklist (Definition of Done for dashboards)
A dashboard/alert set is considered ready when:
1) All required dimensions exist and are populated for top hosted models.
2) Each dashboard supports: per-model drilldown, per-region drilldown, and kernel_mode comparisons.
3) Alerts fire in staging with synthetic regressions and do not fire on low-sample windows.
4) Links to runbooks are present:
   - Perf canary rollout runbook
   - FP8/INT8 regression triage playbook
   - Nsight profiling guide
5) A documented owner exists for each alert group (SRE vs Runtime).

---

## Escalation and triage pointers
When an alert fires:
1) Confirm sample size + warm-up status.
2) Check GPU health (throttling/clock drift) and driver/container consistency.
3) Check fallback rate: a spike often indicates a shape-guard or alignment edge case.
4) If regression is real, trigger rollback (auto or manual) and open a triage ticket with:
   - affected slice (model/region/gpu_sku/kernel_mode)
   - start time and graphs
   - benchmark artifact links and git SHAs
   - any recent config changes (scheduler_profile/batching_policy)

Owners:
- Rollback decision: Rafael Mendes (SRE) with Runtime on-call
- Kernel investigation: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
- Scheduler investigation: Sofia Ivanova / Omar El-Sayed

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-26: Tightened H100 TTFT threshold from +10% to +8% after reducing measurement variance; added fallback-rate gating.
- 2026-02-18: Added required labels (`kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`) to gating criteria; added warm-up exclusion.
- 2026-02-10: Initial dashboard set defined for benchmark + canary monitoring.

## Standard panels and definitions (what every dashboard should include)
To avoid dashboard drift, each FP8/INT8 dashboard should include:
1) **Scope selector row:** `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`, `cohort`, `workload_mode`
2) **SLO summary tiles:** TTFT p95, TPOT p95, error rate, fallback rate
3) **Confidence tiles:** request count per slice, warm-up status, variance indicator
4) **Artifact links:** last benchmark artifact (runner output), related Nsight traces (if captured)

---

## Alerting specification (perf canary + fleet safety)
### Alert routing
- **Page (PagerDuty / SRE on-call):** customer-impacting regressions in canary/treatment cohorts, or fleet health issues.
- **Slack (#eng-runtime, #eng-sre):** informational and investigation alerts.
- **Ticket (Linear/Jira):** repeated low-severity issues or noisy alerts requiring tuning.

### Canary alert thresholds (default profiles)
Thresholds are applied on **canary vs control** deltas for the same slice.

**Profile A: H100 (hosted primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +20ms** for 2/3 windows (10m windows)
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +6%** and **> +0.8ms/token** for 2/3 windows
- Error rate (5xx + timeouts): **> +0.15% absolute** for 2 windows
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.0% absolute** (indicates shape-guard or hidden correctness/perf issues)

**Profile B: A100 (legacy primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +25ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +1.0ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.20% absolute**
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.5% absolute**

**Profile C: Cost SKU (L4 / similar)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +12%** and **> +30ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +1.2ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.25% absolute**
- GPU memory headroom regression: **< 1.5GiB** sustained (risk of OOM)

**Notes on thresholds**
- The percent + absolute dual condition avoids false positives on very low-latency slices.
- Profiles can be tightened after initial stabilization.
- Any change to profiles must be reviewed by SRE + Runtime perf owner (Noah Patel) and documented in the perf-canary threshold PR.

### Auto-rollback triggers
Auto-rollback is enabled only when all conditions hold:
- Slice meets minimum sample size (see above)
- Regression persists for >= 20 minutes
- No ongoing incident declared for unrelated infrastructure events

**Rollback triggers (any):**
- TTFT p95 breach (per profile)
- TPOT p95 breach (per profile)
- Error rate breach (per profile)
- GPU OOM events > 0 in canary cohort for that slice

**Rollback action:** disable `kernel_mode=fp8` or `kernel_mode=int8` for the affected `(model, region, gpu_sku)` slice; keep baseline enabled.

### Fleet safety alerts (benchmark + canary)
These do not require control/canary comparison.
- GPU thermal throttling detected on benchmark nodes: warn in #eng-runtime (re-run required)
- ECC uncorrectable error > 0: page SRE, cordon node pool
- GPU memory used > 97% sustained for 10m for a slice: warn (risk of OOM during longer-context traffic)
- Queue depth p95 above configured limits for 15m: warn + suggest scheduler/batching investigation

---

## Dashboard/alert implementation notes
### Tagging requirements
If the following tags are missing, the metric will not be used for gating:
- `kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`, `model`, `region`, `cohort`

Owners:
- Runtime metric tagging: Serving Runtime (Scheduling) — Julian Moore
- Dashboard implementation: Eng Platform Observability — Allison Grant

### Recommended PromQL patterns (examples)
**TTFT p95 by cohort (example pattern):**
- Use `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (rate(runtime_ttft_ms_bucket[5m])))`

**Delta calculation (canary vs control):**
- Compute p95 for each cohort and then calculate `(canary - control) / control` for % deltas.

**Minimum sample size (requests):**
- `sum by (model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (increase(runtime_requests_total[10m]))`

### Annotation and event overlays
All rollout dashboards must show annotations for:
- Flag enable/disable events (kernel-mode changes)
- Runtime release version changes
- Driver/container changes on node pools

---

## Operational checklist (Definition of Done for dashboards)
A dashboard/alert set is considered ready when:
1) All required dimensions exist and are populated for top hosted models.
2) Each dashboard supports: per-model drilldown, per-region drilldown, and kernel_mode comparisons.
3) Alerts fire in staging with synthetic regressions and do not fire on low-sample windows.
4) Links to runbooks are present:
   - Perf canary rollout runbook
   - FP8/INT8 regression triage playbook
   - Nsight profiling guide
5) A documented owner exists for each alert group (SRE vs Runtime).

---

## Escalation and triage pointers
When an alert fires:
1) Confirm sample size + warm-up status.
2) Check GPU health (throttling/clock drift) and driver/container consistency.
3) Check fallback rate: a spike often indicates a shape-guard or alignment edge case.
4) If regression is real, trigger rollback (auto or manual) and open a triage ticket with:
   - affected slice (model/region/gpu_sku/kernel_mode)
   - start time and graphs
   - benchmark artifact links and git SHAs
   - any recent config changes (scheduler_profile/batching_policy)

Owners:
- Rollback decision: Rafael Mendes (SRE) with Runtime on-call
- Kernel investigation: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
- Scheduler investigation: Sofia Ivanova / Omar El-Sayed

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-26: Tightened H100 TTFT threshold from +10% to +8% after reducing measurement variance; added fallback-rate gating.
- 2026-02-18: Added required labels (`kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`) to gating criteria; added warm-up exclusion.
- 2026-02-10: Initial dashboard set defined for benchmark + canary monitoring.
text:
## Overview
This page defines the Grafana dashboards and alerting rules used for the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint and for staged rollout monitoring (perf canary → expanded cohorts). It standardizes:
- What we measure (TTFT, TPOT, throughput, errors, GPU health, scheduler signals)
- How we slice results (model/GPU/region/kernel-mode/workload)
- How we alert (thresholds, noise suppression, sample-size rules, routing)

**Primary consumers:** Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling), SRE, and Infra during benchmarking and rollout.

**Out of scope:** Customer-facing charts/claims. See Product guidance and benchmarking standards for what can be externally shared.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1) Make benchmark runs comparable and debuggable (kernel wins vs scheduler/queuing artifacts).
2) Provide fast rollback signals during canary (TTFT/TPOT regressions, error spikes, GPU memory regressions).
3) Support the ADR/kernel auto-selection rollout model: enable per model × region × GPU SKU × kernel-mode.

### Non-goals
- Replace deep profiling (Nsight/CUPTI). Dashboards should help *choose the next trace to capture*, not replace traces.
- Provide a single global threshold for all models/SKUs. We use threshold profiles by SKU + workload class.

---

## Data sources and metric conventions
### Data sources
- **Runtime metrics (Prometheus):** request/queue/cost/perf histograms and counters emitted by the serving runtime.
- **GPU exporter (Prometheus):** utilization, memory used, power/clock, ECC, throttling where available.
- **Perf canary service:** cohort membership + comparison-to-baseline outputs.
- **Benchmark runner metadata:** git SHA, container image tag, CUDA/driver, GPU clocks, model hash, config ID.

### Naming and tags (must-have dimensions)
All panels and alerts should be filterable by the following labels (exact label keys below). If a metric is missing one of these, it is not eligible for canary gating.

**Required labels**
- `model` (hosted catalog name; includes version pin where applicable)
- `model_family` (optional; e.g., llama, mistral, qwen)
- `gpu_sku` (e.g., `h100-sxm`, `a100-80gb`, `l4`)
- `region` (e.g., `us-east`, `eu-west`)
- `kernel_mode` (one of: `baseline`, `fp8`, `int8`)
- `workload_mode` (one of: `prefill`, `decode`, `mixed`)
- `route` (API route or internal route name; benchmark harness uses `bench/<suite>/<case>`)
- `cohort` (for rollout: `control`, `canary`, `treatment`; benchmark runs use `benchmark`)

**Strongly recommended labels**
- `scheduler_profile` (e.g., `default`, `mixed_prefill_decode_v2`)
- `batching_policy` (e.g., `cb_enabled`, `cb_disabled`)
- `quant_profile` (e.g., `fp8_e4m3fn`, `int8_w8a8_per_channel`)

### Percentiles and aggregation rules
- TTFT and TPOT are tracked as histograms; dashboard defaults:
  - **TTFT:** p50 and p95
  - **TPOT:** p50 and p95
- For canary alerting, we alert on **p95** and sometimes **p99** for customer-impacting cohorts.
- Compare like-for-like: same `(model, gpu_sku, region, route, workload_mode)` and same time window.

### Minimum sample size / noise suppression (rollout)
Alerts that compare canary vs control must satisfy:
- At least **N = 200 requests** per slice per window (default 10m), or the alert is suppressed.
- A **warm-up exclusion** of the first 5 minutes after enabling a kernel-mode in a cohort.
- If fewer than 3 windows meet N over the last 30m, the slice is marked **low confidence** and does not trigger auto-rollback.

---

## Dashboard inventory
### 1) FP8/INT8 — Benchmark overview (suite health)
**Purpose:** Verify benchmark runs are stable and comparable; catch environment drift quickly.

**Key panels (default grouping: model × gpu_sku):**
- Run metadata table: git SHA, runtime image, driver/CUDA, clock-lock status, node pool, MIG/partitioning
- TTFT p50/p95 by kernel_mode (baseline/fp8/int8)
- TPOT p50/p95 by kernel_mode
- Throughput (tokens/s) by kernel_mode
- Error rate and retry rate
- Variance view: p95 bands across repeated runs (shows measurement stability)

**Notes:** This dashboard is used during the benchmark sprint daily triage. Any anomalous spikes should be correlated with driver/clock differences before filing a kernel regression.

### 2) FP8/INT8 — Kernel comparison (prefill vs decode)
**Purpose:** Isolate where wins come from: prefill kernels (matmul/attention) vs decode kernels + KV cache behavior.

**Panels:**
- Split latency: `prefill_ms` vs `decode_ms` (stacked time series)
- TTFT vs prompt length buckets
- TPOT vs generation length buckets
- Kernel_mode win/loss heatmap by seq-len regime (short/medium/long)
- Launch overhead proxy: requests/sec vs GPU util (spot underutilization)

**Required filters:** `workload_mode` and prompt/gen bucket.

### 3) FP8/INT8 — Scheduler + queueing breakdown
**Purpose:** Validate that end-to-end improvements are not being masked by queueing/backpressure; catch regressions caused by scheduler knobs.

**Panels:**
- Queue depth p50/p95
- Time-in-queue p50/p95
- Batch size distribution (prefill and decode)
- Continuous batching window / effective delay
- Backpressure counters (rate)
- Concurrency (inflight requests) by cohort

**Interpretation tip:** A kernel that is faster in microbench can look worse end-to-end if it increases fragmentation or reduces effective batch sizes.

### 4) GPU health + capacity guardrails
**Purpose:** Ensure benchmark and canary results are not polluted by throttling, ECC, or memory pressure; detect fleet issues.

**Panels:**
- GPU utilization (%) by node and aggregated
- GPU memory used (GiB) and memory headroom
- Power draw / clocks (where available)
- Throttling indicators (thermal/power)
- ECC corrected/uncorrected errors
- Node-level noisy neighbor signal (CPU steal, network saturation) if available

**Guardrail:** Any benchmark case where throttling is detected should be labeled **invalid** and re-run with clock pinning and isolated capacity.

### 5) Perf canary — rollout monitoring (control vs canary)
**Purpose:** Operational dashboard for staged enablement of new kernel modes.

**Panels (default: model × region × gpu_sku):**
- Canary enablement status (flag state and cohort allocation)
- TTFT p95: canary vs control (absolute + % delta)
- TPOT p95: canary vs control (absolute + % delta)
- Tokens/s: canary vs control
- Error rate (5xx, timeouts) and fallback rate
- Fallback reasons (shape-guard, unsupported path, safety rollback)

**Callouts:**
- A small TTFT regression can be acceptable if TPOT and tokens/s improve and no customer SLOs are violated; default policy is still to rollback on TTFT p95 breaches unless explicitly waived.

---

## Standard panels and definitions (what every dashboard should include)
To avoid dashboard drift, each FP8/INT8 dashboard should include:
1) **Scope selector row:** `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`, `cohort`, `workload_mode`
2) **SLO summary tiles:** TTFT p95, TPOT p95, error rate, fallback rate
3) **Confidence tiles:** request count per slice, warm-up status, variance indicator
4) **Artifact links:** last benchmark artifact (runner output), related Nsight traces (if captured)

---

## Alerting specification (perf canary + fleet safety)
### Alert routing
- **Page (PagerDuty / SRE on-call):** customer-impacting regressions in canary/treatment cohorts, or fleet health issues.
- **Slack (#eng-runtime, #eng-sre):** informational and investigation alerts.
- **Ticket (Linear/Jira):** repeated low-severity issues or noisy alerts requiring tuning.

### Canary alert thresholds (default profiles)
Thresholds are applied on **canary vs control** deltas for the same slice.

**Profile A: H100 (hosted primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +20ms** for 2/3 windows (10m windows)
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +6%** and **> +0.8ms/token** for 2/3 windows
- Error rate (5xx + timeouts): **> +0.15% absolute** for 2 windows
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.0% absolute** (indicates shape-guard or hidden correctness/perf issues)

**Profile B: A100 (legacy primary)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +25ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +8%** and **> +1.0ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.20% absolute**
- Fallback rate increase: **> +2.5% absolute**

**Profile C: Cost SKU (L4 / similar)**
- TTFT p95 regression: **> +12%** and **> +30ms**
- TPOT p95 regression: **> +10%** and **> +1.2ms/token**
- Error rate: **> +0.25% absolute**
- GPU memory headroom regression: **< 1.5GiB** sustained (risk of OOM)

**Notes on thresholds**
- The percent + absolute dual condition avoids false positives on very low-latency slices.
- Profiles can be tightened after initial stabilization.
- Any change to profiles must be reviewed by SRE + Runtime perf owner (Noah Patel) and documented in the perf-canary threshold PR.

### Auto-rollback triggers
Auto-rollback is enabled only when all conditions hold:
- Slice meets minimum sample size (see above)
- Regression persists for >= 20 minutes
- No ongoing incident declared for unrelated infrastructure events

**Rollback triggers (any):**
- TTFT p95 breach (per profile)
- TPOT p95 breach (per profile)
- Error rate breach (per profile)
- GPU OOM events > 0 in canary cohort for that slice

**Rollback action:** disable `kernel_mode=fp8` or `kernel_mode=int8` for the affected `(model, region, gpu_sku)` slice; keep baseline enabled.

### Fleet safety alerts (benchmark + canary)
These do not require control/canary comparison.
- GPU thermal throttling detected on benchmark nodes: warn in #eng-runtime (re-run required)
- ECC uncorrectable error > 0: page SRE, cordon node pool
- GPU memory used > 97% sustained for 10m for a slice: warn (risk of OOM during longer-context traffic)
- Queue depth p95 above configured limits for 15m: warn + suggest scheduler/batching investigation

---

## Dashboard/alert implementation notes
### Tagging requirements
If the following tags are missing, the metric will not be used for gating:
- `kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`, `model`, `region`, `cohort`

Owners:
- Runtime metric tagging: Serving Runtime (Scheduling) — Julian Moore
- Dashboard implementation: Eng Platform Observability — Allison Grant

### Recommended PromQL patterns (examples)
**TTFT p95 by cohort (example pattern):**
- Use `histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (rate(runtime_ttft_ms_bucket[5m])))`

**Delta calculation (canary vs control):**
- Compute p95 for each cohort and then calculate `(canary - control) / control` for % deltas.

**Minimum sample size (requests):**
- `sum by (model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode, cohort) (increase(runtime_requests_total[10m]))`

### Annotation and event overlays
All rollout dashboards must show annotations for:
- Flag enable/disable events (kernel-mode changes)
- Runtime release version changes
- Driver/container changes on node pools

---

## Operational checklist (Definition of Done for dashboards)
A dashboard/alert set is considered ready when:
1) All required dimensions exist and are populated for top hosted models.
2) Each dashboard supports: per-model drilldown, per-region drilldown, and kernel_mode comparisons.
3) Alerts fire in staging with synthetic regressions and do not fire on low-sample windows.
4) Links to runbooks are present:
   - Perf canary rollout runbook
   - FP8/INT8 regression triage playbook
   - Nsight profiling guide
5) A documented owner exists for each alert group (SRE vs Runtime).

---

## Escalation and triage pointers
When an alert fires:
1) Confirm sample size + warm-up status.
2) Check GPU health (throttling/clock drift) and driver/container consistency.
3) Check fallback rate: a spike often indicates a shape-guard or alignment edge case.
4) If regression is real, trigger rollback (auto or manual) and open a triage ticket with:
   - affected slice (model/region/gpu_sku/kernel_mode)
   - start time and graphs
   - benchmark artifact links and git SHAs
   - any recent config changes (scheduler_profile/batching_policy)

Owners:
- Rollback decision: Rafael Mendes (SRE) with Runtime on-call
- Kernel investigation: Hiro Tanaka / Grace Kim
- Scheduler investigation: Sofia Ivanova / Omar El-Sayed

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-26: Tightened H100 TTFT threshold from +10% to +8% after reducing measurement variance; added fallback-rate gating.
- 2026-02-18: Added required labels (`kernel_mode`, `gpu_sku`) to gating criteria; added warm-up exclusion.
