  - Contains a stable schema for RevRec and month-end evidence.

### 4.2 Derived aggregates used for monitoring
- observability.meter_sanity_hourly
  - Hourly aggregates by (deployment_mode, meter_name, account_id, region, model, endpoint_family)
  - Measures include event_quantity, ledger_quantity, drift_abs, drift_pct, null_rates, duplicate_rates, freshness_lag_p50/p95

### 4.3 Data contracts and SLAs
- Usage events ingestion SLA (p95): available in telemetry.usage_events_v3 within 30 minutes of request_end_ts.
- Recon export SLA (p95): finance.recon_export_daily updated by 08:00 UTC for prior UTC day.
- Ledger posting SLA (p95): billing.ledger_line_items available within 2 hours of end-of-hour for Hosted API; within 6 hours for Dedicated (due to pool rollups).

## 5. Dashboard surfaces
We publish two surfaces with equivalent underlying metrics:
1) Grafana: "Billing / Meter Sanity" folder (source-of-truth for alert rules)
2) Redwood Console: Operations → Metering Sanity (simplified view for Finance/RevOps)

Grafana is required for paging alerts; Console is required for self-serve drilldowns (account-level) with access controls.

## 6. Dashboard layout (Grafana)
### 6.1 Overview: Health summary (last 24h)
Panels (all filterable by deployment_mode, meter_name, region, model, endpoint_family):
- Freshness Lag (p50/p95) for usage_events_v3 and ledger_line_items
- Drift vs Ledger (p50, p95, max) with both quantity and amount_usd views
- Duplicate Event Rate (overall)
- Null/Unknown Dimension Rate (overall)
- Top 10 accounts by drift_abs and drift_pct (with min volume guardrail)

Recommended annotations:
- Metering deploys (tagged with git SHA)
- Backfills and replays
- Month-end usage freeze windows

### 6.2 Drift deep-dive: Events vs Ledger
Panels:
- Drift time series (hourly) by meter_name
- Drift heatmap by region × model (last 24h)
- Drift by endpoint_family (chat, completions, embeddings, rerank)
- Account leaderboard with drilldown links (Console + query link)

Drift calculations:
- drift_abs = event_quantity - ledger_quantity
- drift_pct = drift_abs / NULLIF(ledger_quantity, 0)
- For amount-based comparisons: compute event_amount_usd using the active rate card snapshot used by billing (shadow rating), and compare to ledger amount_usd.

Guardrails:
- Do not alert on drift_pct when ledger_quantity < 10,000 tokens (or equivalent units) in the window; use drift_abs thresholds instead.
- Dedicated: compare GPU-seconds aggregates at daily granularity in addition to hourly (autoscaling transitions can cause hour-boundary effects).

### 6.3 Completeness: Null/Unknown dimensions
Panels:
- Null rate by dimension (region, model, account_id, request_id, deployment_mode)
- Null rate by service/source (edge, control_plane, backfill)
- Top offending accounts and top offending models

Definitions:
- null_rate(dim) = COUNT_IF(dim IS NULL OR dim IN ('', 'unknown')) / COUNT(*)

### 6.4 Uniqueness: Duplicates and idempotency signals
Panels:
- Duplicate event rate (overall and by endpoint_family)
- Dedupe effectiveness: events_deduped / events_received (from metering pipeline counters)
- Idempotency key coverage: % requests with idempotency_key present
- "Same request_id, multiple billed quantities" detector

Duplicate detection (monitoring-only; not used to bill):
- A record is considered a duplicate candidate if (account_id, request_id, request_end_ts_bucket_5m, meter_name) appears > 1.
- Also track idempotency collisions: (account_id, idempotency_key) appears with different request payload hashes.

### 6.5 Cache-hit attribution integrity
Panels:
- % events with cache_hit populated
- Billed vs raw tokens split over time
- Cache-hit rate by model and endpoint_family

Why it matters:
- Missing cache_hit attribution breaks billing fairness narratives and pricing experiment conclusions.

### 6.6 Dedicated: GPU-seconds sanity
Panels:
- GPU-seconds by pool_id and account_id (daily + hourly)
- Idle vs active split (if available)
- Autoscaling transition counts and correlation with drift

Notes:
- Dedicated is permitted to have higher posting lag; the alert thresholds differ (see Section 7).

## 7. Alerting spec (thresholds, severity, routing)
Alerts are defined in Grafana/alert-rules and routed via PagerDuty + Slack. Severity is determined by expected billing impact and confidence (i.e., avoiding noisy false positives).

### 7.1 Severity levels
- SEV2 (page): likely invoice-impacting within 24 hours or blocks month-end close.
- SEV3 (ticket + Slack): requires investigation within 1 business day.
- INFO (annotation only): tracked but no action unless correlated with drift.

### 7.2 Paging policy (Hosted API)
A) Drift vs ledger (hourly)
- SEV2 if BOTH:
  - ABS(drift_pct) > 0.75% for 2 consecutive hours AND ledger_quantity >= 5,000,000 tokens/hour, OR
  - ABS(event_amount_usd - ledger_amount_usd) > $2,500/hour (shadow-rated)
- SEV3 if:
  - ABS(drift_pct) > 0.30% for 3 consecutive hours AND ledger_quantity >= 1,000,000 tokens/hour

B) Duplicate event rate
- SEV2 if duplicate_rate > 0.20% for 30 minutes AND total_events >= 200,000 in window
- SEV3 if duplicate_rate > 0.05% for 2 hours

C) Null/unknown dimension rate
- SEV2 if any required dimension null_rate > 1.0% for 30 minutes
- SEV3 if any required dimension null_rate > 0.2% for 2 hours

D) Freshness lag
- SEV2 if usage_events_v3 freshness p95 > 180 minutes for 30 minutes
- SEV3 if usage_events_v3 freshness p95 > 60 minutes for 60 minutes

### 7.3 Paging policy (Dedicated)
A) Drift vs ledger (daily primary; hourly secondary)
- SEV2 if daily ABS(drift_pct) > 1.0% AND ABS(drift_amount_usd) > $5,000 for a single account/day
- SEV3 if daily ABS(drift_pct) > 0.5% across all accounts combined

B) Freshness lag
- SEV2 if ledger posting freshness p95 > 12 hours
- SEV3 if ledger posting freshness p95 > 6 hours

### 7.4 Routing
- SEV2 pages: PagerDuty "Platform SRE" with "Telemetry" secondary.
- SEV3: Slack #eng-billing-telemetry + Linear ticket auto-created in "ENG Billing Integrity" project.
- Finance notification for SEV2 drift: Slack #finance-billing (message includes affected accounts and estimated $ impact).

## 8. Drilldown workflow (what to do when an alert fires)
For any SEV2/SEV3 alert, the dashboard must provide a one-click path to:
1) Identify which dimension(s) drove the drift (account/model/region/endpoint)
2) Determine whether the issue is:
   - late arrivals (freshness lag)
   - duplicates (idempotency/dedupe failure)
   - missing dimensions (null-rate)
   - semantic meter change (token counting change, cache attribution change)
3) Identify change correlation:
   - recent deploy SHA(s)
   - backfill/replay jobs
   - experiment flag rollouts

Minimum drilldown links included in panel descriptions:
- "Open in Console: Account detail" (access controlled)
- "Run Snowflake query" link (pre-filled query template)
- "Runbook: Meter drift triage" link (SRE runbook)

## 9. Implementation details
### 9.1 Data computation
- A scheduled job (meter-sanity-check) computes observability.meter_sanity_hourly on a 15-minute cadence for the prior 6 hours, then daily backfill for the prior 7 days.
- All computations must be idempotent and partition-aware.

### 9.2 Query patterns (Snowflake)
- Prefer approximate distinct counts only for exploratory panels; alerting queries must use exact aggregations.
- All alert queries must include volume guardrails to avoid paging on tiny denominators.
- Time windows default to UTC; day-based panels use UTC day boundaries. (Customer exports may use customer-configured TZ; do not compare those to ledger without explicit TZ normalization.)

### 9.3 Access control
Because billing telemetry is customer-sensitive:
- Grafana folder requires "Billing Observability" role.
- Console page requires Finance/RevOps/Eng roles; account drilldowns require explicit approval.
- Query links must reference approved views (no raw request payload access).

## 10. Change management and audit trail
- Any meter semantics change (e.g., token counting, retry attribution, cache-hit billing) must run in shadow-billing compare mode for a minimum of 7 days.
- During shadow mode, dashboards must show "old vs new" meter drift panels and explicitly label which version is being alerted on.
- Alert threshold changes require review from Aisha Bello + Logan Wright; for drift thresholds, also notify Emily Stone.

## 11. Rollout plan
1) Week 1: Ship observability.meter_sanity_hourly + Grafana overview (no paging, SEV3 tickets only).
2) Week 2: Enable SEV2 paging for freshness and null-rate (low false positive risk).
3) Week 3: Enable SEV2 paging for drift vs ledger after threshold tuning (validate against historical incidents).
4) Week 4: Add Dedicated GPU-seconds panels + daily drift alerting.

## 12. Open questions
- Do we page on shadow-rated amount drift or strictly on quantity drift until pricing snapshots are fully consistent across systems?
- Should we maintain separate guardrails for OpenAI-compatible endpoints vs Redwood-native endpoints given known tokenization differences pre-alignment?
- For embeddings batching: do we alert on "inputs" count drift separately from token drift to catch batch-accounting regressions faster?

## 13. Appendix: Panel checklist (must-haves)
Every alert-linked panel MUST:
- State the exact definition (numerator/denominator)
- Include filter dimensions
- Include volume guardrails
- Link to drilldown query + runbook
- Show last deploy/backfill annotation context

source.title: Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) — Control Plane Service Overview
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.content:
## Overview
Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, versioning, and distributing **shadow traffic policies** that mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets.

STPS does **not** run inference and does **not** participate on the user request path directly. It produces a compiled policy artifact that the data plane (Smart Routing / Edge Router) consumes to make mirroring decisions deterministically and safely.

This document covers:
- Service responsibilities and boundaries
- API surface (internal + Console backend)
- Validation rules and guardrails
- Storage model and versioning
- Migrations and rollout considerations
- Integration points (routing, rollouts, telemetry, audit)

---

## Responsibilities
### In scope
- Persist shadow traffic policy configs attached to:
  - **Routes** (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings)
  - **Rollouts / experiments** (canary and A/B objects)
- Provide deterministic **bucketing parameters** used by data plane (sampling %, hashing inputs, salts)
- Validate policies against platform guardrails (sampling limits, timeouts, target constraints, data handling flags)
- Version policies and enable safe rollback (immutability of historical versions)
- Distribute policy updates via the config distribution system
- Emit audit log events for create/update/disable actions

### Explicitly out of scope
- Running shadow requests (handled by routing/data plane)
- Storing prompts/responses or scoring outputs (telemetry + scoring pipeline)
- Customer-defined arbitrary code execution for eval/scoring

---

## Service interfaces
### Upstream callers
- **Console backend (Rollouts service)**: creates/updates policies as part of experiment/rollout configuration.
- **CLI/automation (internal)**: used by SRE and internal dogfood enablement flows.

### Downstream consumers
- **Smart Routing / Edge Router**: reads compiled shadow policy bundles and enforces fail-open mirroring.
- **Telemetry ingestion**: consumes policy IDs/versions for joinability and debugging (no prompt content from STPS).
- **Audit log service**: records configuration changes.

---

## API surface
STPS exposes an internal HTTP API (and gRPC in Private deployments where required) with a stable resource model.

### Resource model
- `shadow_policy`: attaches to either a `route_id` or a `rollout_id` (mutually exclusive).
- Policies are **versioned**. Any update produces a new immutable version.

### Endpoints (internal)
1) Create policy
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies`
- Creates version `1`.

2) Update policy (creates new version)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}/versions`
- Creates version `N+1`.

3) Get policy (latest)
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}`

4) Get specific version
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}/versions/{version}`

5) Resolve effective policy for a route/rollout
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies:resolve?route_id=...` or `...:resolve?rollout_id=...`
- Applies precedence rules (see Integration with Rollouts).

6) Validate only (dry-run)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies:validate`
- Returns validation results and computed derived fields (e.g., normalized weights).

7) Disable policy (soft-disable)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}:disable`
- Produces a new version with `enabled=false`.

### Permissions and RBAC
Authorization is enforced via the shared Control Plane Auth middleware.
- Required scopes:
  - `routes.write` to attach/update route-level policies
  - `rollouts.write` to attach/update rollout-level policies
  - `shadow_traffic.admin` for override operations (e.g., raising sampling cap for internal tenants)
- Policies are tenant-scoped; cross-tenant access is blocked.

### Idempotency
- `POST` endpoints accept `Idempotency-Key`.
- STPS stores request hashes to avoid duplicate versions on retries.

---

## Policy schema (v1)
STPS stores the policy as JSON (schema-validated) and also produces a **compiled bundle** for routing consumption.

### Canonical fields
Required/typical fields (non-exhaustive):
- `enabled`: boolean
- `mode`: `"shadow"` (reserved for future modes)
- `sample_rate`: float in [0, 1]
- `bucketing`:
  - `strategy`: `"consistent_hash"` (v1)
  - `hash_inputs`: ordered list (e.g., `tenant_id`, `route_id`, `user_fingerprint`)
  - `salt_ref`: reference to a tenant-scoped secret salt stored in KMS (Hosted/Dedicated) or local secret store (Private)
- `targets`: list of shadow targets
  - Each target references a *variant* (model version, quant profile, routing policy, prompt template)
  - `weight`: integer, normalized at compile time
  - `timeout_ms`: per-target shadow timeout (must be <= global cap)
- `request_mutations`:
  - `add_headers_allowlist`: list of allowed headers to pass to shadow path
  - `drop_headers`: list of headers to remove
  - `metadata_behavior`: `"copy" | "minimal"` (minimal is default)
- `fail_open`: required true in v1 (shadow path failures must not affect control path)
- `limits`:
  - `max_shadow_qps`: optional clamp
  - `max_shadow_inflight`: optional clamp
- `data_handling`:
  - `redaction_profile`: `"default" | "strict" | "none"` (tenant-dependent; Private may permit different defaults)
  - `retention_class`: `"short" | "extended"` (enforced downstream; stored here for joinability)
- `telemetry`:
  - `dimensions_allowlist_profile`: `"shadow_v1_safe"` (ties to cardinality controls)

### Example payload (illustrative)
```json
{
  "enabled": true,
  "mode": "shadow",
  "sample_rate": 0.02,
  "bucketing": {
    "strategy": "consistent_hash",
    "hash_inputs": ["tenant_id", "route_id", "user_fingerprint"],
    "salt_ref": "kms://shadow-bucketing-salt/tenant/tn_9f2c..."
  },
  "targets": [
    {
      "target_id": "variant:mdl_llama3_70b_q4_kvopt_v2",
      "weight": 100,
      "timeout_ms": 1200
    }
  ],
  "request_mutations": {
    "add_headers_allowlist": ["x-request-id", "x-tenant-tag"],
    "drop_headers": ["authorization"],
    "metadata_behavior": "minimal"
  },
  "fail_open": true,
  "limits": {
    "max_shadow_qps": 50
  },
  "data_handling": {
    "redaction_profile": "default",
    "retention_class": "short"
  },
  "telemetry": {
    "dimensions_allowlist_profile": "shadow_v1_safe"
  }
}
```

---

## Validation and guardrails
STPS performs strict validation at write-time (create/update) and at compile-time (bundle generation). Invalid policies are rejected.

### Validation categories
1) Sampling and cost control
- `sample_rate` must be within `[0.0, 0.10]` by default.
  - Higher caps require `shadow_traffic.admin` and an allowlisted tenant flag.
- If both `sample_rate` and `max_shadow_qps` are set, STPS verifies `max_shadow_qps` is <= tenant cap.

2) Target constraints
- At least 1 target required when `enabled=true`.
- Max targets (v1): 3.
- Target IDs must reference existing variant objects (validated via Rollouts/Variants registry).
- `timeout_ms` per target must be within `[200, 1500]` ms (default caps; may vary by deployment mode).

3) Fail-open enforcement
- `fail_open` must be `true` (v1). Any attempt to set `false` is rejected.
- Circuit breaker behavior is configured in routing; STPS only stores policy intent and caps.

4) Header/metadata safety
- Only allowlisted headers can be forwarded to shadow path.
- Sensitive headers are always dropped: `authorization`, `cookie`, `x-api-key` (non-overridable).
- `metadata_behavior=minimal` is default; `copy` requires an allowlisted tenant flag due to downstream cardinality risk.

5) Data handling flags
- `redaction_profile` must be compatible with tenant classification:
  - Regulated Dedicated/Private tenants may require `strict`.
  - `none` is only available for internal tenants and explicitly approved sandboxes.

6) Telemetry cardinality compliance
- Policy must reference an approved `dimensions_allowlist_profile`.
- Any attempt to add free-form dimensions in the policy is rejected (dimensions are defined centrally in telemetry standards; policy only selects a profile).

### Validation output
The `:validate` endpoint returns:
- `valid: true|false`
- list of errors with machine-readable codes (e.g., `SAMPLE_RATE_TOO_HIGH`, `TARGET_UNKNOWN`, `HEADER_NOT_ALLOWED`)
- derived normalized fields (e.g., weights normalized to sum=1000)

---

## Storage model and versioning
STPS uses Postgres (Hosted/Dedicated control plane) and supports a local Postgres/SQLite option in Private deployments.

### Tables (logical)
1) `shadow_policies`
- `policy_id` (ULID)
- `tenant_id`
- `attachment_type` (`route` or `rollout`)
- `attachment_id` (route_id or rollout_id)
- `created_at`
- `created_by`
- `current_version`

2) `shadow_policy_versions`
- `policy_id`
- `version` (int)
- `state` (`active`, `disabled`)
- `spec_json` (JSONB)
- `compiled_bundle_json` (JSONB)
- `schema_version` (int)
- `created_at`, `created_by`
- `change_note` (string; required for Console updates, optional for automation)

3) `shadow_policy_idempotency`
- `tenant_id`
- `idempotency_key`
- `request_hash`
- `policy_id`, `version`
- TTL-based cleanup

### Version immutability
- Spec and compiled bundle for a version are immutable.
- Rollback is implemented by setting `current_version` pointer (or creating a new version identical to a prior version, depending on the caller’s preference). Console uses “create new version” to preserve auditability.

---

## Compilation and distribution
STPS generates a compiled bundle that the routing layer can consume efficiently.

### What compilation does
- Normalizes and validates weights
- Resolves target references into routing-ready identifiers
- Computes derived caps (e.g., effective timeout, clamped QPS)
- Attaches a `policy_fingerprint` used for cache keys and debugging

### Distribution mechanism
- STPS publishes compiled bundles to the Config Distribution System (CDS):
  - Keyed by `tenant_id` + `route_id` (for route policies) and by `tenant_id` + `rollout_id` (for rollout policies)
  - Routing subscribes and caches bundles in-memory with short TTL

### Consistency model
- Control plane is eventually consistent to data plane.
- Target: 99% of policy updates visible in routing within **60 seconds**.
- During propagation delays, routing continues using the last known good bundle.

---

## Integration points
### Smart Routing / Edge Router
- Routing pulls compiled bundle and enforces:
  - Deterministic bucketing
  - Mirroring timeouts
  - Backpressure/circuit breakers
  - Fail-open semantics
- Routing emits telemetry events that include:
  - `shadow_policy_id`
  - `shadow_policy_version`
  - `policy_fingerprint`

### Rollouts / Experiments
Precedence rules for `:resolve`:
1) If a request is under an active rollout with an attached shadow policy, use rollout policy.
2) Else if the route has an attached shadow policy, use route policy.
3) Else shadowing is disabled.

Notes:
- Rollout-level policies are the primary mechanism for canary comparisons.
- Route-level policies are intended for continuous monitoring (e.g., always shadow 0.5% to a safety-tuned variant).

### Telemetry and scoring
- STPS does not store prompts/responses.
- STPS must stay consistent with telemetry standards by only allowing selection of pre-approved allowlist profiles.

### Audit logs
STPS writes audit events for:
- Create policy
- Update policy (new version)
- Disable policy
- Attach/detach to route/rollout

Audit event fields include tenant, actor, policy id/version, attachment id, and change note.

---

## Migrations and backward compatibility
### Schema versioning
- Policy specs include `schema_version`.
- STPS supports reading older versions and re-compiling to current compiled format where safe.

### Migrating existing route configs
- Initial rollout adds `shadow_policy_id` as an optional reference on route and rollout objects.
- Default behavior for existing routes: no shadow policy attached.

### Safe rollout plan (control plane)
1) Deploy STPS with read-only endpoints and compile pipeline enabled.
2) Add support in Console backend to call `:validate` and display errors.
3) Enable write endpoints behind a feature flag for internal tenants.
4) Gradually allow Dedicated beta tenants.

### Downgrade strategy
- If STPS is rolled back, routing continues operating without shadowing (no effect on control path).
- Existing policies remain in DB; re-enable after forward deploy.

---

## Operational considerations
### SLO targets (control plane)
- API availability (STPS): 99.9% monthly
- P95 latency:
  - `GET` endpoints: < 150 ms
  - `POST` validate/create/update: < 400 ms
- Policy propagation to routing: 99% < 60 seconds

### Metrics
- `stps_requests_total{endpoint,code}`
- `stps_validation_errors_total{code}`
- `stps_policy_publish_latency_seconds`
- `stps_compilation_failures_total`
- `stps_idempotency_replay_total`

### Alerts (high signal)
- Publish failures > 0 for 10m (ticket)
- Compilation failures > 1% for 10m (page when impacting production tenants)
- P95 latency regression > 2x baseline for 15m (ticket)

### Oncall and ownership
- Primary: Eng Platform (Control Plane)
- Escalation: Smart Routing on data plane symptoms; SRE for broad control plane degradation

---

## Security and compliance notes
- Tenant isolation is enforced at the DB and service layer.
- Bucketing salt references must point to approved secret stores (KMS/HSM in Hosted/Dedicated; local secret store in Private).
- Header forwarding is allowlist-based with non-overridable drops for sensitive headers.
- Change notes are required for Console-driven edits to improve auditability.

---

## Known limitations (v1)
- No customer-defined custom dimensions in policy (must use allowlist profiles).
- No time-based scheduling in policy (e.g., “shadow only during business hours”). Use Rollouts scheduling.
- No per-segment sampling in policy (e.g., by geography); segmentation is handled in routing policies/rollout assignment.

---

## Contacts
- Control plane / STPS: Benji Okafor (implementation lead), Ethan Park (engineering lead)
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Smart Routing integration: Ravi Menon, Mina Haddad
- Telemetry constraints: Nadia Rahman
- Security review: Marcus Lin

Required/typical fields (non-exhaustive):
- `enabled`: boolean
- `mode`: `"shadow"` (reserved for future modes)
- `sample_rate`: float in [0, 1]
- `bucketing`:
  - `strategy`: `"consistent_hash"` (v1)
  - `hash_inputs`: ordered list (e.g., `tenant_id`, `route_id`, `user_fingerprint`)
  - `salt_ref`: reference to a tenant-scoped secret salt stored in KMS (Hosted/Dedicated) or local secret store (Private)
- `targets`: list of shadow targets
  - Each target references a *variant* (model version, quant profile, routing policy, prompt template)
  - `weight`: integer, normalized at compile time
  - `timeout_ms`: per-target shadow timeout (must be <= global cap)
- `request_mutations`:
  - `add_headers_allowlist`: list of allowed headers to pass to shadow path
  - `drop_headers`: list of headers to remove
  - `metadata_behavior`: `"copy" | "minimal"` (minimal is default)
- `fail_open`: required true in v1 (shadow path failures must not affect control path)
- `limits`:
  - `max_shadow_qps`: optional clamp
  - `max_shadow_inflight`: optional clamp
- `data_handling`:
  - `redaction_profile`: `"default" | "strict" | "none"` (tenant-dependent; Private may permit different defaults)
  - `retention_class`: `"short" | "extended"` (enforced downstream; stored here for joinability)
- `telemetry`:
  - `dimensions_allowlist_profile`: `"shadow_v1_safe"` (ties to cardinality controls)

### Example payload (illustrative)
```json
{
  "enabled": true,
  "mode": "shadow",
  "sample_rate": 0.02,
  "bucketing": {
    "strategy": "consistent_hash",
    "hash_inputs": ["tenant_id", "route_id", "user_fingerprint"],
    "salt_ref": "kms://shadow-bucketing-salt/tenant/tn_9f2c..."
  },
  "targets": [
    {
      "target_id": "variant:mdl_llama3_70b_q4_kvopt_v2",
      "weight": 100,
      "timeout_ms": 1200
    }
  ],
  "request_mutations": {
    "add_headers_allowlist": ["x-request-id", "x-tenant-tag"],
    "drop_headers": ["authorization"],
    "metadata_behavior": "minimal"
  },
  "fail_open": true,
  "limits": {
    "max_shadow_qps": 50
  },
  "data_handling": {
    "redaction_profile": "default",
    "retention_class": "short"
  },
  "telemetry": {
    "dimensions_allowlist_profile": "shadow_v1_safe"
  }
}
```

---

## Validation and guardrails
STPS performs strict validation at write-time (create/update) and at compile-time (bundle generation). Invalid policies are rejected.

### Validation categories
1) Sampling and cost control
- `sample_rate` must be within `[0.0, 0.10]` by default.
  - Higher caps require `shadow_traffic.admin` and an allowlisted tenant flag.
- If both `sample_rate` and `max_shadow_qps` are set, STPS verifies `max_shadow_qps` is <= tenant cap.

2) Target constraints
- At least 1 target required when `enabled=true`.
- Max targets (v1): 3.
- Target IDs must reference existing variant objects (validated via Rollouts/Variants registry).
- `timeout_ms` per target must be within `[200, 1500]` ms (default caps; may vary by deployment mode).

3) Fail-open enforcement
- `fail_open` must be `true` (v1). Any attempt to set `false` is rejected.
- Circuit breaker behavior is configured in routing; STPS only stores policy intent and caps.

4) Header/metadata safety
- Only allowlisted headers can be forwarded to shadow path.
- Sensitive headers are always dropped: `authorization`, `cookie`, `x-api-key` (non-overridable).
- `metadata_behavior=minimal` is default; `copy` requires an allowlisted tenant flag due to downstream cardinality risk.

5) Data handling flags
- `redaction_profile` must be compatible with tenant classification:
  - Regulated Dedicated/Private tenants may require `strict`.
  - `none` is only available for internal tenants and explicitly approved sandboxes.

6) Telemetry cardinality compliance
- Policy must reference an approved `dimensions_allowlist_profile`.
- Any attempt to add free-form dimensions in the policy is rejected (dimensions are defined centrally in telemetry standards; policy only selects a profile).

### Validation output
The `:validate` endpoint returns:
- `valid: true|false`
- list of errors with machine-readable codes (e.g., `SAMPLE_RATE_TOO_HIGH`, `TARGET_UNKNOWN`, `HEADER_NOT_ALLOWED`)
- derived normalized fields (e.g., weights normalized to sum=1000)

---

## Storage model and versioning
STPS uses Postgres (Hosted/Dedicated control plane) and supports a local Postgres/SQLite option in Private deployments.

### Tables (logical)
1) `shadow_policies`
- `policy_id` (ULID)
- `tenant_id`
- `attachment_type` (`route` or `rollout`)
- `attachment_id` (route_id or rollout_id)
- `created_at`
- `created_by`
- `current_version`

2) `shadow_policy_versions`
- `policy_id`
- `version` (int)
- `state` (`active`, `disabled`)
- `spec_json` (JSONB)
- `compiled_bundle_json` (JSONB)
- `schema_version` (int)
- `created_at`, `created_by`
- `change_note` (string; required for Console updates, optional for automation)

3) `shadow_policy_idempotency`
- `tenant_id`
- `idempotency_key`
- `request_hash`
- `policy_id`, `version`
- TTL-based cleanup

### Version immutability
- Spec and compiled bundle for a version are immutable.
- Rollback is implemented by setting `current_version` pointer (or creating a new version identical to a prior version, depending on the caller’s preference). Console uses “create new version” to preserve auditability.

---

## Compilation and distribution
STPS generates a compiled bundle that the routing layer can consume efficiently.

### What compilation does
- Normalizes and validates weights
- Resolves target references into routing-ready identifiers
- Computes derived caps (e.g., effective timeout, clamped QPS)
- Attaches a `policy_fingerprint` used for cache keys and debugging

### Distribution mechanism
- STPS publishes compiled bundles to the Config Distribution System (CDS):
  - Keyed by `tenant_id` + `route_id` (for route policies) and by `tenant_id` + `rollout_id` (for rollout policies)
  - Routing subscribes and caches bundles in-memory with short TTL

### Consistency model
- Control plane is eventually consistent to data plane.
- Target: 99% of policy updates visible in routing within **60 seconds**.
- During propagation delays, routing continues using the last known good bundle.

---

## Integration points
### Smart Routing / Edge Router
- Routing pulls compiled bundle and enforces:
  - Deterministic bucketing
  - Mirroring timeouts
  - Backpressure/circuit breakers
  - Fail-open semantics
- Routing emits telemetry events that include:
  - `shadow_policy_id`
  - `shadow_policy_version`
  - `policy_fingerprint`

### Rollouts / Experiments
Precedence rules for `:resolve`:
1) If a request is under an active rollout with an attached shadow policy, use rollout policy.
2) Else if the route has an attached shadow policy, use route policy.
3) Else shadowing is disabled.

Notes:
- Rollout-level policies are the primary mechanism for canary comparisons.
- Route-level policies are intended for continuous monitoring (e.g., always shadow 0.5% to a safety-tuned variant).

### Telemetry and scoring
- STPS does not store prompts/responses.
- STPS must stay consistent with telemetry standards by only allowing selection of pre-approved allowlist profiles.

### Audit logs
STPS writes audit events for:
- Create policy
- Update policy (new version)
- Disable policy
- Attach/detach to route/rollout

Audit event fields include tenant, actor, policy id/version, attachment id, and change note.

---

## Migrations and backward compatibility
### Schema versioning
- Policy specs include `schema_version`.
- STPS supports reading older versions and re-compiling to current compiled format where safe.

### Migrating existing route configs
- Initial rollout adds `shadow_policy_id` as an optional reference on route and rollout objects.
- Default behavior for existing routes: no shadow policy attached.

### Safe rollout plan (control plane)
1) Deploy STPS with read-only endpoints and compile pipeline enabled.
2) Add support in Console backend to call `:validate` and display errors.
3) Enable write endpoints behind a feature flag for internal tenants.
4) Gradually allow Dedicated beta tenants.

### Downgrade strategy
- If STPS is rolled back, routing continues operating without shadowing (no effect on control path).
- Existing policies remain in DB; re-enable after forward deploy.

---

## Operational considerations
### SLO targets (control plane)
- API availability (STPS): 99.9% monthly
- P95 latency:
  - `GET` endpoints: < 150 ms
  - `POST` validate/create/update: < 400 ms
- Policy propagation to routing: 99% < 60 seconds

### Metrics
- `stps_requests_total{endpoint,code}`
- `stps_validation_errors_total{code}`
- `stps_policy_publish_latency_seconds`
- `stps_compilation_failures_total`
- `stps_idempotency_replay_total`

### Alerts (high signal)
- Publish failures > 0 for 10m (ticket)
- Compilation failures > 1% for 10m (page when impacting production tenants)
- P95 latency regression > 2x baseline for 15m (ticket)

### Oncall and ownership
- Primary: Eng Platform (Control Plane)
- Escalation: Smart Routing on data plane symptoms; SRE for broad control plane degradation

---

## Security and compliance notes
- Tenant isolation is enforced at the DB and service layer.
- Bucketing salt references must point to approved secret stores (KMS/HSM in Hosted/Dedicated; local secret store in Private).
- Header forwarding is allowlist-based with non-overridable drops for sensitive headers.
- Change notes are required for Console-driven edits to improve auditability.

---

## Known limitations (v1)
- No customer-defined custom dimensions in policy (must use allowlist profiles).
- No time-based scheduling in policy (e.g., “shadow only during business hours”). Use Rollouts scheduling.
- No per-segment sampling in policy (e.g., by geography); segmentation is handled in routing policies/rollout assignment.

---

## Contacts
- Control plane / STPS: Benji Okafor (implementation lead), Ethan Park (engineering lead)
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Smart Routing integration: Ravi Menon, Mina Haddad
- Telemetry constraints: Nadia Rahman
- Security review: Marcus Lin
text:
## Overview
Shadow Traffic Policy Service (STPS) is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, versioning, and distributing **shadow traffic policies** that mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets.

STPS does **not** run inference and does **not** participate on the user request path directly. It produces a compiled policy artifact that the data plane (Smart Routing / Edge Router) consumes to make mirroring decisions deterministically and safely.

This document covers:
- Service responsibilities and boundaries
- API surface (internal + Console backend)
- Validation rules and guardrails
- Storage model and versioning
- Migrations and rollout considerations
- Integration points (routing, rollouts, telemetry, audit)

---

## Responsibilities
### In scope
- Persist shadow traffic policy configs attached to:
  - **Routes** (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings)
  - **Rollouts / experiments** (canary and A/B objects)
- Provide deterministic **bucketing parameters** used by data plane (sampling %, hashing inputs, salts)
- Validate policies against platform guardrails (sampling limits, timeouts, target constraints, data handling flags)
- Version policies and enable safe rollback (immutability of historical versions)
- Distribute policy updates via the config distribution system
- Emit audit log events for create/update/disable actions

### Explicitly out of scope
- Running shadow requests (handled by routing/data plane)
- Storing prompts/responses or scoring outputs (telemetry + scoring pipeline)
- Customer-defined arbitrary code execution for eval/scoring

---

## Service interfaces
### Upstream callers
- **Console backend (Rollouts service)**: creates/updates policies as part of experiment/rollout configuration.
- **CLI/automation (internal)**: used by SRE and internal dogfood enablement flows.

### Downstream consumers
- **Smart Routing / Edge Router**: reads compiled shadow policy bundles and enforces fail-open mirroring.
- **Telemetry ingestion**: consumes policy IDs/versions for joinability and debugging (no prompt content from STPS).
- **Audit log service**: records configuration changes.

---

## API surface
STPS exposes an internal HTTP API (and gRPC in Private deployments where required) with a stable resource model.

### Resource model
- `shadow_policy`: attaches to either a `route_id` or a `rollout_id` (mutually exclusive).
- Policies are **versioned**. Any update produces a new immutable version.

### Endpoints (internal)
1) Create policy
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies`
- Creates version `1`.

2) Update policy (creates new version)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}/versions`
- Creates version `N+1`.

3) Get policy (latest)
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}`

4) Get specific version
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}/versions/{version}`

5) Resolve effective policy for a route/rollout
- `GET /v1/shadow-policies:resolve?route_id=...` or `...:resolve?rollout_id=...`
- Applies precedence rules (see Integration with Rollouts).

6) Validate only (dry-run)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies:validate`
- Returns validation results and computed derived fields (e.g., normalized weights).

7) Disable policy (soft-disable)
- `POST /v1/shadow-policies/{policy_id}:disable`
- Produces a new version with `enabled=false`.

### Permissions and RBAC
Authorization is enforced via the shared Control Plane Auth middleware.
- Required scopes:
  - `routes.write` to attach/update route-level policies
  - `rollouts.write` to attach/update rollout-level policies
  - `shadow_traffic.admin` for override operations (e.g., raising sampling cap for internal tenants)
- Policies are tenant-scoped; cross-tenant access is blocked.

### Idempotency
- `POST` endpoints accept `Idempotency-Key`.
- STPS stores request hashes to avoid duplicate versions on retries.

---

## Policy schema (v1)
STPS stores the policy as JSON (schema-validated) and also produces a **compiled bundle** for routing consumption.

### Canonical fields
Required/typical fields (non-exhaustive):
- `enabled`: boolean
- `mode`: `"shadow"` (reserved for future modes)
- `sample_rate`: float in [0, 1]
- `bucketing`:
  - `strategy`: `"consistent_hash"` (v1)
  - `hash_inputs`: ordered list (e.g., `tenant_id`, `route_id`, `user_fingerprint`)
  - `salt_ref`: reference to a tenant-scoped secret salt stored in KMS (Hosted/Dedicated) or local secret store (Private)
- `targets`: list of shadow targets
  - Each target references a *variant* (model version, quant profile, routing policy, prompt template)
  - `weight`: integer, normalized at compile time
  - `timeout_ms`: per-target shadow timeout (must be <= global cap)
- `request_mutations`:
  - `add_headers_allowlist`: list of allowed headers to pass to shadow path
  - `drop_headers`: list of headers to remove
  - `metadata_behavior`: `"copy" | "minimal"` (minimal is default)
- `fail_open`: required true in v1 (shadow path failures must not affect control path)
- `limits`:
  - `max_shadow_qps`: optional clamp
  - `max_shadow_inflight`: optional clamp
- `data_handling`:
  - `redaction_profile`: `"default" | "strict" | "none"` (tenant-dependent; Private may permit different defaults)
  - `retention_class`: `"short" | "extended"` (enforced downstream; stored here for joinability)
- `telemetry`:
  - `dimensions_allowlist_profile`: `"shadow_v1_safe"` (ties to cardinality controls)

```json

  "mode": "shadow",
  "sample_rate": 0.02,

    "strategy": "consistent_hash",
    "hash_inputs": ["tenant_id", "route_id", "user_fingerprint"],
    "salt_ref": "kms://shadow-bucketing-salt/tenant/tn_9f2c..."
  },
  "targets": [
    {
      "target_id": "variant:mdl_llama3_70b_q4_kvopt_v2",
      "weight": 100,
      "timeout_ms": 1200
    }
  ],
  "request_mutations": {
    "add_headers_allowlist": ["x-request-id", "x-tenant-tag"],
    "drop_headers": ["authorization"],
    "metadata_behavior": "minimal"
  },
  "fail_open": true,
  "limits": {
    "max_shadow_qps": 50
  },
  "data_handling": {
    "redaction_profile": "default",
    "retention_class": "short"
```

- Circuit breaker behavior is configured in routing; STPS only stores policy intent and caps.

4) Header/metadata safety
- Only allowlisted headers can be forwarded to shadow path.
- Sensitive headers are always dropped: `authorization`, `cookie`, `x-api-key` (non-overridable).
- `metadata_behavior=minimal` is default; `copy` requires an allowlisted tenant flag due to downstream cardinality risk.

5) Data handling flags

  - Regulated Dedicated/Private tenants may require `strict`.
  - `none` is only available for internal tenants and explicitly approved sandboxes.

6) Telemetry cardinality compliance
- Policy must reference an approved `dimensions_allowlist_profile`.
- Any attempt to add free-form dimensions in the policy is rejected (dimensions are defined centrally in telemetry standards; policy only selects a profile).

### Validation output
The `:validate` endpoint returns:

- list of errors with machine-readable codes (e.g., `SAMPLE_RATE_TOO_HIGH`, `TARGET_UNKNOWN`, `HEADER_NOT_ALLOWED`)
- derived normalized fields (e.g., weights normalized to sum=1000)

---

## Storage model and versioning

1) `shadow_policies`

- `attachment_id` (route_id or rollout_id)
- `created_at`
- `created_by`
- `current_version`

2) `shadow_policy_versions`

- `version` (int)

- `spec_json` (JSONB)

- `schema_version` (int)
- `created_at`, `created_by`
- `change_note` (string; required for Console updates, optional for automation)

- Rollback is implemented by setting `current_version` pointer (or creating a new version identical to a prior version, depending on the caller’s preference). Console uses “create new version” to preserve auditability.

---

STPS generates a compiled bundle that the routing layer can consume efficiently.

  - Keyed by `tenant_id` + `route_id` (for route policies) and by `tenant_id` + `rollout_id` (for rollout policies)

source.title: Cost Model Service (cost-model-api) — Service Overview
source.author: Ethan Park
source.body:
## Summary
Cost Model Service (service name: **cost-model-api**) provides a single, versioned implementation for inference cost estimation and cost breakdowns across Redwood products.

It is the canonical source for:
- **Per-request cost estimates** and standardized breakdown taxonomy (prefill/decode, cache, batch efficiency, overhead allocation, quantization deltas)
- **Aggregated cost breakdowns** for Console dashboards (bounded-cardinality responses)
- **What-if projections** used by Optimize to quantify ROI from configuration changes (batching/caching/quantization)

This document describes architecture, dependencies, SLOs, ownership/on-call, and rollout strategy.

---

## Service owners
**Primary owner team:** Platform Control Plane
- Service/API lead: Ethan Park
- Control plane integration: Benji Okafor, Cole Summers
- Auth/tenancy: Kaitlyn Nguyen

**Reliability partner:** SRE
- Reliability lead: Rafael Mendes

**Key dependencies owners:**
- Telemetry & Analytics: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman
- Runtime metrics/instrumentation: Sofia Ivanova, Omar El-Sayed, Grace Kim
- Finance alignment: Laura Bennett, Rishi Malhotra

---

## Where this service sits
The Cost Model Service is an internal service + shared library that standardizes cost calculations. It is intentionally *not* the billing system of record.

### Primary callers
- **Redwood Console backend** (hot path for cost breakdown pages; calls are cacheable)
- **Redwood Optimize backend** (what-if projections; heavier compute but guarded and bounded)
- **Finance/analytics pipelines** (offline reconciliations and reporting windows; typically via batch exports or aggregated endpoints)

---

## High-level architecture (text diagram)

```
            +-----------------------+
            |  Redwood Console BE   |
            |  (dashboards, APIs)   |
            +-----------+-----------+
                        |
                        | (internal, mTLS)
                        v
+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|                cost-model-api                 |
|----------------------------------------------|
|  1) Request validation + tenancy/authz        |
|  2) Version negotiation (schema + semantics)  |
|  3) Cost computation engine                   |
|     - cost breakdown taxonomy                 |
|     - overhead allocation policy              |
|     - quantization profile deltas             |
|  4) Response bounding + aggregation guards    |
|  5) Cache (read-through) for hot keys         |
+-----------+-------------------+---------------+
            |                   |
            |                   |
            v                   v
  +------------------+   +---------------------+
  | Telemetry Store  |   | Model Catalog       |
  | (token + runtime |   | (model + quant      |
  |  metrics, lag)   |   |  profile metadata)  |
  +------------------+   +---------------------+
            |
            v
  +------------------+
  | Drift Monitor Job|
  | (scheduled; SLI) |
  +------------------+
            |
            v
  +------------------+
  | Observed Spend / |
  | Billing Primitives|
  +------------------+

            +-----------------------+
            |   Redwood Optimize    |
            | (recommendations/ROI) |
            +-----------+-----------+
                        |
                        v
                 (what-if endpoints)
```

Notes:
- **Console** calls are latency-sensitive; the service is designed to be cache-friendly and returns bounded responses.
- **Optimize** calls can be more compute-heavy, but are constrained by request guardrails (limits on parameter ranges, cardinality, and time horizon).
- A **drift monitoring job** compares predicted vs observed spend to produce a correctness/drift SLI.

---

## Dependencies

### Required upstream data
1) **Telemetry Store** (authoritative inputs for per-request and aggregated cost)
- Token accounting: prompt/prefill tokens, decode tokens, output tokens
- Cache metrics: KV cache hit/miss rates and bytes (when available), cache eligibility flags
- Scheduling metrics: effective batch size, queue time, batching window outcomes
- Runtime stage breakdown: prefill vs decode wall time and/or token accounting split

Owners: Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman)

2) **Model Catalog**
- Model identifiers, versions, architecture class
- Hardware compatibility metadata
- **Quantization profile IDs** and relevant metadata used by the cost model

Owner: Platform / Model Catalog maintainers (in coordination with Applied ML)

3) **Overhead allocation policy**
- Finance-approved policy to allocate shared costs to request-attributable vs overhead buckets
- Reporting windows and reconciliation rules

Owners: Finance/FP&A (Laura Bennett, Rishi Malhotra) + Platform

### Downstream integrations
- Console cost breakdown views (feature flag gated)
- Optimize ROI estimates and recommendation guardrails
- Billing hooks alignment (shared primitives; may intentionally differ for invoicing semantics; see ADRs)

---

## Data flow & request types

### Request types supported (v1)
- **Estimate**: Per-request estimated cost + breakdown (for request detail / tracing contexts)
- **Breakdown**: Aggregated cost breakdown over a bounded query window (for dashboards)
- **What-if**: Projected deltas based on configuration changes (for Optimize)

### Query bounding (hard requirements)
To prevent cardinality and latency blowups, the service enforces:
- Maximum time window per request (e.g., dashboard query windows must be bounded; long-range queries require pre-aggregation)
- Maximum number of group-by dimensions
- Maximum response size (rows and bytes)
- Allowed parameter ranges for what-if (e.g., batch size multipliers, cache warm-rate assumptions, quantization profile sets)

The exact limits are maintained in the API standards addendum and service config.

---

## Cost computation model (implementation overview)
The cost-model-api combines three layers:

1) **Standard Cost Spec (schema + semantics)**
- Versioned schemas for inputs/outputs (e.g., cost_model_spec v1)
- Explicit semantics for:
  - Prefill vs decode attribution
  - Cache hits vs misses vs ineligible requests
  - Batch efficiency and queue-time effects
  - Quantization profile deltas (throughput/memory impacts; quality guardrails)
  - Overhead allocation buckets

2) **Deterministic computation engine**
- Pure functions over canonical inputs where possible
- Stable rounding rules and currency/unit conventions
- Explicit “unknown/unavailable” handling for missing telemetry fields (no silent backfill that changes semantics)

3) **Confidence/uncertainty for projections (What-if only)**
- What-if returns a point estimate plus a confidence indicator when driven by empirically learned parameters
- Guardrails prevent projections when input uncertainty is too high or when inputs are out of policy bounds

---

## SLAs / SLOs (service-level)
Detailed SLO definitions live in the SLO page (see related pages). This section summarizes the operational targets used by Platform + SRE.

### Availability SLO
- **99.9%** monthly availability for core endpoints used by Console
- Degraded-mode behavior is allowed (see “Fallbacks”)

### Latency SLO (p95)
- Estimate/Breakdown endpoints (Console path): **p95 < 250ms** for cache-hit and **p95 < 600ms** for cache-miss under typical dashboard query bounds
- What-if endpoint (Optimize path): **p95 < 2.0s** for bounded scenarios

### Correctness / Drift SLI
- A scheduled drift job compares predicted costs (from cost-model primitives) to observed spend attribution over an agreed reporting window.
- Alerting is based on drift thresholds that account for telemetry lag and overhead allocation windows.

### Error budget policy
- If latency or errors threaten Console UX, the Console integration must automatically fall back to the legacy breakdown path (flag-controlled), and SRE may request a rollout pause.

---

## Reliability: fallbacks and failure modes

### Client fallbacks (required)
- Console must implement:
  - Timeouts (short)
  - Retries (bounded)
  - Fallback behavior (legacy cost breakdown implementation) behind a feature flag

- Optimize must implement:
  - Timeouts (longer)
  - No unbounded retries
  - Safe default behavior (suppress recommendation / show “insufficient confidence”) when the what-if endpoint is unavailable

### Common failure modes
- **Telemetry lag / partial ingestion** → drift alerts, missing fields, inconsistent breakdowns
- **Schema mismatch / version negotiation errors** → 4xx from service; clients should downgrade gracefully
- **Cardinality explosion** from overly broad group-bys → request rejected with explicit error code
- **Cache stampede** on common dashboard keys → mitigated via request coalescing + short TTL caching

---

## Deployment & environments
- Environments: dev, staging, prod
- Deployed as a standard internal control-plane service with:
  - mTLS service-to-service auth
  - Tenant-aware authorization checks
  - Per-route rate limits (stricter for what-if)

Config highlights:
- Cache TTLs are endpoint-specific
- Feature flags control:
  - Console adoption percentage
  - Optimize what-if enablement
  - Strictness of schema enforcement (e.g., rejecting vs warning on unknown fields)

---

## On-call & escalation

### On-call rotation
- **Primary:** Platform Control Plane on-call (cost-model-api)
- **Secondary:** SRE on-call (platform services)

### Escalation
- Telemetry ingestion incidents: page Telemetry & Analytics on-call
- Runtime instrumentation regressions: page Runtime Scheduling/Kernels on-call
- Finance reconciliation concerns (non-urgent): route to FP&A partner (Rishi Malhotra) via ticket

### Operational readiness requirements
Before enabling Console in production beyond initial canary:
- Dashboards exist for latency, error rates, request volume, version distribution, and drift
- Runbook is current and linked in service alerts
- Client fallback is verified in staging with forced fault injection

---

## Observability
Metrics emitted by cost-model-api:
- Request volume by endpoint + tenant tier (bounded cardinality)
- Latency (p50/p95/p99) by endpoint
- Error rates by error class (validation, auth, upstream dependency, compute timeout)
- Cache hit ratio (endpoint-level)
- Schema version distribution (requested vs served)
- Drift metrics (from scheduled job) and telemetry lag indicators

Logging/tracing:
- Trace IDs propagated from Console/Optimize
- Logs must not include raw prompts or customer content; only derived counts and stable identifiers

---

## Rollout strategy

### Phase 0 — Service bootstrap (completed)
- Ship service skeleton, auth/tenancy, and schema package
- Implement bounded endpoints with deterministic behavior

### Phase 1 — Staging integrations
- Console integration behind flag with fallback enabled
- Optimize integration behind flag for internal tenants only
- Validate schema version negotiation behavior

### Phase 2 — Production canary
- Enable for a small set of internal tenants and low-risk customers
- Monitor: latency, errors, schema version distribution, and drift SLI
- Compare Console breakdowns vs legacy for a sample of dashboard queries

### Phase 3 — Expand adoption
- Increase Console coverage gradually (e.g., 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
- Expand Optimize what-if access with strict guardrails
- Align billing hooks to consume the same primitives (document intentional differences)

### Rollback plan
- Immediate rollback via feature flag to legacy breakdowns (Console)
- Disable what-if endpoint exposure (Optimize) and freeze new recommendation rollouts
- If drift alerts are driven by telemetry lag: widen lag tolerance window temporarily and open follow-up work with Telemetry

---

## Open items / follow-ups
- Finalize drift thresholds by deployment mode (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
- Reduce dependency on high-cardinality telemetry dimensions for dashboard queries (prefer pre-aggregations)
- Document a clear customer-support explanation template for “Console vs invoice differences” rooted in the standard taxonomy and reporting windows

---

## Appendix: operational checklist (quick)
- [ ] Pager routes to Platform Control Plane on-call
- [ ] Dashboards present in Grafana (latency/errors/version/drift)
- [ ] Alerts link to runbook
- [ ] Client timeouts + fallback verified
- [ ] Request bounds tested (reject oversized queries)
- [ ] Schema version negotiation tested across Console/Optimize client library versions

- Optimize integration behind flag for internal tenants only
- Validate schema version negotiation behavior

### Phase 2 — Production canary
- Enable for a small set of internal tenants and low-risk customers
- Monitor: latency, errors, schema version distribution, and drift SLI
- Compare Console breakdowns vs legacy for a sample of dashboard queries

### Phase 3 — Expand adoption
- Increase Console coverage gradually (e.g., 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
- Expand Optimize what-if access with strict guardrails
- Align billing hooks to consume the same primitives (document intentional differences)

### Rollback plan
- Immediate rollback via feature flag to legacy breakdowns (Console)
- Disable what-if endpoint exposure (Optimize) and freeze new recommendation rollouts
- If drift alerts are driven by telemetry lag: widen lag tolerance window temporarily and open follow-up work with Telemetry

---

## Open items / follow-ups
- Finalize drift thresholds by deployment mode (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
- Reduce dependency on high-cardinality telemetry dimensions for dashboard queries (prefer pre-aggregations)
- Document a clear customer-support explanation template for “Console vs invoice differences” rooted in the standard taxonomy and reporting windows

---

## Appendix: operational checklist (quick)
- [ ] Pager routes to Platform Control Plane on-call
- [ ] Dashboards present in Grafana (latency/errors/version/drift)
- [ ] Alerts link to runbook
- [ ] Client timeouts + fallback verified
- [ ] Request bounds tested (reject oversized queries)
- [ ] Schema version negotiation tested across Console/Optimize client library versions

source.title: How-to: Integrate Redwood Console with the Cost Model API (v1)
source.author: Chloe Martin
source.content:
## Purpose
This page documents the recommended integration pattern for **Redwood Console** backend services calling the **Cost Model API (v1)**.

Console uses the Cost Model API to power:
- Cost breakdown tables (prefill vs decode, cache hit attribution, overhead)
- Time-series dashboards (bounded, aggregated breakdowns)
- Drill-down views for a request or small cohort

The intent is that Console uses a **single canonical source** of cost semantics and breakdown taxonomy, rather than duplicating logic.

## Audience
- Console backend engineers and dashboard engineers
- On-call engineers debugging cost discrepancies in Console

## Quick start (TL;DR)
1) Use the shared client library (preferred) with **schema version negotiation enabled**.
2) Use **bounded queries** (time windows, group-by dimensions, and result limits).
3) Add **multi-layer caching** in Console for hot dashboard paths (60s default TTL).
4) When the Cost Model API is degraded, **fall back** to existing Console aggregates (feature-flagged) and surface a UI banner.
5) Validate changes against **staging fixtures** and golden datasets before rollout.

---

## Prerequisites
### Access + service identity
- Console services must authenticate to Cost Model API using standard service-to-service auth (mTLS / workload identity as configured by Platform).
- Ensure the calling service has the IAM permission:
  - `costmodel.estimates.read` (for request-level estimate/breakdown)
  - `costmodel.aggregates.read` (for time-window aggregates)

If you get `403` errors in staging, file an access request with Platform Control Plane (tag Kaitlyn Nguyen).

### Required context
All calls must include tenant context. In Console, this should come from the authenticated user/session and be forwarded to the backend service.

Console must supply:
- `org_id` (required)
- `project_id` (required)
- `environment` (required; `prod|staging|dev`)

Do not infer tenant context from URL parameters without auth validation.

---

## Preferred integration approach
### Use the shared client library
Console should not hand-roll HTTP calls except in emergency debugging tools.

Supported clients:
- **Go** (Console aggregator services): `go.redwood.ai/costmodel/client`
- **TypeScript/Node** (if applicable): `@redwood-inference/cost-model-client`

Client features you should rely on:
- Timeouts + retries (idempotent requests only)
- Version negotiation (`schema_version` pinning + fallback)
- Standard error mapping (4xx vs 5xx vs policy guardrails)
- Request bounding helpers (max ranges, max group-by)

---

## Endpoints Console typically uses
Console primarily uses aggregated breakdown endpoints for dashboards, plus request-level breakdown for drill-down.

### 1) Aggregate breakdown (dashboards)
Use this for time-series charts and tables.

Conceptual operation: “Give me cost breakdown for org/project over a bounded time window, optionally grouped by a small set of dimensions.”

Common groupings:
- `model_id`
- `route`
- `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `region`

Hard rules for Console:
- Prefer **one aggregate request** per widget rather than N small requests.
- Keep `group_by` small (0–2 dimensions). More than that will amplify cardinality.

### 2) Request breakdown (drill-down)
Use this for request detail pages (or small cohorts).

Hard rules for Console:
- Batch request IDs (if the API supports it) rather than calling per-request in a loop.
- Avoid powering infinite-scroll UIs by repeatedly calling request-level endpoints. Prefer aggregate endpoints and sampled drill-down.

---

## Example usage (Go)
The exact package names may change; prefer whatever is vendored in the monorepo under `internal/clients/costmodel`.

```go
import (
  "context"
  "time"

  costmodel "go.redwood.ai/costmodel/client"
)

func FetchRouteCostBreakdown(ctx context.Context, orgID, projectID string, start, end time.Time) (*costmodel.AggregateBreakdownResponse, error) {
  c := costmodel.NewClient(costmodel.Config{
    BaseURL:               "https://cost-model-api.service.consul",
    DefaultSchemaVersion:  "cost-model/v1",
    Timeout:              750 * time.Millisecond,
    Retries:              2,
  })

  req := &costmodel.AggregateBreakdownRequest{
    OrgID:        orgID,
    ProjectID:    projectID,
    Environment:  "prod",
    TimeStart:    start,
    TimeEnd:      end,
    Granularity:  "5m", // dashboards: 1m/5m/1h only
    GroupBy:      []string{"route"},
    Metrics:      []string{"total_cost_usd", "prefill_cost_usd", "decode_cost_usd", "overhead_cost_usd"},
    MaxGroups:    200,  // guardrail: bound response size
    MaxSeries:    1000, // guardrail for time-series points
  }

  // Client negotiates a compatible schema version if server rejects the pinned version.
  resp, err := c.AggregateBreakdown(ctx, req)
  if err != nil {
    return nil, err
  }
  return resp, nil
}
```

Notes:
- Console should treat the Cost Model API as an internal dependency with strict timeouts. Do not allow slow cost computation to cascade into overall page latency.
- Prefer `Granularity=5m` for most dashboard widgets; use `1m` only for short windows.

---

## Caching guidance (Console)
Cost Model API is designed to be fast, but dashboard workloads are spiky and repetitive. Console should cache aggressively.

### What to cache
Cache the **entire Cost Model API response** for a given query.

Cache key should include (at minimum):
- `org_id`, `project_id`, `environment`
- endpoint name + normalized query params
- `schema_version` actually used (after negotiation)
- time range + granularity
- `group_by` and `filters`

### Recommended TTLs
- Dashboard aggregate breakdown: **60 seconds** default TTL
- High-traffic landing pages: **30 seconds** if freshness concerns
- Drill-down by request_id: **5 minutes** (request costs don’t change unless backfilled; see caveat below)

### Cache layers
- L1: in-process LRU (fast path; helps thundering herd within a single pod)
- L2: shared Redis cache for Console backend (recommended for dashboards)

### Backfill / late telemetry caveat
Some cost inputs can be corrected by backfills (e.g., telemetry lag, late-arriving KV cache stats). For Console UI correctness:
- Prefer a small “data freshness” indicator on cost widgets.
- Do not cache aggregate responses longer than 5 minutes.
- If a user requests “Last 24h”, compute from **rolling cached chunks** (e.g., 5m buckets), not a single massive response.

### Thundering herd prevention
Use singleflight / request coalescing keyed by the cache key to ensure only one in-flight request per unique query.

---

## Bounds, pagination, and cardinality rules
Cost queries are naturally high-cardinality. The API enforces policy guardrails; Console should stay well within them.

### Time windows
Guidelines for dashboards:
- Default: max window **24 hours** per widget
- Deep history: use pre-aggregated pipelines (Analytics) or request coarser granularity (1h)

If you need >24h at 5m granularity, discuss with Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright / Mei Lin) because this is usually a product/design smell.

### Group-by limits
- Recommended: 0–2 group-by dimensions
- Avoid: `group_by=[route, model_id, region]` unless strictly bounded with strong filters

### Result limits
Set explicit bounds on response sizes:
- `max_groups` (e.g., 200)
- `max_series` (e.g., 1000)
- Prefer “Top N by total_cost_usd” semantics when available

### Pagination
If an endpoint returns `next_page_token`:
- Always implement pagination loops with a hard cap (e.g., max 5 pages)
- Surface partial results and a warning rather than looping unbounded

Pseudo-pattern:
1) Request page 1
2) While token != empty AND pages < maxPages: request next page
3) If maxPages hit: stop and log `costmodel.pagination_truncated=1`

---

## Error handling + fallback behavior
Console must not become unavailable if Cost Model API is degraded.

### Recommended timeout/retry policy
- Timeout: 500–750ms for dashboard paths; 1–2s for drill-down
- Retries: at most 1–2 retries for safe/idempotent reads
- Use jittered exponential backoff; do not retry `4xx`

### Common error classes
- `400/422` Invalid request bounds or unsupported fields: fix Console query construction; do not retry.
- `429` Rate limited: rely on caching; reduce widget fanout; consider adding server-side batching.
- `503/504` Temporary outage/overload: fail fast and use fallback.

### Fallback behavior (required)
All Console surfaces using cost-model must support:
- Feature flag: `console.cost_model_api.enabled` (per environment)
- Feature flag: `console.cost_model_api.fallback_to_legacy` (default ON during rollout)

When fallback triggers:
- Use legacy Console aggregation logic (existing pipeline) for that widget/page
- Emit metrics:
  - `console.costmodel.fallback.count`
  - `console.costmodel.fallback.reason` (timeout|5xx|rate_limit|schema)
- UI: show a non-blocking banner “Cost breakdown temporarily using legacy estimator; values may differ from invoice.”

---

## Testing against staging fixtures
Console changes must be validated against known-good datasets before production rollout.

### Recommended test types
1) **Contract tests (schema + semantics)**
- Ensure Console can parse responses for `cost-model/v1`
- Ensure unknown fields are ignored safely

2) **Golden fixtures (deterministic outputs)**
- Use fixture datasets produced by the validation harness
- Snapshot a small set of queries that match Console widgets

3) **Backtesting (drift checks)**
- Compare aggregate outputs vs observed spend attribution (within defined thresholds)

### How to run locally (typical)
Set env vars (names may vary; follow monorepo conventions):
- `COST_MODEL_BASE_URL=https://cost-model-api.staging.redwood.internal`
- `COST_MODEL_SCHEMA_VERSION=cost-model/v1`
- `COST_MODEL_TIMEOUT_MS=750`

Run Console backend tests with recorded fixtures:
```bash
# Pull latest staging fixtures used by validation harness
rw costmodel fixtures pull --dataset console_breakdown_v1 --out ./testdata/costmodel

# Run Console contract + golden tests
go test ./... -run TestCostModel -update-golden=false
```

### Staging sanity checklist (before enabling the flag)
For each of the main Console cost views:
- Load time window: Last 1h, 24h
- Group-by: route, model_id
- Validate that:
  - totals are non-negative
  - prefill + decode + overhead == total (within rounding tolerance)
  - cache attribution fields are present when cache is enabled
  - p95 API latency stays within budget during normal load

---

## Observability (what Console should emit)
When calling Cost Model API, emit client-side metrics to correlate widget regressions with API behavior.

Required metrics/tags:
- `console.costmodel.client.latency_ms` (p50/p95)
- `console.costmodel.client.status_code`
- `console.costmodel.client.endpoint` (aggregate|request)
- `console.costmodel.client.schema_version_used`
- `console.costmodel.client.cache_hit` (l1|l2|miss)
- `console.costmodel.client.query_cardinality_estimate` (best-effort)

Logging:
- Log full request bounds (time window, group_by, limits), but **do not** log customer prompts or raw request payloads that may contain sensitive text.

---

## Troubleshooting
### “Console cost breakdown does not match invoice”
This is expected during rollout windows if:
- Console is still using legacy fallback for part of traffic
- Telemetry lag/backfills changed cost attribution after the fact
- Invoice includes intentional billing-specific adjustments (taxes, credits, rounding rules) that are out of scope for cost model primitives

Actions:
1) Check if fallback is active (`console.costmodel.fallback.count`)
2) Check Cost Model API drift dashboard (SRE-owned)
3) Confirm the widget is using the correct time window + granularity
4) If mismatch persists, open a thread with Finance (Laura Bennett / Rishi Malhotra) and include:
   - org_id/project_id
   - time range
   - schema version used
   - breakdown totals

### “Latency spike on cost-model endpoint affects Console page load”
Actions:
- Confirm Console timeouts are enforced (do not wait >750ms on dashboard paths)
- Verify caching is functioning (L2 hit rate)
- Reduce fanout: consolidate multiple widget queries into one aggregate query where possible
- If persistent, file an incident and include:
  - endpoint, group_by, time range, max_groups/max_series
  - request volume and concurrency

---

## Ownership + support
- Cost Model API service owner: Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- Telemetry inputs/data quality: Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright)
- Console integration owner: Console Backend (Chloe Martin / Jada Williams)
- SLO/on-call: SRE (Rafael Mendes)

For changes to required fields, open a PR against the versioned schema package and tag:
- `@platform-control-plane`
- `@console-backend`
- `@telemetry-analytics`

---

## Appendix: Recommended widget patterns
### Pattern A: Top routes by cost (Last 24h)
- One aggregate query
- `group_by=[route]`
- `max_groups=50`
- `granularity=1h`
- Cache TTL: 60s

### Pattern B: Model cost split (Last 1h)
- One aggregate query
- `group_by=[model_id]`
- `granularity=5m`
- Cache TTL: 30–60s

### Pattern C: Drill-down (single request)
- One request breakdown query
- Cache TTL: 5m
- UI: show schema version + “data freshness” timestamp
