source.title: Perf Baseline Dashboards Spec (Q1 2026)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.body:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana (and optionally Looker) dashboards and alerting used to report and triage results from the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project.

The dashboards must support:
- Baseline reporting for key metrics (TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput)
- Root-cause oriented drilldowns (saturation, queueing, kernel selection, cache behavior)
- Run-to-run comparison and regression attribution
- Direct linking from dashboard → run manifest → traces/logs/artifacts

This spec is intentionally prescriptive: consistent naming, tags, and panel layout are required so that engineers can move between runs, models, regions, and SKUs without relearning the UI.

## Goals
1) Provide a single “source of truth” view for a benchmark run (or run set) including:
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- Throughput (tokens/s, req/s) and concurrency
- Saturation indicators (GPU utilization, KV cache pressure, queue wait)
- Noise/variance indicators

2) Enable regression triage in < 15 minutes for most cases by answering:
- What changed? (runtime build, scheduler profile, kernel profile, driver/CUDA, workload seed)
- Where did the time go? (queue wait vs prefill vs decode)
- Is it capacity/saturation? (GPU busy %, SM occupancy proxy, memory BW proxy, KV cache hits/evictions)
- Is it a kernel selection / fallback issue?

3) Standardize drilldowns and links:
- “Open run manifest”
- “Open trace (Tempo)”
- “Open logs (Loki)” and “Open scheduler debug bundle”

## Non-goals
- Customer-facing benchmark dashboards
- Replacing perf-canary gating output (these dashboards complement gating, they do not define pass/fail)
- Building a general-purpose production SLO dashboard (production SLO dashboards remain in eng-sre; this is for benchmark runs and canary runs)

## Definitions (must match ADR-0172)
All metric names and boundaries must match: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0172-benchmark-metrics-and-run-manifest-format

- TTFT (time-to-first-token): server-side elapsed time from request accepted by Redwood ingress/runtime to first token emitted on the server response stream (“first token on wire” at the server). Excludes client read/backpressure.
- TPOT (time-per-output-token): (server-side time between first token and end of generation) / (number of output tokens - 1). For non-streaming responses, measured from internal first-token event to internal last-token event.
- Throughput:
  - tokens/s: total output tokens / wall time, reported per replica and aggregated
  - req/s: request completions / second (for embeddings and non-streaming)

Cache state dimension:
- cold: prefix/KV cache disabled or empty
- warm: prefix/KV cache enabled and warmed per workload definition (see representative workload suite v1)

## Data sources
### 1) Bench results store (authoritative)
- Storage: ClickHouse cluster “obs-analytics”
- Database/table (canonical): bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
- Grain: one row per run_id x metric x percentile x (optional) cohort
- Key columns (expected):
  - run_id, run_group
  - created_at
  - model_id, model_family
  - workload_id, workload_family
  - region, gpu_sku
  - runtime_build (git SHA/tag), runtime_version
  - scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
  - cache_state, streaming_mode
  - metric_name (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_s, req_per_s, queue_wait_ms, …)
  - percentile (p50, p95, p99) or value_type (mean, max)
  - value
  - sample_size

Note: Grafana queries should prefer ClickHouse for percentiles computed from raw samples when available. When only summarized percentiles are stored, clearly label as “reported percentile”.

### 2) Prometheus for real-time canary runs
- Primary: redwood-benchmark Prometheus job scraped from perf-canary and benchmark harness
- Metrics prefix: redwood_bench_* and redwood_runtime_* (bench-tagged)
- Use Prometheus only for “last N hours” canary visibility; use ClickHouse for historical baselines.

### 3) Traces/logs/artifacts
- Traces: Tempo, trace_id stored in ClickHouse as bench_trace_id where available
- Logs: Loki (bench-tagged), run_id must be a log label
- Artifacts: S3-compatible bucket “perf-artifacts” (internal), path includes run_id

## Required tagging / dimensions (contract)
Dashboards assume the following tags exist on every run. If a run is missing a dimension, panels may be incomplete; the run should be considered “invalid for baseline”.

Required dimensions:
- run_id (string, unique)
- run_group (string; e.g., “q1-2026-baseline-freeze”, “nightly-main”, “release-branch-2026.02”)
- model_id (string)
- workload_id (string)
- region (string; at least us-east, eu-west)
- gpu_sku (string; e.g., H100-80GB, A100-80GB, L40S)
- runtime_build (string; git SHA or release tag)
- scheduler_profile (string)
- kernel_profile (string)
- cache_state (cold|warm)
- streaming_mode (streaming|non_streaming)

Strongly recommended dimensions (improve attribution):
- driver_version, cuda_version
- container_image (digest)
- power_mode / clocks_locked (boolean)
- instance_type
- replica_count

## Dashboard set (navigation)
We will publish a small, consistent set of dashboards in Grafana folder:
Folder: “Perf Baselines (Serving Runtime)”

Dashboards:
1) Perf Baseline — Overview
2) Perf Baseline — Run Compare
3) Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
4) Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
5) Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution

Looker (optional): “Perf Baseline — Executive Summary” (tables + trends; no deep triage)

## Global dashboard variables (Grafana)
All dashboards must expose the same variable names and defaults.

Variables (type → source):
- run_group (query → ClickHouse distinct run_group; default: q1-2026-baseline-freeze if present)
- run_id (query → ClickHouse distinct run_id filtered by run_group; allow multi-select)
- baseline_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select; default pinned to baseline anchor build)
- candidate_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select)
- model_id (query → distinct)
- workload_id (query → distinct)
- region (query → distinct)
- gpu_sku (query → distinct)
- cache_state (custom: cold,warm)
- streaming_mode (custom: streaming,non_streaming)

Conventions:
- If a panel is meaningful only for streaming, it must explicitly filter streaming_mode=streaming.
- For comparisons, always compute (candidate - baseline) and (candidate / baseline) where applicable.

## Dashboard 1: Perf Baseline — Overview
Purpose: one-page health view for a selected run_id (or run set), covering core metrics and quick links.

Layout (top → bottom):

### A) Run header (text panel)
Must display:
- run_id, run_group
- model_id, workload_id
- region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build, container_image
- scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
- cache_state, streaming_mode
- sample_size

Also include:
- Link: “Open run manifest”
- Link: “Open traces (Tempo) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open logs (Loki) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open artifacts folder (S3 browser)”

### B) Core latency percentiles (time series or stat grid)
Panels (stat w/ sparkline where time window supports):
- TTFT p50 (ms)
- TTFT p95 (ms)
- TTFT p99 (ms)
- TPOT p50 (ms)
- TPOT p95 (ms)
- TPOT p99 (ms)

Display requirements:
- Units: milliseconds
- Each stat must show last value + delta vs baseline_run_id when both baseline and candidate are selected
- Include sample_size and run_count in tooltip

### C) Throughput and completion
Panels:
- tokens/s (mean) and tokens/s (p95 of per-window)
- req/s (for embeddings and non-streaming)
- completed requests, error count, timeout count

### D) Quick saturation indicators (single-value + threshold colors)
Panels:
- GPU busy % (if available; else “GPU time / wall time” proxy)
- Avg batch size (decode) and avg batch size (prefill)
- Queue wait p95 (ms)
- KV cache hit rate (if enabled)
- KV cache eviction rate (evictions/min)

Color thresholds (initial; tune after variance calibration):
- Queue wait p95: green < 5ms; yellow 5–20ms; red > 20ms
- KV evictions: green < 10/min; yellow 10–100/min; red > 100/min

### E) Top regressions within run set (table)
If multi-select run_id is used, show a table sorted by worst TTFT p99 and worst TPOT p99, including:
- run_id
- ttft_p99_ms
- tpot_p99_ms
- tokens_per_s
- queue_wait_p95_ms
- kernel_fallback_rate

## Dashboard 2: Perf Baseline — Run Compare
Purpose: side-by-side comparison baseline_run_id vs candidate_run_id and clear deltas.

### A) Summary delta table (table)
Rows (metrics):
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- tokens/s
- req/s (if relevant)
- queue_wait p95
- batch_assembly_ms p95
- kernel_fallback_rate
- kv_hit_rate

Columns:
- baseline value
- candidate value
- absolute delta
- relative delta (%)
- sample_size baseline/candidate

Formatting:
- Relative delta should be colored by perf-regression policy thresholds (see confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds)

### B) Percentile curves (if raw samples are available)
Panels:
- TTFT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)
- TPOT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)

If raw samples not available, show p50/p95/p99 only and label clearly.

### C) Decomposition (stacked bar)
Panels:
- TTFT decomposition: queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill_compute + first_token_emit_overhead
- End-to-end generation decomposition: decode_compute + scheduling_overhead + streaming_overhead

Note: decomposition requires benchmark instrumentation; if not present, panel should show “missing metric” state with a link to ENG-4826.

## Dashboard 3: Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
Purpose: answer “is this a saturation/overload/queueing issue?”

Panels:
1) Concurrency over time (requested vs achieved)
2) Queue depth (avg/max) and queue wait percentiles
3) Batch assembly time percentiles
4) Batch size distribution (histogram-like table)
5) Backpressure/load shed counters:
   - requests_throttled_total
   - requests_dropped_total
   - overload_mode_seconds
6) GPU utilization proxies:
   - gpu_busy_percent (preferred)
   - sm_occupancy_proxy / kernel_active_ratio (fallback)
   - hbm_bw_proxy (fallback)
7) KV cache pressure:
   - kv_cache_bytes_used
   - kv_cache_bytes_free
   - kv_cache_evictions_total

Interpretation guidance (text panel at top):
- Rising queue wait with flat GPU busy often indicates scheduler bottleneck, dispatch overhead, or batch assembly inefficiency.
- Rising GPU busy with rising TTFT p99 indicates saturation; compare to batch size and backpressure.
- High eviction rate combined with long-context prefill can produce TTFT tail spikes even if GPU busy is stable.

## Dashboard 4: Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
Purpose: make noise visible and prevent overreacting to expected variance.

Panels:
1) Run-to-run variance (box plot) for TTFT p95/p99 by (model_id, workload_id, gpu_sku, region)
2) Warmup effect:
   - TTFT p95 over first N requests vs steady state
   - tokens/s over time
3) Host / placement indicators (if captured):
   - CPU steal %
   - NUMA remote memory ratio
   - GPU clocks (min/avg/max) and clock throttling count
4) Confidence / sample size panel:
   - sample_size
   - bootstrap CI width (if computed)

Required annotation:
- Every panel must support “show baseline freeze window” annotation (vertical region) when run_group=q1-2026-baseline-freeze.

## Dashboard 5: Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution
Purpose: pinpoint regressions tied to kernel selection changes or scheduler behavior changes.

Panels:
1) Kernel selection breakdown (table):
   - kernel_name
   - selection_rate (%)
   - fallback_reason (if any)
   - associated sequence-length regime
2) Kernel fallback rate over time
3) Scheduler fairness/latency tradeoff indicators:
   - per-tenant fairness index (if benchmark simulates tenants)
   - queue discipline mode (label)
4) Sequence length distributions (from workload definition + observed):
   - prompt tokens histogram (bins)
   - output tokens histogram (bins)
5) Correlation panel:
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs kernel_fallback_rate
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs queue_wait_p95

Drilldown links:
- “Open kernel debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)
- “Open scheduler debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)

## Standard links and URL conventions
### Run manifest
- Stored at: s3://perf-artifacts/manifests/{run_id}/run-manifest.json
- Grafana link label: “Run manifest”
- If run manifest is mirrored into ClickHouse as JSON text, also provide “View manifest in table” link.

### Traces
- Tempo query must use:
  - tag: run_id={run_id}
  - optional: model_id, workload_id
- Grafana link label: “Traces (Tempo)”

### Logs
- Loki query must use label selector:
  - {run_id="{run_id}", app=~"bench-runner|redwood-runtime"}
- Grafana link label: “Logs (Loki)”

### Artifacts
- Base path: s3://perf-artifacts/runs/{run_id}/
- Expected artifacts:
  - scheduler.jsonl
  - kernel_selection.jsonl
  - perfetto_trace.pb
  - stdout.log, stderr.log
  - results.json

## Alerting spec (Grafana + perf-canary integration)
Dashboards provide visibility; alerting drives action.

### Alert categories
1) Perf-canary regression alerts (nightly + release gating mode)
2) Benchmark infrastructure health (missing metrics, run failures, unusually high variance)

### Alert routing
- “Perf Regression (page)” → on-call (eng-serving-runtime primary) when:
  - run_group is a release gating group OR regression is severe
  - AND metric breach meets “regression” threshold (not just noise)
- “Perf Regression (triage)” → #eng-perf-triage (Slack) for non-gating nightly regressions
- “Benchmark Infra” → #eng-benchmarking for harness/fleet issues

### Initial alert thresholds (policy-aligned)
Use confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds as canonical. Until calibrated, implement conservative defaults:

- TTFT p99 regression (streaming workloads):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.15 AND absolute delta ≥ 10ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.08 AND absolute delta ≥ 5ms
- TPOT p99 regression:
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.12 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.2ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.06 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.1ms
- Throughput regression (tokens/s):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.90
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.95

Noise guardrails:
- Do not alert unless sample_size ≥ minimum configured per workload family
- Suppress alerts when benchmark infra health indicates instability (e.g., GPU clocks unlocked + high throttling)

### Alert annotations
Every regression alert must include:
- baseline_run_id, candidate_run_id
- model_id, workload_id, region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build(s), scheduler_profile(s), kernel_profile(s)
- Links: Run Compare dashboard with variables set; run manifest; traces

## Panel naming conventions
- Prefix with metric family: “TTFT”, “TPOT”, “Throughput”, “Queueing”, “KV Cache”, “Kernel Selection”, “Infra/Noise”
- Include percentile in title: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms)”
- Include cohort in title if filtered: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms) — long-prefill”

## Dashboard QA / acceptance criteria
A dashboard release is acceptable when:
1) Selecting any run_id from q1-2026-baseline-freeze produces non-empty values for core panels (TTFT/TPOT/throughput) and correct units.
2) Drilldown links resolve correctly for at least 3 sample runs (manifest + traces + logs).
3) Run Compare produces correct deltas and percent change formatting.
4) At least one workload per family (chat streaming, long-context, embeddings) has meaningful saturation panels.
5) No panel uses ambiguous metric definitions (must match ADR-0172).

## Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (Dec 2025): Publish dashboards with core metrics and run header + links. Validate tags and manifest linking.
- Phase 2 (Jan 2026): Add saturation/queueing panels as scheduler instrumentation lands (ENG-4826, PR-21503).
- Phase 3 (Feb 2026): Add kernel attribution panels as kernel debug dump ships (PR-21519).
- Phase 4 (late Q1 2026): Turn on alerting for nightly; then gating mode after a 2-week dry run.

## Ownership and change management
- Dashboard owner (implementation): Sergio Costa
- Standards owner (metrics + tagging): Allison Grant
- Alert routing owner: Aisha Bello

Changes to:
- metric definitions → require ADR update or explicit sign-off in perf standards page
- alert thresholds → require review by eng-sre + perf program owner (Jonas Weber)

## Appendix: Example ClickHouse query templates (reference)
Note: these are illustrative; actual queries live in Grafana panel definitions.

A) Fetch TTFT percentiles for a run
SELECT percentile, value
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id = '${run_id}'
  AND metric_name = 'ttft_ms'
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99');

B) Fetch baseline vs candidate for delta table
SELECT
  metric_name,
  percentile,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${baseline_run_id}') AS baseline_value,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${candidate_run_id}') AS candidate_value,
  (candidate_value - baseline_value) AS abs_delta,
  (candidate_value / baseline_value) AS rel_ratio
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id IN ('${baseline_run_id}','${candidate_run_id}')
  AND metric_name IN ('ttft_ms','tpot_ms','tokens_per_s','queue_wait_ms')
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99')
GROUP BY metric_name, percentile;

## Appendix: Known gaps (as of last_updated)
- Not all runs have trace_id populated; until then, the “Traces (Tempo)” link should fall back to a query by run_id label.
- KV cache metrics are incomplete for some SKUs; panels must tolerate missing fields.
- Some embeddings workloads do not emit tokens/s; throughput panel must switch to req/s when workload_family=embeddings.

title: Perf Baseline Dashboards Spec (Q1 2026)
preview:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana (and optionally Looker) dashboards and alerting used to report and triage results from the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project.

The dashboards must support:
- Baseline reporting for key metrics (TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput)
- Root-cause oriented drilldowns (saturation, queueing, kernel selection, cache behavior)
- Run-to-run comparison and regression attribution
- Direct linking from dashboard → run manifest → traces/logs/artifacts

This spec is intentionally prescriptive: consistent naming, tags, and panel layout are required so that engineers can move between runs, models, regions, and SKUs without relearning the UI.

## Goals
1) Provide a single “source of truth” view for a benchmark run (or run set) including:
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- Throughput (tokens/s, req/s) and concurrency
- Saturation indicators (GPU utilization, KV cache pressure, queue wait)
- Noise/variance indicators

2) Enable regression triage in < 15 minutes for most cases by answering:
- What changed? (runtime build, scheduler profile, kernel profile, driver/CUDA, workload seed)
- Where did the time go? (queue wait vs prefill vs decode)
- Is it capacity/saturation? (GPU busy %, SM occupancy proxy, memory BW proxy, KV cache hits/evictions)
- Is it a kernel selection / fallback issue?

3) Standardize drilldowns and links:
- “Open run manifest”
- “Open trace (Tempo)”
- “Open logs (Loki)” and “Open scheduler debug bundle”

## Non-goals
- Customer-facing benchmark dashboards
- Replacing perf-canary gating output (these dashboards complement gating, they do not define pass/fail)
- Building a general-purpose production SLO dashboard (production SLO dashboards remain in eng-sre; this is for benchmark runs and canary runs)

## Definitions (must match ADR-0172)
All metric names and boundaries must match: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0172-benchmark-metrics-and-run-manifest-format

- TTFT (time-to-first-token): server-side elapsed time from request accepted by Redwood ingress/runtime to first token emitted on the server response stream (“first token on wire” at the server). Excludes client read/backpressure.
- TPOT (time-per-output-token): (server-side time between first token and end of generation) / (number of output tokens - 1). For non-streaming responses, measured from internal first-token event to internal last-token event.
- Throughput:
  - tokens/s: total output tokens / wall time, reported per replica and aggregated
  - req/s: request completions / second (for embeddings and non-streaming)

Cache state dimension:
- cold: prefix/KV cache disabled or empty
- warm: prefix/KV cache enabled and warmed per workload definition (see representative workload suite v1)

## Data sources
### 1) Bench results store (authoritative)
- Storage: ClickHouse cluster “obs-analytics”
- Database/table (canonical): bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
- Grain: one row per run_id x metric x percentile x (optional) cohort
- Key columns (expected):
  - run_id, run_group
  - created_at
  - model_id, model_family
  - workload_id, workload_family
  - region, gpu_sku
  - runtime_build (git SHA/tag), runtime_version
  - scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
  - cache_state, streaming_mode
  - metric_name (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_s, req_per_s, queue_wait_ms, …)
  - percentile (p50, p95, p99) or value_type (mean, max)
  - value
  - sample_size

Note: Grafana queries should prefer ClickHouse for percentiles computed from raw samples when available. When only summarized percentiles are stored, clearly label as “reported percentile”.

### 2) Prometheus for real-time canary runs
- Primary: redwood-benchmark Prometheus job scraped from perf-canary and benchmark harness
- Metrics prefix: redwood_bench_* and redwood_runtime_* (bench-tagged)
- Use Prometheus only for “last N hours” canary visibility; use ClickHouse for historical baselines.

### 3) Traces/logs/artifacts
- Traces: Tempo, trace_id stored in ClickHouse as bench_trace_id where available
- Logs: Loki (bench-tagged), run_id must be a log label
- Artifacts: S3-compatible bucket “perf-artifacts” (internal), path includes run_id

## Required tagging / dimensions (contract)
Dashboards assume the following tags exist on every run. If a run is missing a dimension, panels may be incomplete; the run should be considered “invalid for baseline”.

Required dimensions:
- run_id (string, unique)
- run_group (string; e.g., “q1-2026-baseline-freeze”, “nightly-main”, “release-branch-2026.02”)
- model_id (string)
- workload_id (string)
- region (string; at least us-east, eu-west)
- gpu_sku (string; e.g., H100-80GB, A100-80GB, L40S)
- runtime_build (string; git SHA or release tag)
- scheduler_profile (string)
- kernel_profile (string)
- cache_state (cold|warm)
- streaming_mode (streaming|non_streaming)

Strongly recommended dimensions (improve attribution):
- driver_version, cuda_version
- container_image (digest)
- power_mode / clocks_locked (boolean)
- instance_type
- replica_count

## Dashboard set (navigation)
We will publish a small, consistent set of dashboards in Grafana folder:
Folder: “Perf Baselines (Serving Runtime)”

Dashboards:
1) Perf Baseline — Overview
2) Perf Baseline — Run Compare
3) Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
4) Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
5) Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution

Looker (optional): “Perf Baseline — Executive Summary” (tables + trends; no deep triage)

## Global dashboard variables (Grafana)
All dashboards must expose the same variable names and defaults.

Variables (type → source):
- run_group (query → ClickHouse distinct run_group; default: q1-2026-baseline-freeze if present)
- run_id (query → ClickHouse distinct run_id filtered by run_group; allow multi-select)
- baseline_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select; default pinned to baseline anchor build)
- candidate_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select)
- model_id (query → distinct)
- workload_id (query → distinct)
- region (query → distinct)
- gpu_sku (query → distinct)
- cache_state (custom: cold,warm)
- streaming_mode (custom: streaming,non_streaming)

Conventions:
- If a panel is meaningful only for streaming, it must explicitly filter streaming_mode=streaming.
- For comparisons, always compute (candidate - baseline) and (candidate / baseline) where applicable.

## Dashboard 1: Perf Baseline — Overview
Purpose: one-page health view for a selected run_id (or run set), covering core metrics and quick links.

Layout (top → bottom):

### A) Run header (text panel)
Must display:
- run_id, run_group
- model_id, workload_id
- region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build, container_image
- scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
- cache_state, streaming_mode
- sample_size

Also include:
- Link: “Open run manifest”
- Link: “Open traces (Tempo) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open logs (Loki) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open artifacts folder (S3 browser)”

### B) Core latency percentiles (time series or stat grid)
Panels (stat w/ sparkline where time window supports):
- TTFT p50 (ms)
- TTFT p95 (ms)
- TTFT p99 (ms)
- TPOT p50 (ms)
- TPOT p95 (ms)
- TPOT p99 (ms)

Display requirements:
- Units: milliseconds
- Each stat must show last value + delta vs baseline_run_id when both baseline and candidate are selected
- Include sample_size and run_count in tooltip

### C) Throughput and completion
Panels:
- tokens/s (mean) and tokens/s (p95 of per-window)
- req/s (for embeddings and non-streaming)
- completed requests, error count, timeout count

### D) Quick saturation indicators (single-value + threshold colors)
Panels:
- GPU busy % (if available; else “GPU time / wall time” proxy)
- Avg batch size (decode) and avg batch size (prefill)
- Queue wait p95 (ms)
- KV cache hit rate (if enabled)
- KV cache eviction rate (evictions/min)

Color thresholds (initial; tune after variance calibration):
- Queue wait p95: green < 5ms; yellow 5–20ms; red > 20ms
- KV evictions: green < 10/min; yellow 10–100/min; red > 100/min

### E) Top regressions within run set (table)
If multi-select run_id is used, show a table sorted by worst TTFT p99 and worst TPOT p99, including:
- run_id
- ttft_p99_ms
- tpot_p99_ms
- tokens_per_s
- queue_wait_p95_ms
- kernel_fallback_rate

## Dashboard 2: Perf Baseline — Run Compare
Purpose: side-by-side comparison baseline_run_id vs candidate_run_id and clear deltas.

### A) Summary delta table (table)
Rows (metrics):
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- tokens/s
- req/s (if relevant)
- queue_wait p95
- batch_assembly_ms p95
- kernel_fallback_rate
- kv_hit_rate

Columns:
- baseline value
- candidate value
- absolute delta
- relative delta (%)
- sample_size baseline/candidate

Formatting:
- Relative delta should be colored by perf-regression policy thresholds (see confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds)

### B) Percentile curves (if raw samples are available)
Panels:
- TTFT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)
- TPOT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)

If raw samples not available, show p50/p95/p99 only and label clearly.

### C) Decomposition (stacked bar)
Panels:
- TTFT decomposition: queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill_compute + first_token_emit_overhead
- End-to-end generation decomposition: decode_compute + scheduling_overhead + streaming_overhead

Note: decomposition requires benchmark instrumentation; if not present, panel should show “missing metric” state with a link to ENG-4826.

## Dashboard 3: Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
Purpose: answer “is this a saturation/overload/queueing issue?”

Panels:
1) Concurrency over time (requested vs achieved)
2) Queue depth (avg/max) and queue wait percentiles
3) Batch assembly time percentiles
4) Batch size distribution (histogram-like table)
5) Backpressure/load shed counters:
   - requests_throttled_total
   - requests_dropped_total
   - overload_mode_seconds
6) GPU utilization proxies:
   - gpu_busy_percent (preferred)
   - sm_occupancy_proxy / kernel_active_ratio (fallback)
   - hbm_bw_proxy (fallback)
7) KV cache pressure:
   - kv_cache_bytes_used
   - kv_cache_bytes_free
   - kv_cache_evictions_total

Interpretation guidance (text panel at top):
- Rising queue wait with flat GPU busy often indicates scheduler bottleneck, dispatch overhead, or batch assembly inefficiency.
- Rising GPU busy with rising TTFT p99 indicates saturation; compare to batch size and backpressure.
- High eviction rate combined with long-context prefill can produce TTFT tail spikes even if GPU busy is stable.

## Dashboard 4: Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
Purpose: make noise visible and prevent overreacting to expected variance.

Panels:
1) Run-to-run variance (box plot) for TTFT p95/p99 by (model_id, workload_id, gpu_sku, region)
2) Warmup effect:
   - TTFT p95 over first N requests vs steady state
   - tokens/s over time
3) Host / placement indicators (if captured):
   - CPU steal %
   - NUMA remote memory ratio
   - GPU clocks (min/avg/max) and clock throttling count
4) Confidence / sample size panel:
   - sample_size
   - bootstrap CI width (if computed)

Required annotation:
- Every panel must support “show baseline freeze window” annotation (vertical region) when run_group=q1-2026-baseline-freeze.

## Dashboard 5: Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution
Purpose: pinpoint regressions tied to kernel selection changes or scheduler behavior changes.

Panels:
1) Kernel selection breakdown (table):
   - kernel_name
   - selection_rate (%)
   - fallback_reason (if any)
   - associated sequence-length regime
2) Kernel fallback rate over time
3) Scheduler fairness/latency tradeoff indicators:
   - per-tenant fairness index (if benchmark simulates tenants)
   - queue discipline mode (label)
4) Sequence length distributions (from workload definition + observed):
   - prompt tokens histogram (bins)
   - output tokens histogram (bins)
5) Correlation panel:
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs kernel_fallback_rate
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs queue_wait_p95

Drilldown links:
- “Open kernel debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)
- “Open scheduler debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)

## Standard links and URL conventions
### Run manifest
- Stored at: s3://perf-artifacts/manifests/{run_id}/run-manifest.json
- Grafana link label: “Run manifest”
- If run manifest is mirrored into ClickHouse as JSON text, also provide “View manifest in table” link.

### Traces
- Tempo query must use:
  - tag: run_id={run_id}
  - optional: model_id, workload_id
- Grafana link label: “Traces (Tempo)”

### Logs
- Loki query must use label selector:
  - {run_id="{run_id}", app=~"bench-runner|redwood-runtime"}
- Grafana link label: “Logs (Loki)”

### Artifacts
- Base path: s3://perf-artifacts/runs/{run_id}/
- Expected artifacts:
  - scheduler.jsonl
  - kernel_selection.jsonl
  - perfetto_trace.pb
  - stdout.log, stderr.log
  - results.json

## Alerting spec (Grafana + perf-canary integration)
Dashboards provide visibility; alerting drives action.

### Alert categories
1) Perf-canary regression alerts (nightly + release gating mode)
2) Benchmark infrastructure health (missing metrics, run failures, unusually high variance)

### Alert routing
- “Perf Regression (page)” → on-call (eng-serving-runtime primary) when:
  - run_group is a release gating group OR regression is severe
  - AND metric breach meets “regression” threshold (not just noise)
- “Perf Regression (triage)” → #eng-perf-triage (Slack) for non-gating nightly regressions
- “Benchmark Infra” → #eng-benchmarking for harness/fleet issues

### Initial alert thresholds (policy-aligned)
Use confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds as canonical. Until calibrated, implement conservative defaults:

- TTFT p99 regression (streaming workloads):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.15 AND absolute delta ≥ 10ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.08 AND absolute delta ≥ 5ms
- TPOT p99 regression:
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.12 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.2ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.06 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.1ms
- Throughput regression (tokens/s):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.90
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.95

Noise guardrails:
- Do not alert unless sample_size ≥ minimum configured per workload family
- Suppress alerts when benchmark infra health indicates instability (e.g., GPU clocks unlocked + high throttling)

### Alert annotations
Every regression alert must include:
- baseline_run_id, candidate_run_id
- model_id, workload_id, region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build(s), scheduler_profile(s), kernel_profile(s)
- Links: Run Compare dashboard with variables set; run manifest; traces

## Panel naming conventions
- Prefix with metric family: “TTFT”, “TPOT”, “Throughput”, “Queueing”, “KV Cache”, “Kernel Selection”, “Infra/Noise”
- Include percentile in title: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms)”
- Include cohort in title if filtered: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms) — long-prefill”

## Dashboard QA / acceptance criteria
A dashboard release is acceptable when:
1) Selecting any run_id from q1-2026-baseline-freeze produces non-empty values for core panels (TTFT/TPOT/throughput) and correct units.
2) Drilldown links resolve correctly for at least 3 sample runs (manifest + traces + logs).
3) Run Compare produces correct deltas and percent change formatting.
4) At least one workload per family (chat streaming, long-context, embeddings) has meaningful saturation panels.
5) No panel uses ambiguous metric definitions (must match ADR-0172).

## Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (Dec 2025): Publish dashboards with core metrics and run header + links. Validate tags and manifest linking.
- Phase 2 (Jan 2026): Add saturation/queueing panels as scheduler instrumentation lands (ENG-4826, PR-21503).
- Phase 3 (Feb 2026): Add kernel attribution panels as kernel debug dump ships (PR-21519).
- Phase 4 (late Q1 2026): Turn on alerting for nightly; then gating mode after a 2-week dry run.

## Ownership and change management
- Dashboard owner (implementation): Sergio Costa
- Standards owner (metrics + tagging): Allison Grant
- Alert routing owner: Aisha Bello

Changes to:
- metric definitions → require ADR update or explicit sign-off in perf standards page
- alert thresholds → require review by eng-sre + perf program owner (Jonas Weber)

## Appendix: Example ClickHouse query templates (reference)
Note: these are illustrative; actual queries live in Grafana panel definitions.

A) Fetch TTFT percentiles for a run
SELECT percentile, value
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id = '${run_id}'
  AND metric_name = 'ttft_ms'
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99');

B) Fetch baseline vs candidate for delta table
SELECT
  metric_name,
  percentile,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${baseline_run_id}') AS baseline_value,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${candidate_run_id}') AS candidate_value,
  (candidate_value - baseline_value) AS abs_delta,
  (candidate_value / baseline_value) AS rel_ratio
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id IN ('${baseline_run_id}','${candidate_run_id}')
  AND metric_name IN ('ttft_ms','tpot_ms','tokens_per_s','queue_wait_ms')
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99')
GROUP BY metric_name, percentile;

## Appendix: Known gaps (as of last_updated)
- Not all runs have trace_id populated; until then, the “Traces (Tempo)” link should fall back to a query by run_id label.
- KV cache metrics are incomplete for some SKUs; panels must tolerate missing fields.
- Some embeddings workloads do not emit tokens/s; throughput panel must switch to req/s when workload_family=embeddings.
text:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana (and optionally Looker) dashboards and alerting used to report and triage results from the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project.

The dashboards must support:
- Baseline reporting for key metrics (TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput)
- Root-cause oriented drilldowns (saturation, queueing, kernel selection, cache behavior)
- Run-to-run comparison and regression attribution
- Direct linking from dashboard → run manifest → traces/logs/artifacts

This spec is intentionally prescriptive: consistent naming, tags, and panel layout are required so that engineers can move between runs, models, regions, and SKUs without relearning the UI.

## Goals
1) Provide a single “source of truth” view for a benchmark run (or run set) including:
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- Throughput (tokens/s, req/s) and concurrency
- Saturation indicators (GPU utilization, KV cache pressure, queue wait)
- Noise/variance indicators

2) Enable regression triage in < 15 minutes for most cases by answering:
- What changed? (runtime build, scheduler profile, kernel profile, driver/CUDA, workload seed)
- Where did the time go? (queue wait vs prefill vs decode)
- Is it capacity/saturation? (GPU busy %, SM occupancy proxy, memory BW proxy, KV cache hits/evictions)
- Is it a kernel selection / fallback issue?

3) Standardize drilldowns and links:
- “Open run manifest”
- “Open trace (Tempo)”
- “Open logs (Loki)” and “Open scheduler debug bundle”

## Non-goals
- Customer-facing benchmark dashboards
- Replacing perf-canary gating output (these dashboards complement gating, they do not define pass/fail)
- Building a general-purpose production SLO dashboard (production SLO dashboards remain in eng-sre; this is for benchmark runs and canary runs)

## Definitions (must match ADR-0172)
All metric names and boundaries must match: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-0172-benchmark-metrics-and-run-manifest-format

- TTFT (time-to-first-token): server-side elapsed time from request accepted by Redwood ingress/runtime to first token emitted on the server response stream (“first token on wire” at the server). Excludes client read/backpressure.
- TPOT (time-per-output-token): (server-side time between first token and end of generation) / (number of output tokens - 1). For non-streaming responses, measured from internal first-token event to internal last-token event.
- Throughput:
  - tokens/s: total output tokens / wall time, reported per replica and aggregated
  - req/s: request completions / second (for embeddings and non-streaming)

Cache state dimension:
- cold: prefix/KV cache disabled or empty
- warm: prefix/KV cache enabled and warmed per workload definition (see representative workload suite v1)

## Data sources
### 1) Bench results store (authoritative)
- Storage: ClickHouse cluster “obs-analytics”
- Database/table (canonical): bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
- Grain: one row per run_id x metric x percentile x (optional) cohort
- Key columns (expected):
  - run_id, run_group
  - created_at
  - model_id, model_family
  - workload_id, workload_family
  - region, gpu_sku
  - runtime_build (git SHA/tag), runtime_version
  - scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
  - cache_state, streaming_mode
  - metric_name (ttft_ms, tpot_ms, tokens_per_s, req_per_s, queue_wait_ms, …)
  - percentile (p50, p95, p99) or value_type (mean, max)
  - value
  - sample_size

Note: Grafana queries should prefer ClickHouse for percentiles computed from raw samples when available. When only summarized percentiles are stored, clearly label as “reported percentile”.

### 2) Prometheus for real-time canary runs
- Primary: redwood-benchmark Prometheus job scraped from perf-canary and benchmark harness
- Metrics prefix: redwood_bench_* and redwood_runtime_* (bench-tagged)
- Use Prometheus only for “last N hours” canary visibility; use ClickHouse for historical baselines.

### 3) Traces/logs/artifacts
- Traces: Tempo, trace_id stored in ClickHouse as bench_trace_id where available
- Logs: Loki (bench-tagged), run_id must be a log label
- Artifacts: S3-compatible bucket “perf-artifacts” (internal), path includes run_id

## Required tagging / dimensions (contract)
Dashboards assume the following tags exist on every run. If a run is missing a dimension, panels may be incomplete; the run should be considered “invalid for baseline”.

Required dimensions:
- run_id (string, unique)
- run_group (string; e.g., “q1-2026-baseline-freeze”, “nightly-main”, “release-branch-2026.02”)
- model_id (string)
- workload_id (string)
- region (string; at least us-east, eu-west)
- gpu_sku (string; e.g., H100-80GB, A100-80GB, L40S)
- runtime_build (string; git SHA or release tag)
- scheduler_profile (string)
- kernel_profile (string)
- cache_state (cold|warm)
- streaming_mode (streaming|non_streaming)

Strongly recommended dimensions (improve attribution):
- driver_version, cuda_version
- container_image (digest)
- power_mode / clocks_locked (boolean)
- instance_type
- replica_count

## Dashboard set (navigation)
We will publish a small, consistent set of dashboards in Grafana folder:
Folder: “Perf Baselines (Serving Runtime)”

Dashboards:
1) Perf Baseline — Overview
2) Perf Baseline — Run Compare
3) Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
4) Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
5) Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution

Looker (optional): “Perf Baseline — Executive Summary” (tables + trends; no deep triage)

## Global dashboard variables (Grafana)
All dashboards must expose the same variable names and defaults.

Variables (type → source):
- run_group (query → ClickHouse distinct run_group; default: q1-2026-baseline-freeze if present)
- run_id (query → ClickHouse distinct run_id filtered by run_group; allow multi-select)
- baseline_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select; default pinned to baseline anchor build)
- candidate_run_id (query → ClickHouse; single-select)
- model_id (query → distinct)
- workload_id (query → distinct)
- region (query → distinct)
- gpu_sku (query → distinct)
- cache_state (custom: cold,warm)
- streaming_mode (custom: streaming,non_streaming)

Conventions:
- If a panel is meaningful only for streaming, it must explicitly filter streaming_mode=streaming.
- For comparisons, always compute (candidate - baseline) and (candidate / baseline) where applicable.

## Dashboard 1: Perf Baseline — Overview
Purpose: one-page health view for a selected run_id (or run set), covering core metrics and quick links.

Layout (top → bottom):

### A) Run header (text panel)
Must display:
- run_id, run_group
- model_id, workload_id
- region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build, container_image
- scheduler_profile, kernel_profile
- cache_state, streaming_mode
- sample_size

Also include:
- Link: “Open run manifest”
- Link: “Open traces (Tempo) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open logs (Loki) filtered by run_id”
- Link: “Open artifacts folder (S3 browser)”

### B) Core latency percentiles (time series or stat grid)
Panels (stat w/ sparkline where time window supports):
- TTFT p50 (ms)
- TTFT p95 (ms)
- TTFT p99 (ms)
- TPOT p50 (ms)
- TPOT p95 (ms)
- TPOT p99 (ms)

Display requirements:
- Units: milliseconds
- Each stat must show last value + delta vs baseline_run_id when both baseline and candidate are selected
- Include sample_size and run_count in tooltip

### C) Throughput and completion
Panels:
- tokens/s (mean) and tokens/s (p95 of per-window)
- req/s (for embeddings and non-streaming)
- completed requests, error count, timeout count

### D) Quick saturation indicators (single-value + threshold colors)
Panels:
- GPU busy % (if available; else “GPU time / wall time” proxy)
- Avg batch size (decode) and avg batch size (prefill)
- Queue wait p95 (ms)
- KV cache hit rate (if enabled)
- KV cache eviction rate (evictions/min)

Color thresholds (initial; tune after variance calibration):
- Queue wait p95: green < 5ms; yellow 5–20ms; red > 20ms
- KV evictions: green < 10/min; yellow 10–100/min; red > 100/min

### E) Top regressions within run set (table)
If multi-select run_id is used, show a table sorted by worst TTFT p99 and worst TPOT p99, including:
- run_id
- ttft_p99_ms
- tpot_p99_ms
- tokens_per_s
- queue_wait_p95_ms
- kernel_fallback_rate

## Dashboard 2: Perf Baseline — Run Compare
Purpose: side-by-side comparison baseline_run_id vs candidate_run_id and clear deltas.

### A) Summary delta table (table)
Rows (metrics):
- TTFT p50/p95/p99
- TPOT p50/p95/p99
- tokens/s
- req/s (if relevant)
- queue_wait p95
- batch_assembly_ms p95
- kernel_fallback_rate
- kv_hit_rate

Columns:
- baseline value
- candidate value
- absolute delta
- relative delta (%)
- sample_size baseline/candidate

Formatting:
- Relative delta should be colored by perf-regression policy thresholds (see confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds)

### B) Percentile curves (if raw samples are available)
Panels:
- TTFT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)
- TPOT empirical CDF (baseline vs candidate)

If raw samples not available, show p50/p95/p99 only and label clearly.

### C) Decomposition (stacked bar)
Panels:
- TTFT decomposition: queue_wait + batch_assembly + prefill_compute + first_token_emit_overhead
- End-to-end generation decomposition: decode_compute + scheduling_overhead + streaming_overhead

Note: decomposition requires benchmark instrumentation; if not present, panel should show “missing metric” state with a link to ENG-4826.

## Dashboard 3: Perf Baseline — Saturation & Queueing
Purpose: answer “is this a saturation/overload/queueing issue?”

Panels:
1) Concurrency over time (requested vs achieved)
2) Queue depth (avg/max) and queue wait percentiles
3) Batch assembly time percentiles
4) Batch size distribution (histogram-like table)
5) Backpressure/load shed counters:
   - requests_throttled_total
   - requests_dropped_total
   - overload_mode_seconds
6) GPU utilization proxies:
   - gpu_busy_percent (preferred)
   - sm_occupancy_proxy / kernel_active_ratio (fallback)
   - hbm_bw_proxy (fallback)
7) KV cache pressure:
   - kv_cache_bytes_used
   - kv_cache_bytes_free
   - kv_cache_evictions_total

Interpretation guidance (text panel at top):
- Rising queue wait with flat GPU busy often indicates scheduler bottleneck, dispatch overhead, or batch assembly inefficiency.
- Rising GPU busy with rising TTFT p99 indicates saturation; compare to batch size and backpressure.
- High eviction rate combined with long-context prefill can produce TTFT tail spikes even if GPU busy is stable.

## Dashboard 4: Perf Baseline — Variance & Noise
Purpose: make noise visible and prevent overreacting to expected variance.

Panels:
1) Run-to-run variance (box plot) for TTFT p95/p99 by (model_id, workload_id, gpu_sku, region)
2) Warmup effect:
   - TTFT p95 over first N requests vs steady state
   - tokens/s over time
3) Host / placement indicators (if captured):
   - CPU steal %
   - NUMA remote memory ratio
   - GPU clocks (min/avg/max) and clock throttling count
4) Confidence / sample size panel:
   - sample_size
   - bootstrap CI width (if computed)

Required annotation:
- Every panel must support “show baseline freeze window” annotation (vertical region) when run_group=q1-2026-baseline-freeze.

## Dashboard 5: Perf Baseline — Kernel/Scheduler Attribution
Purpose: pinpoint regressions tied to kernel selection changes or scheduler behavior changes.

Panels:
1) Kernel selection breakdown (table):
   - kernel_name
   - selection_rate (%)
   - fallback_reason (if any)
   - associated sequence-length regime
2) Kernel fallback rate over time
3) Scheduler fairness/latency tradeoff indicators:
   - per-tenant fairness index (if benchmark simulates tenants)
   - queue discipline mode (label)
4) Sequence length distributions (from workload definition + observed):
   - prompt tokens histogram (bins)
   - output tokens histogram (bins)
5) Correlation panel:
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs kernel_fallback_rate
   - scatter: TTFT p99 vs queue_wait_p95

Drilldown links:
- “Open kernel debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)
- “Open scheduler debug dump for run_id” (artifact path)

## Standard links and URL conventions
### Run manifest
- Stored at: s3://perf-artifacts/manifests/{run_id}/run-manifest.json
- Grafana link label: “Run manifest”
- If run manifest is mirrored into ClickHouse as JSON text, also provide “View manifest in table” link.

### Traces
- Tempo query must use:
  - tag: run_id={run_id}
  - optional: model_id, workload_id
- Grafana link label: “Traces (Tempo)”

### Logs
- Loki query must use label selector:
  - {run_id="{run_id}", app=~"bench-runner|redwood-runtime"}
- Grafana link label: “Logs (Loki)”

### Artifacts
- Base path: s3://perf-artifacts/runs/{run_id}/
- Expected artifacts:
  - scheduler.jsonl
  - kernel_selection.jsonl
  - perfetto_trace.pb
  - stdout.log, stderr.log
  - results.json

## Alerting spec (Grafana + perf-canary integration)
Dashboards provide visibility; alerting drives action.

### Alert categories
1) Perf-canary regression alerts (nightly + release gating mode)
2) Benchmark infrastructure health (missing metrics, run failures, unusually high variance)

### Alert routing
- “Perf Regression (page)” → on-call (eng-serving-runtime primary) when:
  - run_group is a release gating group OR regression is severe
  - AND metric breach meets “regression” threshold (not just noise)
- “Perf Regression (triage)” → #eng-perf-triage (Slack) for non-gating nightly regressions
- “Benchmark Infra” → #eng-benchmarking for harness/fleet issues

### Initial alert thresholds (policy-aligned)
Use confluence://architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/perf-regression-policy-and-thresholds as canonical. Until calibrated, implement conservative defaults:

- TTFT p99 regression (streaming workloads):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.15 AND absolute delta ≥ 10ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.08 AND absolute delta ≥ 5ms
- TPOT p99 regression:
  - page if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.12 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.2ms
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≥ 1.06 AND absolute delta ≥ 0.1ms
- Throughput regression (tokens/s):
  - page if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.90
  - triage if candidate/baseline ≤ 0.95

Noise guardrails:
- Do not alert unless sample_size ≥ minimum configured per workload family
- Suppress alerts when benchmark infra health indicates instability (e.g., GPU clocks unlocked + high throttling)

### Alert annotations
Every regression alert must include:
- baseline_run_id, candidate_run_id
- model_id, workload_id, region, gpu_sku
- runtime_build(s), scheduler_profile(s), kernel_profile(s)
- Links: Run Compare dashboard with variables set; run manifest; traces

## Panel naming conventions
- Prefix with metric family: “TTFT”, “TPOT”, “Throughput”, “Queueing”, “KV Cache”, “Kernel Selection”, “Infra/Noise”
- Include percentile in title: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms)”
- Include cohort in title if filtered: e.g., “TTFT p99 (ms) — long-prefill”

## Dashboard QA / acceptance criteria
A dashboard release is acceptable when:
1) Selecting any run_id from q1-2026-baseline-freeze produces non-empty values for core panels (TTFT/TPOT/throughput) and correct units.
2) Drilldown links resolve correctly for at least 3 sample runs (manifest + traces + logs).
3) Run Compare produces correct deltas and percent change formatting.
4) At least one workload per family (chat streaming, long-context, embeddings) has meaningful saturation panels.
5) No panel uses ambiguous metric definitions (must match ADR-0172).

## Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (Dec 2025): Publish dashboards with core metrics and run header + links. Validate tags and manifest linking.
- Phase 2 (Jan 2026): Add saturation/queueing panels as scheduler instrumentation lands (ENG-4826, PR-21503).
- Phase 3 (Feb 2026): Add kernel attribution panels as kernel debug dump ships (PR-21519).
- Phase 4 (late Q1 2026): Turn on alerting for nightly; then gating mode after a 2-week dry run.

## Ownership and change management
- Dashboard owner (implementation): Sergio Costa
- Standards owner (metrics + tagging): Allison Grant
- Alert routing owner: Aisha Bello

Changes to:
- metric definitions → require ADR update or explicit sign-off in perf standards page
- alert thresholds → require review by eng-sre + perf program owner (Jonas Weber)

## Appendix: Example ClickHouse query templates (reference)
Note: these are illustrative; actual queries live in Grafana panel definitions.

A) Fetch TTFT percentiles for a run
SELECT percentile, value
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id = '${run_id}'
  AND metric_name = 'ttft_ms'
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99');

B) Fetch baseline vs candidate for delta table
SELECT
  metric_name,
  percentile,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${baseline_run_id}') AS baseline_value,
  anyIf(value, run_id='${candidate_run_id}') AS candidate_value,
  (candidate_value - baseline_value) AS abs_delta,
  (candidate_value / baseline_value) AS rel_ratio
FROM bench_results.perf_metrics_v1
WHERE run_id IN ('${baseline_run_id}','${candidate_run_id}')
  AND metric_name IN ('ttft_ms','tpot_ms','tokens_per_s','queue_wait_ms')
  AND percentile IN ('p50','p95','p99')
GROUP BY metric_name, percentile;

## Appendix: Known gaps (as of last_updated)
- Not all runs have trace_id populated; until then, the “Traces (Tempo)” link should fall back to a query by run_id label.
- KV cache metrics are incomplete for some SKUs; panels must tolerate missing fields.
- Some embeddings workloads do not emit tokens/s; throughput panel must switch to req/s when workload_family=embeddings.

source.title: Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer: design, operator guide, and rollout
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and cost budgets. It includes design rationale, runtime components, operator playbooks, dashboarding requirements, and a safe rollout plan.

Intended audience
- Platform operators and on-call engineers
- Runtime and serving engineers
- Client SDK and API owners

Why we built APHL
- Observation: a small fraction of request patterns (e.g., prompt templates, system messages, high-frequency webhook patterns) dominate 75% of tail latency because early tokens are not hot in the prefix/KV cache.
- Goal: reduce p95/p99 generation latency for known safe prefixes without increasing token cost or violating tenant SLOs.
- Constraints: must be safe for multi-tenant environment, respect tenant rate limits, and be observable and reversible.

High-level design
-----------------
1) Hint model: clients optionally attach a prefetch hint to a request. A hint is an opaque identifier + optional priority tag + TTL. Hints are advisory; runtime enforces limits.
2) Hint evaluation policy: central policy service scores hints using: past-hit-rate, cost-estimate (tokens), SLO-burn risk, tenant-priority, and regional capacity.
3) Adaptive prefetch actions: runtime can perform one of: no-op, local-prefetch (on same GPU host), remote-prefetch (in dedicated caching tier), or speculative-warm invocation (low-cost model variant to warm KV).
4) Safety controls: per-tenant token budget, global prefetch budget, and backpressure signals from autoscaler.

System components
- Client SDK: adds optional header X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=H123;priority=low;ttl=30s
- Hint Policy Service (HPS): evaluates hints, returns decision tokens and expected cost estimate. Exposes API: POST /v1/hint/evaluate
- Prefetch Controller (runtime side): enforces action, emits traces and metrics, and records which cache shard received prefetch.
- Budget Ledger: centralized budgeting service tracking per-tenant and global prefetch token usage.

Runtime integration notes
- Prefetch work must be queued with lower priority than production inference to avoid SLO interference. Use the existing backlog queue with QoS weight 0.2.
- Prefetch KV writes set a separate TTL namespace: prefix-cache:hint:<hint_id> to avoid eviction collisions with on-path caches.
- When speculative-warm invocations are used, they run on quantized low-cost variants with max_tokens=8 and max_concurrency=2 per tenant region.

Config table (defaults)
| parameter | default | description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 2_000_000 | Global budget across all tenants per minute |
| tenant_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 50_000 | Default tenant budget; overridden by enterprise contracts |
| hint_eval_max_latency_ms | 50 | HPS timeout; hint treated as absent after this |
| prefetch_qos_weight | 0.2 | Relative priority weight for prefetch tasks |
| speculative_variant_model | redwood-lite-q4_8bit | Low-cost model used for speculative warms |

Operator runbook: day-to-day (short)
1) Detection
  - Alert: PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (see Alerting section) fires when global ledger consumption > 90% in 5m.
  - Dashboard: Prefetch hit-rate per region, per-tenant top 20 consumers.
2) Initial mitigation
  - Scale down prefetch window: set global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min -> 25% via Helm override: helm upgrade runtime --set prefetch.globalBudget=500000
  - Opt-out noisy tenants: rpc call to Budget Ledger: POST /admin/ledger/adjust?tenant=TENANT_ID&action=opt_out
3) Recovery verification
  - Confirm p99 latency for affected routes decreases after throttling.
  - Run negative-safety-check: sample recent generations for quality regression.

Alerting and dashboards (required panels/alerts)
- Panels (must exist on platform AppOps dashboard):
  - Prefetch budget burn rate (global + region) — timeseries (tokens/min)
  - Prefetch hit-rate (percentage of prefetches that produced cache hits) — heatmap by shard
  - Prefetch-induced cold-start reductions (delta p95 vs baseline) — bar chart per model variant
  - Top 50 hint producers (tenant_id, hint_id, tokens_used) — table with owner links
- Alerts (defaults):
  - PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN: global_consumption_5m / global_budget > 0.9 for 5m -> Sev3 (pager to platform-duty)
  - PREFETCH_HIGH_MISS_RATE: prefetch_hit_rate < 0.2 for 10m -> Sev4 (Slack ops)
  - PREFETCH_SLO_IMPACT: prefetch_backpressure_ratio > 0.05 and p99_inference_latency_increase > 10% over 15m -> Sev2 (on-call)

Runbook: incident steps (detailed)
1) Triage
  - Identify scope: is the issue global, region-specific, tenant-specific? Use the "Top hint producers" panel.
  - Correlate with autoscaler metrics and host load: if host_cpu_util > 85% and prefetch queue length > 1000, treat as resource contention.
2) Immediate actions (ordered)
  a) Toggle prefetch warmers to readonly (no speculative runs): curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/toggle -d '{"speculative":false}'
  b) Reduce speculative_variant_model concurrency: curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/update -d '{"speculative_concurrency":1}'
  c) If still burning budget, flip global circuit: kubectl -n runtime patch configmap prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
3) Post-incident
  - Run smoke-generation suite on a sampled set of templates to confirm no regression in output quality or new latency spikes.
  - Update incident timeline and mark triage owner.

Safe rollout plan (canary -> ramp -> GA)
- Canary (week 0): enable APHL for internal platform tenants only. Goals: validate HPS latency <= 50ms, prefetch hit-rate >= 0.35 for top templates, and no increase in p99 for high-priority tenants.
- Early customer opt-in (week 1–2): invite 5 low-risk customers on dedicated pools. Monitor budgets and runbook.
- Controlled ramp (week 3–6): increase tenant inclusion by 20% weekly. Add automation to opt-out tenants that exceed budget thresholds.
- GA: enable by default for self-serve tenants with prefetch budget set to conservative defaults.

Telemetry and metrics contract (minimal list)
- prefetch.requests_total (labels: region, tenant_id, hint_id, action)
- prefetch.tokens_consumed_total (labels: region, tenant_id)
- prefetch.hit_total (labels: region, shard_id)
- prefetch.speculative_runs_total (labels: region, model_variant)
- prefetch.evaluation_latency_ms_bucket (labels: hps_node)
- prefetch.burn_ratio (gauge) = tokens_consumed / configured_budget

Operational knobs exposed (CLI/examples)
- Evaluate a hint (local test):
  curl -XPOST https://hps.internal/v1/hint/evaluate -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"hint_id":"banner-home-v2","tenant":"acme-corp","context_samples":5}'

- Temporarily throttle global budget via kubectl:
  kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min":"500000"}}'

- Opt a tenant out from prefetching:
  curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/tenants/acme-corp/opt_out -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN'

Testing and evaluation checklist
- Unit tests: HPS decision logic (edge cases: stale hints, malformed hints).
- Integration tests: Prefetch end-to-end with KV writes, TTL handling, and eviction simulation.
- Load tests: synthetic traffic to validate that prefetch does not increase request queue delay by >1%.
- Regression tests: generation quality smoke-run against registered prompt-set (must pass 95% of baseline exact-match tests).

Common failures and troubleshooting tips
- Symptom: high prefetch miss rate (>80%)
  - Likely cause: hints mis-identified or context mismatch. Action: inspect context_samples reported by HPS and compare tokenized prefixes.
- Symptom: unexpected budget burn with small hit benefit
  - Likely cause: runaway speculative runs or tenant misconfiguration. Action: check speculative_runs_total by tenant and opt-out; reduce speculative_concurrency.
- Symptom: increased p99 for high-priority tenant after APHL enable
  - Likely cause: prefetch backpressure. Action: disable prefetch on tenant immediately and escalate to runtime team to review QoS scheduler.

Rollback criteria and procedure
- Rollback triggers:
  - Sustained PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (>= 95%) for 15m despite mitigations.
  - SLO regressions: p99 increase > 15% for any priority-1 tenant for 10m.
- Procedure:
  1) kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
  2) POST to Budget Ledger to clear queued prefetch entries: curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/clear_queues
  3) Run verification job: /tools/smoke/llm_latency_check.sh --routes=critical

Open questions and follow-ups
- Long-term: should hints be persisted as part of model metadata for better lifecycle tracking?
- Research: quantify cost/latency tradeoffs for multi-token speculative warms (we currently use max_tokens=8 as heuristic).
- Observability: add correlation ids from client SDK through HPS to Prefetch Controller for 100% traceability.

Appendix: example hint formats and sample logs
- Header example (HTTP):
  X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=banner-home-v2;priority=med;ttl=30s;signature=abcd1234

- HPS API example request JSON:
  {
    "hint_id": "banner-home-v2",
    "tenant": "acme-corp",
    "recent_templates": ["Welcome v2", "Promo slot"],
    "samples": 3
  }

- Sample Prefetch Controller log excerpt:
  [2026-02-14T09:12:03Z] PREFETCH_DECISION tenant=acme-corp hint=banner-home-v2 action=local_prefetch tokens=24 hit_est=0.62 cost_est=18ms_consume node=rt-12

Contact and ownership
- Primary owner: eng-platform (Ava Chen)
- Runtime integration: eng-serving-runtime (Miguel Santos)
- HPS and budgeting: eng-infra (Priya Kapoor)

Change history
- 2025-11-10: Draft authored and internal review started.
- 2025-12-02: Added telemetry contract and operator knobs after load test.
- 2026-02-14: Published: updated rollout plan and runbook based on canary feedback.

End of document

title: Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer: design, operator guide, and rollout
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and cost budgets. It includes design rationale, runtime components, operator playbooks, dashboarding requirements, and a safe rollout plan.

Intended audience
- Platform operators and on-call engineers
- Runtime and serving engineers
- Client SDK and API owners

Why we built APHL
- Observation: a small fraction of request patterns (e.g., prompt templates, system messages, high-frequency webhook patterns) dominate 75% of tail latency because early tokens are not hot in the prefix/KV cache.
- Goal: reduce p95/p99 generation latency for known safe prefixes without increasing token cost or violating tenant SLOs.
- Constraints: must be safe for multi-tenant environment, respect tenant rate limits, and be observable and reversible.

High-level design
-----------------
1) Hint model: clients optionally attach a prefetch hint to a request. A hint is an opaque identifier + optional priority tag + TTL. Hints are advisory; runtime enforces limits.
2) Hint evaluation policy: central policy service scores hints using: past-hit-rate, cost-estimate (tokens), SLO-burn risk, tenant-priority, and regional capacity.
3) Adaptive prefetch actions: runtime can perform one of: no-op, local-prefetch (on same GPU host), remote-prefetch (in dedicated caching tier), or speculative-warm invocation (low-cost model variant to warm KV).
4) Safety controls: per-tenant token budget, global prefetch budget, and backpressure signals from autoscaler.

System components
- Client SDK: adds optional header X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=H123;priority=low;ttl=30s
- Hint Policy Service (HPS): evaluates hints, returns decision tokens and expected cost estimate. Exposes API: POST /v1/hint/evaluate
- Prefetch Controller (runtime side): enforces action, emits traces and metrics, and records which cache shard received prefetch.
- Budget Ledger: centralized budgeting service tracking per-tenant and global prefetch token usage.

Runtime integration notes
- Prefetch work must be queued with lower priority than production inference to avoid SLO interference. Use the existing backlog queue with QoS weight 0.2.
- Prefetch KV writes set a separate TTL namespace: prefix-cache:hint:<hint_id> to avoid eviction collisions with on-path caches.
- When speculative-warm invocations are used, they run on quantized low-cost variants with max_tokens=8 and max_concurrency=2 per tenant region.

Config table (defaults)
| parameter | default | description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 2_000_000 | Global budget across all tenants per minute |
| tenant_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 50_000 | Default tenant budget; overridden by enterprise contracts |
| hint_eval_max_latency_ms | 50 | HPS timeout; hint treated as absent after this |
| prefetch_qos_weight | 0.2 | Relative priority weight for prefetch tasks |
| speculative_variant_model | redwood-lite-q4_8bit | Low-cost model used for speculative warms |

Operator runbook: day-to-day (short)
1) Detection
  - Alert: PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (see Alerting section) fires when global ledger consumption > 90% in 5m.
  - Dashboard: Prefetch hit-rate per region, per-tenant top 20 consumers.
2) Initial mitigation
  - Scale down prefetch window: set global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min -> 25% via Helm override: helm upgrade runtime --set prefetch.globalBudget=500000
  - Opt-out noisy tenants: rpc call to Budget Ledger: POST /admin/ledger/adjust?tenant=TENANT_ID&action=opt_out
3) Recovery verification
  - Confirm p99 latency for affected routes decreases after throttling.
  - Run negative-safety-check: sample recent generations for quality regression.

Alerting and dashboards (required panels/alerts)
- Panels (must exist on platform AppOps dashboard):
  - Prefetch budget burn rate (global + region) — timeseries (tokens/min)
  - Prefetch hit-rate (percentage of prefetches that produced cache hits) — heatmap by shard
  - Prefetch-induced cold-start reductions (delta p95 vs baseline) — bar chart per model variant
  - Top 50 hint producers (tenant_id, hint_id, tokens_used) — table with owner links
- Alerts (defaults):
  - PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN: global_consumption_5m / global_budget > 0.9 for 5m -> Sev3 (pager to platform-duty)
  - PREFETCH_HIGH_MISS_RATE: prefetch_hit_rate < 0.2 for 10m -> Sev4 (Slack ops)
  - PREFETCH_SLO_IMPACT: prefetch_backpressure_ratio > 0.05 and p99_inference_latency_increase > 10% over 15m -> Sev2 (on-call)

Runbook: incident steps (detailed)
1) Triage
  - Identify scope: is the issue global, region-specific, tenant-specific? Use the "Top hint producers" panel.
  - Correlate with autoscaler metrics and host load: if host_cpu_util > 85% and prefetch queue length > 1000, treat as resource contention.
2) Immediate actions (ordered)
  a) Toggle prefetch warmers to readonly (no speculative runs): curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/toggle -d '{"speculative":false}'
  b) Reduce speculative_variant_model concurrency: curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/update -d '{"speculative_concurrency":1}'
  c) If still burning budget, flip global circuit: kubectl -n runtime patch configmap prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
3) Post-incident
  - Run smoke-generation suite on a sampled set of templates to confirm no regression in output quality or new latency spikes.
  - Update incident timeline and mark triage owner.

Safe rollout plan (canary -> ramp -> GA)
- Canary (week 0): enable APHL for internal platform tenants only. Goals: validate HPS latency <= 50ms, prefetch hit-rate >= 0.35 for top templates, and no increase in p99 for high-priority tenants.
- Early customer opt-in (week 1–2): invite 5 low-risk customers on dedicated pools. Monitor budgets and runbook.
- Controlled ramp (week 3–6): increase tenant inclusion by 20% weekly. Add automation to opt-out tenants that exceed budget thresholds.
- GA: enable by default for self-serve tenants with prefetch budget set to conservative defaults.

Telemetry and metrics contract (minimal list)
- prefetch.requests_total (labels: region, tenant_id, hint_id, action)
- prefetch.tokens_consumed_total (labels: region, tenant_id)
- prefetch.hit_total (labels: region, shard_id)
- prefetch.speculative_runs_total (labels: region, model_variant)
- prefetch.evaluation_latency_ms_bucket (labels: hps_node)
- prefetch.burn_ratio (gauge) = tokens_consumed / configured_budget

Operational knobs exposed (CLI/examples)
- Evaluate a hint (local test):
  curl -XPOST https://hps.internal/v1/hint/evaluate -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"hint_id":"banner-home-v2","tenant":"acme-corp","context_samples":5}'

- Temporarily throttle global budget via kubectl:
  kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min":"500000"}}'

- Opt a tenant out from prefetching:
  curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/tenants/acme-corp/opt_out -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN'

Testing and evaluation checklist
- Unit tests: HPS decision logic (edge cases: stale hints, malformed hints).
- Integration tests: Prefetch end-to-end with KV writes, TTL handling, and eviction simulation.
- Load tests: synthetic traffic to validate that prefetch does not increase request queue delay by >1%.
- Regression tests: generation quality smoke-run against registered prompt-set (must pass 95% of baseline exact-match tests).

Common failures and troubleshooting tips
- Symptom: high prefetch miss rate (>80%)
  - Likely cause: hints mis-identified or context mismatch. Action: inspect context_samples reported by HPS and compare tokenized prefixes.
- Symptom: unexpected budget burn with small hit benefit
  - Likely cause: runaway speculative runs or tenant misconfiguration. Action: check speculative_runs_total by tenant and opt-out; reduce speculative_concurrency.
- Symptom: increased p99 for high-priority tenant after APHL enable
  - Likely cause: prefetch backpressure. Action: disable prefetch on tenant immediately and escalate to runtime team to review QoS scheduler.

Rollback criteria and procedure
- Rollback triggers:
  - Sustained PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (>= 95%) for 15m despite mitigations.
  - SLO regressions: p99 increase > 15% for any priority-1 tenant for 10m.
- Procedure:
  1) kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
  2) POST to Budget Ledger to clear queued prefetch entries: curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/clear_queues
  3) Run verification job: /tools/smoke/llm_latency_check.sh --routes=critical

Open questions and follow-ups
- Long-term: should hints be persisted as part of model metadata for better lifecycle tracking?
- Research: quantify cost/latency tradeoffs for multi-token speculative warms (we currently use max_tokens=8 as heuristic).
- Observability: add correlation ids from client SDK through HPS to Prefetch Controller for 100% traceability.

Appendix: example hint formats and sample logs
- Header example (HTTP):
  X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=banner-home-v2;priority=med;ttl=30s;signature=abcd1234

- HPS API example request JSON:
  {
    "hint_id": "banner-home-v2",
    "tenant": "acme-corp",
    "recent_templates": ["Welcome v2", "Promo slot"],
    "samples": 3
  }

- Sample Prefetch Controller log excerpt:
  [2026-02-14T09:12:03Z] PREFETCH_DECISION tenant=acme-corp hint=banner-home-v2 action=local_prefetch tokens=24 hit_est=0.62 cost_est=18ms_consume node=rt-12

Contact and ownership
- Primary owner: eng-platform (Ava Chen)
- Runtime integration: eng-serving-runtime (Miguel Santos)
- HPS and budgeting: eng-infra (Priya Kapoor)

Change history
- 2025-11-10: Draft authored and internal review started.
- 2025-12-02: Added telemetry contract and operator knobs after load test.
