source.title: Operational Scenarios Run Matrix — Platform Team Playbook
source.author: Maya Thompson
source.body:
Overview:
This page provides a prescriptive, scenario-driven run matrix for platform engineers and on-call responders. It consolidates detection signals, immediate mitigation steps, service owners, dashboard panels to inspect, and escalation criteria for the most frequent operational scenarios we observe across Redwood’s serving runtime and platform stack.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: Reduce mean time to mitigation (MTTM) by giving responders a short, prioritized checklist tailored to each scenario.
- Scope: Platform control plane, serving runtime (GPU pools, batching layer, KV/prefix cache), routing and queuing, observability and billing pipelines. Does NOT cover application-level business logic outages (see customer-facing product runbooks).
- Audience: Platform SREs, runtime engineers, on-call rotations, incident commanders.

Owners and contact matrix:
- Platform SRE (pager): oncall-platform@redwood.com
- Serving Runtime (pager): oncall-runtime@redwood.com
- Capacity/Autoscale: capacity-ops@redwood.com
- Escalation chain: oncall -> senior oncall -> runtime lead -> engineering manager -> incident commander (IC)

How to use this matrix:
1) Identify the scenario by matching the top-level trigger (alerts or user reports).
2) Follow the Immediate Triage checklist for the scenario verbatim where possible.
3) Run the short investigation queries listed. If the problem is not resolved within the Fast Mitigation Window, escalate per escalation thresholds.
4) For any mitigation that alters routing, capacity, or model variants, record the change in the incident timeline and notify product stakeholders.

Scenario Matrix (summary table):
| Scenario ID | Trigger (typical alert) | Primary owner | Fast mitigation (first 10 min) | Escalation threshold | Dashboard panels to inspect |
| S-01 | Tenant foreground tail latency > 2x SLO for > 3m | Platform SRE | 1) Check per-tenant SLI panels 2) Validate batching queue length 3) Failover to lower-latency model variant for impacted tenant | If latency > 2x SLO for 15m or > 5% request error rate -> escalate to runtime lead | latency-by-tenant, batching-queue-depth, model-variant-calls |
| S-02 | GPU pool eviction or preemption rate > 1% /min | GPU Fleet Team | 1) Pause new allocations to affected pool 2) Drain and reprovision nodes from burst pool 3) Switch high-priority tenants to reserved pools | If preemptions continue after 10m or capacity drop > 10% -> escalate to capacity-ops | gpu-pool-health, eviction-events, tenant-placement |
| S-03 | KV/prefix cache miss ratio spike (cache hit rate drop > 20%) | Serving Runtime | 1) Inspect cache hit heatmap 2) Temporarily increase cache memory for hot-shard 3) Enable prefix-caching fallback for prioritized routes | If hit-rate recovers < 10% within 5m -> escalate to runtime lead | kv-cache-hitrate, prefix-cache-errors, request-latency-by-route |
| S-04 | Routing controller error budget burn (excess 5xx count) | Platform SRE | 1) Validate routing policy logs 2) Toggle safe-mode routing (prefer fallback variants) 3) Throttle non-critical background workloads | If 5xx persists > 10m or error budget > 40% -> escalate to oncall-runtime | routing-controller-errors, 5xx-rate-by-route, traffic-split-overview |
| S-05 | Cost anomaly: token spend spike > 3x baseline for a tenant | Finance + Platform | 1) Freeze high-cost tenant routes (soft block) 2) Switch tenant to cheaper model-quantization profile 3) Notify customer-success for billing hold | If unusual spend continues > 30min or affects multiple tenants -> notify finance and escalate to manager | token-cost-by-tenant, model-profile-usage, billing-alerts |

Detailed scenario playbook: S-03 KV/prefix cache miss spike
- Detection signals:
  - Grafana alert: kv_prefix_cache_hitrate_drop (threshold: 20% relative drop sustained > 2m)
  - An uptick in per-request end-to-end latency for short prompts (P95 increase)

- Fast triage (0–10 minutes):
  1) Open dashboards: kv-cache-hitrate (panel), prefix-cache-errors (panel), request-latency-by-route (panel).
  2) Run the query (PromQL):
     sum(rate(kv_cache_hits_total[1m])) / (sum(rate(kv_cache_hits_total[1m])) + sum(rate(kv_cache_misses_total[1m])))
  3) Identify hot shards: top 5 shard IDs by miss rate.
  4) Apply temporary cache increase for shard X (example CLI):
     rctl cache set --shard X --memory 12GB --ttl 600s
  5) If memory change not available, enable prefix fallbacks for critical routes:
     rctl routing set --route <route-id> --prefix-cache-fallback enabled

- Fast mitigation window (10–30 minutes):
  - Monitor for hit-rate recovery to within 5% of baseline. If recovered, keep changes and create a short follow-up ticket to analyze root cause.
  - If not recovered, snapshot cache eviction metrics and escalate.

- Post-incident remediation checklist:
1) Revert temporary cache allocations if they cause cross-tenant memory pressure.
2) Record the shard IDs and root cause hypothesis in incident notes.
3) Open a follow-up action: investigate prefix miss patterns, add targeted caching for long-tail prompt prefixes, and consider quota adjustments for noisy tenants.

Escalation and decision rules (quick reference):
- Escalate to runtime lead when: any S-01/S-03/S-04 persists past defined time window or multiple independent SLOs are failing concurrently.
- Declare SEV-1 when: global P99 latency > 3x SLO AND > 20% tenant impact simultaneously.
- Use conservative rollbacks: any routing change that increases global token cost by > 15% requires manager approval.

Operational knobs, quick commands and safety table:
| Action | CLI (example) | Safety check |
| Increase per-shard cache | rctl cache set --shard <id> --memory <GB> | Verify node free memory > 20% and cross-tenant memory pressure |
| Failover to fallback model | rctl model route --route <id> --fallback-model <id> | Confirm fallback model latency profile in model-catalog dashboard |
| Pause allocations to pool | rctl pool pause --pool <name> | Ensure reserved pool capacity >= expected burst |
| Toggle routing safe-mode | rctl routing safe-mode enable| Check circuit-breaker counts and ensure incremental rollout |

Run example: resolving S-01 (tenant tail latency) — quick play
1) Confirm alert and identify affected tenant IDs from alert payload.
2) Check batching queue depth and batch latency for tenant:
   - PromQL example: sum(rate(batch_process_latency_seconds_count{tenant="<id>"}[2m]))
3) If queue depth > 2x baseline, reduce batching window for that tenant only:
   rctl batching set --tenant <id> --max-delay-ms 10 --min-batch 1
4) If batching change insufficient, enable model variant fallback for tenant:
   rctl model variant set --tenant <id> --route <route> --variant low-latency-v2
5) Notify customer-success and add incident note with all enacted changes.

Dashboards and alert hygiene recommendations:
- Minimum panels for each incident response board: global latency heatmap, top 20 tenant latency, batching queue depth by tenant, GPU pool utilization, KV cache hit heatmap, 5xx rate by route, token cost over time.
- Alerting principles: 1) Use noise-reduced thresholds (short windows + anomaly detection) 2) Correlate cost and latency alerts 3) Prefer per-tenant alerts for top-50 tenants, aggregated alerts for the long tail.

Retrospective triggers (when to open an RCA):
- Any SEV-1 in the platform domain.
- Repeated S-03 events affecting same shard more than twice in 14 days.
- Cost anomalies > 2x baseline sustained for > 24 hours.

Related runbooks and references:
- Cold start warmup orchestration runbook (link above)
- Token cost attribution playbook (billing team)
- Batching and queueing design doc (internal model-serving standard)

Appendix: sample incident timeline template (use in incident notes)
1) Detection time (UTC)
2) Alert source and query
3) First mitigation action and actor
4) State at 10m (metrics snapshot)
5) Escalations made and times
6) Resolution action and time
7) Postmortem owner and target due date

Contact and escalation quick list:
- Pager: oncall-platform@redwood.com
- Runtime slack channel: #runtime-oncall (urgent threads only)
- Capacity ops: capacity-ops@redwood.com

Document maintenance:
- Owners: Platform SRE (primary), Serving Runtime (secondary).
- Review cadence: quarterly or after any SEV-1.
- Change log: 2025-11-10 (created by Maya Thompson), 2026-02-14 (expanded S-03 playbook, added cost anomaly row).

End of document.

title: Operational Scenarios Run Matrix — Platform Team Playbook

Overview:
This page provides a prescriptive, scenario-driven run matrix for platform engineers and on-call responders. It consolidates detection signals, immediate mitigation steps, service owners, dashboard panels to inspect, and escalation criteria for the most frequent operational scenarios we observe across Redwood’s serving runtime and platform stack.

Purpose and scope:
- Purpose: Reduce mean time to mitigation (MTTM) by giving responders a short, prioritized checklist tailored to each scenario.
- Scope: Platform control plane, serving runtime (GPU pools, batching layer, KV/prefix cache), routing and queuing, observability and billing pipelines. Does NOT cover application-level business logic outages (see customer-facing product runbooks).
- Audience: Platform SREs, runtime engineers, on-call rotations, incident commanders.

Owners and contact matrix:
- Platform SRE (pager): oncall-platform@redwood.com
- Serving Runtime (pager): oncall-runtime@redwood.com
- Capacity/Autoscale: capacity-ops@redwood.com
- Escalation chain: oncall -> senior oncall -> runtime lead -> engineering manager -> incident commander (IC)

How to use this matrix:
1) Identify the scenario by matching the top-level trigger (alerts or user reports).
2) Follow the Immediate Triage checklist for the scenario verbatim where possible.
3) Run the short investigation queries listed. If the problem is not resolved within the Fast Mitigation Window, escalate per escalation thresholds.
4) For any mitigation that alters routing, capacity, or model variants, record the change in the incident timeline and notify product stakeholders.

source.title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-02-13)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
# Summary
Console preview is now powered by the control plane simulation endpoint and we are back on track for the beta cohort kickoff.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Control plane: shipped simulate_policy_v1 for a single route + region (latency p95 45ms).
- Console: integrated live preview with clear warnings for unsupported cases (multi-region, multiple models).
- Beta: solutions team completed a dry run with two design partners and captured feedback.

# Risks / blockers
- Multi-region simulation remains unscoped; could be requested by beta customers.

# Next week
- Add basic multi-region support to simulation (best-effort) or document a limitation.
- Publish beta onboarding doc and track adoption metrics.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (control plane), Maya Srinivasan (console), Jordan Blake (solutions), Rishi Malhotra (customer success)

source.title: Perf-canary gates for hardware tuning profiles (H100/B200/L40S)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **perf-canary gates** and **dashboards** used to validate the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack during:
1) **Pre-merge (CI)**: prevent known regressions from landing.
2) **Post-deploy (staged rollout)**: catch production-only failures (fleet variance, noisy neighbors, real traffic mix) and provide fast rollback signals.

The gates are designed to be:
- **Safe-by-default**: strict on correctness/stability and tail latency.
- **Measurable and repeatable**: pinned workload matrix + standardized summary artifact.
- **Profile-aware**: thresholds differ by profile intent (e.g., H100 balanced vs throughput-first; B200 experimental).

> Wording note: these are **engineering validation gates** and define “expected behavior” for rollouts. They are not customer SLAs.

---

## Where these gates run
### Pre-merge (CI)
- System: **perf-canary**
- Trigger:
  - Required on PRs that touch: scheduler defaults, kernel selection, profile pack schema/loader, KV-cache budgets, allocator/memory guardrails.
  - Optional/manual for doc-only or non-runtime changes.
- Runner pools:
  - **H100**: standard perf-canary GPU runners.
  - **L40S**: dedicated L40S runner queue (known to be more variable; see “Noise handling”).
  - **B200**: limited availability; gate behavior depends on runner availability (see “B200 experimental”).
- Output artifacts:
  - `perf_summary.json` (normalized metrics + pass/fail per gate)
  - `workload_matrix.csv` (per workload bucket metrics)
  - `runtime_startup.log` (profile selection + compatibility matrix)

### Post-deploy (staged rollout)
- Environments:
  - **Hosted**: canary pools by region, then staged rollout.
  - **Dedicated**: staging pools, then select customer pools (opt-in / low-risk).
  - **Private**: not auto-deployed; signals come from support escalations + customer-run validation using the same dashboard views.
- Comparison mode:
  - **Control vs treatment pools** (same model versions and routing policies; only profile pack version or profile toggle differs).

---

## Workload matrix (what the canary runs)
The canary enumerates a fixed workload matrix (owned by Benchmarks: Rina Kobayashi / Claire Dubois). See the source-of-truth page: *Workload matrix for hardware profiles*.

At a high level, each GPU SKU runs:
- **Model families**: Llama-class (chat), Mistral-class (chat), and one “long-context” representative.
- **Sequence buckets**:
  - Short: prefill 128–512 tokens, decode 128–512 tokens
  - Medium: prefill 1k–2k, decode 512–1k
  - Long-context: prefill 8k–16k, decode 256–1k
- **Concurrency**: low (latency), medium (balanced), high (throughput).
- **Modes**: streaming and non-streaming.

Important: thresholds below are applied to **bucketed results** and to the **aggregate scorecard** (to avoid “average hides tail” failures).

---

## Metrics used by gates (definitions)
The gates are evaluated on both absolute correctness and relative regressions vs a baseline.

### Latency
- **TTFT p50/p95**: time-to-first-token for streaming requests.
- **E2E latency p50/p95**: end-to-end request latency for non-streaming requests.
- **TPOT p50/p95**: time-per-output-token during decode (proxy for steady-state decode speed).

### Throughput / efficiency
- **Decode tokens/sec (aggregate)**: steady-state decode throughput at the specified concurrency.
- **GPU-ms/token (proxy cost)**: GPU time normalized by output tokens (lower is better). For CI we use runtime-exported GPU timing; in prod we use sampled tracing spans.

### Stability / correctness
- **OOM count**: number of OOMs or allocator hard failures.
- **Crash count**: runtime crash / restart in the job window.
- **5xx rate**: request-level errors.
- **Timeout rate**: request timeout or forced cancellation.

### Scheduler health (guardrails)
- **Queue wait p95**: time spent waiting for a scheduling slot.
- **Backpressure events**: rate limiting / queue-full drops (should be zero in perf jobs).
- **Prefill/decode fairness**: heuristic score derived from tail latency skew between prefill and decode under mixed loads.

### Profile selection correctness
- **Selected profile** (`runtime_selected_profile`) and **override source** (`runtime_profile_override_source`) must match expected values for the test.

---

## Gate policy: blocking vs non-blocking
### Blocking (must pass to merge / proceed)
- OOMs, crashes, correctness errors.
- Latency regressions beyond profile-specific bounds.
- Severe throughput regressions beyond bounds.

### Non-blocking (warn-only)
- B200 perf deltas when runner availability is limited and confidence is low.
- Known flaky metrics on L40S runners when variance is above the noise threshold (still blocks on OOM/crash).

---

## Thresholds by profile (CI gates)
All thresholds below are evaluated as **relative change** versus baseline build on the same runner class.

Baseline selection:
- CI compares the PR build to the most recent `main` build artifact on the same commit window.
- Each bucket uses median of **N=3** runs (unless otherwise specified).

### H100 profile (balanced) — blocking
Intended use: general Hosted/Dedicated pools where tail latency matters.

Pass criteria (all must hold):
- **Stability**
  - OOM count = 0
  - Crash count = 0
  - 5xx rate <= +0.10% absolute (and <= 0.20% total)
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p50: <= **+5%**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+8%**
  - E2E p95 (non-streaming): <= **+10%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **-2%** (no meaningful regressions)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **+3%**
- **Scheduler health**
  - Queue wait p95: <= **+10%**
  - Backpressure drops: must remain **0** in perf jobs

### H100 profile (throughput-first) — blocking with different bounds
Intended use: internal “throughput pools” / batch-heavy workloads.

Pass criteria:
- **Stability**: same as H100 balanced
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+15%** (explicit trade-off)
  - E2E p95: <= **+15%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **+8%** (must improve)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **-5%** (must improve)

### L40S profile — blocking with memory guardrails
Intended use: cost-sensitive workloads; tighter memory envelope.

Pass criteria:
- **Stability (strict)**
  - OOM count = 0 (any OOM is a hard fail)
  - Crash count = 0
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+10%** (short/medium buckets)
  - Long-context bucket TTFT p95: <= **+15%** (variance tolerated, but must not spike)
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **-5%**
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **+5%**
- **Memory/allocator health**
  - Peak reserved GPU memory must stay within the configured budget (reported as `kv_cache_budget_enforced=true` and `runtime_mem_budget_violation_count=0`).

### B200 profile (experimental) — mixed policy
Intended use: limited fleet; gated behind feature flag; explicit fallback required.

Gate behavior:
- If **B200 runners available**: blocking on stability, softer on perf.
- If **no B200 runners available**: non-blocking; job reports “skipped” and requires manual sign-off during rollout.

Pass criteria when runners available:
- **Stability**
  - OOM count = 0
  - Crash count = 0
  - 5xx rate <= +0.10% absolute (and <= 0.25% total)
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+12%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **0%** (no regressions)

Additional required checks:
- Runtime logs must confirm driver/CUDA compatibility checks passed (startup line contains `b200_profile_compat=OK`).

---

## Post-deploy rollout gates (production / staged rollout)
Production gates are evaluated over **15-minute windows** per pool/region and compared to the control pool.

### Rollout dashboards (Grafana)
- **Perf Canary – Hardware Profiles Overview**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/perf-hwprofiles/overview
  - Use this for a single “go/no-go” view across pools.
- **Runtime – Profile Selection & Overrides**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-profiles/selection
  - Confirms which profile was selected, and whether an override was applied.
- **Runtime – Tail Latency (TTFT/TPOT) by Workload Bucket**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-latency/ttft-tpot
- **Runtime – OOM / Allocator / KV Cache Budgeting**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-memory/kv-cache
- **Scheduler – Queueing & Fairness**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-scheduler/health

### Production rollback thresholds (default)
Unless a rollout plan specifies stricter criteria, rollback is required if any of the following are true in treatment vs control:
- **p95 TTFT**: > **+15%** for 2 consecutive 15-min windows
- **p95 E2E latency**: > **+15%** for 2 consecutive 15-min windows
- **5xx rate**: > **+0.25% absolute** (or > **2x** control) for 15 minutes
- **OOMs**: any sustained increase (>= 1 OOM event in a 15-min window) in a pool with otherwise stable traffic
- **Restart rate**: > **2x** control

B200 experimental additional rule:
- If p95 TTFT > **+10%** for 1 window **or** any unexplained error spike occurs, disable B200 profile in that pool and fall back to H100-equivalent baseline behavior (per rollout runbook).

Alert routing:
- Alerts page: **#eng-runtime-canary** (primary), **#eng-sre** (secondary)
- On-call escalation: Runtime oncall → SRE oncall if rollback is required or incident declared.

---

## How to interpret failures (triage checklist)
When a gate fails, follow this order:
1) **Verify profile selection**
   - Dashboard: *Runtime – Profile Selection & Overrides*
   - Confirm `runtime_selected_profile` matches expectation (e.g., `h100-balanced-vX.Y`).
   - Confirm override source (`fleet_default`, `helm_values`, `env_override`, `per_pool_override`).
2) **Check stability first**
   - Any OOM/crash: treat as a release blocker unless clearly explained by a known runner issue.
3) **Bucket-level diagnosis**
   - Determine which bucket regressed (long-context vs short; high concurrency vs low; streaming vs non-streaming).
4) **Look for known signatures**
   - Prefill/decode fairness regressions: spikes in TTFT p95 with stable TPOT.
   - Memory fragmentation/KV cache: increasing reserved memory with stable allocated memory; budget enforcement toggles missing.
   - Kernel mismatch: throughput drop isolated to one seq-len bucket.
5) **Decide action**
   - If CI: rerun once only if runner instability is suspected and no code changes happened; otherwise fix forward.
   - If prod: follow rollout runbook; disable profile per pool if rollback thresholds are met.

---

## Noise handling and rerun policy
Perf canaries are inherently noisy; we constrain noise with:
- Warm-up runs excluded from scoring.
- Median-of-3 aggregation in CI.
- Dedicated runner pools where possible.

Rerun policy:
- **Allowed**: one rerun if failure is within 2% of threshold and there is evidence of runner instability (e.g., concurrent host load, runner health alerts).
- **Not allowed**: rerun to “fish” for a pass when failure is large or consistent across buckets.

Known noisy area:
- L40S long-context bucket has higher variance. We still treat **OOMs/crashes as hard fails**.

---

## Metrics emitted (names used in dashboards)
Key time series (Prometheus-style):
- `runtime_selected_profile{pool,region,gpu_sku}`
- `runtime_profile_override_source{source}`
- `runtime_request_ttft_ms_bucket{workload_bucket,streaming}`
- `runtime_request_latency_ms_bucket{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_decode_tpot_ms_bucket{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_decode_tokens_per_sec{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_gpu_ms_per_token{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_oom_total{reason}`
- `runtime_restarts_total`
- `runtime_http_5xx_total`
- `scheduler_queue_wait_ms_bucket{queue}`
- `runtime_mem_budget_violation_total{type}`

If you add/rename metrics, update:
- This page
- Dashboard queries
- Alert rules in the observability pack

---

## Change management
Any change to thresholds or gate logic requires:
- Review from **Benchmarks (Claire Dubois or Rina Kobayashi)** and **SRE (Sean Gallagher or Allison Grant)**.
- A short note in the PR describing:
  - Why the threshold changed
  - Which profile(s) it impacts
  - Expected trade-off (throughput vs tail)

---

## Appendix: Quick links
- Rollout runbook: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/rollout-runbook-hardware-tuning-profiles
- Known issues: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/known-issues/hardware-profile-known-issues-and-mitigations
- Workload matrix: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/workload-matrix-for-hardware-profiles

Owners:
- Dashboards/alerts: Aisha Bello (Eng Platform)
- Perf regression tooling: Kira Volkov
- Workload methodology: Claire Dubois

title: Perf-canary gates for hardware tuning profiles (H100/B200/L40S)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the **perf-canary gates** and **dashboards** used to validate the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack during:
1) **Pre-merge (CI)**: prevent known regressions from landing.
2) **Post-deploy (staged rollout)**: catch production-only failures (fleet variance, noisy neighbors, real traffic mix) and provide fast rollback signals.

The gates are designed to be:
- **Safe-by-default**: strict on correctness/stability and tail latency.
- **Measurable and repeatable**: pinned workload matrix + standardized summary artifact.
- **Profile-aware**: thresholds differ by profile intent (e.g., H100 balanced vs throughput-first; B200 experimental).

> Wording note: these are **engineering validation gates** and define “expected behavior” for rollouts. They are not customer SLAs.

---

## Where these gates run
### Pre-merge (CI)
- System: **perf-canary**
- Trigger:
  - Required on PRs that touch: scheduler defaults, kernel selection, profile pack schema/loader, KV-cache budgets, allocator/memory guardrails.
  - Optional/manual for doc-only or non-runtime changes.
- Runner pools:
  - **H100**: standard perf-canary GPU runners.
  - **L40S**: dedicated L40S runner queue (known to be more variable; see “Noise handling”).
  - **B200**: limited availability; gate behavior depends on runner availability (see “B200 experimental”).
- Output artifacts:
  - `perf_summary.json` (normalized metrics + pass/fail per gate)
  - `workload_matrix.csv` (per workload bucket metrics)
  - `runtime_startup.log` (profile selection + compatibility matrix)

### Post-deploy (staged rollout)
- Environments:
  - **Hosted**: canary pools by region, then staged rollout.
  - **Dedicated**: staging pools, then select customer pools (opt-in / low-risk).
  - **Private**: not auto-deployed; signals come from support escalations + customer-run validation using the same dashboard views.
- Comparison mode:
  - **Control vs treatment pools** (same model versions and routing policies; only profile pack version or profile toggle differs).

---

## Workload matrix (what the canary runs)
The canary enumerates a fixed workload matrix (owned by Benchmarks: Rina Kobayashi / Claire Dubois). See the source-of-truth page: *Workload matrix for hardware profiles*.

At a high level, each GPU SKU runs:
- **Model families**: Llama-class (chat), Mistral-class (chat), and one “long-context” representative.
- **Sequence buckets**:
  - Short: prefill 128–512 tokens, decode 128–512 tokens
  - Medium: prefill 1k–2k, decode 512–1k
  - Long-context: prefill 8k–16k, decode 256–1k
- **Concurrency**: low (latency), medium (balanced), high (throughput).
- **Modes**: streaming and non-streaming.

Important: thresholds below are applied to **bucketed results** and to the **aggregate scorecard** (to avoid “average hides tail” failures).

---

## Metrics used by gates (definitions)
The gates are evaluated on both absolute correctness and relative regressions vs a baseline.

- **TTFT p50/p95**: time-to-first-token for streaming requests.
- **E2E latency p50/p95**: end-to-end request latency for non-streaming requests.
- **TPOT p50/p95**: time-per-output-token during decode (proxy for steady-state decode speed).

### Throughput / efficiency
- **Decode tokens/sec (aggregate)**: steady-state decode throughput at the specified concurrency.
- **GPU-ms/token (proxy cost)**: GPU time normalized by output tokens (lower is better). For CI we use runtime-exported GPU timing; in prod we use sampled tracing spans.

### Stability / correctness
- **OOM count**: number of OOMs or allocator hard failures.
- **Crash count**: runtime crash / restart in the job window.
- **5xx rate**: request-level errors.
- **Timeout rate**: request timeout or forced cancellation.

### Scheduler health (guardrails)

- **Backpressure events**: rate limiting / queue-full drops (should be zero in perf jobs).
- **Prefill/decode fairness**: heuristic score derived from tail latency skew between prefill and decode under mixed loads.

### Profile selection correctness
- **Selected profile** (`runtime_selected_profile`) and **override source** (`runtime_profile_override_source`) must match expected values for the test.

---

### Blocking (must pass to merge / proceed)
- OOMs, crashes, correctness errors.
- Latency regressions beyond profile-specific bounds.
- Severe throughput regressions beyond bounds.

### Non-blocking (warn-only)
- B200 perf deltas when runner availability is limited and confidence is low.
- Known flaky metrics on L40S runners when variance is above the noise threshold (still blocks on OOM/crash).

- Each bucket uses median of **N=3** runs (unless otherwise specified).

- **Stability**
  - OOM count = 0
  - Crash count = 0
  - 5xx rate <= +0.10% absolute (and <= 0.20% total)
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p50: <= **+5%**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+8%**
  - E2E p95 (non-streaming): <= **+10%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **-2%** (no meaningful regressions)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **+3%**
- **Scheduler health**
  - Queue wait p95: <= **+10%**
  - Backpressure drops: must remain **0** in perf jobs

### H100 profile (throughput-first) — blocking with different bounds

  - TTFT p95: <= **+15%** (explicit trade-off)
  - E2E p95: <= **+15%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **+8%** (must improve)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **-5%** (must improve)

  - TTFT p95: <= **+8%**
  - E2E p95 (non-streaming): <= **+10%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **-2%** (no meaningful regressions)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **+3%**
- **Scheduler health**
  - Queue wait p95: <= **+10%**
  - Backpressure drops: must remain **0** in perf jobs

### H100 profile (throughput-first) — blocking with different bounds
Intended use: internal “throughput pools” / batch-heavy workloads.

Pass criteria:
- **Stability**: same as H100 balanced
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+15%** (explicit trade-off)
  - E2E p95: <= **+15%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **+8%** (must improve)
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **-5%** (must improve)

### L40S profile — blocking with memory guardrails
Intended use: cost-sensitive workloads; tighter memory envelope.

Pass criteria:
- **Stability (strict)**
  - OOM count = 0 (any OOM is a hard fail)
  - Crash count = 0
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+10%** (short/medium buckets)
  - Long-context bucket TTFT p95: <= **+15%** (variance tolerated, but must not spike)
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **-5%**
  - GPU-ms/token: <= **+5%**
- **Memory/allocator health**
  - Peak reserved GPU memory must stay within the configured budget (reported as `kv_cache_budget_enforced=true` and `runtime_mem_budget_violation_count=0`).

### B200 profile (experimental) — mixed policy
Intended use: limited fleet; gated behind feature flag; explicit fallback required.

Gate behavior:
- If **B200 runners available**: blocking on stability, softer on perf.
- If **no B200 runners available**: non-blocking; job reports “skipped” and requires manual sign-off during rollout.

Pass criteria when runners available:
- **Stability**
  - OOM count = 0
  - Crash count = 0
  - 5xx rate <= +0.10% absolute (and <= 0.25% total)
- **Latency**
  - TTFT p95: <= **+12%**
- **Throughput/Efficiency**
  - Decode tokens/sec: >= **0%** (no regressions)

Additional required checks:
- Runtime logs must confirm driver/CUDA compatibility checks passed (startup line contains `b200_profile_compat=OK`).

---

## Post-deploy rollout gates (production / staged rollout)
Production gates are evaluated over **15-minute windows** per pool/region and compared to the control pool.

### Rollout dashboards (Grafana)
- **Perf Canary – Hardware Profiles Overview**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/perf-hwprofiles/overview
  - Use this for a single “go/no-go” view across pools.
- **Runtime – Profile Selection & Overrides**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-profiles/selection
  - Confirms which profile was selected, and whether an override was applied.
- **Runtime – Tail Latency (TTFT/TPOT) by Workload Bucket**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-latency/ttft-tpot
- **Runtime – OOM / Allocator / KV Cache Budgeting**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-memory/kv-cache
- **Scheduler – Queueing & Fairness**
  - https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/runtime-scheduler/health

### Production rollback thresholds (default)
Unless a rollout plan specifies stricter criteria, rollback is required if any of the following are true in treatment vs control:
- **p95 TTFT**: > **+15%** for 2 consecutive 15-min windows
- **p95 E2E latency**: > **+15%** for 2 consecutive 15-min windows
- **5xx rate**: > **+0.25% absolute** (or > **2x** control) for 15 minutes
- **OOMs**: any sustained increase (>= 1 OOM event in a 15-min window) in a pool with otherwise stable traffic
- **Restart rate**: > **2x** control

B200 experimental additional rule:
- If p95 TTFT > **+10%** for 1 window **or** any unexplained error spike occurs, disable B200 profile in that pool and fall back to H100-equivalent baseline behavior (per rollout runbook).

Alert routing:
- Alerts page: **#eng-runtime-canary** (primary), **#eng-sre** (secondary)
- On-call escalation: Runtime oncall → SRE oncall if rollback is required or incident declared.

---

## How to interpret failures (triage checklist)
When a gate fails, follow this order:
1) **Verify profile selection**
   - Dashboard: *Runtime – Profile Selection & Overrides*
   - Confirm `runtime_selected_profile` matches expectation (e.g., `h100-balanced-vX.Y`).
   - Confirm override source (`fleet_default`, `helm_values`, `env_override`, `per_pool_override`).
2) **Check stability first**
   - Any OOM/crash: treat as a release blocker unless clearly explained by a known runner issue.
3) **Bucket-level diagnosis**
   - Determine which bucket regressed (long-context vs short; high concurrency vs low; streaming vs non-streaming).
4) **Look for known signatures**
   - Prefill/decode fairness regressions: spikes in TTFT p95 with stable TPOT.
   - Memory fragmentation/KV cache: increasing reserved memory with stable allocated memory; budget enforcement toggles missing.
   - Kernel mismatch: throughput drop isolated to one seq-len bucket.
5) **Decide action**
   - If CI: rerun once only if runner instability is suspected and no code changes happened; otherwise fix forward.
   - If prod: follow rollout runbook; disable profile per pool if rollback thresholds are met.

---

## Noise handling and rerun policy
Perf canaries are inherently noisy; we constrain noise with:
- Warm-up runs excluded from scoring.
- Median-of-3 aggregation in CI.
- Dedicated runner pools where possible.

Rerun policy:
- **Allowed**: one rerun if failure is within 2% of threshold and there is evidence of runner instability (e.g., concurrent host load, runner health alerts).
- **Not allowed**: rerun to “fish” for a pass when failure is large or consistent across buckets.

Known noisy area:
- L40S long-context bucket has higher variance. We still treat **OOMs/crashes as hard fails**.

---

## Metrics emitted (names used in dashboards)
Key time series (Prometheus-style):
- `runtime_selected_profile{pool,region,gpu_sku}`
- `runtime_profile_override_source{source}`
- `runtime_request_ttft_ms_bucket{workload_bucket,streaming}`
- `runtime_request_latency_ms_bucket{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_decode_tpot_ms_bucket{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_decode_tokens_per_sec{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_gpu_ms_per_token{workload_bucket}`
- `runtime_oom_total{reason}`
- `runtime_restarts_total`
- `runtime_http_5xx_total`
- `scheduler_queue_wait_ms_bucket{queue}`
- `runtime_mem_budget_violation_total{type}`

If you add/rename metrics, update:
- This page
- Dashboard queries
- Alert rules in the observability pack

---

## Change management
Any change to thresholds or gate logic requires:
- Review from **Benchmarks (Claire Dubois or Rina Kobayashi)** and **SRE (Sean Gallagher or Allison Grant)**.
- A short note in the PR describing:
  - Why the threshold changed
  - Which profile(s) it impacts
  - Expected trade-off (throughput vs tail)

---

## Appendix: Quick links
- Rollout runbook: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/rollout-runbook-hardware-tuning-profiles
- Known issues: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/known-issues/hardware-profile-known-issues-and-mitigations
- Workload matrix: confluence://eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/workload-matrix-for-hardware-profiles

Owners:
- Dashboards/alerts: Aisha Bello (Eng Platform)
- Perf regression tooling: Kira Volkov
- Workload methodology: Claire Dubois

source.title: Demo Tenant Reset Runbook (GTM Sales Demos)
source.author: Devon Price
source.body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the standard, repeatable procedure to reset the Redwood **sales demo tenant** (“demo org”) to a known-good state.

A correct reset accomplishes all of the following:
- Resets mutable state (test projects/routes/evals, demo app artifacts) to the standard baseline.
- Re-seeds **known-good demo scenarios** (projects, routes, prompt sets, eval fixtures).
- Rotates demo API keys (least-privilege, audited) and safely revokes old keys.
- Re-populates Console dashboards via synthetic traffic (cost/latency/errors/rollouts) so demos don’t start with empty graphs.
- Validates demo route **model pins** and **safe fallback policy**.

This procedure is designed to be fast (minutes) and safe (guardrails prevent impacting non-demo tenants).

---

## Audience
- Sales Engineering (SE) running live demos
- Eng Platform on-call supporting demo enablement
- SRE/Infra responders for capacity, quota, and incident escalation

If you are an AE: do not run resets. Ask the assigned SE or #demo-oncall.

---

## Ownership and support
- Demo technical owner: **Markus Klein**
- Provision/reset automation: **Devon Price**
- Demo data pipelines (synthetic traffic + seeds): **Mei Lin**
- Control plane/auth (RBAC + key scopes): **Ethan Park**
- SRE escalation: **Sean Gallagher**
- Alerts/observability: **Allison Grant**

Slack:
- Primary: **#demo-oncall** (pager-backed during business hours)
- Secondary: **#eng-platform**
- Incidents: **#eng-sre**

---

## Preconditions
You must have all of the following before running a reset:
- Membership in the demo org with the role: `DemoTenantAdmin`
- Access to the internal CLI (`rwctl`) and the demo control plane environment
- Access to the secrets manager entry for demo keys (Vault/1Password) as `demo-secrets-writer`

Required tools:
- `rwctl` v0.34.0+ (pinned for demo stability)
- `curl` (or Postman) for smoke tests

---

## Definitions and identifiers (do not change)
These identifiers are referenced by automation, dashboards, and training materials.

- Org slug: `redwood-demo`
- Primary demo project (default): `demo-core`
- Parallel demo projects (namespaces): `demo-01` … `demo-05`
- Synthetic traffic project: `demo-telemetry`
- Environment tag: `env=demo`
- Regions used in demos:
  - Primary: `us-east-1`
  - Secondary (fallback demo): `us-west-2`
  - Optional EMEA variant: `eu-west-1` (use only with EMEA talk track)

Guardrail: automation refuses to run if `org_slug != redwood-demo`.

---

## Quick start (SE: “I need to demo in 10 minutes”)
If you only have time for the minimum reset, do this:
1) Run the demo healthcheck (baseline):
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format pretty`
2) Run the standard reset:
   - `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard`
3) Start synthetic traffic (15–90 seconds to visible Console activity):
   - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 8m`
4) Confirm the four green checks:
   - API chat streaming works
   - Console cost + latency dashboards show data for the last 15 minutes
   - Rollouts view shows a canary event (synthetic)
   - Model pins report “OK”

If any of the above fails, see **Troubleshooting**.

---

## Expected timing (planning guidance)
| Step | Goal | Typical time | Max time before escalation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Healthcheck baseline | Catch obvious breakage early | 30–60s | 2m |
| Reset (standard) | Clear + recreate baseline | 2–4m | 8m |
| Key rotation (optional) | New keys + revoke old | 3–6m | 12m |
| Seed scenarios | Prompt sets/evals/routes present | 1–3m | 6m |
| Synthetic traffic visible | Dashboards not empty | 1–2m | 5m |
| Smoke tests | End-to-end demo happy path | 2–5m | 10m |

Escalate to #demo-oncall if a step hits “max time”.

---

## Standard reset procedure (recommended)
### Step 0 — Confirm no live demo is in progress
1) In Slack (#sales and #demo-oncall), confirm no one is actively using `redwood-demo`.
2) If uncertain, set the demo lock (prevents concurrent resets and avoids mid-demo data loss):
   - `rwctl demo lock acquire --org redwood-demo --ttl 20m --reason "Reset prior to scheduled demo"`

### Step 1 — Baseline healthcheck
Run:
- `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include quotas,models,telemetry,console --format pretty`

Interpretation:
- **PASS**: proceed
- **WARN**: proceed but note the warning (often dashboard freshness)
- **FAIL**: stop and troubleshoot (common: quotas or model availability)

Save output for audit/debug:
- `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format json > /tmp/demo-healthcheck.json`

### Step 2 — Reset tenant state
Run the standard reset (idempotent):
- `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard --confirm`

What “standard” does:
- Ensures the canonical org/projects exist (`demo-core`, `demo-01..05`, `demo-telemetry`).
- Deletes demo-created ephemeral resources (temporary routes, failed rollout experiments, old eval runs, demo app scratch artifacts).
- Resets rate limits/quotas to the demo defaults.
- Re-applies baseline RBAC mappings for SE access.

Notes:
- Do not use `--mode wipe-all` unless instructed by Eng Platform; it’s slower and usually unnecessary.
- If reset takes >8 minutes, stop and escalate (see **Troubleshooting: reset slow/stuck**).

### Step 3 — Re-seed demo scenarios (known-good)
Run:
- `rwctl demo seed --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1 --confirm`

The seed bundle must include:
- Routes:
  - `chat-streaming` (streaming enabled)
  - `structured-output` (JSON schema constrained)
  - `tool-calling` (function/tool calling)
  - `economics-batching-on` / `economics-batching-off`
  - `reliability-fallback` (forced fallback policy for demo narrative)
- Prompt sets + eval fixtures:
  - `support-agent-quality` (regression-friendly)
  - `retrieval-rerank-smoke` (short, stable)
- Rollout artifacts:
  - A synthetic canary rollout record (used in Console tour)

Validation (should print a summary of counts):
- `rwctl demo seed status --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1`

### Step 4 — Validate model pins + safe fallback policy
Run:
- `rwctl demo models verify --org redwood-demo --routes all`

Expected results:
- Each demo route shows:
  - `Pinned model: <family>@<version>`
  - `Allowed fallbacks: <family variants> in <regions>`
  - `Status: OK`

If any route shows `DRIFT` or `UNAVAILABLE`:
- Do not edit pins manually.
- Escalate to #demo-oncall and reference the ADR on pinning/fallback.

### Step 5 — (Optional but recommended weekly) Rotate demo API keys
Rotate keys:
- `rwctl demo keys rotate --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --ttl 30d --confirm`

Rules:
- Keys must be scoped to demo routes/projects only (least privilege).
- Keys must be stored only in the approved secret location (no pasting into Slack).
- Revoke old keys within the same session unless instructed otherwise.

Post-rotation checklist:
- Update the SE “demo runner” app config to the new key location.
- Re-run smoke tests (Step 7).

### Step 6 — Start synthetic traffic generator (for Console dashboards)
Start the preset generator:
- `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m --qps 1.5`

What it generates (approximate):
- 70% chat streaming
- 15% structured output
- 10% tool calling
- 5% induced errors (to populate error analysis views)

If your next demo is >30 minutes away, use 20 minutes duration so graphs stay warm:
- `--duration 20m`

### Step 7 — Verification smoke tests (API + Console)
#### 7A) API smoke tests (copy/paste)
Set env vars:
- `export RW_DEMO_API_KEY="$(rwctl demo keys read --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --format raw)"`
- `export RW_BASE_URL="https://api.redwood.example"` (use the internal demo endpoint if different)

1) Streaming chat:
- `curl -N -H "Authorization: Bearer $RW_DEMO_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "$RW_BASE_URL/v1/chat/completions" \
  -d '{"route":"chat-streaming","stream":true,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Give me a 2-sentence overview of Redwood Inference."}]}'`

Expected:
- Tokens stream within 1–2 seconds.
- No disconnects.

2) Structured output:
- `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $RW_DEMO_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "$RW_BASE_URL/v1/responses" \
  -d '{"route":"structured-output","input":"Extract: Name=Riley, Plan=Pro, Seats=12","response_format":{"type":"json_schema","json_schema":{"name":"account","schema":{"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"},"plan":{"type":"string"},"seats":{"type":"integer"}},"required":["name","plan","seats"]}}}}'`

Expected:
- Valid JSON with `name`, `plan`, `seats`.

3) Tool calling:
- Run the demo runner app (preferred), or call the route directly if your test harness supports it.
- Expected: model requests the tool with well-formed arguments and the response completes.

#### 7B) Console verification (dashboards)
In Console (Org: `redwood-demo`):
- Switch to project: `demo-telemetry`
- Time range: “Last 15 minutes”

Verify:
- **Latency dashboard** shows p50/p95 lines (not empty).
- **Cost dashboard** shows token breakdown by route.
- **Errors view** shows a small non-zero error count (from synthetic traffic).
- **Rollouts view** shows at least one canary record and both variants visible.

If graphs are empty after 5 minutes, see **Troubleshooting: dashboards empty**.

### Step 8 — Release demo lock
- `rwctl demo lock release --org redwood-demo`

---

## Verification checklist (printable)
Use this list before every customer-facing demo.

**Tenant + access**
- [ ] I can log into Console for `redwood-demo`
- [ ] My role shows as `DemoTenantAdmin` or `DemoTenantOperator`
- [ ] Demo lock is not held by someone else

**API happy path**
- [ ] Streaming chat route returns tokens within 1–2 seconds
- [ ] Structured output route returns valid JSON
- [ ] Tool calling route completes with the expected tool invocation

**Reliability**
- [ ] `rwctl demo models verify` shows `OK` (no drift)
- [ ] Fallback route demonstrates region/model variant fallback without errors

**Economics/controls**
- [ ] Batching/caching routes exist (on/off) and return responses
- [ ] Token-level breakdown appears in Console

**Observability**
- [ ] Latency dashboard shows p50/p95
- [ ] Cost dashboard shows per-route spend
- [ ] Error analysis shows at least one synthetic error
- [ ] Rollouts view has a canary example

**Security sanity**
- [ ] Demo keys are least-privilege and stored in approved secret manager
- [ ] No customer data is present (synthetic only)

---

## Troubleshooting
### A) Reset fails immediately with guardrail error
Symptoms:
- CLI refuses to run with a message like “org is not demo” or “environment guardrail triggered”.

Actions:
- Confirm you used `--org redwood-demo`.
- Confirm your `RW_ENV` is set to `demo` (if applicable).
- Do not bypass guardrails. Escalate to #eng-platform.

### B) 429 throttling during smoke tests or demos
Symptoms:
- API returns `429` or requests stall under load.

Actions:
1) Check quotas:
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include quotas`
2) Reduce synthetic traffic QPS if running:
   - `rwctl demo traffic stop --org redwood-demo`
3) If you have parallel demos, move to an isolated project (`demo-02`, `demo-03`, etc.) and use the matching scoped key.
4) If still 429ing, follow the capacity/quota runbook and escalate to #demo-oncall.

### C) Console dashboards are empty
Symptoms:
- No charts populate (latency/cost/errors) even after reset.

Actions:
1) Confirm synthetic traffic is running:
   - `rwctl demo traffic status --org redwood-demo`
2) Restart traffic generator:
   - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m`
3) Confirm Console is pointed to the correct project (`demo-telemetry`) and time range (“Last 15 minutes”).
4) Check telemetry ingestion via healthcheck:
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include telemetry,console`
5) If ingestion is failing, escalate to #demo-oncall and include `/tmp/demo-healthcheck.json`.

### D) RBAC / SSO role mapping is broken (can’t access org)
Symptoms:
- You can authenticate but can’t see `redwood-demo`, or actions are denied.

Actions:
- Do not attempt manual role edits in Console during a live prep.
- Escalate to #demo-oncall; tag **Ethan Park**.
- Workaround (SE only): switch to backup screenshots/recording while access is restored.

### E) Model pin drift or pinned model unavailable
Symptoms:
- `rwctl demo models verify` shows `DRIFT` or `UNAVAILABLE`.

Actions:
1) Re-run seed status:
   - `rwctl demo seed status --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1`
2) Do not “just pick a new model” in Console; this destabilizes the talk track and expected outputs.
3) Escalate to #demo-oncall; tag **Mina Haddad** or **Ravi Menon**.

### F) Streaming disconnects (region-specific)
Symptoms:
- Streaming starts then disconnects mid-response, often in `eu-west-1`.

Actions:
- Switch demo region to `us-east-1` for the live portion.
- Use backup screenshots for the “global regions” narrative.
- File/attach logs via healthcheck output; escalate to #demo-oncall.

### G) Reset is slow or stuck
Symptoms:
- `rwctl demo reset` runs >8 minutes or hangs.

Actions:
1) Check for lock contention:
   - `rwctl demo lock status --org redwood-demo`
2) Cancel and retry once.
3) If repeated, escalate with:
   - CLI output
   - time started
   - healthcheck JSON

---

## Backup mode (when live systems are unhealthy)
If demo environment is degraded and the customer meeting cannot slip:
- Use the latest approved backup recording and annotated screenshots (Sales Enablement package).
- Keep the narrative aligned: API capabilities → production economics → reliability → observability → enterprise/security.
- After the call, open a #demo-oncall thread with what failed and the meeting timestamp.

---

## Change management
- Seed bundle `known-good-v1` is updated **monthly** (or sooner if a demo regression is found).
- Model pin changes require approval from Demo Technical Owner (Markus Klein) and must follow the pinning ADR.
- Any change that affects the talk track must be communicated to Sales Enablement (Soojin Lee) and SE leads.

---

## Appendix: Common commands
- Healthcheck:
  - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format pretty`
- Reset:
  - `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard --confirm`
- Seed:
  - `rwctl demo seed --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1 --confirm`
- Verify model pins:
  - `rwctl demo models verify --org redwood-demo --routes all`
- Start/stop traffic:
  - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m`
  - `rwctl demo traffic stop --org redwood-demo`
- Rotate keys:
  - `rwctl demo keys rotate --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --ttl 30d --confirm`

---

## Document history
- 2025-11-21: Initial runbook published (reset + seed + traffic generator)
- 2026-01-09: Added key rotation workflow + least-privilege reminders
- 2026-02-12: Updated verification checklist and troubleshooting for dashboard empty states + streaming region issues

- Re-populates Console dashboards via synthetic traffic (cost/latency/errors/rollouts) so demos don’t start with empty graphs.

---

## Audience

---

- Regions used in demos:
  - Primary: `us-east-1`
  - Secondary (fallback demo): `us-west-2`
  - Optional EMEA variant: `eu-west-1` (use only with EMEA talk track)

---

## Quick start (SE: “I need to demo in 10 minutes”)
If you only have time for the minimum reset, do this:
1) Run the demo healthcheck (baseline):
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format pretty`
2) Run the standard reset:
   - `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard`
3) Start synthetic traffic (15–90 seconds to visible Console activity):
   - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 8m`
4) Confirm the four green checks:
   - API chat streaming works
   - Console cost + latency dashboards show data for the last 15 minutes
   - Rollouts view shows a canary event (synthetic)

If any of the above fails, see **Troubleshooting**.

---

## Expected timing (planning guidance)
| Step | Goal | Typical time | Max time before escalation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Healthcheck baseline | Catch obvious breakage early | 30–60s | 2m |
| Reset (standard) | Clear + recreate baseline | 2–4m | 8m |
| Key rotation (optional) | New keys + revoke old | 3–6m | 12m |
| Seed scenarios | Prompt sets/evals/routes present | 1–3m | 6m |
| Synthetic traffic visible | Dashboards not empty | 1–2m | 5m |
| Smoke tests | End-to-end demo happy path | 2–5m | 10m |

Escalate to #demo-oncall if a step hits “max time”.

---

## Standard reset procedure (recommended)
### Step 0 — Confirm no live demo is in progress
1) In Slack (#sales and #demo-oncall), confirm no one is actively using `redwood-demo`.
2) If uncertain, set the demo lock (prevents concurrent resets and avoids mid-demo data loss):
   - `rwctl demo lock acquire --org redwood-demo --ttl 20m --reason "Reset prior to scheduled demo"`

### Step 1 — Baseline healthcheck
Run:
- `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include quotas,models,telemetry,console --format pretty`

Interpretation:
- **PASS**: proceed
- **WARN**: proceed but note the warning (often dashboard freshness)
- **FAIL**: stop and troubleshoot (common: quotas or model availability)

Save output for audit/debug:
- `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format json > /tmp/demo-healthcheck.json`

### Step 2 — Reset tenant state
Run the standard reset (idempotent):
- `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard --confirm`

What “standard” does:
- Ensures the canonical org/projects exist (`demo-core`, `demo-01..05`, `demo-telemetry`).
- Deletes demo-created ephemeral resources (temporary routes, failed rollout experiments, old eval runs, demo app scratch artifacts).
- Resets rate limits/quotas to the demo defaults.
- Re-applies baseline RBAC mappings for SE access.

Notes:
- Do not use `--mode wipe-all` unless instructed by Eng Platform; it’s slower and usually unnecessary.
- If reset takes >8 minutes, stop and escalate (see **Troubleshooting: reset slow/stuck**).

### Step 3 — Re-seed demo scenarios (known-good)
Run:
- `rwctl demo seed --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1 --confirm`

The seed bundle must include:
- Routes:
  - `chat-streaming` (streaming enabled)
  - `structured-output` (JSON schema constrained)
  - `tool-calling` (function/tool calling)
  - `economics-batching-on` / `economics-batching-off`
  - `reliability-fallback` (forced fallback policy for demo narrative)
- Prompt sets + eval fixtures:
  - `support-agent-quality` (regression-friendly)
  - `retrieval-rerank-smoke` (short, stable)
- Rollout artifacts:
  - A synthetic canary rollout record (used in Console tour)

Validation (should print a summary of counts):
- `rwctl demo seed status --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1`

### Step 4 — Validate model pins + safe fallback policy
Run:
- `rwctl demo models verify --org redwood-demo --routes all`

Expected results:
- Each demo route shows:
  - `Pinned model: <family>@<version>`
  - `Allowed fallbacks: <family variants> in <regions>`
  - `Status: OK`

If any route shows `DRIFT` or `UNAVAILABLE`:
- Do not edit pins manually.
- Escalate to #demo-oncall and reference the ADR on pinning/fallback.

### Step 5 — (Optional but recommended weekly) Rotate demo API keys
Rotate keys:
- `rwctl demo keys rotate --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --ttl 30d --confirm`

Rules:
- Keys must be scoped to demo routes/projects only (least privilege).
- Keys must be stored only in the approved secret location (no pasting into Slack).
- Revoke old keys within the same session unless instructed otherwise.

Post-rotation checklist:
- Update the SE “demo runner” app config to the new key location.
- Re-run smoke tests (Step 7).

### Step 6 — Start synthetic traffic generator (for Console dashboards)
Start the preset generator:
- `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m --qps 1.5`

What it generates (approximate):
- 70% chat streaming
- 15% structured output
- 10% tool calling
- 5% induced errors (to populate error analysis views)

If your next demo is >30 minutes away, use 20 minutes duration so graphs stay warm:
- `--duration 20m`

### Step 7 — Verification smoke tests (API + Console)
#### 7A) API smoke tests (copy/paste)
Set env vars:
- `export RW_DEMO_API_KEY="$(rwctl demo keys read --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --format raw)"`
- `export RW_BASE_URL="https://api.redwood.example"` (use the internal demo endpoint if different)

1) Streaming chat:
- `curl -N -H "Authorization: Bearer $RW_DEMO_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "$RW_BASE_URL/v1/chat/completions" \
  -d '{"route":"chat-streaming","stream":true,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Give me a 2-sentence overview of Redwood Inference."}]}'`

Expected:
- Tokens stream within 1–2 seconds.
- No disconnects.

2) Structured output:
- `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $RW_DEMO_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "$RW_BASE_URL/v1/responses" \
  -d '{"route":"structured-output","input":"Extract: Name=Riley, Plan=Pro, Seats=12","response_format":{"type":"json_schema","json_schema":{"name":"account","schema":{"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"},"plan":{"type":"string"},"seats":{"type":"integer"}},"required":["name","plan","seats"]}}}}'`

Expected:
- Valid JSON with `name`, `plan`, `seats`.

3) Tool calling:
- Run the demo runner app (preferred), or call the route directly if your test harness supports it.
- Expected: model requests the tool with well-formed arguments and the response completes.

#### 7B) Console verification (dashboards)
In Console (Org: `redwood-demo`):
- Switch to project: `demo-telemetry`
- Time range: “Last 15 minutes”

Verify:
- **Latency dashboard** shows p50/p95 lines (not empty).
- **Cost dashboard** shows token breakdown by route.
- **Errors view** shows a small non-zero error count (from synthetic traffic).
- **Rollouts view** shows at least one canary record and both variants visible.

If graphs are empty after 5 minutes, see **Troubleshooting: dashboards empty**.

### Step 8 — Release demo lock
- `rwctl demo lock release --org redwood-demo`

---

## Verification checklist (printable)
Use this list before every customer-facing demo.

**Tenant + access**
- [ ] I can log into Console for `redwood-demo`
- [ ] My role shows as `DemoTenantAdmin` or `DemoTenantOperator`
- [ ] Demo lock is not held by someone else

**API happy path**
- [ ] Streaming chat route returns tokens within 1–2 seconds
- [ ] Structured output route returns valid JSON
- [ ] Tool calling route completes with the expected tool invocation

**Reliability**
- [ ] `rwctl demo models verify` shows `OK` (no drift)
- [ ] Fallback route demonstrates region/model variant fallback without errors

**Economics/controls**
- [ ] Batching/caching routes exist (on/off) and return responses
- [ ] Token-level breakdown appears in Console

**Observability**
- [ ] Latency dashboard shows p50/p95
- [ ] Cost dashboard shows per-route spend
- [ ] Error analysis shows at least one synthetic error
- [ ] Rollouts view has a canary example

**Security sanity**
- [ ] Demo keys are least-privilege and stored in approved secret manager
- [ ] No customer data is present (synthetic only)

---

## Troubleshooting
### A) Reset fails immediately with guardrail error
Symptoms:
- CLI refuses to run with a message like “org is not demo” or “environment guardrail triggered”.

Actions:
- Confirm you used `--org redwood-demo`.
- Confirm your `RW_ENV` is set to `demo` (if applicable).
- Do not bypass guardrails. Escalate to #eng-platform.

### B) 429 throttling during smoke tests or demos
Symptoms:
- API returns `429` or requests stall under load.

Actions:
1) Check quotas:
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include quotas`
2) Reduce synthetic traffic QPS if running:
   - `rwctl demo traffic stop --org redwood-demo`
3) If you have parallel demos, move to an isolated project (`demo-02`, `demo-03`, etc.) and use the matching scoped key.
4) If still 429ing, follow the capacity/quota runbook and escalate to #demo-oncall.

### C) Console dashboards are empty
Symptoms:
- No charts populate (latency/cost/errors) even after reset.

Actions:
1) Confirm synthetic traffic is running:
   - `rwctl demo traffic status --org redwood-demo`
2) Restart traffic generator:
   - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m`
3) Confirm Console is pointed to the correct project (`demo-telemetry`) and time range (“Last 15 minutes”).
4) Check telemetry ingestion via healthcheck:
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --include telemetry,console`
5) If ingestion is failing, escalate to #demo-oncall and include `/tmp/demo-healthcheck.json`.

### D) RBAC / SSO role mapping is broken (can’t access org)
Symptoms:
- You can authenticate but can’t see `redwood-demo`, or actions are denied.

Actions:
- Do not attempt manual role edits in Console during a live prep.
- Escalate to #demo-oncall; tag **Ethan Park**.
- Workaround (SE only): switch to backup screenshots/recording while access is restored.

### E) Model pin drift or pinned model unavailable
Symptoms:
- `rwctl demo models verify` shows `DRIFT` or `UNAVAILABLE`.

Actions:
1) Re-run seed status:
   - `rwctl demo seed status --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1`
2) Do not “just pick a new model” in Console; this destabilizes the talk track and expected outputs.
3) Escalate to #demo-oncall; tag **Mina Haddad** or **Ravi Menon**.

### F) Streaming disconnects (region-specific)
Symptoms:
- Streaming starts then disconnects mid-response, often in `eu-west-1`.

Actions:
- Switch demo region to `us-east-1` for the live portion.
- Use backup screenshots for the “global regions” narrative.
- File/attach logs via healthcheck output; escalate to #demo-oncall.

### G) Reset is slow or stuck
Symptoms:
- `rwctl demo reset` runs >8 minutes or hangs.

Actions:
1) Check for lock contention:
   - `rwctl demo lock status --org redwood-demo`
2) Cancel and retry once.
3) If repeated, escalate with:
   - CLI output
   - time started
   - healthcheck JSON

---

## Backup mode (when live systems are unhealthy)
If demo environment is degraded and the customer meeting cannot slip:
- Use the latest approved backup recording and annotated screenshots (Sales Enablement package).
- Keep the narrative aligned: API capabilities → production economics → reliability → observability → enterprise/security.
- After the call, open a #demo-oncall thread with what failed and the meeting timestamp.

---

## Change management
- Seed bundle `known-good-v1` is updated **monthly** (or sooner if a demo regression is found).
- Model pin changes require approval from Demo Technical Owner (Markus Klein) and must follow the pinning ADR.
- Any change that affects the talk track must be communicated to Sales Enablement (Soojin Lee) and SE leads.

---

## Appendix: Common commands
- Healthcheck:
  - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format pretty`
- Reset:
  - `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard --confirm`
- Seed:
  - `rwctl demo seed --org redwood-demo --bundle known-good-v1 --confirm`
- Verify model pins:
  - `rwctl demo models verify --org redwood-demo --routes all`
- Start/stop traffic:
  - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 12m`
  - `rwctl demo traffic stop --org redwood-demo`
- Rotate keys:
  - `rwctl demo keys rotate --org redwood-demo --scope demo-runner --ttl 30d --confirm`

---

## Document history
- 2025-11-21: Initial runbook published (reset + seed + traffic generator)
- 2026-01-09: Added key rotation workflow + least-privilege reminders
- 2026-02-12: Updated verification checklist and troubleshooting for dashboard empty states + streaming region issues
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the standard, repeatable procedure to reset the Redwood **sales demo tenant** (“demo org”) to a known-good state.

A correct reset accomplishes all of the following:
- Resets mutable state (test projects/routes/evals, demo app artifacts) to the standard baseline.
- Re-seeds **known-good demo scenarios** (projects, routes, prompt sets, eval fixtures).
- Rotates demo API keys (least-privilege, audited) and safely revokes old keys.
- Re-populates Console dashboards via synthetic traffic (cost/latency/errors/rollouts) so demos don’t start with empty graphs.
- Validates demo route **model pins** and **safe fallback policy**.

This procedure is designed to be fast (minutes) and safe (guardrails prevent impacting non-demo tenants).

---

## Audience
- Sales Engineering (SE) running live demos
- Eng Platform on-call supporting demo enablement
- SRE/Infra responders for capacity, quota, and incident escalation

If you are an AE: do not run resets. Ask the assigned SE or #demo-oncall.

---

## Ownership and support
- Demo technical owner: **Markus Klein**
- Provision/reset automation: **Devon Price**
- Demo data pipelines (synthetic traffic + seeds): **Mei Lin**
- Control plane/auth (RBAC + key scopes): **Ethan Park**
- SRE escalation: **Sean Gallagher**
- Alerts/observability: **Allison Grant**

Slack:
- Primary: **#demo-oncall** (pager-backed during business hours)
- Secondary: **#eng-platform**
- Incidents: **#eng-sre**

---

## Preconditions
You must have all of the following before running a reset:
- Membership in the demo org with the role: `DemoTenantAdmin`
- Access to the internal CLI (`rwctl`) and the demo control plane environment
- Access to the secrets manager entry for demo keys (Vault/1Password) as `demo-secrets-writer`

Required tools:
- `rwctl` v0.34.0+ (pinned for demo stability)
- `curl` (or Postman) for smoke tests

---

## Definitions and identifiers (do not change)
These identifiers are referenced by automation, dashboards, and training materials.

- Org slug: `redwood-demo`
- Primary demo project (default): `demo-core`
- Parallel demo projects (namespaces): `demo-01` … `demo-05`
- Synthetic traffic project: `demo-telemetry`
- Environment tag: `env=demo`
- Regions used in demos:
  - Primary: `us-east-1`
  - Secondary (fallback demo): `us-west-2`
  - Optional EMEA variant: `eu-west-1` (use only with EMEA talk track)

Guardrail: automation refuses to run if `org_slug != redwood-demo`.

---

## Quick start (SE: “I need to demo in 10 minutes”)
If you only have time for the minimum reset, do this:
1) Run the demo healthcheck (baseline):
   - `rwctl demo healthcheck --org redwood-demo --format pretty`
2) Run the standard reset:
   - `rwctl demo reset --org redwood-demo --mode standard`
3) Start synthetic traffic (15–90 seconds to visible Console activity):
   - `rwctl demo traffic start --org redwood-demo --profile sales-demo --duration 8m`
4) Confirm the four green checks:
   - API chat streaming works
   - Console cost + latency dashboards show data for the last 15 minutes
   - Rollouts view shows a canary event (synthetic)
   - Model pins report “OK”

If any of the above fails, see **Troubleshooting**.

---

## Expected timing (planning guidance)
| Step | Goal | Typical time | Max time before escalation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Healthcheck baseline | Catch obvious breakage early | 30–60s | 2m |
| Reset (standard) | Clear + recreate baseline | 2–4m | 8m |
| Key rotation (optional) | New keys + revoke old | 3–6m | 12m |
| Seed scenarios | Prompt sets/evals/routes present | 1–3m | 6m |
| Synthetic traffic visible | Dashboards not empty | 1–2m | 5m |
| Smoke tests | End-to-end demo happy path | 2–5m | 10m |

Escalate to #demo-oncall if a step hits “max time”.

---

## Standard reset procedure (recommended)
### Step 0 — Confirm no live demo is in progress
1) In Slack (#sales and #demo-oncall), confirm no one is actively using `redwood-demo`.
2) If uncertain, set the demo lock (prevents concurrent resets and avoids mid-demo data loss):
   - `rwctl demo lock acquire --org redwood-demo --ttl 20m --reason "Reset prior to scheduled demo"`

### Step 1 — Baseline healthcheck
Run:

Interpretation:
- **PASS**: proceed
- **WARN**: proceed but note the warning (often dashboard freshness)

- Deletes demo-created ephemeral resources (temporary routes, failed rollout experiments, old eval runs, demo app scratch artifacts).
- Resets rate limits/quotas to the demo defaults.

### Step 7 — Verification smoke tests (API + Console)

2) Structured output:

Expected:

3) Tool calling:

#### 7B) Console verification (dashboards)

### Step 8 — Release demo lock
- `rwctl demo lock release --org redwood-demo`

---

## Verification checklist (printable)
Use this list before every customer-facing demo.

**Tenant + access**
- [ ] I can log into Console for `redwood-demo`
- [ ] My role shows as `DemoTenantAdmin` or `DemoTenantOperator`
- [ ] Demo lock is not held by someone else

**API happy path**

- [ ] Rollouts view has a canary example

**Security sanity**

- [ ] No customer data is present (synthetic only)

---

## Troubleshooting
### A) Reset fails immediately with guardrail error
