- Per-tenant heatmap: cost per 30s window, estimated_token_count distribution, quantization hit-rate.
- Edge action audit dashboard: counts by rule_id, action_latency_p99, and rollback_rate.
- Key alerts: csf.high_cost_spike, csf.rule-failure-rate (action application failures >1% over 5m), csf-audit-delayed (audit events lag > 1m).

Metrics to instrument (recommended list)

- csf.cost_score (gauge) by tenant, region
- csf.action_published_count (counter) by action_type, rule_id
- csf.action_apply_success (counter) by edge_adapter, rule_id
- csf.audit_event_lag_ms (histogram)
- router.throttle_applied (counter) by tenant, severity

Audit and compliance

- Audit event schema example (JSON as string stored in ClickHouse):

  {"ts":"2026-02-12T14:05:02Z","tenant_id":"tenant-123","rule_id":"RS-throt-05","action":"BUCKET_AND_THROTTLE","cause":{ "cost_score":12.3, "window":"30s"},"decision":"hard","actor":"csf-v2","trace":"trace-id-abc"}

- Retention: ClickHouse live store 90 days, S3 cold store 7 years for customer-sensitive actions.

Rollout plan and migration notes

- Phase 0 (canary): route 5% of internal traffic through CSF in staging; ensure audit events and edge adapters correctly apply advisory hints.
- Phase 1: enable soft actions for 20% of production traffic, monitor action_apply_success >= 99.5% and audit_event_lag < 200ms.
- Phase 2: enable hard actions for non-critical routes and low-tier tenants after two weeks of stable metrics.
- Phase 3: full rollout with configurable tenant exemptions and manual override UI in Console.

Traceability and testing requirements

- Every rule change must include unit tests for scoring logic, integration test for fanout path, and a staged A/B test that validates no >1.5% user-visible regression on key prompt sets.
- Synthetic test harness: run population-scale synthetic traffic for 24h to capture stability under churn.

Known limitations and future work

- Current scoring relies on estimated_token_count emitted by runtime; requests with server-side streamed inputs (client-side continuation) can underreport. Improving hybrid-token-telemetry is next.
- Integration with Dedicated tenant hardware labels (GPU topology) for more granular cost-modeling is planned.

Retrospective template (to be used after any CSF-triggered incident)

- Incident summary: short problem statement and impact window.
- Timeline: detection, automations, manual mitigations, resolution.
- Root cause analysis: telemetry evidence and contributing factors.
- Action items: prioritized list with owners and due dates.

Ownership and contacts

- System owner: runtime-team (oncall: runtime-sre rotation)
- Edge adapters owner: eng-platform-gateway team
- Auditing and billing mappings: finance-legal (billing-integrations)
- Escalation path: primary oncall -> platform oncall -> engineering director (see contacts page).

Appendix: example rule YAML (for rule RS-quant-01)

---
rule_id: RS-quant-01
description: "Advise quantization when per-token cost and cache misses indicate high cost"
trigger:
  - metric: estimated_cost_per_token
    comparator: ">"
    value: 2.5 * baseline
  - metric: KV_cache_hit_ratio
    comparator: "<"
    value: 30
action:
  type: FORCE_QUANTIZE
  mode: soft
  ttl_seconds: 5
owner: runtime-team
---

Contact and references

- Slack channels: #platform-csf, #runtime-alerts
- Related docs: runtime token telemetry spec, billing mapping schema, edge adapter implementation notes.

End of document
text:
Summary

This playbook describes the design, operational runbook, dashboard mappings, and rollout plan for the Cost Signal Fanout (CSF) system and its Edge Actioning component. CSF converts runtime cost telemetry into low-latency signals that inform per-request and per-tenant mitigation actions at the edge (API gateway, routing layer, or model selector). The goal is to keep unit economics predictable while preserving latency and quality SLOs.

Background and motivation

- Problem: spikes in per-token cost (e.g., due to unexpectedly long sequences, model cascade fallbacks, or noisy tenants) create unpredictable spend and SLA tension. Traditional billing alerts are slow and coarse-grained.
- Objective: provide sub-second cost-aware decisions that can 1) reduce incremental cost without violating latency SLOs, 2) protect core tenants from noisy neighbors, and 3) provide observability and auditor-friendly traces for every action.
- Scope: hosted and dedicated deployments where runtime can emit token-level or request-estimated cost signals. Private deployments require local adapter but follow same contract.

Architecture overview

1) Telemetry producers
   - Serving runtime: emits per-request estimated_token_count, model_variant_id, quantization_profile_id, KV_cache_hit_ratio, and estimated_gpu_ms as JSON to our Kafka cost-topic.
   - Edge proxies (gateway): emits request metadata (tenant_id, route, latency_budget_ms, client_priority).

2) Cost Signal Fanout (CSF) service
   - Ingest: Kafka consumer that enriches runtime signals with billing mappings and tenant quotas.
   - Scoring: computes a short-lived cost_score and risk_score using sliding-window aggregations (30s, 5m) and decaying counters.
   - Fanout: publishes lightweight action messages to a high-throughput pub/sub channel (NATS) for the edge to consume. Action messages are versioned and typed (SCALE_DOWN_HINT, FORCE_QUANTIZE, BUCKET_AND_THROTTLE, FALLBACK_TO_CHEAPER_MODEL).

3) Edge Actioning adapters
   - Gateway adapter: consumes action messages, applies transient token-level hints in a request context to the routing decision. Hints are advisory by default and require safety checks (see guards).
   - Router adapter: enforces hard actions for tenant-level throttles or prioritized eviction.

4) Observability and auditing
   - Every action emits an audit-event (immutable) that includes cause (cost_score), rule_id, decision, and pre/post metrics. Audit-events are stored in ClickHouse for queries and in S3 cold storage for compliance.

SLOs and safety constraints

- Latency SLO: 95% of in-region requests must remain < 200ms p99 target unchanged for read-only paths. Any cost actions that increase tail latency must be denied.
- Quality floor: automated quantization fallback only allowed if evaluated on model variant A/B tests with <2% ROUGE-change-like metric regression on target prompt sets.
- Economic threshold: per-tenant short-window cost delta (30s) must exceed 3x baseline before hard throttles are permitted.
- Action TTL: edge hints expire in 5 seconds unless refreshed.

Safety guards (non-exhaustive)

- Latency guard: do not apply actions that add >10ms expected additional compute path unless overridden by incident commander.
- Blacklist guard: customers configured with "no-cost-mitigation" label in billing config are exempt.
- Critical routes guard: routes tagged with "high-availability" in routing manifest cannot be hard-throttled; only advisory hints allowed.

Config and rule catalog (excerpt)

| rule_id | trigger_condition | action_type | soft_vs_hard | owner | notes |
| RS-quant-01 | estimated_cost_per_token > 2.5x baseline and KV_cache_hit_ratio < 30% | FORCE_QUANTIZE | soft | runtime-team | advisory quantization hint to model selector |
| RS-throt-05 | tenant_30s_cost_delta >= 3x baseline and recent_errors < 1% | BUCKET_AND_THROTTLE | hard | sres | tenant-level fair-share throttle |
| RS-fb-02 | fallback_count_last_5m > 10 and latency_spike_timeline | FALLBACK_TO_CHEAPER_MODEL | soft | model-onboarding | triggers canary fallback policy |

Operational runbook: actionable steps

1) Detection and alerting
   - Alert: csf.high_cost_spike (severity P3) triggers when global CSF cost_score 95th percentile exceeds configured threshold for 2 consecutive 30s windows.
   - On alert, SRE oncall should open an incident in Jira and run initial triage (see section below).

2) Initial triage (10 minutes)
   - Check dashboards: CSF-overview, per-tenant cost heatmap, edge-action-audit-log.
   - Identify if spike is tenant-specific or global. If tenant-specific, note tenant_id and owner.
   - If global and due to model cascade fallback, consult runtime release notes and model-variant rollout dashboards.

3) Apply manual mitigations (if automated mitigation did not resolve)
   - For noisy tenant: apply temporary BUCKET throttle with 5s token-window and 20% capacity reduction using router-adapter CLI:

   rtrctl apply-throttle --tenant tenant-123 --window 5s --capacity 0.8 --reason "noisy-neighbor-emergency"

   - To enforce cheaper model fallback globally for non-critical routes:

   csfctl publish-action --type FALLBACK_TO_CHEAPER_MODEL --routes "/async/**" --ttl 30s --rule RS-fb-02

4) Post-incident follow-up (within 3 business days)
   - Run retrospective: include cost delta timeline, root cause, actions taken, and followup owners.
   - If action was triggered by an overly aggressive rule, update rule thresholds and deploy small canary to ensure correctness.

Dashboards and alerts mapping

- CSF Overview dashboard: global cost_score timelines, fanout-rate, action-rate, and action-latency histogram.
- Per-tenant heatmap: cost per 30s window, estimated_token_count distribution, quantization hit-rate.
- Edge action audit dashboard: counts by rule_id, action_latency_p99, and rollback_rate.
- Key alerts: csf.high_cost_spike, csf.rule-failure-rate (action application failures >1% over 5m), csf-audit-delayed (audit events lag > 1m).

Metrics to instrument (recommended list)

- csf.cost_score (gauge) by tenant, region
- csf.action_published_count (counter) by action_type, rule_id
- csf.action_apply_success (counter) by edge_adapter, rule_id
- csf.audit_event_lag_ms (histogram)
- router.throttle_applied (counter) by tenant, severity

Audit and compliance

- Audit event schema example (JSON as string stored in ClickHouse):

  {"ts":"2026-02-12T14:05:02Z","tenant_id":"tenant-123","rule_id":"RS-throt-05","action":"BUCKET_AND_THROTTLE","cause":{ "cost_score":12.3, "window":"30s"},"decision":"hard","actor":"csf-v2","trace":"trace-id-abc"}

- Retention: ClickHouse live store 90 days, S3 cold store 7 years for customer-sensitive actions.

Rollout plan and migration notes

- Phase 0 (canary): route 5% of internal traffic through CSF in staging; ensure audit events and edge adapters correctly apply advisory hints.
- Phase 1: enable soft actions for 20% of production traffic, monitor action_apply_success >= 99.5% and audit_event_lag < 200ms.
- Phase 2: enable hard actions for non-critical routes and low-tier tenants after two weeks of stable metrics.
- Phase 3: full rollout with configurable tenant exemptions and manual override UI in Console.

Traceability and testing requirements

- Every rule change must include unit tests for scoring logic, integration test for fanout path, and a staged A/B test that validates no >1.5% user-visible regression on key prompt sets.
- Synthetic test harness: run population-scale synthetic traffic for 24h to capture stability under churn.

Known limitations and future work

- Current scoring relies on estimated_token_count emitted by runtime; requests with server-side streamed inputs (client-side continuation) can underreport. Improving hybrid-token-telemetry is next.
- Integration with Dedicated tenant hardware labels (GPU topology) for more granular cost-modeling is planned.

Retrospective template (to be used after any CSF-triggered incident)

- Incident summary: short problem statement and impact window.
- Timeline: detection, automations, manual mitigations, resolution.
- Root cause analysis: telemetry evidence and contributing factors.
- Action items: prioritized list with owners and due dates.

Ownership and contacts

- System owner: runtime-team (oncall: runtime-sre rotation)
- Edge adapters owner: eng-platform-gateway team
- Auditing and billing mappings: finance-legal (billing-integrations)
- Escalation path: primary oncall -> platform oncall -> engineering director (see contacts page).

Appendix: example rule YAML (for rule RS-quant-01)

---
rule_id: RS-quant-01
description: "Advise quantization when per-token cost and cache misses indicate high cost"
trigger:
  - metric: estimated_cost_per_token
    comparator: ">"
    value: 2.5 * baseline
  - metric: KV_cache_hit_ratio
    comparator: "<"
    value: 30
action:
  type: FORCE_QUANTIZE
  mode: soft
  ttl_seconds: 5
owner: runtime-team
---

Contact and references

- Slack channels: #platform-csf, #runtime-alerts
- Related docs: runtime token telemetry spec, billing mapping schema, edge adapter implementation notes.

End of document

source.title: Routing Policy Failover Controller — Operational Runbook
source.author: Tomáš Novák
source.body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-to-day operations for the **Routing Policy Failover Controller** ("failover-controller"): enabling/disabling automation, using **dry-run** mode, handling **approval workflows**, performing **rollbacks**, and troubleshooting.

The controller evaluates region-level impairment signals (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity exhaustion) and (optionally) executes routing policy changes to shift traffic away from impacted regions while preventing oscillation and overload.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Hosted API + Dedicated routing policy flips across regions
- Controller operation modes (disabled / dry-run / execute with approvals / execute fully automated)
- Anti-flap behavior (hold-down timers, rate limits)
- Rollback and stabilization workflows
- Auditability and incident-bot integration

**Out of scope**
- Customer-specific Private deployment automation (see Private guidance)
- Changing SLO contract language
- Core serving runtime changes

## Service overview
**Components**
- **failover-controller**: control loop that reads signal inputs and recommends/executes policy actions.
- **policy-engine**: deterministic evaluation of signal hierarchy; outputs recommended routing actions.
- **region-health-service**: aggregates raw signals into region-level health states with TTL + jitter.
- **routing control plane**: applies policy changes (route weights, region allow/deny, fallback model/region).
- **audit log**: emits structured failover events (recommendation/execution/rollback) with evidence.
- **incident-bot**: posts summaries to incident channels when actions occur.

**Primary on-call ownership**
- **Platform On-call (Routing/Control Plane)** owns controller health and routing action execution.
- **SRE On-call** owns incident coordination and go/no-go for production experiments/game days.

**Escalations**
- Routing policy tech lead: Ravi Menon
- Failover architecture lead: Miles Thornton
- SRE delivery lead: Rafael Mendes
- Network reachability lead: Victor Alvarez

## Preconditions / safety principles
1) **Prefer safety over speed** when signal quality is uncertain.
2) **No single noisy metric** should trigger failover without corroboration (per ADR-042).
3) **Rate-limit changes** to avoid policy flapping (per ADR-043).
4) **Failing over can cause overload**; verify destination capacity headroom and circuit breakers.
5) Keep an **always-available escape hatch**: ability to disable execution and/or freeze routing.

## Controller modes
The controller can be run in one of these modes. Confirm current mode before making changes.

| Mode | Description | Typical usage |
|---|---|---|
| Disabled | Controller runs no decision loop (or is scaled to 0). | Emergency stop; maintenance. |
| Observe-only | Controller evaluates signals and writes internal metrics/logs, but does not emit recommendations. | Early rollout; validating inputs. |
| Dry-run | Controller emits recommendations + audit events marked DRY_RUN, but does not execute. | Staging validation; pre-prod rehearsal; incident analysis. |
| Execute w/ approvals | Controller executes only after on-call approval within a time window. | Production initial rollout; game days. |
| Execute (auto) | Controller executes automatically within safety limits. | Mature state after proven signals + guardrails. |

## Key configuration knobs (production defaults)
These values are managed in the controller config (helm values + config store) and should match ADRs.

**Signal evaluation (high level)**
- Authoritative signals (highest priority):
  - Cross-region reachability failing (synthetic probes) for >= **60s**
  - Region-health-service state = **UNREACHABLE** or **SEVERE**
- Corroborating signals:
  - 5xx burn rate (fast): **14x / 5m** window
  - 5xx burn rate (slow): **6x / 30m** window
  - Tail latency burn: p99 > SLO threshold with **6x / 30m** burn
  - Capacity headroom below threshold for >= **2m**

**Anti-flap / dampening**
- Corroboration requirement: at least **2 independent sources** unless reachability is failing.
- Hold-down timer after a flip: **20 minutes** (no further flips for the same route/tenant unless reachability is hard-down).
- Minimum time in healthy state before rollback: **15 minutes** stable.

**Action rate limits (blast radius protection)**
- Max automated flips per tenant per hour: **2**
- Max automated flips per route per hour: **3**
- Max % traffic shift in a single step (Hosted): **50%** unless explicitly overridden by approval
- Dedicated: drain workflow must be acknowledged; do not hard-cut unless approved

## Operational quick-start (what to do first)
When a page indicates a possible regional impairment:
1) Open the **Failover Signals Dashboard** and confirm whether signals are region-scoped or service-scoped.
2) Check the controller status: mode, last evaluations, and recent actions.
3) If production impact is possible and automation is in execute mode, ensure approvals are working or disable execution.

## Access and tooling
**CLI**: `failoverctl` (internal tool)
- Auth: SSO + production break-glass group (Platform On-call)
- All commands should attach a reason string (for auditability).

**Kubernetes**
- Namespace: `routing-system`
- Deployment: `failover-controller`

**Audit events**
- Stream: `audit.failover_events`
- Dashboard panel: “Automation Decisions (with evidence)”

## Common procedures

### 1) Check current status (mode, health, last action)
1. Run:
   - `failoverctl status --env prod`
2. Confirm:
   - `mode` (disabled/dry-run/execute)
   - `config_version`
   - `signal_ingestion_ok` (region-health-service + probes)
   - `last_decision_at` within last 60s
   - `last_action` details (route/tenant/region/from->to)
3. If controller loop appears stalled, jump to Troubleshooting ("Controller not making decisions").

### 2) Enable dry-run (recommended first step)
Use dry-run to validate that decisions match expectation without changing traffic.

Pre-checks:
- Confirm on-call lead and comms channel are established (incident channel or #eng-sre).
- Verify dashboards have the required panels and annotations enabled.

Steps:
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Validate signal pipeline after alert"`
2. Watch for:
   - DRY_RUN audit events
   - incident-bot summaries posted (should say DRY_RUN)
3. Validate recommendations against current known symptoms.

Exit criteria:
- Recommendations are stable (no oscillation), evidence includes expected signals.

### 3) Enable execute mode with approvals (production)
This is the default for early production rollout and game days.

Pre-checks (go/no-go):
- Destination region headroom >= **25%** for relevant GPU pools (Hosted) and Dedicated pools per capacity plan.
- API gateway circuit breakers are at failover-safe settings (timeouts/retry budgets) for the route.
- Synthetic reachability probes are green for target region.
- SRE on-call agrees to proceed; rollback authority assigned.

Steps:
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode execute-with-approvals --reason "Enable guarded automation"`
2. Confirm approval provider:
   - `failoverctl approvals status --env prod`
3. Confirm per-tenant safety limits are active:
   - `failoverctl safety-limits get --env prod`

What happens next:
- Controller will emit a recommendation with an approval window (default **10 minutes**).
- Approval is required per action (route/tenant pair).

### 4) Approve a recommended action
Use this when the controller proposes a flip and you have validated symptoms.

Steps:
1. Inspect evidence:
   - `failoverctl recommendations list --env prod --since 30m`
   - `failoverctl recommendation show --env prod --id <REC_ID>`
2. Confirm evidence aligns with ADR-042 priority order (e.g., reachability or corroborated 5xx burn).
3. Confirm destination region capacity and error budget risk.
4. Approve:
   - `failoverctl approve --env prod --id <REC_ID> --reason "Confirmed us-east reachability failures; shift to eu-west"`
5. Monitor execution:
   - `failoverctl actions tail --env prod --route <ROUTE> --duration 15m`

### 5) Disable execution immediately (emergency stop)
Use if:
- False positives are causing unsafe flips
- Controller is flapping
- You suspect a bad config rollout

Preferred: freeze execution but keep signal evaluation.
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Emergency stop: prevent automated flips"`

If CLI/control plane is degraded:
1. `kubectl -n routing-system scale deploy/failover-controller --replicas=0`
2. Pin routing policies manually (Routing On-call) until stable.

Post-step:
- Announce in incident channel that automation execution is disabled and why.

### 6) Rollback an action (revert routing policy)
Rollback policy is governed by ADR-043. Rollback can be automated (after stability) or manual.

Manual rollback is appropriate when:
- Failover caused overload in target region
- Root cause is not region-wide (service-only)
- Customer tier constraints require restoration

Steps:
1. Identify the action:
   - `failoverctl actions list --env prod --route <ROUTE> --since 2h`
2. Verify anti-flap timers:
   - `failoverctl anti-flap status --env prod --route <ROUTE>`
3. Prepare rollback with a controlled ramp (recommended):
   - 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100% over 10–20 minutes (depending on SLO burn)
4. Execute rollback:
   - `failoverctl rollback --env prod --action-id <ACTION_ID> --strategy ramp --reason "Target region saturation; restore primary"`
5. Monitor:
   - 5xx rate, p99 latency, queue depth, capacity headroom, retry-after rates

### 7) Dedicated pool drain coordination (when failover impacts Dedicated)
Dedicated failover must coordinate with drain/evacuation to protect reserved workloads.

Steps:
1. Confirm Dedicated customer(s) and pools affected:
   - `failoverctl dedicated impacted --env prod --region <REGION>`
2. Start drain workflow (do not hard-cut without approval):
   - `failoverctl dedicated drain start --env prod --pool <POOL_ID> --reason "Regional impairment"`
3. Track progress metrics:
   - `failoverctl dedicated drain status --env prod --pool <POOL_ID>`
4. Only after drain gates are satisfied, approve/execute routing shift.

## Validation checklist (after enabling or after an action)
Within 5–15 minutes, confirm:
- Error rate returns to baseline; no sustained elevated 5xx
- p95/p99 latency stabilizes within SLO thresholds
- No surge in retries or reconnects (especially streaming)
- Target region capacity headroom remains >= 15–25%
- Audit events are being emitted and linked to the incident timeline
- Customer-visible comms are aligned (status page / support macros if needed)

## Troubleshooting

### A) Controller not making decisions (status shows stale last_decision_at)
Symptoms:
- `last_decision_at` older than 2–3 minutes
- No new recommendation metrics

Checks:
1. Pod health:
   - `kubectl -n routing-system get pods -l app=failover-controller`
2. Logs:
   - `kubectl -n routing-system logs deploy/failover-controller --since=15m`
3. Confirm region-health-service availability and latency.
4. Confirm perf-canary probe ingestion is current.

Fixes:
- If crash looping due to missing signal stream, switch to dry-run and apply hotfix configuration (missing-signal tolerant), then restart.
- If downstream control plane is degraded, keep controller in dry-run to avoid compounding issues.

Escalate:
- Ethan Park (control plane) if routing API is failing
- Allison Grant if telemetry ingestion appears delayed or missing

### B) Recommendations look wrong (false positives)
Common causes:
- Tail-latency spikes due to client retry storms rather than region impairment
- Partial control plane degradation misclassified as region reachability
- Metric label cardinality or aggregation issues causing incorrect burn calculations

Actions:
1. Verify signal evidence bundle:
   - Does the recommendation cite at least two corroborating sources?
2. Compare reachability probes vs. internal service metrics.
3. Increase dampening temporarily (with SRE approval) and keep execution disabled.
4. File a follow-up issue with examples (REC_IDs) and links to dashboards.

### C) Flapping / oscillation between regions
Immediate response:
1. Disable execution:
   - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Automation flapping"`
2. Freeze routing policies at the current safest state.

Diagnosis:
- Check hold-down timer settings and whether emergency override is being triggered.
- Look for correlated signal volatility (reachability intermittent, capacity borderline).

Fixes:
- Increase hold-down timer; adjust corroboration logic per ADR-042.
- Adjust capacity thresholds; ensure target region has enough headroom before allowing flips.

### D) Approvals not working (cannot approve or approvals timing out)
Checks:
- `failoverctl approvals status --env prod`
- Confirm you are in the correct on-call group.
- Confirm incident-bot / approval provider is reachable.

Fallback:
- Temporarily switch to manual routing change (Routing On-call) while leaving controller in dry-run for evidence.

### E) Failover executed but customer still errors (no improvement)
Possible causes:
- Client DNS caching / TTL behavior delaying traffic shift
- Gateway retry budgets amplifying overload
- Streaming clients not handling redirect/reconnect correctly

Actions:
1. Verify actual traffic distribution by region (not just policy state).
2. Check DNS TTL/anycast notes and whether expected shift time matches reality.
3. Confirm gateway circuit breakers and retry-after behavior are active.
4. If streaming-specific: correlate disconnects with redirect events.

## Audit and documentation requirements
For any production execution (including game days):
- Ensure a reason is attached to mode changes, approvals, and rollbacks.
- Link REC_ID/ACTION_ID in the incident timeline.
- Confirm audit log retention meets enterprise expectations for affected tiers.
- Add a short summary in the incident channel and (if needed) a support note.

## Game day / controlled failover notes (operator-specific)
During controlled tests:
- Default posture: **execute-with-approvals**.
- Limit blast radius to approved tenants/routes/models.
- Use staged ramps (traffic weights) unless the scenario explicitly tests hard cut.
- Pre-write rollback commands and confirm authority.

## Post-incident / post-test tasks
1. Return mode to the intended steady state (usually dry-run or execute-with-approvals depending on rollout stage).
2. Verify alert tuning: ensure no excess paging noise.
3. Create follow-up tasks for:
   - Signal quality gaps
   - Dashboard/annotation gaps
   - Anti-flap/rollback tuning
   - Runbook edits based on operator confusion

## Appendix: Suggested commands
- Status:
  - `failoverctl status --env prod`
- Mode changes:
  - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "..."`
  - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode execute-with-approvals --reason "..."`
- Recommendations:
  - `failoverctl recommendations list --env prod --since 30m`
  - `failoverctl recommendation show --env prod --id <REC_ID>`
- Approvals:
  - `failoverctl approve --env prod --id <REC_ID> --reason "..."`
- Actions and rollback:
  - `failoverctl actions list --env prod --since 2h`
  - `failoverctl rollback --env prod --action-id <ACTION_ID> --strategy ramp --reason "..."`
- Emergency stop:
  - `kubectl -n routing-system scale deploy/failover-controller --replicas=0`

## Changelog
- 2026-02-12: Added approval failure fallback path; clarified anti-flap and ramp rollback guidance; added Dedicated drain coordination section.
- 2026-01-20: Updated defaults to match ADR-043; added audit requirements and incident-bot expectations.
- 2026-01-05: Initial version (dry-run + execute-with-approvals workflows).

- **policy-engine**: deterministic evaluation of signal hierarchy; outputs recommended routing actions.
- **region-health-service**: aggregates raw signals into region-level health states with TTL + jitter.
- **routing control plane**: applies policy changes (route weights, region allow/deny, fallback model/region).
- **audit log**: emits structured failover events (recommendation/execution/rollback) with evidence.
- **incident-bot**: posts summaries to incident channels when actions occur.

**Primary on-call ownership**
- **Platform On-call (Routing/Control Plane)** owns controller health and routing action execution.
- **SRE On-call** owns incident coordination and go/no-go for production experiments/game days.

**Escalations**
- Routing policy tech lead: Ravi Menon
- Failover architecture lead: Miles Thornton
- SRE delivery lead: Rafael Mendes
- Network reachability lead: Victor Alvarez

## Preconditions / safety principles
1) **Prefer safety over speed** when signal quality is uncertain.
2) **No single noisy metric** should trigger failover without corroboration (per ADR-042).
3) **Rate-limit changes** to avoid policy flapping (per ADR-043).
4) **Failing over can cause overload**; verify destination capacity headroom and circuit breakers.
5) Keep an **always-available escape hatch**: ability to disable execution and/or freeze routing.

## Controller modes
The controller can be run in one of these modes. Confirm current mode before making changes.

| Mode | Description | Typical usage |
|---|---|---|
| Disabled | Controller runs no decision loop (or is scaled to 0). | Emergency stop; maintenance. |
| Observe-only | Controller evaluates signals and writes internal metrics/logs, but does not emit recommendations. | Early rollout; validating inputs. |
| Dry-run | Controller emits recommendations + audit events marked DRY_RUN, but does not execute. | Staging validation; pre-prod rehearsal; incident analysis. |
| Execute w/ approvals | Controller executes only after on-call approval within a time window. | Production initial rollout; game days. |
| Execute (auto) | Controller executes automatically within safety limits. | Mature state after proven signals + guardrails. |

## Key configuration knobs (production defaults)
These values are managed in the controller config (helm values + config store) and should match ADRs.

**Signal evaluation (high level)**
- Authoritative signals (highest priority):
  - Cross-region reachability failing (synthetic probes) for >= **60s**
  - Region-health-service state = **UNREACHABLE** or **SEVERE**
- Corroborating signals:
  - 5xx burn rate (fast): **14x / 5m** window
  - 5xx burn rate (slow): **6x / 30m** window
  - Tail latency burn: p99 > SLO threshold with **6x / 30m** burn
  - Capacity headroom below threshold for >= **2m**

**Anti-flap / dampening**
- Corroboration requirement: at least **2 independent sources** unless reachability is failing.
- Hold-down timer after a flip: **20 minutes** (no further flips for the same route/tenant unless reachability is hard-down).
- Minimum time in healthy state before rollback: **15 minutes** stable.

**Action rate limits (blast radius protection)**
- Max automated flips per tenant per hour: **2**
- Max automated flips per route per hour: **3**
- Max % traffic shift in a single step (Hosted): **50%** unless explicitly overridden by approval
- Dedicated: drain workflow must be acknowledged; do not hard-cut unless approved

## Operational quick-start (what to do first)
When a page indicates a possible regional impairment:
1) Open the **Failover Signals Dashboard** and confirm whether signals are region-scoped or service-scoped.
2) Check the controller status: mode, last evaluations, and recent actions.
3) If production impact is possible and automation is in execute mode, ensure approvals are working or disable execution.

## Access and tooling
**CLI**: `failoverctl` (internal tool)
- Auth: SSO + production break-glass group (Platform On-call)
- All commands should attach a reason string (for auditability).

**Kubernetes**
- Namespace: `routing-system`
- Deployment: `failover-controller`

**Audit events**
- Stream: `audit.failover_events`
- Dashboard panel: “Automation Decisions (with evidence)”

## Common procedures

### 1) Check current status (mode, health, last action)
1. Run:
   - `failoverctl status --env prod`
2. Confirm:
   - `mode` (disabled/dry-run/execute)
   - `config_version`
   - `signal_ingestion_ok` (region-health-service + probes)
   - `last_decision_at` within last 60s
   - `last_action` details (route/tenant/region/from->to)
3. If controller loop appears stalled, jump to Troubleshooting ("Controller not making decisions").

### 2) Enable dry-run (recommended first step)
Use dry-run to validate that decisions match expectation without changing traffic.

Pre-checks:
- Confirm on-call lead and comms channel are established (incident channel or #eng-sre).
- Verify dashboards have the required panels and annotations enabled.

Steps:
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Validate signal pipeline after alert"`
2. Watch for:
   - DRY_RUN audit events
   - incident-bot summaries posted (should say DRY_RUN)
3. Validate recommendations against current known symptoms.

Exit criteria:
- Recommendations are stable (no oscillation), evidence includes expected signals.

### 3) Enable execute mode with approvals (production)
This is the default for early production rollout and game days.

Pre-checks (go/no-go):
- Destination region headroom >= **25%** for relevant GPU pools (Hosted) and Dedicated pools per capacity plan.
- API gateway circuit breakers are at failover-safe settings (timeouts/retry budgets) for the route.
- Synthetic reachability probes are green for target region.
- SRE on-call agrees to proceed; rollback authority assigned.

Steps:
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode execute-with-approvals --reason "Enable guarded automation"`
2. Confirm approval provider:
   - `failoverctl approvals status --env prod`
3. Confirm per-tenant safety limits are active:
   - `failoverctl safety-limits get --env prod`

What happens next:
- Controller will emit a recommendation with an approval window (default **10 minutes**).
- Approval is required per action (route/tenant pair).

### 4) Approve a recommended action
Use this when the controller proposes a flip and you have validated symptoms.

Steps:
1. Inspect evidence:
   - `failoverctl recommendations list --env prod --since 30m`
   - `failoverctl recommendation show --env prod --id <REC_ID>`
2. Confirm evidence aligns with ADR-042 priority order (e.g., reachability or corroborated 5xx burn).
3. Confirm destination region capacity and error budget risk.
4. Approve:
   - `failoverctl approve --env prod --id <REC_ID> --reason "Confirmed us-east reachability failures; shift to eu-west"`
5. Monitor execution:
   - `failoverctl actions tail --env prod --route <ROUTE> --duration 15m`

### 5) Disable execution immediately (emergency stop)
Use if:
- False positives are causing unsafe flips
- Controller is flapping
- You suspect a bad config rollout

Preferred: freeze execution but keep signal evaluation.
1. `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Emergency stop: prevent automated flips"`

If CLI/control plane is degraded:
1. `kubectl -n routing-system scale deploy/failover-controller --replicas=0`
2. Pin routing policies manually (Routing On-call) until stable.

Post-step:
- Announce in incident channel that automation execution is disabled and why.

### 6) Rollback an action (revert routing policy)
Rollback policy is governed by ADR-043. Rollback can be automated (after stability) or manual.

Manual rollback is appropriate when:
- Failover caused overload in target region
- Root cause is not region-wide (service-only)
- Customer tier constraints require restoration

Steps:
1. Identify the action:
   - `failoverctl actions list --env prod --route <ROUTE> --since 2h`
2. Verify anti-flap timers:
   - `failoverctl anti-flap status --env prod --route <ROUTE>`
3. Prepare rollback with a controlled ramp (recommended):
   - 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100% over 10–20 minutes (depending on SLO burn)
4. Execute rollback:
   - `failoverctl rollback --env prod --action-id <ACTION_ID> --strategy ramp --reason "Target region saturation; restore primary"`
5. Monitor:
   - 5xx rate, p99 latency, queue depth, capacity headroom, retry-after rates

### 7) Dedicated pool drain coordination (when failover impacts Dedicated)
Dedicated failover must coordinate with drain/evacuation to protect reserved workloads.

Steps:
1. Confirm Dedicated customer(s) and pools affected:
   - `failoverctl dedicated impacted --env prod --region <REGION>`
2. Start drain workflow (do not hard-cut without approval):
   - `failoverctl dedicated drain start --env prod --pool <POOL_ID> --reason "Regional impairment"`
3. Track progress metrics:
   - `failoverctl dedicated drain status --env prod --pool <POOL_ID>`
4. Only after drain gates are satisfied, approve/execute routing shift.

## Validation checklist (after enabling or after an action)
Within 5–15 minutes, confirm:
- Error rate returns to baseline; no sustained elevated 5xx
- p95/p99 latency stabilizes within SLO thresholds
- No surge in retries or reconnects (especially streaming)
- Target region capacity headroom remains >= 15–25%
- Audit events are being emitted and linked to the incident timeline
- Customer-visible comms are aligned (status page / support macros if needed)

## Troubleshooting

### A) Controller not making decisions (status shows stale last_decision_at)
Symptoms:
- `last_decision_at` older than 2–3 minutes
- No new recommendation metrics

Checks:
1. Pod health:
   - `kubectl -n routing-system get pods -l app=failover-controller`
2. Logs:
   - `kubectl -n routing-system logs deploy/failover-controller --since=15m`
3. Confirm region-health-service availability and latency.
4. Confirm perf-canary probe ingestion is current.

Fixes:
- If crash looping due to missing signal stream, switch to dry-run and apply hotfix configuration (missing-signal tolerant), then restart.
- If downstream control plane is degraded, keep controller in dry-run to avoid compounding issues.

Escalate:
- Ethan Park (control plane) if routing API is failing
- Allison Grant if telemetry ingestion appears delayed or missing

### B) Recommendations look wrong (false positives)
Common causes:
- Tail-latency spikes due to client retry storms rather than region impairment
- Partial control plane degradation misclassified as region reachability
- Metric label cardinality or aggregation issues causing incorrect burn calculations

Actions:
1. Verify signal evidence bundle:
   - Does the recommendation cite at least two corroborating sources?
2. Compare reachability probes vs. internal service metrics.
3. Increase dampening temporarily (with SRE approval) and keep execution disabled.
4. File a follow-up issue with examples (REC_IDs) and links to dashboards.

### C) Flapping / oscillation between regions
Immediate response:
1. Disable execution:
   - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "Automation flapping"`
2. Freeze routing policies at the current safest state.

Diagnosis:
- Check hold-down timer settings and whether emergency override is being triggered.
- Look for correlated signal volatility (reachability intermittent, capacity borderline).

Fixes:
- Increase hold-down timer; adjust corroboration logic per ADR-042.
- Adjust capacity thresholds; ensure target region has enough headroom before allowing flips.

### D) Approvals not working (cannot approve or approvals timing out)
Checks:
- `failoverctl approvals status --env prod`
- Confirm you are in the correct on-call group.
- Confirm incident-bot / approval provider is reachable.

Fallback:
- Temporarily switch to manual routing change (Routing On-call) while leaving controller in dry-run for evidence.

### E) Failover executed but customer still errors (no improvement)
Possible causes:
- Client DNS caching / TTL behavior delaying traffic shift
- Gateway retry budgets amplifying overload
- Streaming clients not handling redirect/reconnect correctly

Actions:
1. Verify actual traffic distribution by region (not just policy state).
2. Check DNS TTL/anycast notes and whether expected shift time matches reality.
3. Confirm gateway circuit breakers and retry-after behavior are active.
4. If streaming-specific: correlate disconnects with redirect events.

## Audit and documentation requirements
For any production execution (including game days):
- Ensure a reason is attached to mode changes, approvals, and rollbacks.
- Link REC_ID/ACTION_ID in the incident timeline.
- Confirm audit log retention meets enterprise expectations for affected tiers.
- Add a short summary in the incident channel and (if needed) a support note.

## Game day / controlled failover notes (operator-specific)
During controlled tests:
- Default posture: **execute-with-approvals**.
- Limit blast radius to approved tenants/routes/models.
- Use staged ramps (traffic weights) unless the scenario explicitly tests hard cut.
- Pre-write rollback commands and confirm authority.

## Post-incident / post-test tasks
1. Return mode to the intended steady state (usually dry-run or execute-with-approvals depending on rollout stage).
2. Verify alert tuning: ensure no excess paging noise.
3. Create follow-up tasks for:
   - Signal quality gaps
   - Dashboard/annotation gaps
   - Anti-flap/rollback tuning
   - Runbook edits based on operator confusion

## Appendix: Suggested commands
- Status:
  - `failoverctl status --env prod`
- Mode changes:
  - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode dry-run --reason "..."`
  - `failoverctl set-mode --env prod --mode execute-with-approvals --reason "..."`
- Recommendations:
  - `failoverctl recommendations list --env prod --since 30m`
  - `failoverctl recommendation show --env prod --id <REC_ID>`
- Approvals:
  - `failoverctl approve --env prod --id <REC_ID> --reason "..."`
- Actions and rollback:
  - `failoverctl actions list --env prod --since 2h`
  - `failoverctl rollback --env prod --action-id <ACTION_ID> --strategy ramp --reason "..."`
- Emergency stop:
  - `kubectl -n routing-system scale deploy/failover-controller --replicas=0`

## Changelog
- 2026-02-12: Added approval failure fallback path; clarified anti-flap and ramp rollback guidance; added Dedicated drain coordination section.
- 2026-01-20: Updated defaults to match ADR-043; added audit requirements and incident-bot expectations.
- 2026-01-05: Initial version (dry-run + execute-with-approvals workflows).

## Purpose
This runbook covers day-to-day operations for the **Routing Policy Failover Controller** ("failover-controller"): enabling/disabling automation, using **dry-run** mode, handling **approval workflows**, performing **rollbacks**, and troubleshooting.

The controller evaluates region-level impairment signals (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity exhaustion) and (optionally) executes routing policy changes to shift traffic away from impacted regions while preventing oscillation and overload.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Hosted API + Dedicated routing policy flips across regions
- Controller operation modes (disabled / dry-run / execute with approvals / execute fully automated)
- Anti-flap behavior (hold-down timers, rate limits)
- Rollback and stabilization workflows
- Auditability and incident-bot integration

**Out of scope**
- Customer-specific Private deployment automation (see Private guidance)
- Changing SLO contract language
- Core serving runtime changes

  - `failoverctl actions list --env prod --since 2h`
  - `failoverctl rollback --env prod --action-id <ACTION_ID> --strategy ramp --reason "..."`
- Emergency stop:
  - `kubectl -n routing-system scale deploy/failover-controller --replicas=0`

## Changelog
- 2026-02-12: Added approval failure fallback path; clarified anti-flap and ramp rollback guidance; added Dedicated drain coordination section.
- 2026-01-20: Updated defaults to match ADR-043; added audit requirements and incident-bot expectations.
- 2026-01-05: Initial version (dry-run + execute-with-approvals workflows).

source.title: Perf Canary Dashboards Spec (Grafana): Per-model latency & throughput
source.author: Logan Wright
source.content:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards used to monitor the production **perf-canary** service. The dashboards are designed for:
- Fast detection of per-model regressions in **p95/p99 latency** and **tokens/sec**
- Fast attribution to likely root cause area (kernel vs scheduler/queueing vs model config vs fleet)
- Direct navigation from an alert into dashboards, traces, and logs

These dashboards intentionally focus on **synthetic canary traffic** only (no customer prompts/data).

## Audience
- Serving Runtime (kernel/scheduling/instrumentation)
- SRE / on-call
- Telemetry / Observability platform
- Release Engineering (rollout gates)

## Dashboard set (Grafana)
We maintain three dashboards under the folder: **Runtime > Perf Canary**.

1) **Perf Canary / Overview**
   - Purpose: “Is anything broken right now?” and quick comparison across models/regions.

2) **Perf Canary / Model Deep Dive**
   - Purpose: detailed breakdown for a single model/version/region/SKU; primary triage view.

3) **Perf Canary / Infrastructure & Attribution**
   - Purpose: isolate queueing vs execution; correlate with runtime build, kernel selection, and fleet changes.

### Naming + discoverability
- Dashboard titles should start with **“Perf Canary / …”**.
- Each dashboard must include a top-of-page “Triage Links” row with:
  - Runbook link
  - Trace search link (pre-filled by model/region)
  - Logs link (perf-canary service)
  - Baseline artifact link (baseline store)

## Global dashboard variables
All dashboards must share the following variables (Grafana templating), in this order:

1) **env**: prod | staging
2) **region**: us-east | us-west | eu-west | ap-southeast (values from metrics)
3) **tier**: hosted | dedicated | private (where emitted)
4) **model**: canonical model identifier (e.g., llama-3-70b-instruct)
5) **model_version**: registry version/hash (optional filter; defaults to “all”)
6) **hardware_sku**: gpu sku (e.g., h100-sxm, a100-80gb)
7) **runtime_build**: runtime release/build tag (optional filter; defaults to “all”)
8) **kernel_id**: kernel selection identifier (optional filter; defaults to “all”)
9) **workload_profile**: canary prompt profile id (e.g., v0-standard)
10) **window**: 5m | 15m | 1h | 6h | 24h (Grafana time range selector is still primary)

Variable defaults:
- env=prod
- region=all
- tier=all
- model=top N (Grafana default set to “All” but pinned dashboard links from alerts will specify a model)

## Metric conventions assumed by dashboards
This spec assumes the perf-canary + runtime emit the following *logical* metrics (final names may differ; keep dashboards updated alongside schema changes):

Latency histograms (milliseconds):
- End-to-end request latency: includes queueing + runtime execution + response handling
- Runtime-only latency: excludes front-door and network
- Prefill latency (runtime)
- Decode latency (runtime)
- Scheduler queueing time

Throughput / efficiency:
- tokens/sec (effective)
- tokens/sec (decode-only, optional)
- batch size distribution
- queue depth (optional, if available)
- KV cache hit rate (where applicable)

### Required label set (and what NOT to include)
Required labels for perf-canary time series:
- env, region, tier
- model, model_version (must be canonical/stable)
- hardware_sku
- runtime_build
- kernel_id
- workload_profile

Explicitly disallowed in dashboard queries (cardinality risk):
- request_id
- prompt_id
- prompt_length_exact (use buckets)
- customer identifiers (must not exist on canary metrics)

## Dashboard 1: Perf Canary / Overview
### Goal
Provide a quick scan of current canary health across models, with emphasis on p95 and tokens/sec.

### Layout
Row A — “Topline regressions”
1) **Table: p95 end-to-end latency vs baseline (by model)**
   - Columns: model, region (optional), observed_p95, baseline_p95, delta_pct, sample_count, last_runtime_build
   - Sort: delta_pct desc
   - Notes: For overview, region may be aggregated (All) but allow drilldown by region.

2) **Table: tokens/sec vs baseline (by model)**
   - Columns: model, observed_tps, baseline_tps, delta_pct, sample_count
   - Sort: delta_pct asc (drops at top)

Row B — “Heatmaps and distributions”
3) **Heatmap: p95 end-to-end latency (model x region)**
4) **Heatmap: tokens/sec (model x region)**

Row C — “SLO-style trend lines (top models)”
5) **Time series: p95 latency (top models)**
6) **Time series: tokens/sec (top models)**
   - Use Grafana “multi-series” with legend showing model + region when region is not ‘all’.

Row D — “Canary health / sanity checks”
7) **Time series: canary request rate**
8) **Time series: error rate (non-2xx / internal error codes)**
9) **Time series: metric ingestion delay (if emitted)**

### Panel behavior requirements
- Every overview panel must allow one-click drilldown to **Model Deep Dive** via dashboard link, carrying variables.
- Tables must include a “min samples” indicator (e.g., show “LOW_SAMPLES” tag or gray rows below threshold).

## Dashboard 2: Perf Canary / Model Deep Dive
### Goal
Primary on-call triage view for a single model. Must answer:
- Is regression real (sample size / noise)?
- Is it queueing/scheduler, runtime execution, or upstream?
- Did anything change (runtime build, kernel id, model version, hardware SKU)?

### Layout
Row A — “Triage Links” (top of dashboard)
- Runbook: /eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert
- Trace search (Tempo/Honeycomb): pre-filter by env/region/service/model
- Logs search (Loki/Splunk): perf-canary service, regression evaluator output
- Baseline artifact (baseline store): model + version + hardware_sku + runtime_build

Row B — “Topline signals (with baseline overlay)”
1) **Time series: end-to-end latency p50/p95/p99**
   - Overlay: baseline p95 and baseline p99 as dashed lines (where available)
   - Annotation: runtime_build changes, kernel_id changes, fleet change events (if event stream exists)

2) **Time series: tokens/sec (effective) + baseline**

3) **Stat: regression evaluator state**
   - Shows: OK / WARN / PAGE
   - Shows: confidence / sample_count / window

Row C — “Breakdown: where time is spent”
4) **Stacked time series: queueing vs runtime (prefill+decode)**
   - queueing_time_ms_p95
   - runtime_prefill_ms_p95
   - runtime_decode_ms_p95

5) **Time series: scheduler queueing p50/p95/p99**
6) **Time series: prefill latency p50/p95/p99**
7) **Time series: decode latency p50/p95/p99**

Row D — “Batching + cache behavior”
8) **Histogram/heatmap: batch size distribution**
   - Display p50/p95 batch size as overlay if available

9) **Time series: KV cache hit rate (avg) (if applicable)**
10) **Time series: average concurrent sequences / in-flight (if available)**

Row E — “Change correlation (attribution helpers)”
11) **Table: runtime_build x kernel_id x observed p95**
    - Purpose: quickly see if regression aligns with a specific kernel selection or build tag.

12) **Table: hardware_sku x observed p95/tps**
    - Purpose: isolate SKU-specific issues.

### Required annotations
The Deep Dive dashboard must show annotations for:
- runtime_build rollout events (from release events feed)
- kernel_id change events (if emitted as events)
- fleet maintenance / GPU driver changes (infra events feed, if present)

If an events feed is not available in a region, omit annotations rather than showing stale data.

## Dashboard 3: Perf Canary / Infrastructure & Attribution
### Goal
A cross-model, cross-region view that helps answer “is this a platform-wide infrastructure issue or a model-specific runtime issue?”

### Layout
Row A — “Queueing and capacity signals”
1) **Time series: scheduler queueing p95 (by region, aggregated over top models)**
2) **Time series: canary request rate vs effective tokens/sec capacity (aggregate)**
3) **Time series: batch size p95 (by region/SKU)**

Row B — “Runtime vs infra split”
4) **Time series: runtime-only p95 (by runtime_build)**
5) **Time series: end-to-end p95 (by runtime_build)**
   - Purpose: if E2E moves but runtime-only does not, suspect upstream/network/edge.

Row C — “Kernel selection sanity”
6) **Table: kernel_id frequency (top kernel_ids per model)**
7) **Time series: p95 by kernel_id (single model selected)**

Row D — “Telemetry health”
8) **Time series: metrics ingestion lag**
9) **Time series: missing-series detector (count of expected series not reporting)**

## Query guidelines (PromQL-style; illustrative)
Dashboards should follow these guidelines:
- Use rate() / increase() over >=5m windows for counts.
- Use histogram_quantile() for p50/p95/p99 latency.
- Do not group by high-cardinality labels.
- Ensure every panel query has an explicit env filter.

Illustrative examples (update to match exact metric names):

1) p95 end-to-end latency:
- histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, model, region, tier, hardware_sku) (rate(perf_canary_request_latency_ms_bucket{env="$env", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m])))

2) tokens/sec:
- sum by (model, region, tier, hardware_sku) (rate(perf_canary_tokens_generated_total{env="$env", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m]))
  /
  sum by (model, region, tier, hardware_sku) (rate(perf_canary_request_duration_seconds_sum{env="$env", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m]))

3) queueing p95:
- histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, model, region) (rate(runtime_scheduler_queue_time_ms_bucket{env="$env", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m])))

4) batch size distribution:
- sum by (le, model, region) (rate(runtime_batch_size_bucket{env="$env", model=~"$model", region=~"$region"}[5m]))

Baseline overlays:
- Baseline values are stored as time series (preferred) or pushed as periodic gauges. The dashboard should treat baseline as read-only reference.

## Alert-to-dashboard linking requirements
Every perf-canary alert payload must include:
- A link to **Perf Canary / Model Deep Dive** with variables pre-set:
  - env, region, tier, model, model_version, hardware_sku, runtime_build, kernel_id, workload_profile
- A link to **Perf Canary / Overview** (for broader blast radius check)
- A link to the runbook

Dashboard links must be stable. If dashboard UID changes, update:
- incident-bot handler formatting
- alert rule annotations
- runbook links

## Trace and log link conventions
### Traces
We standardize on the following tags/fields for trace filtering:
- service.name: perf-canary
- redwood.model: <canonical model>
- redwood.model_version
- cloud.region
- redwood.runtime_build
- redwood.kernel_id

The “Trace search” link in dashboards should open the tracing UI with at least:
- region + model pinned
- a time range matching the alert firing window (e.g., last 30m)

### Logs
The “Logs search” link should filter:
- kubernetes.namespace = perf-canary
- kubernetes.cluster/region
- fields: model, runtime_build, regression_eval_result

## Usability and performance requirements
- Dashboard load time: <5s for default views in prod.
- Default queries should aggregate; only Deep Dive should allow high dimensionality.
- Avoid panels that render >2k series at once.
- For histogram-heavy views, prefer p50/p95/p99 lines over full heatmaps unless explicitly needed.

## Ownership and change management
- Primary owner: Telemetry Platform (eng-platform), with review by SRE and Serving Runtime.
- Any change to metric names/labels must include:
  1) Dashboard update PR (observability-pack)
  2) A short note in the runtime/perf-canary release notes
  3) Verification in staging that panels render and links resolve

## Acceptance checklist (for new panels or dashboards)
- Variables and filters match the standard set
- Panels render in prod for at least 2 models and 2 regions
- Baseline overlays display correctly (or panel explicitly notes “baseline unavailable”)
- Drilldowns work: Overview -> Deep Dive; alert link -> Deep Dive
- Trace and log links open with correct filters
- No new high-cardinality label usage introduced

## Appendix: “Top model” and “high-risk model” views
We keep two saved views (Grafana dashboard links with preset model regex):
- Top models: model=~"(llama-3-70b-instruct|qwen2.5-72b-instruct|mixtral-8x22b-instruct|...)"
- High-risk models: newly onboarded or recently kernel-optimized (maintained by runtime)

The exact regex list is maintained in the control plane config (source of truth) and should be periodically reconciled with dashboard saved views.
